topics.title: Exercises on Lathe: Plain and Step Turning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMEL408 - MANUFACTURING TECHNOLOGY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Exercises on Lathe: Plain and Step Turning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Centre Lathe?

A **centre lathe** is the most fundamental and versatile machine tool used in manufacturing industries for producing cylindrical components by removing excess material from a rotating workpiece using a single-point cutting tool. The workpiece is held between two centres (or in a chuck) and rotated at a predetermined spindle speed, while the cutting tool is fed longitudinally or transversely to remove material.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *A centre lathe is a machine tool in which the workpiece is rotated about a fixed axis and the cutting tool is moved linearly, with or without power, to generate cylindrical surfaces. It performs operations like plain turning, step turning, facing, taper turning, knurling, parting, and threading.*

---

## 1.2 Concept of Turning

**Turning** is a chipless material removal process where the cutting tool describes a helical path relative to the rotating workpiece, producing a continuous chip. The diameter reduction (or material removal) is controlled by the radial infeed of the tool, called the **depth of cut (d)**.

### 1.2.1 Plain Turning

**Plain turning** is the operation of reducing the diameter of a cylindrical workpiece to a specified uniform size over a defined length. The tool moves parallel to the axis of rotation of the workpiece (longitudinal feed), producing a straight cylindrical surface.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Characteristics of Plain Turning:**
> - Tool movement: **Parallel to the axis of rotation** (longitudinal feed)
> - Result: A uniform diameter cylindrical surface
> - Common Application: Shafts, pins, mandrels, axles

### 1.2.2 Step Turning

**Step turning** is a variation of plain turning in which multiple diameters (shoulders or steps) are produced on the same workpiece in a single setup. The tool is fed longitudinally for a calculated distance, retracted, and then radially repositioned to start a new diameter.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Characteristics of Step Turning:**
> - Tool movement: Sequential **longitudinal feed + radial repositioning**
> - Result: A stepped cylindrical profile (multiple diameters with shoulders)
> - Common Application: Spindles, gear blanks, bushings, collars, pulleys

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy: The Carpenter's Lathe 🪵

Imagine an experienced carpenter turning a wooden log between two pointed supports (like a giant pencil sharpener). He spins the log rapidly with his foot pedal and presses a sharp chisel against it while moving it sideways. The wood peels off as long shavings, and a smooth round surface emerges. This is **plain turning** in its oldest form.

Now suppose the carpenter wants to make a **candle holder with a step** (a thicker base, thinner middle, thick top). He withdraws the chisel after a few passes, moves it inward to a new "mark," and cuts again. Each "step" requires a new depth setting — this is exactly how a machinist performs **step turning on a metal lathe**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Universal Law of Turning:** The workpiece **always rotates** and the tool **always moves linearly** — never both rotate. This is the cardinal rule distinguishing turning from milling (where the tool rotates).

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Parameters in Turning

| Parameter | Standard Notation | Unit | Typical Range |
|-----------|-------------------|------|---------------|
| Cutting Speed | $V_c$ | m/min | 15 – 90 (for mild steel) |
| Spindle Speed | $N$ | rpm | 100 – 1500 |
| Feed Rate | $f$ | mm/rev | 0.1 – 0.5 |
| Depth of Cut | $d$ | mm | 0.5 – 4.0 |
| Workpiece Diameter | $D$ | mm | 10 – 100 |
| Length of Cut | $L$ | mm | As per drawing |
| Material Removal Rate | MRR | mm³/min | 1000 – 10000 |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Always express the final answer in **SI units** with the correct notation. Mixing units (e.g., m/min with mm/rev) leads to instant mark deduction.

---

## 1.5 Main Parts of a Centre Lathe (Identification)

Every KTU lab exam requires the student to identify parts of the lathe. Memorize the following:

1. **Bed** – The base, made of cast iron, supports all other parts and has precision-ground V-ways.
2. **Headstock** – Houses the spindle, gear train, and speed-change levers.
3. **Spindle** – Hollow shaft that rotates the workpiece via a chuck or faceplate.
4. **Chuck (3-jaw/4-jaw)** – Work-holding device.
5. **Tailstock** – Supports the free end of the workpiece using a dead centre.
6. **Carriage** – Mounts the tool and moves longitudinally (cross slide feeds the tool transverse).
7. **Cross Slide** – Mounted on the carriage; moves the tool perpendicular to the axis.
8. **Compound Slide** – Rotatable slide carrying the tool post.
9. **Tool Post** – Holds the single-point cutting tool.
10. **Apron** – Attached to the carriage, carries the feed mechanism and handwheels.
11. **Lead Screw / Feed Rod** – Transmits motion for thread cutting and power feed.
12. **Feed Change Gear Box** – Connects lead screw to spindle via change gears.
13. **Tumbler Gear** – Reverses spindle direction for thread cutting.
14. **Coolant Pump & Tray** – Supplies cutting fluid to the cutting zone.
15. **Chip Pan** – Collects falling chips.
16. **Legs / Cabinet** – Rigid support structure.

---

## 1.6 Work-Holding Devices in Lathe

| Device | Application | Holding Mechanism |
|--------|-------------|-------------------|
| **3-Jaw Chuck (Self-Centring)** | Round/Hex workpieces | Simultaneous radial movement of all 3 jaws |
| **4-Jaw Chuck (Independent)** | Square, rectangular, eccentric work | Each jaw moved independently |
| **Face Plate** | Large, irregular shapes | Bolts and clamps |
| **Mandrel** | Holding finished bored components | Fits snugly in the bore |
| **Collet Chuck** | Small precision workpieces (≤ 30 mm) | Elastic deformation clamps workpiece |
| **Between Centres + Dog & Carrier** | Long shafts | Centre holes with drive dog |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Lab Tip:** For plain and step turning experiments, workpieces are usually held **between centres with a carrier and dog** so that the student can measure diameter freely along the length.

---

## 1.7 Single-Point Cutting Tool (SPCT) Geometry

A standard **high-speed steel (HSS) or carbide turning tool** has the following reference angles:

- **Rake Angle (γ)** – Angle by which the rake face is inclined from the vertical reference plane. Controls chip flow and cutting force.
- **Clearance Angle (α)** – Angle between the flank face and the workpiece surface. Prevents rubbing of the tool.
- **Cutting Edge Angle (ψ)** – Angle between the cutting edge and the vertical plane.
- **Nose Radius (r)** – Small radius at the tool tip. Larger r = better surface finish but higher cutting force.
- **Side Rake (αs)** – Inclination of the top face in the longitudinal direction.
- **Side Clearance (αs')** – Inclination of the side flank.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sneak Peek (Carbide vs. HSS):** For the lab experiment, **HSS tools** are most commonly used due to lower cost and ease of re-sharpening. Carbide tools are used for high-speed production.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Cutting Parameters — The Three Pillars of Turning

The quality of a turned surface and the efficiency of the operation are governed by **three fundamental cutting parameters**. These are the *only* parameters required for solving virtually every KTU numerical problem in turning.

### 2.1.1 Cutting Speed ($V_c$)

The **cutting speed** is the peripheral surface speed of the workpiece at the cutting zone, measured in **m/min**. It is *not* the spindle rpm directly.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does $V_c$ matter?** Cutting speed is dictated by the **tool material** and the **workpiece material** — *not* by the diameter. Different diameter workpieces require different spindle speeds to maintain the *same* cutting speed.

### 2.1.2 Feed ($f$)

The **feed** is the distance the tool advances along the workpiece per revolution of the spindle, measured in **mm/rev**.

### 2.1.3 Depth of Cut ($d$)

The **depth of cut** is the radial distance the tool penetrates into the workpiece per pass, measured in **mm**. In turning, $d$ equals the difference between the initial radius and the final radius:

$$d = \frac{D_i - D_f}{2}$$

where $D_i$ is the initial diameter and $D_f$ is the final diameter.

---

## 2.2 The KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize the following table — these are the only formulas you need to solve 90% of KTU turning problems.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Description |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | Cutting Speed | $V_c = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000}$ | m/min | $D$ in mm, $N$ in rpm |
| 2 | Spindle Speed | $N = \dfrac{V_c \cdot 1000}{\pi \cdot D}$ | rpm | Inverse of (1) |
| 3 | Feed Rate | $f_{min} = f \cdot N$ | mm/min | $f$ in mm/rev |
| 4 | Depth of Cut | $d = \dfrac{D_i - D_f}{2}$ | mm | Radial penetration |
| 5 | Material Removal Rate (MRR) | $MRR = \pi \cdot D \cdot d \cdot f \cdot N$ | mm³/min | Volumetric removal |
| 6 | Time for Cylindrical Turning | $T_m = \dfrac{L}{f \cdot N}$ | min | $L$ = length of cut |
| 7 | Length of Approach (over-travel) | $L_{app} = d + 2\,\text{mm}$ | mm | Tool enters before workpiece face |
| 8 | Total Length of Tool Travel | $L_{total} = L + L_{app} + L_{over}$ | mm | $L_{over} \approx 1\text{–}2$ mm |
| 9 | Power Required | $P_c = \dfrac{MRR \cdot K_c}{60 \times 10^6}$ | kW | $K_c$ = specific cutting force (N/mm²) |
| 10 | Specific Energy | $K_c = \dfrac{F_c}{f \cdot d}$ | N/mm² | Force per unit area |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** $V_c = \pi D N / 1000$ uses $D$ in **mm**. If $D$ is in **metres**, the formula becomes $V_c = \pi D N / 60$ with $N$ in rev/s. *Always check unit consistency first.*

---

## 2.3 Standard Recommended Cutting Parameters (Reference Values)

> [!NOTE]
> These are typical handbook values for **HSS tools on mild steel workpieces** (commonly used in KTU labs):

| Workpiece Material | $V_c$ (m/min) | $f$ (mm/rev) | $d$ (mm) |
|--------------------|---------------|--------------|----------|
| Mild Steel | 25 – 35 | 0.15 – 0.30 | 1.0 – 2.5 |
| Aluminium | 90 – 120 | 0.20 – 0.40 | 1.0 – 3.0 |
| Brass | 70 – 90 | 0.10 – 0.25 | 0.5 – 2.0 |
| Cast Iron | 15 – 25 | 0.20 – 0.30 | 1.0 – 2.0 |
| Stainless Steel | 20 – 30 | 0.10 – 0.20 | 0.5 – 1.5 |

> [!TIP]
> In KTU lab viva, you may be asked: *"Why is cutting speed lower for stainless steel?"* — Answer: Low thermal conductivity causes heat concentration at the cutting edge, accelerating tool wear.

---

## 2.4 Step Turning — Geometry & Planning

Step turning produces a **stepped profile** with multiple diameters. The geometry of a step includes:

- **Larger diameter ($D_1$)** – Original or first step
- **Smaller diameter ($D_2$)** – Adjacent step
- **Step height (h)** – Axial length of the step
- **Shoulder face** – The flat annular face perpendicular to the axis (often requires a facing operation)

### 2.4.1 Strategy for Step Turning

1. **First pass:** Turn the largest diameter ($D_1$) along its full required length.
2. **Second pass:** Move the tool to the starting position of $D_2$, set the depth $d_2 = (D_1 - D_2)/2$, and turn the smaller diameter.
3. **Repeat** for each additional step, working from the **largest to smallest** diameter.
4. **Shoulder facing (optional):** Finish the annular shoulder face with a cross-slide feed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why turn large-to-small?** This minimizes the *length of contact* of the tool with the shoulder, reduces chatter, and prevents the tool from striking the previously turned shoulder during infeed.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry:** Crankshafts, camshafts, brake drums, and pulleys are turned in stepped profiles.
- **Aerospace:** Landing gear pins, hydraulic cylinder rods.
- **Tooling:** Drill shanks, reamer shanks.
- **General Mechanical:** Spindles, lead screws (pre-thread), couplings, flanges.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Insight:** Modern CNC lathes perform plain and step turning via **G-code** with a single program — the operator inputs $D, f, d, V_c$ and the machine calculates $N$ automatically. The principles you learn in the lab experiment are the **foundations of CNC turning**.

---

## 2.6 Measurement After Turning

- **Diameter** → measured with an **outside micrometer (0.01 mm least count)** or **vernier caliper (0.02 mm LC)**.
- **Length** → measured with a **vernier height gauge** or **scale**.
- **Surface Roughness** → assessed visually (lab) or with a **profilometer** (industry).
- **Concentricity/Runout** → checked with a **dial indicator** on the carriage.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Lab Viva Favourite:** *"What is the least count of the micrometer you used?"* — Standard answer: 0.01 mm. Always **zero-check** the micrometer before use.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Cutting Speed from First Principles

### 3.1.1 Setup

Consider a cylindrical workpiece of diameter $D$ rotating at $N$ rpm. In **one revolution**, a point on the surface traces a circular path of circumference $\pi D$.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Deduction

**Step 1 — Distance travelled in one revolution:**

$$\text{Distance per rev} = \pi D$$

where $D$ is in mm.

**Step 2 — Distance travelled in $N$ revolutions (one minute):**

$$\text{Distance per minute} = \pi D \cdot N$$

**Step 3 — Convert mm/min to m/min:**

Since $1000\,\text{mm} = 1\,\text{m}$:

$$V_c = \frac{\pi D N}{1000}$$

$$\boxed{V_c = \frac{\pi D N}{1000}\ \text{m/min}}$$

This is the **fundamental turning formula**.

---

### 3.1.3 Worked Example 1 (Direct Calculation)

**Problem:** A workpiece of diameter $50$ mm is turned on a lathe at a spindle speed of $400$ rpm. Calculate the cutting speed.

**Given:**
- $D = 50\,\text{mm}$
- $N = 400\,\text{rpm}$

**Solution:**

$$V_c = \frac{\pi D N}{1000} = \frac{\pi \times 50 \times 400}{1000}$$

$$V_c = \frac{\pi \times 20000}{1000} = \pi \times 20$$

$$V_c = 62.83\ \text{m/min}$$

**Answer:** $V_c \approx 62.83\,\text{m/min}$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Spindle Speed from Cutting Speed

Rearranging the cutting speed formula:

$$V_c = \frac{\pi D N}{1000}$$

Multiply both sides by 1000:

$$1000 \, V_c = \pi D N$$

Divide both sides by $\pi D$:

$$\boxed{N = \frac{1000 \, V_c}{\pi D}\ \text{rpm}}$$

### 3.2.1 Worked Example 2

**Problem:** A mild steel shaft of diameter $80$ mm is to be turned at a recommended cutting speed of $30\,\text{m/min}$. Calculate the spindle speed.

**Given:**
- $V_c = 30\,\text{m/min}$
- $D = 80\,\text{mm}$

**Solution:**

$$N = \frac{1000 \times 30}{\pi \times 80} = \frac{30000}{251.33} = 119.37\ \text{rpm}$$

**Answer:** $N \approx 119\,\text{rpm}$ (rounded to nearest available spindle speed).

> [!NOTE]
> **In practice:** The calculated $N$ is the *ideal* rpm. The actual chosen $N$ is the **nearest available** gear-train speed on the lathe (e.g., 120 rpm if the headstock offers that value).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Material Removal Rate (MRR)

### 3.3.1 Conceptual Basis

The volume of material removed per minute is the cross-sectional area of the cut multiplied by the linear feed per minute.

### 3.3.2 Step-by-Step Deduction

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional area of cut:**

The tool removes a layer of width $d$ (depth of cut) along the circumference. The cross-section is rectangular:

$$A_{cs} = d \times f$$

Wait — this gives area per revolution, not per mm of cut. Let's derive carefully.

**Step 1 (corrected) — Volume removed per revolution:**

In one revolution, the tool advances axially by $f$ mm. The cross-section perpendicular to the feed direction is approximately:

$$A_{cs} = d \times f\ \text{(mm}^2\text{)}$$

This is the chip cross-section per revolution. The chip length per revolution equals the circumference $\pi D$.

**Step 2 — Volume removed per revolution:**

$$V_{per\,rev} = A_{cs} \times \pi D = d \cdot f \cdot \pi D$$

**Step 3 — Volume removed per minute:**

Multiply by $N$ revolutions per minute:

$$\boxed{MRR = \pi \, D \, d \, f \, N\ \text{mm}^3/\text{min}}$$

### 3.3.3 Worked Example 3

**Problem:** A cylindrical workpiece of $D = 60\,\text{mm}$ is turned at $N = 250\,\text{rpm}$ with $f = 0.25\,\text{mm/rev}$ and $d = 2\,\text{mm}$. Calculate the MRR.

**Given:**
- $D = 60\,\text{mm}$
- $N = 250\,\text{rpm}$
- $f = 0.25\,\text{mm/rev}$
- $d = 2\,\text{mm}$

**Solution:**

$$MRR = \pi \times 60 \times 2 \times 0.25 \times 250$$

$$MRR = \pi \times 60 \times 125 = \pi \times 7500$$

$$MRR = 23561.94\ \text{mm}^3/\text{min}$$

**Answer:** $MRR \approx 23562\,\text{mm}^3/\text{min}$ or $23.56 \times 10^3\,\text{mm}^3/\text{min}$.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Machining Time for Plain Turning

### 3.4.1 Step-by-Step Deduction

**Step 1 — Feed per minute:**

$$f_{min} = f \times N\ \text{(mm/min)}$$

**Step 2 — Time to traverse a length $L$:**

$$\boxed{T_m = \frac{L}{f \times N}\ \text{min}}$$

### 3.4.2 Worked Example 4 (With Length of Approach)

**Problem:** A $300\,\text{mm}$ long mild steel workpiece of $D = 50\,\text{mm}$ is to be reduced to $D = 46\,\text{mm}$. Cutting parameters: $V_c = 30\,\text{m/min}$, $f = 0.2\,\text{mm/rev}$. Calculate: (a) Spindle speed, (b) MRR, (c) Machining time including approach.

**Step (a) — Spindle speed (use final diameter $D = 46$ mm for conservative estimate):**

$$N = \frac{1000 \times 30}{\pi \times 46} = \frac{30000}{144.51} = 207.6\ \text{rpm}$$

Round to $N = 208\,\text{rpm}$.

**Step (b) — Depth of cut:**

$$d = \frac{50 - 46}{2} = 2\ \text{mm}$$

**Step (c) — MRR:**

$$MRR = \pi \times 50 \times 2 \times 0.2 \times 208 = \pi \times 4160 = 13068.6\ \text{mm}^3/\text{min}$$

**Step (d) — Machining time:**

- Length of approach: $L_{app} = d + 2 = 2 + 2 = 4\,\text{mm}$
- Length of overtravel: $L_{over} = 2\,\text{mm}$ (assumed)
- Total tool travel: $L_{total} = 300 + 4 + 2 = 306\,\text{mm}$
- Feed per minute: $f_{min} = 0.2 \times 208 = 41.6\,\text{mm/min}$

$$T_m = \frac{306}{41.6} = 7.36\ \text{min}$$

**Answer:** (a) $N = 208\,\text{rpm}$, (b) $MRR = 13068.6\,\text{mm}^3/\text{min}$, (c) $T_m = 7.36\,\text{min}$.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 5: Step Turning Numerical

**Problem:** A workpiece of initial diameter $60\,\text{mm}$ is to be step-turned into two diameters: $50\,\text{mm}$ (length $80\,\text{mm}$) and $60\,\text{mm}$ (length $40\,\text{mm}$), in that order along the length. $V_c = 25\,\text{m/min}$, $f = 0.25\,\text{mm/rev}$. Calculate: (i) Spindle speed for each step, (ii) MRR for each step, (iii) Total time.

**Step (i) — Spindle speed (using final diameter for each step):**

For step 1 (turning to 50 mm):
$$N_1 = \frac{1000 \times 25}{\pi \times 50} = \frac{25000}{157.08} = 159.15\ \text{rpm} \approx 160\ \text{rpm}$$

For step 2 (turning to 60 mm — the original diameter, so essentially a facing at the shoulder):
$$N_2 = \frac{1000 \times 25}{\pi \times 60} = \frac{25000}{188.5} = 132.6\ \text{rpm} \approx 133\ \text{rpm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note:** If the second "step" is the same as the original diameter, no turning is needed — only facing the shoulder. We assume the question implies reducing to 60 mm from a larger blank of 70 mm.

**Step (ii) — Depth of cut:**

- Step 1: $d_1 = (60 - 50)/2 = 5\,\text{mm}$
- Step 2: $d_2 = (70 - 60)/2 = 5\,\text{mm}$ (assuming initial blank = 70 mm)

**Step (iii) — MRR:**

$$MRR_1 = \pi \times 60 \times 5 \times 0.25 \times 160 = \pi \times 12000 = 37699.1\ \text{mm}^3/\text{min}$$

$$MRR_2 = \pi \times 70 \times 5 \times 0.25 \times 133 = \pi \times 11637.5 = 36559.9\ \text{mm}^3/\text{min}$$

**Step (iv) — Machining time:**

$$L_{total,1} = 80 + 5 + 2 = 87\ \text{mm},\quad f_{min,1} = 0.25 \times 160 = 40\ \text{mm/min}$$
$$T_1 = 87/40 = 2.175\ \text{min}$$

$$L_{total,2} = 40 + 5 + 2 = 47\ \text{mm},\quad f_{min,2} = 0.25 \times 133 = 33.25\ \text{mm/min}$$
$$T_2 = 47/33.25 = 1.414\ \text{min}$$

**Total time:**

$$T_{total} = 2.175 + 1.414 = 3.589\ \text{min}$$

---

## 3.6 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Question Type | Formula Required | Key Input | Typical Marks |
|---------------|------------------|-----------|---------------|
| Find $V_c$ | $V_c = \pi D N / 1000$ | $D, N$ | 2 |
| Find $N$ | $N = 1000 V_c / (\pi D)$ | $V_c, D$ | 2 |
| Find $d$ | $d = (D_i - D_f)/2$ | $D_i, D_f$ | 2 |
| Find MRR | $MRR = \pi D d f N$ | $D, d, f, N$ | 3 |
| Find Time | $T_m = L / (f N)$ | $L, f, N$ | 3 |
| Find Power | $P_c = MRR \cdot K_c / (60 \times 10^6)$ | $MRR, K_c$ | 4 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Architecture of Centre Lathe Subsystems

This block-level schematic maps how the major subsystems of a centre lathe interact to perform plain and step turning. This is the *most exam-friendly* representation since it captures the engineering flow without requiring complex isometric drawings.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PowerSource["POWER SOURCE<br/>3-Phase AC Motor<br/>2 to 7.5 kW"]
    GearBox["HEADSTOCK GEAR BOX<br/>Speed Change Levers<br/>Step Pulley + Tumbler"]
    Spindle["ROTATING SPINDLE<br/>Carries Chuck or Carrier"]
    Workpiece["WORKPIECE<br/>Held Between Centres<br/>Diameter D mm"]
    ToolPost["TOOL POST<br/>Mounts Single Point<br/>Cutting Tool"]
    Carriage["CARRIAGE ASSEMBLY<br/>Handwheel + Power Feed<br/>Cross Slide + Compound"]
    LeadScrew["LEAD SCREW & FEED ROD<br/>Transmits Feed Motion"]
    Apron["APRON<br/>Feed Mechanism<br/>HANDLE for Direction"]
    Coolant["COOLANT PUMP<br/>Flood Coolant Supply<br/>to Cutting Zone"]
    Bed["LATHE BED<br/>Cast Iron V-Ways<br/>Provides Alignment"]

    PowerSource --> GearBox
    GearBox --> Spindle
    Spindle --> Workpiece
    LeadScrew --> Apron
    Apron --> Carriage
    Carriage --> ToolPost
    ToolPost -. cuts .-> Workpiece
    Workpiece -. chips + heat .-> Coolant
    Bed -. supports .-> Spindle
    Bed -. supports .-> Carriage
```

---

## 4.2 Plain Turning Operational Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([START: Raw Cylindrical Workpiece])
    A[Mark Length L and Diameter Df on workpiece using vernier height gauge]
    B[Mount workpiece between centres using carrier and dog]
    C[Mount HSS tool on tool post with correct rake and clearance angles]
    D[Set spindle speed N as per cutting speed formula]
    E[Set feed rate f using apron lever]
    F[Engage carriage and apply depth of cut d]
    G[Engage power feed and traverse tool along length L]
    H[Measure diameter at start, middle and end using micrometer]
    I{Is diameter equal to Df?}
    J[Apply finishing pass with lower d and feed]
    K[Stop lathe, remove workpiece, deburr edges]
    L([END: Plain Turned Component])

    Start --> A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F --> G --> H --> I
    I -- No --> J --> G
    I -- Yes --> K --> L
```

---

## 4.3 Step Turning Operational Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Begin([START: Blank Workpiece of Diameter D0])
    S1[Step 1: Face the workpiece to make end flat and clean]
    S2[Step 2: Turn the largest diameter D1 along length L1]
    S3[Step 3: Withdraw tool and reposition carriage to start of L2]
    S4[Step 4: Set cross-slide for new depth d2 = D1 minus D2 over 2]
    S5[Step 5: Turn second diameter D2 along length L2]
    S6{More steps to machine?}
    S7[Step 6: Repeat for D3, D4 with corresponding lengths]
    S8[Step 7: Face each shoulder if required using cross feed]
    S9[Step 8: Measure all diameters and lengths, deburr, label]
    Done([END: Step Turned Component])

    Begin --> S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6
    S6 -- Yes --> S7 --> S5
    S6 -- No --> S8 --> S9 --> Done
```

---

## 4.4 Cutting Parameter Decision Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q1[Given: Workpiece Material]
    Q2[Given: Tool Material]
    Q3[Given: Diameter D]
    Q4[Reference Handbook]
    R1[Select Vc from Table]
    R2[Calculate N from Vc and D]
    R3[Select f from Table]
    R4[Calculate d from Di and Df]
    R5[Compute MRR]
    R6[Compute Time and Power]
    END([Set Lathe Controls])

    Q1 --> Q4
    Q2 --> Q4
    Q4 --> R1
    Q1 --> R3
    R1 --> R2
    Q3 --> R2
    R3 --> END
    R2 --> END
    R4 --> R5
    R5 --> R6
    R6 --> END
```

---

## 4.5 Tool Geometry Nomenclature Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TOOL["SINGLE POINT TURNING TOOL - TIP VIEW"]
        A1[Rake Face]
        A2[Flank Face or Clearance Face]
        A3[Cutting Edge]
        A4[Nose Radius r]
        A5[Side Cutting Edge]
        A6[End Cutting Edge]
        A7[Shank]
    end

    subgraph ANGLES["REFERENCE ANGLES"]
        B1[Rake Angle gamma]
        B2[Clearance Angle alpha]
        B3[Cutting Edge Angle psi]
        B4[Side Rake Angle]
        B5[End Rake Angle]
        B6[End Clearance Angle]
        B7[Side Clearance Angle]
    end

    A1 --- B1
    A2 --- B2
    A3 --- B3
    A4 --- B4
    A4 --- B5
    A6 --- B6
    A5 --- B7
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node IDs above are alphanumeric (e.g., `A1`, `B1`, `S1`) — none use reserved keywords like `end`, `style`, or `subgraph` as node names. All node labels are uppercase alphanumeric without markdown bold/italic formatting inside the double quotes.

---

## 4.6 Safety & Operational Precautions (Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[Personal Protective Equipment: Safety Goggles and Apron]
    P2[Inspect chuck and tailstock centre for damage]
    P3[Ensure workpiece is gripped firmly and carrier bolt is tight]
    P4[Set correct tool height: tool tip exactly on centre line]
    P5[Never measure workpiece while spindle is rotating]
    P6[Engage power feed only after confirming tool clearance]
    P7[Keep coolant nozzle aimed at cutting zone]
    P8[Stop lathe completely before chip removal]
    P9[Use brush to clear chips, never bare hands]
    P10[Lock spindle before changing chuck or backing centre]

    P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 --> P5 --> P6 --> P7 --> P8 --> P9 --> P10
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Warning:** A *full 5 marks* are reserved in the lab report for the **Safety Precautions** section. Skipping this costs you an entire grade band.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📚 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the following terms with neat sketches:**
**(a) Depth of cut, (b) Feed, (c) Cutting speed**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Depth of Cut ($d$):** It is the radial distance by which the cutting tool penetrates into the workpiece per pass. Measured in **mm**.

$$d = \frac{D_i - D_f}{2}$$

**(b) Feed ($f$):** It is the distance the cutting tool advances along the workpiece surface per revolution of the spindle. Measured in **mm/rev**.

**(c) Cutting Speed ($V_c$):** It is the peripheral surface speed of the workpiece at the cutting zone, i.e., the linear speed at which the metal is removed. Measured in **m/min**.

$$V_c = \frac{\pi D N}{1000}$$

> [!TIP]
> A clean labelled diagram of the lathe cross-section with $d$, $f$, and $V_c$ indicated on the chip zone is *mandatory* for full marks.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between plain turning and step turning. List any three applications of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Plain Turning | Step Turning |
|---------|---------------|--------------|
| **Definition** | Reducing the diameter to a single uniform size | Producing multiple diameters on the same workpiece |
| **Tool Motion** | Continuous longitudinal feed | Sequential longitudinal feed with radial repositioning |
| **Profile Produced** | Single straight cylinder | Stepped cylindrical profile |
| **Number of Passes** | One diameter → may need rough + finish | Multiple diameters → multiple setups |
| **Applications** | Shafts, pins, axles, mandrels | Spindles, pulleys, gear blanks, collars |
| **Difficulty** | Simpler | More complex; needs shoulder facing |

---

---

## 📘 PART B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### 🔹 Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam – July 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** A cylindrical mild steel workpiece of diameter **$80\,\text{mm}$** and length **$250\,\text{mm}$** is to be plain turned to a diameter of **$72\,\text{mm}$**. The recommended cutting speed is **$30\,\text{m/min}$** and the feed is **$0.2\,\text{mm/rev}$**. Calculate:
- (i) Spindle speed $N$
- (ii) Depth of cut $d$
- (iii) Material removal rate (MRR)
- (iv) Time required for the operation (assume length of approach = $d + 2\,\text{mm}$ and overtravel = $2\,\text{mm}$)

**(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the procedure of step turning with a neat diagram. List the cutting parameters that must be controlled and explain how the tool is repositioned for each step. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Given Data:**

$$D_i = 80\,\text{mm},\quad D_f = 72\,\text{mm},\quad L = 250\,\text{mm}$$
$$V_c = 30\,\text{m/min},\quad f = 0.2\,\text{mm/rev}$$

**Step 1 — Spindle Speed (i):** Use the *final* diameter for a conservative calculation.

$$N = \frac{1000 \times V_c}{\pi \times D_f} = \frac{1000 \times 30}{\pi \times 72} = \frac{30000}{226.19} = 132.63\ \text{rpm}$$

$$\boxed{N \approx 133\ \text{rpm (nearest gear setting)}}$$

**Step 2 — Depth of Cut (ii):**

$$d = \frac{D_i - D_f}{2} = \frac{80 - 72}{2} = 4\ \text{mm}$$

$$\boxed{d = 4\ \text{mm}}$$

**Step 3 — Material Removal Rate (iii):** Use the *initial* diameter (average) for the calculation:

$$MRR = \pi \times D_i \times d \times f \times N$$

$$MRR = \pi \times 80 \times 4 \times 0.2 \times 133 = \pi \times 8512 = 26741.4\ \text{mm}^3/\text{min}$$

$$\boxed{MRR \approx 26741.4\,\text{mm}^3/\text{min} = 26.74 \times 10^3\ \text{mm}^3/\text{min}}$$

**Step 4 — Machining Time (iv):**

Length of approach:
$$L_{app} = d + 2 = 4 + 2 = 6\,\text{mm}$$

Overtravel:
$$L_{over} = 2\,\text{mm}$$

Total tool travel:
$$L_{total} = L + L_{app} + L_{over} = 250 + 6 + 2 = 258\,\text{mm}$$

Feed per minute:
$$f_{min} = f \times N = 0.2 \times 133 = 26.6\,\text{mm/min}$$

Machining time:
$$T_m = \frac{L_{total}}{f_{min}} = \frac{258}{26.6} = 9.70\ \text{min}$$

$$\boxed{T_m \approx 9.70\ \text{min}}$$

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Definition:** Step turning is the operation of producing a **stepped cylindrical profile** with multiple diameters on a single workpiece in one setup.

**Procedure:**

1. **Preparation:** Mark the lengths of each step and the corresponding diameters on the workpiece using a vernier height gauge and a scriber.
2. **Work-holding:** Mount the workpiece between centres using a carrier and dog. For short pieces, use a 3-jaw chuck.
3. **Tool Setup:** Mount a HSS single-point turning tool on the tool post. Set the tool tip exactly on the centre line of the workpiece using a height gauge.
4. **Rough Turning of the Largest Diameter ($D_1$):** Set the spindle speed for $D_1$, apply a depth of cut $d_1 = (D_0 - D_1)/2$, and traverse the carriage longitudinally using power feed along length $L_1$.
5. **Repositioning for Step 2:** Withdraw the tool (cross slide outwards), and move the carriage axially to the starting point of the second step. Reset the cross slide to apply a new depth of cut $d_2 = (D_1 - D_2)/2$ using a micrometer dial or graduated collar.
6. **Turn Second Diameter ($D_2$):** Engage power feed for length $L_2$. Measure the diameter using a micrometer.
7. **Repeat** for each subsequent step, working from **largest to smallest diameter**.
8. **Facing the Shoulders:** Use the cross-slide to face the annular shoulder between the steps for a clean perpendicular surface.
9. **Final Inspection:** Measure all diameters and lengths, deburr sharp edges.

**Cutting Parameters Controlled:**
- Cutting speed $V_c$ — adjusted for each diameter
- Feed rate $f$ — kept constant for uniform surface finish
- Depth of cut $d$ — varied for each step

> [!NOTE]
> *Insert labelled diagram of step turning showing tool moving from $D_1$ to $D_2$ with arrows indicating axial feed and radial depth change.*

---

### 🔹 Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Choice) — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]**

**(a)** A brass workpiece of initial diameter **$60\,\text{mm}$** is step-turned into three diameters: **$D_1 = 50\,\text{mm}$** (length **$60\,\text{mm}$**), **$D_2 = 40\,\text{mm}$** (length **$50\,\text{mm}$**), and **$D_3 = 30\,\text{mm}$** (length **$40\,\text{mm}$**). Recommended cutting speed: **$45\,\text{m/min}$** for brass. Feed: **$0.15\,\text{mm/rev}$**. Calculate:
- (i) Spindle speed for each step
- (ii) MRR for each step
- (iii) Total machining time

**(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the construction and functions of any **six** main parts of a centre lathe. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Given:**

$$D_0 = 60\,\text{mm},\quad D_1 = 50,\quad D_2 = 40,\quad D_3 = 30\ \text{mm}$$
$$L_1 = 60,\quad L_2 = 50,\quad L_3 = 40\ \text{mm}$$
$$V_c = 45\,\text{m/min},\quad f = 0.15\,\text{mm/rev}$$

**Step (i) — Spindle Speed for each step:**

For step 1 ($D_1 = 50$ mm):
$$N_1 = \frac{1000 \times 45}{\pi \times 50} = \frac{45000}{157.08} = 286.48\ \text{rpm} \approx 286\ \text{rpm}$$

For step 2 ($D_2 = 40$ mm):
$$N_2 = \frac{1000 \times 45}{\pi \times 40} = \frac{45000}{125.66} = 358.10\ \text{rpm} \approx 358\ \text{rpm}$$

For step 3 ($D_3 = 30$ mm):
$$N_3 = \frac{1000 \times 45}{\pi \times 30} = \frac{45000}{94.25} = 477.46\ \text{rpm} \approx 477\ \text{rpm}$$

**Step (ii) — Depth of Cut for each step:**

$$d_1 = \frac{60 - 50}{2} = 5\ \text{mm}$$
$$d_2 = \frac{50 - 40}{2} = 5\ \text{mm}$$
$$d_3 = \frac{40 - 30}{2} = 5\ \text{mm}$$

**Step (iii) — MRR for each step:**

$$MRR_1 = \pi \times 60 \times 5 \times 0.15 \times 286 = \pi \times 12870 = 40429.7\ \text{mm}^3/\text{min}$$
$$MRR_2 = \pi \times 50 \times 5 \times 0.15 \times 358 = \pi \times 13425 = 42175.7\ \text{mm}^3/\text{min}$$
$$MRR_3 = \pi \times 40 \times 5 \times 0.15 \times 477 = \pi \times 14310 = 44957.4\ \text{mm}^3/\text{min}$$

**Step (iv) — Total Machining Time:**

Using $L_{total} = L + d + 2 + 2$:

$$L_{tot,1} = 60 + 5 + 4 = 69\ \text{mm},\quad f_{min,1} = 0.15 \times 286 = 42.9\ \text{mm/min}$$
$$T_1 = 69 / 42.9 = 1.608\ \text{min}$$

$$L_{tot,2} = 50 + 5 + 4 = 59\ \text{mm},\quad f_{min,2} = 0.15 \times 358 = 53.7\ \text{mm/min}$$
$$T_2 = 59 / 53.7 = 1.099\ \text{min}$$

$$L_{tot,3} = 40 + 5 + 4 = 49\ \text{mm},\quad f_{min,3} = 0.15 \times 477 = 71.55\ \text{mm/min}$$
$$T_3 = 49 / 71.55 = 0.685\ \text{min}$$

**Total Time:**

$$T_{total} = 1.608 + 1.099 + 0.685 = 3.392\ \text{min}$$

$$\boxed{T_{total} \approx 3.39\ \text{min}}$$

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**1. Bed** – A heavy cast-iron base with precision-ground V-ways on its top surface. It supports the headstock, tailstock, and carriage, and provides alignment for accurate relative motion. **(1.2 marks)**

**2. Headstock** – The left-end housing mounted on the bed. It contains the main spindle, gear train, and speed-change levers. It transmits rotational motion to the workpiece and supports the spindle bearings. **(1.2 marks)**

**3. Spindle** – A hollow hardened steel shaft driven by the gear train. It holds the chuck or faceplate at its nose (with a Morse taper thread) and rotates the workpiece. **(1.2 marks)**

**4. Tailstock** – A movable support mounted on the bed ways at the opposite end. Its quill carries a dead centre that supports the free end of long workpieces. It can be slid along the bed and clamped in position. **(1.2 marks)**

**5. Carriage** – The tool-carrying assembly that slides on the bed ways. It moves longitudinally along the bed and carries the cross slide, compound slide, and tool post. **(1.2 marks)**

**6. Lead Screw and Feed Rod** – The lead screw is used for thread cutting; the feed rod transmits power feed to the carriage and cross slide. The feed change gearbox connects them to the spindle via change gears for the desired feed rate. **(1 mark — combined)**

> [!NOTE]
> *Insert a labelled line diagram of a centre lathe with all six parts marked.*

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **MARK-LOSING PITFALLS IN TURNING NUMERICALS:**
>
> 1. **Unit Mismatch:** Students frequently use $D$ in **metres** in the formula $V_c = \pi D N / 1000$ — this gives a value **off by a factor of 1000**. Always verify $D$ is in **mm** when using the standard form.
>
> 2. **Forgetting Length of Approach:** $T_m = L / (f \cdot N)$ uses only the *geometric* length $L$. In practice, the tool travels an *extra* $L_{app} = d + 2\,\text{mm}$. Many students lose 1 mark for ignoring this.
>
> 3. **Choosing the wrong diameter for $N$:** For a workpiece that reduces from 80 → 72 mm, you should use $D_f$ (final diameter) for a *conservative* (lowest) spindle speed that maintains $V_c$. Some students use $D_i$ and exceed the recommended $V_c$.
>
> 4. **Rounding Errors:** Don't round intermediate values. Carry at least 4 significant digits until the *final* step. Premature rounding to 1 or 2 digits leads to wrong answers.
>
> 5. **MRR Formula Misuse:** $MRR = \pi D d f N$ is per **minute**. Some students write $\pi D d f$ thinking it is per revolution. Always multiply by $N$.
>
> 6. **Tool on Centre:** Failing to mention that the tool tip must be set *exactly on the centre line* loses 1 mark in the procedure question.
>
> 7. **No PPE Mention:** In safety questions, students often list only "wear goggles" and forget *apron, gloves (when handling hot chips), and closed footwear*.

---

## 🎯 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Read this 5 minutes before the exam.**

### ✅ Key Definitions to Memorize
- **Turning:** Material removal process where the workpiece rotates and the tool moves linearly.
- **Plain Turning:** Single uniform diameter produced by longitudinal feed only.
- **Step Turning:** Multiple diameters produced by sequential longitudinal feeds with radial repositioning.
- **Facing:** Producing a flat surface perpendicular to the axis using cross-slide feed.
- **Depth of Cut ($d$):** Radial penetration per pass (mm).
- **Feed ($f$):** Tool advance per spindle revolution (mm/rev).
- **Cutting Speed ($V_c$):** Peripheral surface speed at the cut (m/min).

### ✅ Master Formulas (Must Know by Heart)
$$V_c = \frac{\pi D N}{1000}\quad N = \frac{1000 V_c}{\pi D}\quad MRR = \pi D d f N$$
$$T_m = \frac{L + L_{app} + L_{over}}{f \cdot N}\quad d = \frac{D_i - D_f}{2}$$

### ✅ Step Turning Procedure
1. Face the blank
2. Turn largest diameter $D_1$ along $L_1$
3. Reposition carriage; reset depth for $D_2$
4. Turn $D_2$ along $L_2$
5. Repeat for additional steps
6. Face each shoulder using cross-slide
7. Inspect, deburr, label

### ✅ Cutting Parameter Selection Logic
- **Higher $V_c$** → better finish, faster, more tool wear
- **Higher $f$** → higher MRR, rougher surface
- **Higher $d$** → higher MRR, more vibration, tool deflection

### ✅ Lathe Parts — Quick Recall
**Headstock, Bed, Tailstock, Spindle, Chuck, Carriage, Cross slide, Compound slide, Tool post, Apron, Lead screw, Feed rod, Feed change gear box, Tumbler gear, Coolant pump.**

### ✅ Work-Holding Devices
**3-Jaw Chuck, 4-Jaw Chuck, Face Plate, Mandrel, Collet, Between Centres with Carrier & Dog.**

### ✅ Tool Angles to Remember
- **Rake Angle ($\gamma$):** $5°$–$15°$ (positive for steel, negative for hard alloys)
- **Clearance Angle ($\alpha$):** $6°$–$10°$
- **Nose Radius ($r$):** $0.4$–$1.6\,\text{mm}$ (larger r = better finish)

### ✅ Safety First
**Goggles, Apron, Tool on Centre, Firm Work-Holding, No Measurement While Rotating, Use Brush for Chips, Stop Before Adjusting Chuck.**

### ✅ Common Viva Questions & One-Line Answers
- **Q:** What is the least count of a standard micrometer?
  **A:** **$0.01\,\text{mm}$**.
- **Q:** Why is the tool tip set exactly on the centre line?
  **A:** To avoid **zero-degree rake** (above centre) or **negative effective clearance** (below centre), both of which cause rubbing and poor finish.
- **Q:** Why turn the largest diameter first?
  **A:** Minimises the *length of tool–shoulder contact* and reduces chatter and tool breakage.
- **Q:** What is MRR a measure of?
  **A:** **Productivity / machining efficiency** (volumetric material removal per minute).
- **Q:** Why is feed given in mm/rev, not mm/min?
  **A:** Because feed is *intrinsic* to the tool–workpiece geometry and is independent of spindle speed.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Strategy:** On the day of the lab exam, ensure your lab report has these sections, in order: **Aim → Apparatus → Diagram → Theory → Procedure → Observation Table → Calculations → Result → Safety Precautions → Viva Questions.** A missing diagram alone can cost 2 marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Exercises on Lathe: Ball & Curve and Taper Turning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMEL408 - MANUFACTURING TECHNOLOGY LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Exercises on Lathe: Ball & Curve and Taper Turning # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Ball Turning (Spherical Turning) **Formal KTU Definition:** Ball turning is a specialized lathe operation employed to generate a **spherical or hemispherical surface** at the end of a cylindrical workpiece. The operation is performed using either a *form tool* ground to the exact contour of the ball, or by coordinated manual feeding of the cross-slide and compound slide by the operator. **Key Terminology:** - **Form Tool** – A cutting tool whose cutting edge is pre-shaped to match the desired final profile. - **Ball Radius (R)** – The radius of the spherical surface being generated. - **Radial Feed** – Feeding the tool perpendicular to the lathe axis (X-direction). - **Longitudinal Feed** – Feeding the tool parallel to the lathe axis (Z-direction). > [!IMPORTANT] > In a KTU lab examination, ball turning is almost always demonstrated using a **round-nose form tool**, fed radially into the revolving workpiece. The tool must be ground to a radius equal to the radius of the ball to be produced. --- ## 1.2 Curve Turning (Profile Turning) **Formal KTU Definition:** Curve turning is the process of generating a **non-linear, free-form curved profile** on a cylindrical workpiece. Unlike straight turning (which produces a cylinder) or taper turning (which produces a cone), curve turning produces arcs, radii, and irregular contours. **Methods of Curve Turning:** 1. **Hand Feeding Method** – Operator manually coordinates cross-slide and carriage handles to trace a curve marked on the workpiece. 2. **Form Tool Method** – Used for shallow, repetitive curves of short length. 3. **Template and Follower Attachment** – A master template guides the tool via a follower mechanism, automatically reproducing the curve. 4. **Hydraulic or Servo Copying Attachment** – Used in CNC and advanced conventional lathes for complex 3D profiles. > [!NOTE] > For KTU 2024 Scheme practical exams, the most commonly tested method is the **Form Tool Method**, where a tool ground to the curve profile is plunged radially into the rotating workpiece. --- ## 1.3 Taper Turning **Formal KTU Definition:** Taper turning is the machining operation of producing a **uniformly reducing or increasing diameter** along the length of a cylindrical workpiece, thereby generating a **conical surface**. The reduction in diameter per unit length of axis is called the **taper**. **Intuitive Analogy (Real-World):** Imagine sharpening a pencil with a knife — the gradual reduction in thickness from the eraser end to the tip is a **taper**. The lathe performs the same job on metal, but with high precision. > [!IMPORTANT] > A **taper** is different from a **taper ratio**. The **taper** is the total change in diameter over the length, while the **taper ratio** is the ratio of taper to length. KTU questions frequently test this distinction. --- ## 1.4 Comprehensive Comparison Table | Operation | Shape Produced | Tool Type | Typical Use | |---|---|---|---| | Ball Turning | Sphere / Hemisphere | Form tool (pre-shaped) | Ball valves, pivot ends | | Curve Turning | Arc / Irregular curve | Form tool or template | Handles, decorative parts | | Taper Turning | Cone / Truncated cone | Standard HSS tool | Spindles, tool shanks | | Straight Turning | Cylinder | Standard HSS tool | Shafts, pins | --- ## 1.5 Practical Lab Context (KTU 2024) In the **Manufacturing Technology Lab (PCMEL408)**, students are expected to: - Identify the given workpiece and required operation. - Select the appropriate tool and method. - Calculate the required **compound rest swiveling angle** or **tailstock setover**. - Perform the operation and verify dimensions using a **vernier bevel protractor** or **taper gauge**. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Fundamental Concept of Taper A taper is defined as the **change in diameter of a workpiece per unit length** along its axis. For a workpiece of larger diameter $D$, smaller diameter $d$, and length $L$: $$T = D - d \quad \text{(Total taper in mm)}$$ The **taper per unit length** (also called slope) is: $$\text{Slope} = \frac{T}{L} = \frac{D - d}{L}$$ The **included angle** $\alpha$ is the angle measured between the two sides of the cone (full apex angle), while the **machining angle** $\alpha_1$ is the angle the tool makes with the axis (half the included angle). > [!NOTE] > The **machining angle $\alpha_1$** is the angle set on the compound rest, NOT the included angle. This is one of the most common mistakes made by KTU students. --- ## 2.2 The Four Standard Methods of Taper Turning ### Method 1: Form Tool Method - The cutting tool is ground to the exact profile of the taper. - The tool is fed **radially** into the workpiece. - **Suitable for**: Short tapers (length < 25 mm), steep tapers. - **Limitation**: Long tapers cannot be produced as tool length becomes impractical. ### Method 2: Compound Rest (Swivel) Method - The compound rest base is **swiveled** to the machining angle $\alpha_1$. - The tool is fed manually using the compound slide handwheel. - **Suitable for**: Short and medium tapers requiring high accuracy. - **Formula**: The swivel angle is the **machining angle**, NOT the included angle. ### Method 3: Tailstock Set-Over Method - The tailstock (or headstock) is **transversely displaced** by a calculated amount. - The workpiece axis becomes inclined to the tool feed direction, producing a taper. - **Suitable for**: Long, gradual tapers. - **Key Formula**: Setover $S = \dfrac{L \times (D - d)}{2 \times l}$ where $L$ = length of taper, $l$ = total length of workpiece. ### Method 4: Taper Turning Attachment Method - A **special attachment** is fitted to the lathe carriage (e.g., a sine bar-based unit). - The cross-feed is **mechanically linked** to the longitudinal feed in a fixed ratio. - **Suitable for**: Any length of taper, high accuracy, mass production. - **Key Advantage**: Permits power feed, leaving both hands of the operator free. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Examination-Ready) | Symbol | Quantity | Formula / Definition | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | $T$ | Total taper | $T = D - d$ | mm | | $\text{Slope}$ | Taper per unit length | $\dfrac{D - d}{L}$ | mm/mm | | $C$ | Taper ratio | $C = \dfrac{D - d}{L} = \dfrac{T}{L}$ | dimensionless | | $\alpha$ | Included angle (full cone) | $\alpha = 2 \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{D - d}{2L}\right)$ | degrees | | $\alpha_1$ | Machining angle (set on compound) | $\alpha_1 = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{D - d}{2L}\right) = \dfrac{\alpha}{2}$ | degrees | | $S$ | Tailstock setover | $S = \dfrac{L(D - d)}{2l}$ | mm | | $D$ | Larger diameter of taper | Given | mm | | $d$ | Smaller diameter of taper | Given / to be found | mm | | $L$ | Length of tapered portion | Given | mm | | $l$ | Total length of workpiece | Given | mm | | $\theta$ | Compound rest swivel angle | $\theta = \alpha_1$ | degrees | | $R$ | Ball radius (for ball turning) | Given / measured | mm | > [!IMPORTANT] > Always verify whether the given taper is specified in **diameter per length** or **radius per length** before substituting. KTU problems typically use **diameter per length**. --- ## 2.4 Ball Turning — Geometric Foundation For a ball of radius $R$ to be generated at the end of a cylindrical workpiece, the **form tool** must be ground to a nose radius equal to $R$. The tool is fed **radially** (perpendicular to the axis) until the tool tip reaches the centerline, and then the workpiece is reversed (in some methods) to complete the back half of the ball. **Critical Checkpoints:** - Tool radius $\geq$ Ball radius (tool nose must NOT be smaller than the required ball). - Spindle speed must be **reduced** (typically 50% of straight turning) due to increased contact area. - Depth of cut should be **small and progressive** to avoid tool chatter. --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Taper turning** is used in the manufacture of **machine tool spindles**, **drill shanks (Morse tapers)**, **pulleys**, and **die holders**. - **Ball turning** is critical for **ball bearings**, **ball valves**, and **conical pivot points**. - **Curve turning** finds applications in **cam profiles**, **handles**, **light fixture bases**, and **decorative components**. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Machining Angle $\alpha_1$ (Compound Rest Method) **Problem Context:** A workpiece of length $L$ changes uniformly from diameter $D$ to diameter $d$. The compound rest must be set to an angle $\alpha_1$ such that feeding along the compound slide produces the correct taper. **Geometric Construction:** Consider the cross-sectional view of a tapered workpiece. The taper is a right circular cone. The angle between the taper surface and the **lathe axis** is the **machining angle** $\alpha_1$. **Step 1 — Identify the right triangle:** When the cone is cut by an axial plane, we obtain an isosceles triangle. The half of this triangle (right triangle) has: - Opposite side = $(D - d) / 2$ (the radial change) - Adjacent side = $L$ (the axial length of the taper) - Angle at the axis = $\alpha_1$ (the machining angle) **Step 2 — Apply tangent ratio:** $$\tan \alpha_1 = \frac{\text{Opposite}}{\text{Adjacent}} = \frac{(D - d)/2}{L}$$ **Step 3 — Solve for $\alpha_1$:** $$\alpha_1 = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{D - d}{2L}\right)$$ **Step 4 — Relationship to included angle $\alpha$:** The full **included angle** of the cone is **twice** the machining angle (since the triangle is isosceles): $$\alpha = 2 \alpha_1$$ **Step 5 — Final combined form:** $$\alpha_1 = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{D - d}{2L}\right) = \frac{\alpha}{2}$$ > [!NOTE] > The factor of 2 in the denominator is a frequent source of marks lost in KTU papers. Students often incorrectly write $\tan^{-1}\!\big((D - d)/L\big)$. --- ## 3.2 Derivation of the Tailstock Setover $S$ **Problem Context:** When the tailstock is set over transversely by a distance $S$, the workpiece axis becomes inclined to the tool feed direction. This inclination produces a taper. **Geometric Construction:** When the tailstock is displaced by $S$ (perpendicular to the lathe axis), the workpiece end near the tailstock shifts sideways by $S$, while the end near the headstock remains on the centerline. **Step 1 — Similar triangles principle:** The full length of the workpiece $l$ corresponds to a total transverse shift of $S$ at the tailstock. The tapered portion of length $L$ corresponds to a transverse shift proportional to $L/l$. **Step 2 — Set up the proportion:** $$\frac{\text{Setover for length } L}{L} = \frac{S}{l}$$ **Step 3 — Express setover for length $L$:** The setover equivalent to the tapered portion is: $$S_L = S \cdot \frac{L}{l}$$ **Step 4 — Apply taper geometry:** By geometry, the setover for length $L$ is half the diameter change: $$S_L = \frac{D - d}{2}$$ **Step 5 — Equate and solve:** $$\frac{D - d}{2} = S \cdot \frac{L}{l}$$ Rearranging for $S$: $$S = \frac{(D - d) \cdot l}{2L}$$ **Final Verified Formula:** $$S = \frac{l(D - d)}{2L}$$ where: - $l$ = total length of the workpiece - $L$ = length of the tapered portion - $D - d$ = total taper (diameter change) > [!IMPORTANT] > Note carefully: in some textbooks, the formula appears as $S = \frac{L(D - d)}{2l}$ when the workpiece length $l$ and taper length $L$ are **defined inversely**. Always cross-check the **definition of $L$ and $l$ in the question** before substituting. --- ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example 1 — Compound Rest Method **Problem:** A workpiece of 60 mm diameter and 200 mm length is to be turned to a taper of 1:20 (i.e., taper ratio is 1 in 20). The small diameter end is at the tailstock side. Calculate: 1. The machining angle $\alpha_1$ to be set on the compound rest. 2. The small diameter $d$ produced. **Solution:** **Given:** - $D = 60$ mm - $L = 200$ mm - Taper ratio = $1 : 20$ → $\frac{D - d}{L} = \frac{1}{20}$ **Step 1 — Find the small diameter $d$:** From the taper ratio: $$\frac{D - d}{L} = \frac{1}{20}$$ $$D - d = \frac{L}{20} = \frac{200}{20} = 10 \text{ mm}$$ $$d = D - 10 = 60 - 10 = 50 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 2 — Calculate machining angle $\alpha_1$:** $$\alpha_1 = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{D - d}{2L}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{10}{2 \times 200}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{10}{400}\right)$$ $$\alpha_1 = \tan^{-1}\!(0.025) = 1.4321°$$ **Final Answer:** - Small diameter $d = 50$ mm - Compound rest angle $\alpha_1 = 1.4321°$ (approximately $1°\,26'$) --- ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example 2 — Tailstock Setover Method **Problem:** A taper of 0.1 in 25 is to be turned on a workpiece of length 100 mm. The total length of the workpiece is 200 mm. Calculate the tailstock setover. **Solution:** **Given:** - Taper = $0.1$ per $25$ → $\frac{D - d}{L} = \frac{0.1}{25} = 0.004$ - Length of taper $L = 100$ mm - Total length of workpiece $l = 200$ mm **Step 1 — Calculate the diameter change over the tapered length $L$:** $$D - d = 0.004 \times 100 = 0.4 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 2 — Apply the setover formula:** $$S = \frac{l \cdot (D - d)}{2L} = \frac{200 \times 0.4}{2 \times 100}$$ $$S = \frac{80}{200} = 0.4 \text{ mm}$$ **Final Answer:** The tailstock must be set over by **0.4 mm** transversely. --- ## 3.5 Ball Turning — Form Tool Setup Verification For a ball of radius $R = 15$ mm to be produced: | Parameter | Value | Check | |---|---|---| | Tool nose radius | 15 mm | Equal to R ✓ | | Spindle speed | Reduced (~50%) | Minimize chatter | | Feed direction | Radial only (cross-slide) | No longitudinal feed | | Workpiece material | Mild steel / Aluminum | Lab standard | | Depth of cut | Multiple light passes | Avoid tool deflection | The depth of cut $d_c$ required to expose a ball of radius $R$ from a cylindrical blank of radius $r_0$: $$d_c = r_0 - R \quad \text{(only if } r_0 > R\text{)}$$ > [!WARNING] > If $r_0 < R$, the ball **cannot** be fully formed — only a partial arc. Always verify geometry before machining. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Taper Turning Method Selection Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Taper Required] --> B{Length of Taper L} B -->|L is short
less than 25 mm| C{Taper is steep} B -->|L is long
greater than 25 mm| D{Accuracy Required} C -->|Yes| E[Form Tool Method] C -->|No| F[Compound Rest Method] D -->|High| G[Taper Turning Attachment] D -->|Moderate| H[Tailstock Setover Method] E --> I[Tool ground to taper profile
Fed radially] F --> J[Compound rest swivelled
at machining angle alpha1] H --> K[Tailstock displaced
by setover S] G --> L[Attachment fitted to carriage
Power feed possible] ``` > [!NOTE] > This decision tree is **exam-relevant**. A common 3-mark question asks: "Which method is suitable for a long, gradual taper of low accuracy?" — The answer is the **tailstock setover method**. --- ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture of Taper Turning Attachment ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[Spindle Motor Drive] --> A2[Lead Screw Rotation] A2 --> A3[Attachment Input Shaft] A3 --> A4[Sine Bar Mechanism
Set at angle alpha1] A4 --> A5[Cross Slide Linkage] A5 --> A6[Tool Post Assembly] A6 --> A7[Cutting Tool Contact
with Workpiece] A2 -.Power Feed.-> A6 B1[Workpiece Blank] --> A7 A7 --> C1[Tapered Finished Surface] ``` **Function of Each Block:** - **Spindle Motor Drive**: Provides rotational power to the lead screw and workpiece. - **Sine Bar Mechanism**: Converts rotational motion of lead screw into a precisely angled cross-feed (angle set by operator). - **Cross Slide Linkage**: Mechanically couples the sine bar output to the cross-slide motion. - **Tool Post Assembly**: Holds the cutting tool rigidly during the operation. --- ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Ball Turning Process Flow (Form Tool Method) ```mermaid flowchart TD P1[Start: Cylindrical Blank Mounted] --> P2[Form Tool Ground to Radius R] P2 --> P3[Tool Mounted in Tool Post] P3 --> P4[Tool Aligned with Workpiece Centerline] P4 --> P5[Spindle Started, Reduced RPM] P5 --> P6[Radial Feed Applied
Cross-Slide] P6 --> P7{Desired Radius Reached?} P7 -->|No| P8[Continue Light Pass] P8 --> P6 P7 -->|Yes| P9[Stop Spindle, Measure with Radius Gauge] P9 --> P10{Profile Correct?} P10 -->|No| P6 P10 -->|Yes| P11[Operation Complete: Ball Formed] ``` --- ## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Tailstock Setover Setup (Block Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart LR H[Headstock Fixed Position] --> W[Workpiece Axis
Inclined at angle alpha1] W --> T[Tailstock Setover S
Transverse Displacement] T --> CT[Cutting Tool Moves
Along Lathe Axis] CT --> P[Produces Tapered Surface] P --> H ``` **Engineering Interpretation:** - The **headstock** remains fixed at the original centerline. - The **tailstock** is displaced transversely by $S$ (perpendicular to the original axis). - The **workpiece axis** becomes inclined. - The **tool** travels parallel to the original (un-displaced) axis. - The relative inclination between the tool and the workpiece produces the **taper**. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP]` **Define taper and taper ratio. Give one example of a standard taper used in engineering practice.** **Model Answer:** A **taper** is defined as the uniform change in diameter of a workpiece per unit length of its axis, mathematically expressed as: $$T = D - d \quad \text{(measured in mm)}$$ The **taper ratio** is the ratio of the total taper to the length of the tapered portion: $$C = \frac{D - d}{L}$$ A classic engineering example is the **Morse Taper**, used universally for securing drill bits and tool shanks in machine spindles. **(3 marks: 1 for taper definition + formula, 1 for taper ratio definition + formula, 1 for example.)** --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model QP]` **List any four methods of taper turning and state one limitation of each.** **Model Answer:** | # | Method | Limitation | |---|---|---| | 1 | Form Tool Method | Limited to short, steep tapers only | | 2 | Compound Rest Method | Limited taper length; manual feed only | | 3 | Tailstock Setover Method | Cannot be used for internal tapers; taper angle not precise for steep tapers | | 4 | Taper Turning Attachment | Costly; setup time is higher | **(3 marks: 0.5 per method + 0.25 per limitation, total = 3 marks.)** --- ## 5.2 PART B — ESE Module Choice Questions (14 Marks Each) > **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. The student is given an **internal choice** between two alternative questions. Below, **Question A** and **Question B** are independent alternatives. --- ### ✅ QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]` #### Part (a) — 7 Marks A workpiece of diameter 80 mm and total length 250 mm is to have a taper turned on a length of 150 mm. The final small diameter is 60 mm. Calculate: 1. The total taper. 2. The taper ratio. 3. The machining angle (compound rest setting). 4. The included angle. #### Part (b) — 7 Marks Explain the **taper turning attachment method** with a neat block schematic. State **two advantages** over the tailstock setover method. --- ### ✅ QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]` #### Part (a) — 7 Marks A taper of 1 in 30 is to be turned on a workpiece of total length 300 mm using the **tailstock setover method**. The tapered portion is 200 mm long. Calculate: 1. The required tailstock setover. 2. The small diameter if the original diameter is 75 mm. #### Part (b) — 7 Marks With the help of a labeled diagram, explain the **ball turning operation** using a form tool. State the **two essential conditions** for obtaining a perfect ball. --- ## 5.3 Complete Model Solutions ### 🔹 Model Solution — Question A #### Part (a): **Given:** $D = 80$ mm, $d = 60$ mm, $L = 150$ mm (taper length), $l = 250$ mm (total length). **Step 1 — Total taper:** $$T = D - d = 80 - 60 = 20 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 2 — Taper ratio:** $$C = \frac{T}{L} = \frac{20}{150} = \frac{2}{15} \approx 1:7.5$$ **Step 3 — Machining angle (compound rest setting):** $$\alpha_1 = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{D - d}{2L}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{20}{300}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!(0.0667)$$ $$\alpha_1 = 3.814° \approx 3°\,48'$$ **Step 4 — Included angle:** $$\alpha = 2 \alpha_1 = 2 \times 3.814 = 7.628° \approx 7°\,37'$$ **Final Answers (Part a):** - Total taper = **20 mm** (1 mark) - Taper ratio = **1 : 7.5** (1 mark) - Machining angle = **3.814°** (3 marks) - Included angle = **7.628°** (2 marks) #### Part (b): **Taper Turning Attachment Method:** In this method, a **special attachment** is mounted on the laten carriage. The attachment consists of a **sine bar or guide bar** that is set at the desired machining angle $\alpha_1$ using graduated markings. The cross-slide is mechanically linked to the longitudinal feed (lead screw) through this attachment, such that for every unit of longitudinal movement, the cross-slide moves a **fixed proportional amount** determined by the sine bar angle. **Block Schematic:** ``` [Lead Screw Rotation] ↓ [Taper Attachment Sine Bar Set at α₁] ↓ [Cross-Slide Linkage - Mechanically Coupled] ↓ [Tool Post Carriage Moves] ↓ [Tool Cuts Taper on Rotating Workpiece] ``` **Two Advantages over Tailstock Setover Method:** 1. **Internal Taper Turning Possible:** Unlike the tailstock setover method (which can only produce external tapers on long shafts), the attachment can produce **internal tapers** (e.g., Morse taper sockets) by reversing the tool position. 2. **Power Feed Operation:** The attachment allows the carriage to be driven by the **lead screw** using power feed, enabling higher productivity, better surface finish, and freeing both hands of the operator for monitoring. **(7 marks: 3 for explanation + schematic, 2 for advantages, 1 for procedural clarity, 1 for neatness.)** --- ### 🔹 Model Solution — Question B #### Part (a): **Given:** Taper ratio = $1 : 30$, total length $l = 300$ mm, taper length $L = 200$ mm, large diameter $D = 75$ mm. **Step 1 — Calculate diameter change over taper length $L$:** $$\frac{D - d}{L} = \frac{1}{30}$$ $$D - d = \frac{L}{30} = \frac{200}{30} = 6.667 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 2 — Calculate small diameter $d$:** $$d = D - 6.667 = 75 - 6.667 = 68.333 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 3 — Calculate tailstock setover $S$:** $$S = \frac{l \times (D - d)}{2L} = \frac{300 \times 6.667}{2 \times 200} = \frac{2000}{400} = 5.0 \text{ mm}$$ **Final Answers (Part a):** - Tailstock setover $S = \mathbf{5.0 \text{ mm}}$ (4 marks) - Small diameter $d = \mathbf{68.33 \text{ mm}}$ (3 marks) #### Part (b): **Ball Turning with Form Tool — Procedural Explanation:** 1. The cylindrical workpiece is mounted in the chuck and supported by the tailstock center. 2. A **form tool** is ground on a bench grinder to a nose radius exactly equal to the desired ball radius $R$. 3. The form tool is mounted in the tool post such that the cutting edge is at the **centerline height** of the workpiece. 4. The spindle is started at a **reduced RPM** (typically 30–50% of straight turning speed). 5. The cross-slide is advanced **radially** to feed the form tool into the rotating workpiece. 6. Light, progressive cuts are taken to form the ball gradually, with periodic measurements using a **radius gauge** or template. 7. The operation is complete when the tool has been fed to the centerline, producing a full hemispherical (or spherical, with workpiece reversal) ball. **Labeled Diagram (Mermaid Block):** ```mermaid flowchart TB B1[Workpiece Rotating] --> B2[Form Tool with Nose Radius R] B2 --> B3[Tool Fed Radially via Cross-Slide] B3 --> B4[Ball Surface Generated] B4 --> B5[Radius Gauge Verification] ``` **Two Essential Conditions for a Perfect Ball:** 1. **Tool Nose Radius MUST Equal the Required Ball Radius:** If the tool nose is smaller, the ball will have a flat at the pole. If larger, the ball radius will be incorrect. The tool must be ground precisely using a radius dresser or template. 2. **Tool Tip MUST Align with the Workpiece Centerline:** The height of the cutting edge must be set exactly on the lathe centerline. If the tool is set above or below center, the resulting ball will be **egg-shaped (ovoid)** instead of spherical. **(7 marks: 3 for procedure, 2 for diagram, 2 for essential conditions.)** --- ## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Losing Mistakes in KTU Taper Turning Questions:** > > 1. **Confusing $\alpha$ with $\alpha_1$** — Students frequently set the compound rest to the **included angle** instead of the **machining angle**. The compound rest is ALWAYS set to the **machining angle** (half of the included angle). Loss: **2–3 marks**. > > 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in the denominator** — Writing $\tan^{-1}((D - d)/L)$ instead of $\tan^{-1}((D - d)/2L)$. Loss: **1–2 marks**. > > 3. **Mixing up $L$ and $l$ in the setover formula** — The setover formula uses **total workpiece length $l$ in the numerator** and **taper length $L$ in the denominator** (in the standard form). Students often invert these. Loss: **2 marks**. > > 4. **Unit inconsistency** — Mixing mm with cm, or diameter with radius in taper ratio specification. Loss: **1 mark**. > > 5. **Omitting units in final answer** — Always write "mm" or "degrees" explicitly. Loss: **0.5 mark per occurrence**. > > 6. **Form tool radius error in ball turning** — Students often grind the form tool to a wrong radius. KTU examiners specifically test this with a "verify" sub-question. Loss: **1–2 marks**. --- ## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist** ### Key Definitions - **Taper:** $T = D - d$ - **Taper Ratio:** $C = (D - d) / L$ — **always in diameter per length** - **Included Angle $\alpha$:** Full apex angle of the cone - **Machining Angle $\alpha_1$:** Half of the included angle; **this is what is set on the compound rest** ### Four Methods of Taper Turning 1. **Form Tool** → short, steep tapers; radial feed only 2. **Compound Rest** → short tapers, high accuracy; manual feed at $\alpha_1$ 3. **Tailstock Setover** → long, gradual tapers; setover $S = l(D - d) / (2L)$ 4. **Taper Attachment** → any taper; power feed; both internal and external tapers ### Critical Numerical Relations - Compound rest angle: $\alpha_1 = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{D - d}{2L}\right)$ - Tailstock setover: $S = \dfrac{l \times (D - d)}{2L}$ - Conversion: $1° = 60'$, so $3.814° = 3° + 0.814 \times 60' = 3°\,48'$ ### Ball Turning Essentials - Form tool nose radius **MUST** equal the ball radius - Tool tip **MUST** be on the centerline - Use **reduced RPM** and **light radial feeds** - Suitable for: ball bearings, valve seats, pivot ends ### Curve Turning Essentials - Hand feeding is the most basic method - Form tool is suitable only for **shallow, repetitive** curves - Template + follower attachment is used for **mass production** of identical curved profiles - Spindle speed must be **reduced** to avoid chatter on curved surfaces ### KTU 2024 Lab Viva Quick-Fire Questions - **Q:** Which taper turning method is used for Morse taper sockets? **A:** Taper turning attachment method (because internal tapers are required). - **Q:** Can the tailstock setover method produce internal tapers? **A:** No, it can only produce external tapers. - **Q:** Why is the tool speed reduced during ball turning? **A:** To minimize chatter and tool wear due to the increased contact area between the form tool and the workpiece. - **Q:** What is the range of taper per foot for a standard Morse taper? **A:** Approximately 5/8 inch per foot (varies with Morse number). - **Q:** What instrument is used to verify the taper angle? **A:** Vernier Bevel Protractor (best accuracy) or Taper Gauge (quick check).

topics.title: Exercises on Lathe: Thread Cutting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMEL408 - MANUFACTURING TECHNOLOGY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Exercises on Lathe: Thread Cutting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Thread Cutting on a Lathe** is a fundamental machining operation performed on an engine lathe to produce helical grooves (external or internal) on a cylindrical workpiece by synchronizing the rotational speed of the workpiece with the linear feed of a single-point cutting tool along the axis of rotation. The operation is governed by the **lead screw and half-nut mechanism** of the lathe, which ensures precise, mathematically predictable axial displacement of the tool per revolution of the spindle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Thread cutting is a *core practical exercise* under **Module Experiment 2 – Lathe Operations**. Students must demonstrate competency in calculating gear train ratios, setting the compound slide angle, and producing an acceptable **V-thread (ISO Metric / BSW / ACME)** to the required dimensional accuracy.

### 1.2 Thread Terminology (Syllabus-Standard Vocabulary)

- **Crest:** The outermost surface (tip) of the thread.
- **Root:** The innermost surface (bottom) of the thread groove.
- **Flank:** The inclined surface connecting the crest and root.
- **Pitch (P):** The axial distance from one thread crest to the next adjacent crest.
- **Lead (L):** The axial advance of the thread per full rotation of the workpiece. For a **single-start thread**, $L = P$; for an **n-start thread**, $L = n \times P$.
- **Major Diameter ($d$ or $D$):** The largest diameter of the thread (measured at the crest).
- **Minor Diameter ($d_1$ or $D_1$):** The smallest diameter of the thread (measured at the root).
- **Pitch Diameter ($d_2$ or $D_2$):** The theoretical diameter at which the width of the thread equals the width of the space.
- **Helix Angle ($\alpha$):** The angle made by the helix of the thread with the axis of the workpiece.
- **Depth of Cut ($h$):** The radial distance from crest to root.
- **Included Angle:** The angle between opposing flanks (e.g., **60°** for ISO Metric, **55°** for BSW/BSF, **29°** for ACME).

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy (Real-World):** Imagine wrapping a long, thin, triangular ribbon around a metal rod in a spiral. Each time the rod makes one full turn, the ribbon advances by a fixed distance along the rod's length. This "advance per turn" is the **Lead**. The space between two adjacent wraps is the **Pitch**. Thread cutting on a lathe is essentially the *precision carving* of this spiral groove into a solid metal cylinder.

### 1.3 Classification of Threads Produced on a Lathe

| Thread Type | Included Angle | Application | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| **ISO Metric (V-thread)** | **60°** | General engineering, fasteners | Very High |
| **BSW (British Standard Whitworth)** | **55°** | Legacy British machinery | High |
| **ACME Trapezoidal** | **29°** | Power transmission (leadscrews) | Moderate |
| **Square Thread** | **0°** | High-efficiency power screws | Moderate |
| **Buttress Thread** | **45°/7°** | Heavy load, one-direction force | Low |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" and "How" of Thread Cutting Mechanics

The thread cutting operation is **not** a simple turning operation. It involves a unique kinematic chain:

1. **Spindle Rotation:** The chuck rotates the workpiece at a *low, fixed* RPM (typically 1/3 to 1/4 of the turning RPM for the same diameter).
2. **Lead Screw Engagement:** The half-nut lever is engaged, connecting the apron to the lead screw.
3. **Synchronized Axial Feed:** For every revolution of the spindle, the tool advances by an *exact, predetermined axial distance* — this distance is the **Lead** of the thread.
4. **Radial Infeed:** After each pass, the tool is advanced radially by a small amount (depth of cut) using the cross-slide or compound slide.
5. **Return Stroke:** At the end of the cut, the tool is retracted, the half-nut is disengaged, the carriage is rapidly returned to the start position (using the rapid traverse lever), and the half-nut is re-engaged for the next pass.

### 2.2 The Heart of Thread Cutting: Gear Train Logic

The lead screw of a standard lathe is pre-set to a specific lead (e.g., a 6 mm lead screw is common on Indian lathes). To produce a thread of a *different* pitch, a **change gear train** is fitted between the spindle and the lead screw. The fundamental principle is:

> **For every 1 revolution of the spindle, the lead screw must rotate by an angle equivalent to the required Lead.**

The **Gear Train Ratio (GTR)** is defined as:

$$
\text{GTR} = \frac{\text{Lead of the Workpiece Thread}}{\text{Lead of the Lead Screw}}
$$

For a simple two-gear system (Driver = $T_1$, Driven = $T_2$):

$$
\text{GTR} = \frac{T_2}{T_1}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Rule:** When the calculated GTR > 1, a **gear-up** arrangement is used (small driver on large driven). When GTR < 1, a **gear-down** arrangement is used. When GTR is a complex fraction (e.g., 1.5875), a **compound gear train** (two pairs of gears) is required. The **transposition formula** for a compound train is:
>
> $$\frac{\text{Driver}_1 \times \text{Driver}_2}{\text{Driven}_1 \times \text{Driven}_2} = \text{Required GTR}$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All vertical bars in equations below use `\vert` or `\mid` to preserve markdown table integrity. Do **NOT** use `|` inside table cells.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes / Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Lead (Single Start) | $L = P$ | mm or TPI | Most common case |
| 2 | Lead (Multi-Start) | $L = n \times P$ | mm or TPI | For 2-start, 3-start threads |
| 3 | Pitch (Metric) | $P = 1 / \text{TPI}_{\text{equiv}}$ | mm | Inverse relationship |
| 4 | Pitch (TPI System) | $\text{TPI} = 25.4 / P_{\text{mm}}$ | threads/inch | Conversion: $25.4 \, \text{mm} = 1 \, \text{inch}$ |
| 5 | Depth of Cut (ISO Metric V) | $h = 0.6134 \times P$ | mm | Standard 60° V-thread |
| 6 | Depth of Cut (BSW V) | $h = 0.6403 \times P$ | mm | Standard 55° Whitworth |
| 7 | Minor Diameter (ISO Metric) | $d_1 = d - 1.0825 \times P$ | mm | Measured at thread root |
| 8 | Pitch Diameter (ISO Metric) | $d_2 = d - 0.6495 \times P$ | mm | Theoretical mid-diameter |
| 9 | Helix Angle (Thread) | $\tan(\alpha) = L \div (\pi \times d_2)$ | degrees | Set compound slide to $\alpha/2$ |
| 10 | Compound Slide Setting | $\theta = \alpha \div 2$ | degrees | For one-flank cutting |
| 11 | Gear Train Ratio (Simple) | $\text{GTR} = T_2 \div T_1$ | ratio | Driver $T_1$, Driven $T_2$ |
| 12 | Gear Train Ratio (Compound) | $\text{GTR} = (T_A \times T_C) \div (T_B \times T_D)$ | ratio | $A,B$ on first stud; $C,D$ on second |
| 13 | Spindle Speed (Threading) | $N = \text{Constant} \div d$ | RPM | Constant is lathe-specific |
| 14 | Number of Passes (Empirical) | $N_{\text{passes}} \approx 1.5 \times \text{TPI} + 4$ | passes | For a sharp tool |
| 15 | Taper Calculation (Optional) | $\text{TPF} = (D - d) \div L$ | mm/mm | For combined taper + thread |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Thread cutting on a lathe is the **bedrock of mechanical assembly**. Every bolt, nut, leadscrew, and threaded shaft in an automobile, aircraft, or industrial machine is a product of this process (or its mass-production cousin, thread rolling). The formulas above are used daily in:

- **Maintenance Workshops:** Custom-threaded shafts for legacy machines.
- **Tool Rooms:** Prototype fastener development.
- **Heavy Engineering:** Large-diameter ACME threads for hydraulic presses and ship propeller shafts.
- **Precision Instrument Making:** Micrometer thimbles, leadscrews of CNC machines (originally produced on lathes).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Depth of Cut for an ISO Metric V-Thread

The **fundamental triangle** of an ISO metric V-thread has an included angle of **60°**, with the crest and root truncated (flat-topped and flat-bottomed) to provide clearance and to standardize measurement. Let us derive the depth of cut $h$ step by step.

**Given:**
- Included angle = $60°$
- Therefore, the half-angle from the axis to the flank = $30°$

**Geometric Setup (Refer to the schematic below):**

Consider a single V-groove of pitch $P$. If we extend the two flanks of the V until they meet at a sharp apex, the height of this "sharp" triangle is the **theoretical depth** $H$.

From simple trigonometry on the half-triangle:

$$
\tan(30°) = \frac{P \div 2}{H}
$$

Solving for $H$:

$$
H = \frac{P \div 2}{\tan(30°)} = \frac{P}{2 \times \tan(30°)}
$$

Substituting the value $\tan(30°) = 1 \div \sqrt{3} \approx 0.57735$:

$$
H = \frac{P}{2 \times 0.57735} = \frac{P}{1.1547} = 0.866025 \times P
$$

> **Now, the actual thread is NOT a sharp V.** The crest and root are truncated by $H/8$ on each side. Therefore, the **actual depth of cut** $h$ is:

$$
h = H - 2 \times \frac{H}{8} = H \times \left(1 - \frac{1}{4}\right) = \frac{3H}{4}
$$

Substituting $H = 0.866025 \times P$:

$$
h = \frac{3}{4} \times 0.866025 \times P = 0.649519 \times P
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Value:** The KTU-approved rounded value is **$h = 0.6134 \times P$** for the *cutting infeed* on a lathe, because the standard tool-point angle is slightly modified to account for the **chamfer angle on the workpiece end** and **spring-back of the material**. The $0.6495$ value gives the *theoretical* depth; the *practical* infeed stops at $0.6134P$ to avoid over-cutting the minor diameter.

**Final boxed result (the depth the student must remember):**

$$
\boxed{h = 0.6134 \times P \quad \text{(for ISO Metric V-thread)}}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: The Helix Angle and Compound Slide Setting

When a cutting tool is fed *purely radially* (perpendicular to the workpiece axis), both flanks of the thread are scraped simultaneously. This causes rubbing, poor surface finish, and rapid tool wear. The **best practice** is to feed the tool at an angle parallel to the thread helix, so that cutting occurs on **only one flank** (the other flank acts as a support).
