**The angle required is the helix angle $\alpha$**, which is the angle the thread helix makes with the axis. From the unrolled helix geometry:

If the workpiece has circumference $\pi \times d_2$ (using pitch diameter) and the thread advances axially by $L$ per turn, the helix is the hypotenuse of a right triangle with:
- Opposite side = $L$ (axial lead)
- Adjacent side = $\pi \times d_2$ (circumference at pitch diameter)

Therefore:

$$
\tan(\alpha) = \frac{L}{\pi \times d_2}
$$

For a **single-start thread** ($L = P$):

$$
\tan(\alpha) = \frac{P}{\pi \times d_2}
$$

**The compound slide is set to half this angle** for one-flank cutting:

$$
\theta_{\text{compound}} = \frac{\alpha}{2}
$$

**Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Standard):**

> A metric thread of nominal diameter **$d = 20 \, \text{mm}$** and pitch **$P = 2.5 \, \text{mm}$** is to be cut. Calculate the helix angle and the compound slide setting. Take $d_2 = d - 0.6495P$.

**Step 1:** Calculate pitch diameter:

$$
d_2 = 20 - 0.6495 \times 2.5 = 20 - 1.62375 = 18.37625 \, \text{mm}
$$

**Step 2:** Calculate helix angle:

$$
\tan(\alpha) = \frac{2.5}{\pi \times 18.37625} = \frac{2.5}{57.7318} = 0.04331
$$

$$
\alpha = \arctan(0.04331) = 2.48°
$$

**Step 3:** Compound slide setting:

$$
\boxed{\theta_{\text{compound}} = \frac{2.48°}{2} = 1.24°}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** You will *always* be required to compute $d_2$ first. Many students mistakenly use the major diameter $d$ in the helix formula, which inflates the angle. **Always use the pitch diameter.**

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Compound Gear Train Calculation

**Problem Statement (KTU-Style):**
> A lathe has a lead screw with **2 TPI** (threads per inch). Calculate the change gears required to cut a thread of **$4 \, \text{TPI}$** on a workpiece. If the simple ratio is not achievable, use a compound train.

**Step 1:** Compute the Gear Train Ratio:

$$
\text{GTR} = \frac{\text{Lead of Workpiece}}{\text{Lead of Lead Screw}} = \frac{1/4}{1/2} = \frac{0.25}{0.5} = 0.5 = \frac{1}{2}
$$

**Step 2:** Check if a simple gear pair works:

A 1:2 ratio can be achieved with, e.g., a 20-tooth driver and a 40-tooth driven gear.

$$
\text{GTR} = \frac{T_2}{T_1} = \frac{40}{20} = 2 \quad \text{(Wrong! We need 0.5)}
$$

**Correction:** For a *gear-down* (GTR < 1), the driver must be **larger** than the driven:

$$
\text{GTR} = \frac{T_2}{T_1} = \frac{20}{40} = 0.5 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 3:** For a more complex example — suppose we need to cut an **ISO metric thread of pitch $P = 1.5 \, \text{mm}$** using a lead screw of **$6 \, \text{mm}$** lead.

**Step 3a:** Convert the metric pitch to its inch-equivalent for comparison:

$$
\text{TPI}_{\text{equiv}} = \frac{25.4}{1.5} = 16.933 \, \text{TPI}
$$

**Step 3b:** Convert the lead screw lead to its TPI equivalent:

$$
\text{TPI}_{\text{LS}} = \frac{25.4}{6} = 4.233 \, \text{TPI}
$$

**Step 3c:** Calculate the Gear Train Ratio:

$$
\text{GTR} = \frac{\text{TPI}_{\text{LS}}}{\text{TPI}_{\text{equiv}}} = \frac{4.233}{16.933} = 0.25 = \frac{1}{4}
$$

**Step 3d:** This is a clean 1:4 ratio. Driver = 20T, Driven = 80T (gear-down). Or compound: 20T driving 40T (first pair), 20T driving 40T (second pair):

$$
\text{GTR} = \frac{20 \times 20}{40 \times 40} = \frac{400}{1600} = 0.25 = \frac{1}{4} \quad \checkmark
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** A common error is to use $P$ directly in mm without converting to TPI. The GTR is *dimensionless*, but the comparison must be in **the same unit system**. For metric threads on a lathe with an imperial lead screw (or vice versa), the $25.4$ conversion factor is **mandatory**.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Decision Table for Thread Cutting Setup

| Material Being Cut | Recommended Tool Material | Infeed Strategy | Coolant |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mild Steel | HSS (High Speed Steel) | Compound slide at $\alpha/2$ | Cutting oil (sulphurised) |
| Cast Iron | HSS or Carbide | Radial infeed (cross-slide) | Dry (or soluble oil mist) |
| Brass | HSS (sharp, polished) | Compound at $\alpha/2$ | Light oil (or dry) |
| Aluminium | HSS (high rake, polished) | Compound at $\alpha/2$ | Kerosene or soluble oil |
| Stainless Steel | Carbide (coated) | Radial with reduced infeed | Heavy cutting oil |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Thread Cutting Process Flow

This diagram maps the complete **block-level functional architecture** of a thread cutting operation on an engine lathe, from workpiece setup to final inspection.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Workpiece Mounting: Centre Lathe Chuck] --> B[Facing and Cylindrical Turning to Major Diameter]
    B --> C[Chamfering the Thread Runout End]
    C --> D[Tool Grinding: 60 degree HSS Threading Tool with Side Clearance]
    D --> E[Compound Slide Setting to Half Helix Angle]
    E --> F[Change Gear Selection and Installation]
    F --> G[Half-Nut Engagement Test: Check Lead]
    G --> H[First Pass: Light Infeed of 0.05 mm]
    H --> I[Return Stroke and Repeat Infeed]
    I --> J[Progressive Passes: Reducing Infeed]
    J --> K{Pitch Check with Screw Pitch Gauge}
    K -->|Correct| L[Final Pass: Spring Cut Zero Infeed]
    K -->|Incorrect| M[Adjust Compound Slide or Change Gears]
    M --> H
    L --> N[Thread Profile Check with Thread Profile Gauge]
    N --> O[Final Measurement: Major and Minor Diameter with Micrometer]
    O --> P[Record Observations and Submit to Examiner]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Gear Train Architecture

This diagram shows the **kinematic chain** of gear power transmission from the spindle to the lead screw.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Spindle Gear Z1] --> B[First Stud Gear Z2]
    B --> C[Intermediate Gear Z3]
    C --> D[Second Stud Gear Z4]
    D --> E[Lead Screw Gear Z5]
    E --> F[Lead Screw and Half-Nut]
    F --> G[Carriage and Tool Post]
    G --> H[Thread is Cut on Workpiece]
    A -.-> A1[Note: Gear Ratio equals Z2 times Z4 divided by Z1 times Z3]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Thread Geometry Cross-Section

A block-level schematic of the V-thread profile showing the relationship between major diameter, pitch diameter, minor diameter, and depth of cut.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Cylindrical Workpiece Axis] --> B[Major Diameter D at Crest]
    B --> C[Pitch Diameter D2 at Mid-Flank]
    C --> D[Minor Diameter D1 at Root]
    D --> E[Depth of Cut h equals 0.6134 times P]
    E --> F[Included Angle 60 degrees for ISO Metric]
    F --> G[Pitch P equals 1 over TPI]
    G --> H[Helix Angle alpha equal to arctan L over pi times D2]
    H --> A
```

## 4.4 Tool Geometry Diagram (Mermaid Block Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Thread Cutting Tool Blank] --> B[Grind Rake Angle: 5 to 15 degrees]
    A --> C[Grind Clearance Angle: 6 to 10 degrees]
    A --> D[Grind Thread Profile Angle: 60 degrees for ISO Metric]
    A --> E[Grind Side Relief Angle: 1 to 2 degrees]
    A --> F[Grind Front Relief Angle: 5 to 10 degrees]
    B --> G[Sharp Cutting Edge on One Flank]
    C --> G
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Tool is Ready for Threading Operation]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Adaptation Note:** As per the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 protocol, the diagrams above are **block-level functional architecture flows** that map the interactions and decision logic of the thread cutting process. These complement the *physical hand-drawn diagrams* that students are required to produce in their **practical record** (typically the tool geometry and the thread cross-section).

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define the following terms with respect to thread cutting: (i) Pitch, (ii) Lead, (iii) Helix angle.**

**Model Answer:**

- **(i) Pitch (P):** Pitch is the axial distance measured **parallel to the axis of the thread** from one point on a thread to the corresponding point on the next adjacent thread. In a single-start thread, it is the distance between two consecutive crests.
- **(ii) Lead (L):** Lead is the **axial distance travelled by the thread (or the nut/screw) in one complete rotation**. For a single-start thread, $L = P$. For an n-start thread, $L = n \times P$.
- **(iii) Helix Angle ($\alpha$):** The helix angle is the angle made by the thread helix (the path of the thread) with the axis of the cylindrical workpiece. It is calculated as $\tan(\alpha) = L \div (\pi \times d_2)$.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is the function of the half-nut mechanism in a lathe during thread cutting?**

**Model Answer:**

The **half-nut mechanism** is a lever-operated clamping device located on the apron of the lathe. Its function during thread cutting is to **engage or disengage the lead screw with the carriage drive mechanism**.

- **When engaged:** The half-nut closes around the lead screw threads, forcing the carriage to move in precise synchronization with the rotation of the lead screw. Since the lead screw is itself driven by the spindle (via the change gear train), the carriage — and hence the cutting tool — moves by an *exact, predetermined axial distance* per spindle revolution. This is the **Lead** of the thread.
- **When disengaged:** The half-nut opens, freeing the carriage from the lead screw. The carriage can then be rapidly returned to the starting position for the next pass.
- **Synchronization:** The half-nut must be engaged at the *exact same spindle position* for each pass to avoid producing a "double thread" or a spiral mismatch on the workpiece.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — With Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**On a centre lathe, it is required to cut an ISO metric external V-thread of nominal diameter $40 \, \text{mm}$ and pitch $3 \, \text{mm}$ on a mild steel workpiece. The lead screw of the lathe has a lead of $6 \, \text{mm}$.**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Calculate the following:
  (i) Depth of cut per side
  (ii) Minor diameter of the thread
  (iii) Pitch diameter of the thread
  (iv) Helix angle of the thread

**(b) [7 Marks]** Determine the change gear train required. Also explain the procedure of cutting the thread with a neat sketch of the tool geometry.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**Part (a) — Thread Calculations:**

**Given:** $d = 40 \, \text{mm}$, $P = 3 \, \text{mm}$, Lead screw lead $L_s = 6 \, \text{mm}$, ISO metric (60° V-thread, single start, so $L = P = 3 \, \text{mm}$).

**(i) Depth of cut (h):**

$$
h = 0.6134 \times P = 0.6134 \times 3 = 1.8402 \, \text{mm}
$$

Since this is the *full* depth, the **depth per side** (radial infeed) is:

$$
\boxed{h_{\text{side}} = 1.8402 \, \text{mm} \approx 1.84 \, \text{mm}}
$$

**(ii) Minor diameter ($d_1$):**

$$
d_1 = d - 1.2268 \times P = 40 - 1.2268 \times 3 = 40 - 3.6804 = 36.3196 \, \text{mm}
$$

$$
\boxed{d_1 \approx 36.32 \, \text{mm}}
$$

**(iii) Pitch diameter ($d_2$):**

$$
d_2 = d - 0.6495 \times P = 40 - 0.6495 \times 3 = 40 - 1.9485 = 38.0515 \, \text{mm}
$$

$$
\boxed{d_2 \approx 38.05 \, \text{mm}}
$$

**(iv) Helix angle ($\alpha$):**

$$
\tan(\alpha) = \frac{L}{\pi \times d_2} = \frac{3}{\pi \times 38.0515} = \frac{3}{119.535} = 0.02510
$$

$$
\alpha = \arctan(0.02510) = 1.438°
$$

$$
\boxed{\alpha \approx 1.44°}
$$

**Part (b) — Gear Train and Procedure:**

**Gear Train Calculation:**

First, convert both leads to a common unit. Since both are already in mm, the Gear Train Ratio is:

$$
\text{GTR} = \frac{L_{\text{workpiece}}}{L_{\text{lead screw}}} = \frac{3}{6} = 0.5 = \frac{1}{2}
$$

This is a **simple gear-down** arrangement. We need the driver to be **twice** the size of the driven (or driver:driven = 2:1... wait, **re-check**):

For a GTR of 0.5, with the simple formula $GTR = T_{\text{driven}} \div T_{\text{driver}}$:

If we choose driver $T_1 = 40$T and driven $T_2 = 20$T:

$$
\text{GTR} = \frac{20}{40} = 0.5 \quad \checkmark
$$

> **Note:** The "driver" is on the spindle side; the "driven" is on the lead screw side. Since we want a *gear-down* (the lead screw turns slowly), a *larger* gear drives a *smaller* gear.

**Recommended Gears (one of several valid answers):**

- **Driver $T_1 = 40$ teeth, Driven $T_2 = 20$ teeth** (simple 1-pair train, ratio 0.5).
- Or: **Compound Train:** $T_A = 60, T_B = 60, T_C = 30, T_D = 60$, giving:

$$
\text{GTR} = \frac{60 \times 30}{60 \times 60} = \frac{1800}{3600} = 0.5 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Procedure for Thread Cutting (Step-by-Step):**

1. **Preparation:** Mount and true the workpiece in the 3-jaw chuck. Set the tool post perpendicular to the workpiece axis.
2. **Turning:** Turn the workpiece outer diameter to the *nominal major diameter* ($40 \, \text{mm}$) with a finishing cut. Apply chamfer at the thread runout end (typically $1.5P \times 45°$).
3. **Tool Grinding:** Grind the HSS tool bit to the **60° thread profile angle** with proper rake ($5°$ to $15°$), clearance ($6°$ to $10°$), and side relief ($1°$ to $2°$). Ensure the **point is sharp** and the **side clearance is on both sides** so the tool does not rub the thread flanks.
4. **Tool Setting:** Mount the tool on the tool post with its **point on the centre line** of the workpiece (use a centre gauge). This is critical for correct thread geometry.
5. **Compound Slide Setting:** Set the compound slide to the **half-helix angle** (here, $1.44° \div 2 = 0.72°$) for one-flank cutting. The tool will feed along the helix, scraping material from one flank only.
6. **Change Gears:** Install the calculated change gears on the quadrant (apron end) and tension the gear train with the tumbler gear.
7. **Engagement Test:** Set the thread dial (or graduated collar) to the correct engagement mark for the thread pitch. Engage the half-nut and make a *zero-depth* scratch pass to verify the thread line is a single, straight helical line (not a spiral or double-thread).
8. **First Pass:** Apply a **light infeed of approximately 0.05 to 0.10 mm** on the compound slide. Engage half-nut and cut along the full length.
9. **Return and Repeat:** Disengage half-nut, return carriage rapidly, and engage at the same spindle position. Increase the infeed by another 0.05–0.10 mm.
10. **Pitch Check:** After 3–4 passes, use a **screw pitch gauge** to verify the thread pitch is correct.
11. **Spring Passes:** As the depth approaches the calculated $1.84 \, \text{mm}$, reduce the infeed to near-zero and make 1–2 "spring cuts" with no further infeed to achieve a clean surface finish.
12. **Final Inspection:** Use a **thread profile gauge (profile projector or optical comparator)** to confirm the thread form, and a **micrometer** to confirm the major and minor diameters.
13. **Parting:** Part off the workpiece (if required) using a parting tool.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] (Alternative Choice)

**Explain in detail the procedure for cutting an ACME thread of $30 \, \text{mm}$ major diameter, $6 \, \text{mm}$ pitch, single-start, on a lathe having a $5 \, \text{mm}$ lead screw.**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Calculate the depth of cut, the core (minor) diameter, the pitch diameter, the helix angle, and the compound slide setting for this ACME thread.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Determine the change gear ratio required. Discuss the difference between ACME and ISO metric threads in terms of geometry and application.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**Part (a) — ACME Thread Calculations:**

**Given:** $D = 30 \, \text{mm}$, $P = 6 \, \text{mm}$, single start ($L = P = 6 \, \text{mm}$), Lead screw lead $L_s = 5 \, \text{mm}$, ACME (29° included angle, half-angle = 14.5°).

> [!NOTE]
> **ACME Thread Geometry:** ACME threads are **trapezoidal** with a 29° included angle. Unlike the sharp V-thread, the **depth equals half the pitch** directly:
> $$h = \frac{P}{2}$$

**(i) Depth of cut (h):**

$$
\boxed{h = \frac{P}{2} = \frac{6}{2} = 3 \, \text{mm}}
$$

**(ii) Minor diameter (core diameter, $d_1$):**

For ACME threads:

$$
d_1 = d - P = 30 - 6 = 24 \, \text{mm}
$$

$$
\boxed{d_1 = 24 \, \text{mm}}
$$

**(iii) Pitch diameter ($d_2$):**

$$
d_2 = d - \frac{P}{2} = 30 - 3 = 27 \, \text{mm}
$$

$$
\boxed{d_2 = 27 \, \text{mm}}
$$

**(iv) Helix angle ($\alpha$):**

$$
\tan(\alpha) = \frac{L}{\pi \times d_2} = \frac{6}{\pi \times 27} = \frac{6}{84.823} = 0.07074
$$

$$
\alpha = \arctan(0.07074) = 4.05°
$$

**(v) Compound slide setting:**

$$
\theta = \frac{\alpha}{2} = \frac{4.05°}{2} = 2.02°
$$

$$
\boxed{\theta \approx 2.0°}
$$

**Part (b) — Gear Train and Comparison:**

**Gear Train Calculation:**

$$
\text{GTR} = \frac{L_{\text{workpiece}}}{L_{\text{lead screw}}} = \frac{6}{5} = 1.2
$$

Since GTR > 1, this is a **gear-up** arrangement. We can use a simple gear pair:

Driver $T_1 = 25$T, Driven $T_2 = 30$T (both available in standard change gear sets):

$$
\text{GTR} = \frac{30}{25} = 1.2 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Comparison: ACME vs ISO Metric V-Thread**

| Parameter | ISO Metric V-Thread | ACME Thread |
|---|---|---|
| **Included Angle** | **60°** | **29°** |
| **Thread Profile** | Triangular (sharp V, truncated) | Trapezoidal |
| **Depth of Cut** | $h = 0.6134 \times P$ | $h = 0.5 \times P$ |
| **Efficiency** | Lower (V-shape causes high friction) | Higher (flanks are nearly perpendicular to load) |
| **Self-Locking** | Yes (for small leads) | No (or weak) |
| **Load Capacity** | Lower (stress concentration at root) | Higher (larger root radius possible) |
| **Manufacturing Ease** | Easier (simple tool grinding) | Harder (precise tool form required) |
| **Typical Application** | Fasteners (bolts, nuts, screws) | Power transmission (leadscrews, machine tool slides) |
| **Standard** | ISO 261 | ASME B1.1 / IS 7003 |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas in Thread Cutting Problems:**
>
> 1. **Using the wrong diameter in the helix angle formula** (using $d$ instead of $d_2$): Loses 1–2 marks. Always compute $d_2$ FIRST.
> 2. **Forgetting the $25.4$ conversion factor** when mixing metric and imperial units: Loses 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Stating the depth as $0.6495P$ instead of the practical cutting infeed of $0.6134P$**: The $0.6495$ is the *theoretical* depth. The tool should be advanced only to $0.6134P$ to allow for the chamfer and spring-back.
> 4. **Setting the compound slide to the full helix angle $\alpha$ instead of $\alpha/2$**: This causes *both* flanks to cut, leading to poor finish. The correct setting is **half the helix angle** for one-flank cutting.
> 5. **In the gear train problem, swapping driver and driven**: A "gear-up" needs a smaller driver; a "gear-down" needs a larger driver. Drawing a sketch is highly recommended to avoid this confusion.
> 6. **Failing to engage the half-nut at the same spindle position** for each pass: Produces a "double thread" or "multi-start" error. The thread dial engagement mark is critical.
> 7. **Forgetting to chamfer the thread runout end**: The chamfer ($1.5P \times 45°$) is required for the nut to start engaging the thread.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Lead vs Pitch:** $L = P$ for single-start; $L = n \times P$ for n-start threads.
- [ ] **Depth of Cut (ISO Metric):** $\boxed{h = 0.6134 P}$ (practical infeed).
- [ ] **Minor Diameter (ISO Metric):** $\boxed{d_1 = d - 1.2268P}$.
- [ ] **Pitch Diameter (ISO Metric):** $\boxed{d_2 = d - 0.6495P}$.
- [ ] **Helix Angle:** $\boxed{\tan(\alpha) = L \div (\pi \times d_2)}$. **Use $d_2$, not $d$!**
- [ ] **Compound Slide Setting:** $\boxed{\theta = \alpha \div 2}$ for one-flank cutting.
- [ ] **Gear Train Ratio:** $\boxed{\text{GTR} = L_{\text{workpiece}} \div L_{\text{lead screw}}}$.
- [ ] **Conversion Factor:** $25.4 \, \text{mm} = 1 \, \text{inch}$. Use it whenever metric/imperial mixing occurs.
- [ ] **Tool Point Angle:** **60°** for ISO Metric, **55°** for BSW, **29°** for ACME.
- [ ] **ACME Depth:** $\boxed{h = P \div 2}$ (trapezoidal — half-pitch rule).
- [ ] **Half-Nut Engagement:** Must be at the *same spindle position* for every pass (use thread dial).
- [ ] **Chamfer:** Always provide $1.5P \times 45°$ chamfer at the runout end.
- [ ] **Tool Position:** The tool point must be exactly on the **centre line** of the workpiece.
- [ ] **Cutting Fluid:** Sulphurised cutting oil for steel; dry for cast iron; light oil for brass/aluminium.
- [ ] **Measurement:** Use **screw pitch gauge** for pitch, **micrometer** for diameter, **thread profile gauge** for form.
- [ ] **Passes:** Approximately $1.5 \times \text{TPI} + 4$ passes; reduce infeed as depth approaches $h$.
- [ ] **Spring Pass:** Final 1–2 passes with zero infeed for surface finish.
- [ ] **Spindle Speed:** $\approx$ 1/3 to 1/4 of the turning RPM for the same diameter.
- [ ] **Safety:** Always retract the tool, disengage the half-nut, and use the rapid return lever at the end of each pass.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Exercises on Lathe: Measurement of Cutting Forces in Turning Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMEL408 - MANUFACTURING TECHNOLOGY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Exercises on Lathe: Measurement of Cutting Forces in Turning Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In metal cutting theory (specifically orthogonal turning), the **cutting force** is the total resistance offered by the workpiece material against the relative motion of the cutting tool. This single physical quantity is resolved into **three orthogonal components** acting on the cutting tool insert, which form the foundation of machinability studies, tool-life prediction, and machine-tool design.

The three measured cutting-force components are:

| Component | Symbol | Direction of Action | Plane |
|---|---|---|---|
| Tangential / Cutting Force | $F_c$ | Tangent to cutting velocity (along $V_c$) | Vertical |
| Feed / Axial Force | $F_t$ | Parallel to feed direction | Horizontal (axial) |
| Radial / Thrust / Passive Force | $F_r$ | Radial inward (perpendicular to workpiece axis) | Horizontal (radial) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Lab Syllabus Note:** The lathe dynamometer (a force-measuring transducer mounted beneath the tool post) measures these three components simultaneously as the tool engages the rotating workpiece. The result is plotted using **Merchant's Circle Diagram**, which converts the measured $(F_c, F_t)$ pair into the shear-plane forces $(F_s, F_n)$ and the chip-tool interface forces $(F, N)$.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are **planing a wooden log with a chisel** held in your hand.

* The **tangential force ($F_c$)** is the *push* you must apply along the chisel's stroke direction to actually *cut* the wood fibre — this is the "hardest" force and does the real cutting work.
* The **feed force ($F_t$)** is the force required to *advance* the chisel horizontally into fresh material with each stroke.
* The **radial force ($F_r$)** is the sideways *squeeze* that pushes the chisel away from the log, perpendicular to the cut — it doesn't cut, but it deflects the tool and causes vibration.

Now place the chisel on a sensitive **bathroom weighing scale** clamped to the workbench. As you push and slice, the scale reads $F_c$ in one axis, a spring gauge on the side reads $F_t$, and a third gauge on the opposite side reads $F_r$. *That* three-gauge rig is conceptually identical to a **piezoelectric / strain-gauge lathe dynamometer**.

### 1.3 Standard Physical & Material Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Constants Used in Force Analysis**
> * Acceleration due to gravity: $g = 9.81 \, m/s^2$
> * Cutting velocity formula constant: $V_c = \dfrac{\pi D N}{1000} \; m/min$ (where $D$ is in mm and $N$ is in rpm)
> * **Specific Cutting Energy ($U_c$)** for mild steel ≈ **2.0 – 2.5 N·m/mm³**; for aluminium ≈ **0.4 – 0.8 N·m/mm³** (these are **workpiece-material dependent empirical ranges**, not constants).
> * Typical range of cutting force $F_c$ for mild-steel turning: **500 N – 2500 N** per mm depth of cut.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Orthogonal Cutting Model (Why Three Components?)

Turning is modelled as **orthogonal cutting** by unrolling the cylindrical workpiece surface onto a flat plane. The cutting edge is treated as a straight line, and the tool moves at velocity $V$ with uncut chip thickness $t_1$ and chip thickness after cut $t_2$.

The three forces arise from **three independent physical phenomena**:

1. **Shearing of material along the shear plane** (gives rise to $F_s$ on the shear plane, resolved into $F_c$ and $F_t$).
2. **Frictional rubbing at the chip–tool interface (rake face)** (gives rise to the friction force $F$, inclined at friction angle $\beta$ to the normal of the rake face).
3. **Elastic recovery and ploughing** at the tool flank (contributes mainly to the radial component $F_r$).

### 2.2 Geometric Parameters of the Cutting System

| Symbol | Parameter | Typical Range (Mild Steel) |
|---|---|---|
| $\alpha$ | Rake angle | $+5^\circ$ to $+15^\circ$ |
| $\phi$ | Shear angle | $25^\circ$ to $45^\circ$ |
| $\beta$ | Friction angle at tool–chip interface | $20^\circ$ to $45^\circ$ |
| $\gamma$ | Clearance angle | $6^\circ$ to $15^\circ$ |
| $r$ | Cutting ratio (chip thickness ratio) $= t_1/t_2$ | $0.4$ to $0.7$ |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they cover 90 % of numerical questions asked on this topic.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{(1) Resultant Cutting Force:}\quad F \;=\; \sqrt{F_c^{\,2} + F_t^{\,2}} \\[4pt]
&\textbf{(2) Merchant's Circle Resolutions:}\\[2pt]
&\quad F_s \;=\; F_c \cos\phi \;-\; F_t \sin\phi \\[2pt]
&\quad F_n \;=\; F_c \sin\phi \;+\; F_t \cos\phi \\[2pt]
&\textbf{(3) Coefficient of Friction:}\quad \mu \;=\; \tan\beta \;=\; \dfrac{F_n}{F_s} \;=\; \dfrac{F_c \sin\phi + F_t \cos\phi}{F_c \cos\phi - F_t \sin\phi} \\[4pt]
&\textbf{(4) Shear Angle (Merchant):}\quad \tan\phi \;=\; \dfrac{r \cos\alpha}{1 \;-\; r \sin\alpha} \\[4pt]
&\textbf{(5) Cutting Velocity:}\quad V_c \;=\; \dfrac{\pi D N}{1000} \;\; \dfrac{m}{min} \\[4pt]
&\textbf{(6) Material Removal Rate:}\quad \text{MRR} \;=\; f \cdot d \cdot V_c \;\; \dfrac{mm^3}{min} \\[4pt]
&\textbf{(7) Power Required at Spindle:}\quad P \;=\; \dfrac{F_c \cdot V_c}{60\,000} \;\; kW \\[4pt]
&\textbf{(8) Specific Cutting Energy:}\quad U_c \;=\; \dfrac{P}{\text{MRR}} \;\; \dfrac{N \cdot m}{mm^3} \\[4pt]
&\textbf{(9) Chip Thickness Ratio:}\quad r \;=\; \dfrac{t_1}{t_2} \;=\; \dfrac{\text{uncut chip thickness}}{\text{chip thickness after cut}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Symbol Glossary:** $D$ = workpiece diameter (mm); $N$ = spindle speed (rpm); $f$ = feed (mm/rev); $d$ = depth of cut (mm); $F_s$ = shear-plane force (N); $F_n$ = normal force on shear plane (N).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Why Cutting-Force Data Is Needed |
|---|---|
| **CNC Machine Design** | Spindle bearings, ball-screws and slide-ways must be sized to withstand peak $F_c$ without deflection. |
| **Tool-Wear Monitoring** | A rising $F_c$ (with constant feed/speed) signals progressive flank wear — basis of *adaptive control* machining. |
| **FEM Tool-Design** | Tool inserts are designed by feeding measured $(F_c, F_t, F_r)$ into finite-element simulations of insert stress. |
| **Cutting-Parameter Optimisation** | Minimum-cost / minimum-time machining cycles are computed from $U_c$ vs $(V_c, f, d)$ data. |
| **Chatter Prediction** | The dynamic component of $F_r$ drives regenerative chatter — stability lobe diagrams use this force. |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Merchant Force Circle (From $F_c, F_t$ to $F_s, F_n$)

**Statement to Prove:**
$$
F_s \;=\; F_c \cos\phi \;-\; F_t \sin\phi \qquad \text{and} \qquad F_n \;=\; F_c \sin\phi \;+\; F_t \cos\phi
$$

**Assumptions**

1. Orthogonal cutting (single straight cutting edge).
2. Tool is sharp (flank contact neglected).
3. Forces are steady-state (no vibration).
4. Shear plane is a flat plane inclined at $\phi$ to the cutting velocity.

**Geometric Setup**

The cutting force $F$ acts on the tool, and by Newton's third law, an equal and opposite reaction acts on the chip. The shear plane is inclined at angle $\phi$ to the cutting direction. The forces $F_c$ (cutting) and $F_t$ (feed) are measured along Cartesian axes $x$ and $y$.

**Step-by-Step Logical Transitions**

We project the shear-plane forces $(F_s, F_n)$ onto the cutting and feed directions:

* The shear force $F_s$ acts *along* the shear plane. Its component along the cutting direction ($F_c$ axis) is $F_s \cos\phi$, and along the feed direction ($F_t$ axis) is $-F_s \sin\phi$ (negative because it opposes feed).

* The normal force $F_n$ acts *perpendicular* to the shear plane. Its component along the cutting direction is $F_n \sin\phi$, and along the feed direction is $F_n \cos\phi$.

Equilibrium of the chip in the $x$-direction (cutting direction):

$$
F_c \;=\; F_s \cos\phi \;+\; F_n \sin\phi \tag{A}
$$

Equilibrium of the chip in the $y$-direction (feed direction):

$$
F_t \;=\; -F_s \sin\phi \;+\; F_n \cos\phi \tag{B}
$$

**Solving (A) and (B) simultaneously** for $F_s$ and $F_n$:

Multiply (A) by $\cos\phi$ and (B) by $\sin\phi$:

$$
F_c \cos\phi \;=\; F_s \cos^2\phi \;+\; F_n \sin\phi \cos\phi
$$

$$
F_t \sin\phi \;=\; -F_s \sin^2\phi \;+\; F_n \sin\phi \cos\phi
$$

Subtracting the second from the first:

$$
F_c \cos\phi \;-\; F_t \sin\phi \;=\; F_s(\cos^2\phi + \sin^2\phi) \;=\; F_s
$$

Multiplying (A) by $\sin\phi$ and (B) by $\cos\phi$ and adding:

$$
F_c \sin\phi \;+\; F_t \cos\phi \;=\; F_n(\sin^2\phi + \cos^2\phi) \;=\; F_n
$$

**Hence proved.**

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Coefficient of Friction $\mu = \tan\beta$

The friction angle $\beta$ is defined as the angle between the normal $N$ to the rake face and the resultant tool-chip friction force $F$. Therefore:

$$
\mu \;=\; \tan\beta
$$

The magnitude of the tool-chip friction force is:

$$
F \;=\; \sqrt{F_c^{\,2} + F_t^{\,2}}
$$

From the Merchant circle geometry:

$$
\sin\beta \;=\; \dfrac{F_n}{F}, \qquad \cos\beta \;=\; \dfrac{F_s}{F}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\tan\beta \;=\; \dfrac{F_n}{F_s} \;=\; \dfrac{F_c \sin\phi + F_t \cos\phi}{F_c \cos\phi - F_t \sin\phi}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** The angle $\beta$ is a single number that captures the *combined* effect of tool-coating, chip material, contact pressure and temperature at the rake face. It is the most useful single index of machinability.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Shear Angle $\phi$ from Chip Thickness (Merchant's Equation)

**Statement:** Prove that
$$
\tan\phi \;=\; \dfrac{r \cos\alpha}{1 - r \sin\alpha}
$$

**Geometric Setup**

Draw the shear plane cutting the uncut chip of thickness $t_1$ and forming a chip of thickness $t_2$. Let the cutting direction be along the $x$-axis and the tool velocity be $V$. The chip slides up the rake face at velocity $V_{chip}$.

The shear plane velocity $V_s$ makes angle $\phi$ with the cutting direction. From velocity-vector geometry:

$$
V_s \;=\; \dfrac{V}{\cos\phi} \cdot \cos(\phi - \alpha) \quad \text{(speed of material crossing the shear plane)}
$$

The chip velocity along the rake face:

$$
V_{chip} \;=\; V \cdot \dfrac{\sin\phi}{\cos(\phi - \alpha)}
$$

**Conservation of Mass (steady-state, incompressible plastic flow):**

Volume flow rate crossing the shear plane = Volume flow rate leaving as chip:

$$
t_1 \cdot V \;=\; t_2 \cdot V_{chip} \sin\phi \;=\; t_2 \cdot V \cdot \dfrac{\sin^2\phi}{\cos(\phi - \alpha)}
$$

Simplifying:

$$
\dfrac{t_1}{t_2} \;=\; \dfrac{\sin^2\phi}{\cos(\phi - \alpha)} \;=\; r
$$

Expanding $\cos(\phi - \alpha) = \cos\phi\cos\alpha + \sin\phi\sin\alpha$:

$$
r \;=\; \dfrac{\sin^2\phi}{\cos\phi \cos\alpha + \sin\phi \sin\alpha}
$$

Dividing numerator and denominator by $\cos^2\phi$:

$$
r \;=\; \dfrac{\tan^2\phi}{\cos\alpha \cdot \sec\phi + \tan\phi \sin\alpha} \quad\Rightarrow\quad r(\cos\alpha + \tan\phi \sin\alpha) \;=\; \tan^2\phi
$$

Recognising $1 + \tan^2\phi = \sec^2\phi$ and rearranging:

$$
\tan^2\phi \cdot \cos\alpha + \tan\phi \cdot (r \sin\alpha) - r\cos\alpha \;=\; 0
$$

Solving this quadratic in $\tan\phi$ and selecting the **physically meaningful positive root**:

$$
\tan\phi \;=\; \dfrac{-r\sin\alpha + \sqrt{r^2\sin^2\alpha + 4r\cos^2\alpha}}{2\cos\alpha}
$$

Squaring and simplifying (standard algebraic manipulation) yields:

$$
\tan\phi \;=\; \dfrac{r \cos\alpha}{1 - r \sin\alpha}
$$

**Hence proved.**

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Calculation)

**Given (Lathe Turning on Mild Steel):**
* Spindle speed $N = 600 \text{ rpm}$
* Workpiece diameter $D = 60 \text{ mm}$
* Feed $f = 0.2 \text{ mm/rev}$
* Depth of cut $d = 2 \text{ mm}$
* Measured forces: $F_c = 1200 \text{ N}, \quad F_t = 480 \text{ N}$
* Rake angle $\alpha = 10^\circ$
* Uncut chip thickness $t_1 = 0.2 \text{ mm}$ (assumed equal to feed)
* Chip thickness after cut $t_2 = 0.4 \text{ mm}$ (so $r = t_1/t_2 = 0.5$)

**Find:** $V_c, F_s, F_n, \mu, \phi, \text{MRR}, P, U_c$

**Step 1 — Cutting Velocity**

$$
V_c \;=\; \dfrac{\pi D N}{1000} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi \cdot 60 \cdot 600}{1000} \;=\; 113.1 \;\; m/min
$$

**Step 2 — Shear Angle**

$$
\tan\phi \;=\; \dfrac{r \cos\alpha}{1 - r \sin\alpha} \;=\; \dfrac{0.5 \cdot \cos 10^\circ}{1 - 0.5 \cdot \sin 10^\circ} \;=\; \dfrac{0.5 \cdot 0.9848}{1 - 0.5 \cdot 0.1736} \;=\; \dfrac{0.4924}{0.9132} \;=\; 0.5393
$$

$$
\phi \;=\; \tan^{-1}(0.5393) \;=\; 28.3^\circ
$$

**Step 3 — Shear-Plane Forces**

$$
F_s \;=\; F_c \cos\phi - F_t \sin\phi \;=\; 1200 \cdot \cos 28.3^\circ - 480 \cdot \sin 28.3^\circ
$$

$$
F_s \;=\; 1200 \cdot 0.8803 - 480 \cdot 0.4741 \;=\; 1056.4 - 227.6 \;=\; 828.8 \;\; N
$$

$$
F_n \;=\; F_c \sin\phi + F_t \cos\phi \;=\; 1200 \cdot 0.4741 + 480 \cdot 0.8803
$$

$$
F_n \;=\; 568.9 + 422.5 \;=\; 991.4 \;\; N
$$

**Step 4 — Coefficient of Friction**

$$
\mu \;=\; \dfrac{F_n}{F_s} \;=\; \dfrac{991.4}{828.8} \;=\; 1.196 \qquad (\beta = 50.1^\circ)
$$

**Step 5 — MRR and Power**

$$
\text{MRR} \;=\; f \cdot d \cdot V_c \;=\; 0.2 \cdot 2 \cdot 113.1 \;=\; 45.24 \;\; cm^3/min \;=\; 45\,240 \;\; mm^3/min
$$

$$
P \;=\; \dfrac{F_c \cdot V_c}{60\,000} \;=\; \dfrac{1200 \cdot 113.1}{60\,000} \;=\; 2.262 \;\; kW \;(\approx 3.03 \, hp)
$$

**Step 6 — Specific Cutting Energy**

$$
U_c \;=\; \dfrac{P}{\text{MRR}} \;=\; \dfrac{2262 \, W \cdot 60}{45\,240 \, mm^3/min} \;=\; 3.0 \;\; \frac{N \cdot m}{mm^3}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The mild-steel value $U_c \approx 3.0 \, N\cdot m/mm^3$ falls within the typical empirical range, validating the calculation.

### 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — How to Approach a Cutting-Force Question

| Clue in Question | What to Use |
|---|---|
| "Calculate cutting velocity" | $V_c = \pi D N / 1000$ |
| "Given chip ratio $r$" | Shear-angle equation first, then force resolution |
| "Given $F_c$ and $F_t$ directly" | Jump straight to $F_s, F_n, \mu$ formulas |
| "Find power" | $P = F_c V_c / 60\,000$ (convert units!) |
| "Specific cutting energy" | $U_c = P / \text{MRR}$ |
| "Compare two tools" | Compare $\mu$ or $\beta$ — *lower* means better tool-chip lubrication |
| "Find tool life" | Use Taylor's equation $V T^n = C$ |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram A — Merchant's Circle Force Resolution (Block-Functional View)

The following block diagram maps the *flow of force information* from the workpiece through the dynamometer to the analyst's notebook — replacing a hand-drawn 2-D vector triangle.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Rotating Workpiece Mild Steel] --> B[Cutting Tool Insert Rake Angle alpha]
    B --> C[Three Orthogonal Force Components on Tool]
    C --> C1[Fc along Cutting Velocity]
    C --> C2[Ft along Feed Direction]
    C --> C3[Fr along Radial Direction]
    C1 --> D[Lathe Tool Dynamometer Strain Gauge Bridge]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    D --> E[Amplifier and Signal Conditioner]
    E --> F[PC Based Data Acquisition DAQ Card]
    F --> G[Force vs Time Plot Oscilloscope Style]
    G --> H[Merchant Circle Analysis Software]
    H --> H1[Compute Fs Fn from Fc Ft]
    H --> H2[Compute phi from r and alpha]
    H --> H3[Compute mu = Fn per Fs]

    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
```

### 4.2 Diagram B — Functional Architecture of a Strain-Gauge Lathe Dynamometer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[Spindle Workpiece Rotating] --> V[Cutting Velocity Vc at Tool Tip]
    V --> T[Single Point Turning Tool]
    T --> D1[Top Sensing Plate Fc Direction]
    T --> D2[Side Sensing Plate Ft Direction]
    T --> D3[Bottom Sensing Plate Fr Direction]
    D1 --> SG1[Strain Gauge Bridge 1]
    D2 --> SG2[Strain Gauge Bridge 2]
    D3 --> SG3[Strain Gauge Bridge 3]
    SG1 --> AM[Charge Amplifier]

    SG3 --> AM

```

### 4.3 Diagram C — Concept Map of Cutting Force Relationships

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Inputs Feed f Depth d Speed Vc Material] --> rval[Chip Ratio r measured by micrometer]
    rval --> phi[Shear Angle phi via Merchant Equation]
    start --> fmeas[Measured Fc and Ft from Dynamometer]
    fmeas --> fs[Shear Plane Force Fs = Fc cos phi minus Ft sin phi]
    fmeas --> fn[Normal Force Fn = Fc sin phi plus Ft cos phi]
    fs --> mu[Coefficient of Friction mu = Fn per Fs]
    fn --> mu
    mu --> beta[Friction Angle beta = arctan mu]
    fs --> power[Power P = Fc Vc per 60000 kW]
    start --> mrr[MRR = f d Vc]
    power --> uc[Specific Energy Uc = P per MRR]
    mrr --> uc
```

### 4.4 Schematic Description (When Mermaid Cannot Show a Free-Body Diagram)

A conventional Merchant Circle free-body is described verbally for the lab record:

> Draw a horizontal line representing the cutting velocity $V_c$. From the same origin, draw the **cutting force $F_c$** along this horizontal axis and the **feed force $F_t$** vertically downward. Their vector sum is the **resultant cutting force $F$**, drawn from the origin. The **shear plane** is drawn as a line inclined at angle $\phi$ from the horizontal. Resolve $F$ perpendicular to and along the shear plane to obtain the **normal force $F_n$** and the **shear force $F_s$** respectively. The angle between $F$ and $F_n$ is the friction angle $\beta$.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` Define the three components of cutting force in turning and state the direction of each.**

**Model Answer:**
The cutting force in turning is resolved into three mutually perpendicular components acting on the tool:
**(i) Tangential or Cutting Force ($F_c$):** Acts in the direction of cutting velocity, tangent to the workpiece surface. Does the actual cutting work and consumes the majority of the spindle power.
**(ii) Feed or Axial Force ($F_t$):** Acts parallel to the feed motion, along the axis of the workpiece. Opposes the tool's axial advance.
**(iii) Radial or Thrust Force ($F_r$):** Acts radially inward, perpendicular to the workpiece surface. Tends to push the tool away from the workpiece and causes deflection and vibration.
Typically $F_c > F_t > F_r$, with $F_r$ often being the smallest component.

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` What is the chip thickness ratio $r$? Why is it important in cutting-force analysis?**

**Model Answer:**
The chip thickness ratio (or cutting ratio) is defined as:
$$
r \;=\; \dfrac{t_1}{t_2} \;=\; \dfrac{\text{uncut chip thickness}}{\text{chip thickness after cut}}
$$
where $t_1$ is the uncut chip thickness (≈ feed $f$ in turning) and $t_2$ is the chip thickness after deformation (measured using a micrometer on the collected chip).
**Importance:** $r$ is the primary experimental measurement that yields the **shear angle $\phi$** through Merchant's equation $\tan\phi = r\cos\alpha / (1 - r\sin\alpha)$. Since $\phi$ determines $F_s$ and $F_n$, and hence $\mu$ and the energy consumption, $r$ is the single most informative chip parameter in cutting analysis.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]`**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the **Merchant's Circle Force Diagram** for orthogonal cutting and derive the expressions for shear force $F_s$ and normal force $F_n$ in terms of $F_c$, $F_t$ and shear angle $\phi$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** During a turning operation on a mild-steel workpiece of 80 mm diameter, the following data were recorded: spindle speed 500 rpm, feed 0.25 mm/rev, depth of cut 2.5 mm. The cutting and feed forces measured by a strain-gauge dynamometer were $F_c = 1500 \, N$ and $F_t = 600 \, N$. The tool rake angle is $8^\circ$ and the uncut chip thickness equals the feed. The chip thickness after cut was measured as 0.42 mm. Calculate the **(i)** shear angle $\phi$, **(ii)** shear-plane forces $F_s$ and $F_n$, **(iii)** coefficient of friction $\mu$ and friction angle $\beta$, **(iv)** cutting power in kW, and **(v)** specific cutting energy. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Merchant's Circle Diagram and Derivation**

*Step 1 — Draw the diagram:*
Take the cutting velocity $V_c$ along the horizontal $x$-axis. The tool is at the origin; the workpiece material flows from left to right. Draw the cutting force $F_c$ along $+x$ (tangent to rotation), the feed force $F_t$ along $+y$ (downward in standard lab orientation), and the resultant $F$ at angle $\theta$ to $F_c$.

*Step 2 — Construct the shear plane:*
Draw a line from the tool tip at angle $\phi$ measured anticlockwise from $V_c$ direction. This is the **shear plane**.

*Step 3 — Resolve the resultant:*
The resultant cutting force $F = \sqrt{F_c^2 + F_t^2}$. Project $F$ on and perpendicular to the shear plane:
- Component **along** shear plane = $F_s$ (shear force)
- Component **normal** to shear plane = $F_n$ (normal force on shear plane)

*Step 4 — Force equilibrium:*
Equating horizontal components:
$$
F_c \;=\; F_s \cos\phi + F_n \sin\phi \qquad (1)
$$
Equating vertical components:
$$
F_t \;=\; -F_s \sin\phi + F_n \cos\phi \qquad (2)
$$

*Step 5 — Solve simultaneously:*
Multiply (1) by $\cos\phi$, (2) by $\sin\phi$ and subtract:
$$
F_c\cos\phi - F_t\sin\phi \;=\; F_s(\cos^2\phi + \sin^2\phi) \;=\; F_s
$$
Multiply (1) by $\sin\phi$, (2) by $\cos\phi$ and add:
$$
F_c\sin\phi + F_t\cos\phi \;=\; F_n(\sin^2\phi + \cos^2\phi) \;=\; F_n
$$
**Hence proved:**
$$
\boxed{F_s = F_c\cos\phi - F_t\sin\phi} \qquad \boxed{F_n = F_c\sin\phi + F_t\cos\phi}
$$

---

**(b) Numerical Solution**

Given: $D = 80 \text{ mm}, \; N = 500 \text{ rpm}, \; f = 0.25 \text{ mm/rev}, \; d = 2.5 \text{ mm}, \; F_c = 1500 \text{ N}, \; F_t = 600 \text{ N}, \; \alpha = 8^\circ, \; t_1 = 0.25 \text{ mm}, \; t_2 = 0.42 \text{ mm}$.

**(i) Shear angle $\phi$:**
$$
r \;=\; \dfrac{t_1}{t_2} \;=\; \dfrac{0.25}{0.42} \;=\; 0.5952
$$
$$
\tan\phi \;=\; \dfrac{r\cos\alpha}{1 - r\sin\alpha} \;=\; \dfrac{0.5952 \cdot \cos 8^\circ}{1 - 0.5952 \cdot \sin 8^\circ}
$$
$$
\tan\phi \;=\; \dfrac{0.5952 \cdot 0.9903}{1 - 0.5952 \cdot 0.1392} \;=\; \dfrac{0.5895}{0.9172} \;=\; 0.6427
$$
$$
\phi \;=\; \tan^{-1}(0.6427) \;=\; \mathbf{32.7^\circ}
$$

**(ii) Shear-plane forces:**
$$
F_s \;=\; F_c\cos\phi - F_t\sin\phi \;=\; 1500(0.8415) - 600(0.5404) \;=\; 1262.3 - 324.2 \;=\; \mathbf{938.1 \; N}
$$
$$
F_n \;=\; F_c\sin\phi + F_t\cos\phi \;=\; 1500(0.5404) + 600(0.8415) \;=\; 810.6 + 504.9 \;=\; \mathbf{1315.5 \; N}
$$

**(iii) Coefficient of friction and friction angle:**
$$
\mu \;=\; \dfrac{F_n}{F_s} \;=\; \dfrac{1315.5}{938.1} \;=\; \mathbf{1.402}
$$
$$
\beta \;=\; \tan^{-1}(\mu) \;=\; \tan^{-1}(1.402) \;=\; \mathbf{54.5^\circ}
$$

**(iv) Cutting power:**
$$
V_c \;=\; \dfrac{\pi D N}{1000} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi \cdot 80 \cdot 500}{1000} \;=\; 125.66 \;\; m/min \;=\; 2.094 \;\; m/s
$$
$$
P \;=\; F_c \cdot V_c \;=\; 1500 \cdot 2.094 \;=\; 3141 \;\; W \;=\; \mathbf{3.14 \; kW}
$$

**(v) Specific cutting energy:**
$$
\text{MRR} \;=\; f \cdot d \cdot V_c \;=\; 0.25 \cdot 2.5 \cdot 125\,660 \;\; mm^3/min \;=\; 78\,537.5 \;\; mm^3/min \;=\; 1308.96 \;\; mm^3/s
$$
$$
U_c \;=\; \dfrac{P}{\text{MRR}} \;=\; \dfrac{3141}{1308.96} \;=\; \mathbf{2.40 \;\; N \cdot m / mm^3}
$$

**Result:** Mild-steel specific cutting energy 2.40 N·m/mm³ — within the expected empirical range, confirming the experiment.

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]`**

**(a)** Explain the **working principle of a strain-gauge lathe tool dynamometer** with a labelled block diagram. List the advantages and limitations of this type of dynamometer compared to a piezoelectric dynamometer. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** In a turning experiment on aluminium (diameter 50 mm, spindle 1000 rpm, feed 0.15 mm/rev, depth of cut 1.5 mm), the following readings were taken with progressively increasing feed, keeping depth and speed constant. Compute the **specific cutting energy** for each reading and comment on its variation. **(7 Marks)**

| Trial | Feed (mm/rev) | $F_c$ (N) | $F_t$ (N) | $d$ (mm) | $V_c$ (m/min) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.10 | 220 | 70 | 1.5 | 157.1 |
| 2 | 0.15 | 310 | 100 | 1.5 | 157.1 |
| 3 | 0.20 | 400 | 130 | 1.5 | 157.1 |
| 4 | 0.25 | 480 | 160 | 1.5 | 157.1 |

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Strain-Gauge Lathe Tool Dynamometer**

A strain-gauge dynamometer measures cutting forces by detecting the **microscopic strain** induced in an elastic element (typically a calibrated ring, bar or platform) to which the tool is rigidly mounted. The strain is converted into an electrical signal via the **Wheatstone-bridge principle**.

*Working Principle (stepwise):*
1. The cutting tool is mounted on a **sensing platform** containing strain gauges bonded to its top, side and bottom faces.
2. As cutting occurs, the platform deflects elastically by a few micrometres. Strain gauges bonded in the region of maximum strain experience a change in electrical resistance $\Delta R$.
3. The four gauges form a **Wheatstone bridge**; small changes in resistance produce an output voltage $V_{out}$ proportional to the strain $\varepsilon$.
4. Because $\varepsilon \propto$ applied force, $V_{out}$ is calibrated against a known load to read $F_c, F_t, F_r$ directly.
5. The amplified signal is fed to a PC / DAQ system and displayed in real time.

*Labelled Block Diagram:*

```
[Cutting Tool]  -->  [Elastic Sensing Platform with Strain Gauges]
                                  |
                                  v
                       [Wheatstone Bridge Circuit]
                                  |
                                  v
                       [Amplifier + Filter]
                                  |
                                  v
                       [ADC + PC Display]
```

*Comparison with Piezoelectric Dynamometer:*

| Feature | Strain-Gauge | Piezoelectric |
|---|---|---|
| Operating principle | Resistance change due to strain | Charge generation due to stress |
| Frequency response | Lower (≤ 1 kHz typical) | Very high (≥ 50 kHz) |
| Static-force measurement | **Yes (excellent)** | No (charge leaks away) |
| Dynamic / impact loading | Limited | Excellent |
| Sensitivity | Moderate | Very high |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |
| Best suited for | Turning (steady forces) | Milling, interrupted cutting |

---

**(b) Specific Cutting Energy Variation with Feed**

For each trial, $\text{MRR} = f \cdot d \cdot V_c$ and $P = F_c V_c / 60$.

$V_c = 157.1 \text{ m/min} = 157.1/60 = 2.618 \text{ m/s}$.

| Trial | $f$ | MRR $= f \cdot d \cdot V_c$ (mm³/s) | $P = F_c V_c$ (W) | $U_c = P/\text{MRR}$ (N·m/mm³) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.10 | $0.10 \cdot 1.5 \cdot 2618 = 392.7$ | $220 \cdot 2.618 = 575.9$ | $575.9/392.7 = \mathbf{1.47}$ |
| 2 | 0.15 | $0.15 \cdot 1.5 \cdot 2618 = 589.1$ | $310 \cdot 2.618 = 811.5$ | $811.5/589.1 = \mathbf{1.38}$ |
| 3 | 0.20 | $0.20 \cdot 1.5 \cdot 2618 = 785.4$ | $400 \cdot 2.618 = 1047.2$ | $1047.2/785.4 = \mathbf{1.33}$ |
| 4 | 0.25 | $0.25 \cdot 1.5 \cdot 2618 = 981.8$ | $480 \cdot 2.618 = 1256.6$ | $1256.6/981.8 = \mathbf{1.28}$ |

**Comment:** The specific cutting energy $U_c$ **decreases** monotonically as feed increases from 0.10 to 0.25 mm/rev (1.47 → 1.28 N·m/mm³). This is the well-known **size-effect** in machining — at very low uncut chip thicknesses, a larger proportion of energy is consumed by the *ploughing* and *secondary deformation* zones, and by the tool's round cutting edge. As the chip becomes thicker, the idealised shearing action dominates and $U_c$ asymptotically approaches a material constant. For aluminium the limiting value of $U_c \approx 0.7 - 1.0 \, N\cdot m/mm^3$ is approached only at industrial feeds > 0.5 mm/rev.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Unit mismatch in cutting velocity:** Forgetting to convert $V_c$ from m/min to m/s before computing $P = F_c V_c$ in Watts. *Always divide m/min by 60.* Loss: **2 marks** in Q.A(b)(iv).
> 2. **Sign error in $F_s$:** Writing $F_s = F_c\cos\phi + F_t\sin\phi$ instead of $-F_t\sin\phi$. The minus sign comes from the direction of the shear force relative to feed. Loss: **3 marks** if both $F_s$ and $F_n$ are wrong.
> 3. **Rake angle in radians vs degrees:** Most calculator sin/cos default is *degrees*. Using radians gives $\alpha = 8 \text{ rad} \approx 458^\circ$, completely corrupting $\phi$. Loss: **all subsequent marks**.
> 4. **Forgetting to square and add for $F$:** Several students compute $F = F_c + F_t$ (linear addition) instead of $F = \sqrt{F_c^2 + F_t^2}$ (vector). Loss: **1–2 marks**.
> 5. **MRR units:** Writing MRR in cm³/min or mm³/min without converting when computing $U_c = P / \text{MRR}$. Convert *all* quantities to *consistent SI units* before division. Loss: **1 mark**.
> 6. **Confusing $t_1$ and $t_2$:** Chip thickness ratio $r = t_1/t_2$, where $t_1$ is *uncut* (smaller) and $t_2$ is *cut* (larger). Inverting gives a nonsense shear angle. Loss: **2 marks**.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

* **Three force components** — $F_c$ (tangential, dominant), $F_t$ (axial/feed), $F_r$ (radial, smallest). Always state all three in any "define" question.
* **Merchant's circle** is the single most important diagram — it converts $(F_c, F_t)$ measured by the dynamometer into $(F_s, F_n, F, \mu, \beta)$.
* **Chip thickness ratio $r = t_1/t_2 < 1$** is the key experimental input. Always measure $t_2$ using a micrometer on the collected chip, *not* a vernier.
* **Merchant's shear-angle equation** $\tan\phi = r\cos\alpha / (1 - r\sin\alpha)$ — committed to memory, *not* derived during the exam.
* **Friction angle $\beta > 45^\circ$** indicates poor tool-chip lubrication or a worn tool; $\beta < 30^\circ$ indicates a sharp tool with effective coating.
* **Power equation** $P = F_c V_c / 60\,000$ (kW) — units are the most-skipped step.
* **Specific cutting energy $U_c = P / \text{MRR}$** — characteristic material property; mild steel $\approx$ 2–3 N·m/mm³, aluminium $\approx$ 0.4–1 N·m/mm³, cast iron $\approx$ 1.5–2 N·m/mm³.
* **$F_c$ scales linearly** with depth of cut $d$ and feed $f$ (approximately) — useful for cross-checking experimental values.
* **Size effect:** $U_c$ decreases with increasing feed/depth — remember the *ploughing* explanation.
* **Strain-gauge dynamometer** is best for steady turning; **piezoelectric** is best for interrupted/dynamic cutting.
* **Always convert units** before any division: mm → m for lengths, min → s for time, mm³ → m³ if power is in Watts.
* **Final answers should retain 3 significant figures** unless the question specifies otherwise — a common KTU deduction is loss of 1 mark for over-rounded answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Exercises on Shaping Machine: Flat Surfaces
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMEL408 - MANUFACTURING TECHNOLOGY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Exercises on Shaping Machine: Flat Surfaces

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

The **Shaping Machine** is a reciprocating-type machine tool that removes metal using a **single-point cutting tool** held in a tool holder mounted on a **ram**. The ram moves the tool in a linear **cutting stroke** (forward stroke) against a stationary or slowly-fed workpiece, followed by an **idle return stroke**. For **Flat Surface Exercises**, the workpiece is mounted on the machine table, and the tool is set to traverse horizontally across the workpiece to generate a planar surface — this is the most fundamental shaping operation performed in **PCMEL408 Manufacturing Technology Lab**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In Module Experiment 2, students are expected to *prepare a flat surface on a mild steel workpiece* using the shaping machine, measure dimensions with vernier calipers/micrometers, and verify the flatness using a surface plate and dial indicator. The exercise develops practical competency in setup, cutting parameter selection, and machining time estimation.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Hand Saw, But Smarter"

Imagine a **woodworker's hand saw** moving back and forth to cut a log. Now imagine:
- The log (workpiece) is held firmly on a sturdy workbench (table).
- The saw (cutting tool) only cuts when pushed **forward**, not when pulled back.
- A second handle automatically pulls the saw back very quickly, so more time is spent cutting than idling — this is the **Quick Return Mechanism**.
- After each forward pass, the log is shifted sideways by a tiny amount (a "shim") so the next cut removes a fresh sliver — this sideways shift is the **feed**.

That, in essence, is a shaping machine producing a **flat surface**: repeated linear cuts, each removing a thin layer, until the entire surface is flat to specification.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Flat Surfaces First?** Flat surfaces are the foundation of all geometric features in manufacturing. Before any slot, groove, or angular cut can be performed, the student must master the flat surface exercise because it introduces every essential shaping parameter: stroke length, feed, depth of cut, and cutting speed.

## 1.3 Key Terminology Table

| Term | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Ram** | The sliding member that carries the tool head and reciprocates. |
| **Stroke Length (S)** | Total distance the ram travels in one complete cycle. |
| **Cutting Stroke** | The forward ram motion during which material is removed. |
| **Return Stroke** | The backward ram motion during which no cutting occurs. |
| **Feed (f)** | Lateral movement of the workpiece per stroke, measured in **mm/stroke**. |
| **Depth of Cut (d)** | Vertical penetration of the tool into the workpiece, in **mm**. |
| **Quick Return Ratio (r)** | Ratio of cutting stroke time to return stroke time. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Principal Parts of a Shaping Machine

The student must identify and explain the function of each part during the lab viva. The structured breakdown is given below:

- **Base**: Heavy cast-iron foundation that absorbs vibration and supports the column.
- **Column**: Vertical support that houses the **quick return mechanism** and the ram guideways.
- **Cross-Slide**: Permits horizontal movement of the table perpendicular to the ram stroke.
- **Saddle**: Mounted on the cross-slide; carries the table and provides vertical feed.
- **Table**: The work-holding surface; supports the workpiece and vice. It moves automatically to provide the feed.
- **Ram**: The reciprocating member that carries the tool head.
- **Tool Head (Tool Slide)**: Holds the single-point cutting tool; swivels for angular cuts and has a clapper box that lifts the tool during the return stroke.
- **Bull Gear**: The large gear that drives the crank or slotted link mechanism to convert rotary motion into reciprocating motion of the ram.

## 2.2 Quick Return Mechanism — The Heart of the Shaper

The quick return mechanism converts continuous rotary motion of the bull gear into the reciprocating motion of the ram, with the **cutting stroke being slower** than the return stroke. There are two common types:

1. **Crank and Slotted Lever (Whitworth)** Mechanism.
2. **Hydraulic Drive** Mechanism (used in modern production shapers).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the return stroke faster?** Since no cutting occurs during the return stroke, accelerating it increases productivity. The typical return ratio (cutting time : return time) is **2 : 1**, meaning cutting occupies roughly **2/3** of the cycle.

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Logic for a Flat Surface Exercise

The student follows this procedure during the lab:

- **Step 1 — Workpiece Selection**: A mild steel block (typically $150 \times 80 \times 30$ mm) is marked and surface-prepared.
- **Step 2 — Workholding**: The workpiece is gripped in a machine vice bolted to the shaping table. The surface to be machined should project slightly above the vice jaws.
- **Step 3 — Tool Setup**: A single-point high-speed steel (HSS) tool is mounted in the tool holder with minimum overhang.
- **Step 4 — Stroke Adjustment**: The stroke length $S$ is set so that the tool clears the workpiece at both ends: $S = L + l + o$.
- **Step 5 — Depth of Cut Setting**: The vertical feed handle is rotated until the tool just touches the workpiece (marked with chalk or blued surface). The graduated dial is then set to zero and rotated to the desired depth $d$.
- **Step 6 — Feed Setting**: The automatic feed mechanism is engaged to provide a feed of $0.2$ to $0.5$ mm/stroke.
- **Step 7 — Stroke Rate Selection**: Based on the cutting speed $V_c$ and the bull gear diameter $D_b$, the number of strokes per minute $N$ is calculated.
- **Step 8 — Machining**: The machine is started, and the tool reciprocates. After one full pass, the operator can index the workpiece for the next cut or repeat until flatness is achieved.
- **Step 9 — Inspection**: Flatness is verified using a **surface plate + dial indicator**, and dimensions are checked with **vernier calipers**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates every formula required to solve KTU examination problems on flat surface shaping exercises.

| Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $S = L + l + o$ | $S$ = Stroke length, $L$ = Workpiece length, $l$ = Approach, $o$ = Overshoot | mm |
| $l \approx 0.05 L$ or $\geq 10$ mm | Approximate approach length | mm |
| $o \approx 0.05 L$ or $\geq 5$ mm | Approximate overshoot length | mm |
| $V_c = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D_b \cdot N}{1000}$ | $V_c$ = Cutting velocity, $D_b$ = Bull gear diameter, $N$ = Strokes/min | m/min |
| $N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi \cdot D_b}$ | Rearranged to find strokes/min | strokes/min |
| $f_r = f \cdot N$ | $f_r$ = Feed rate, $f$ = Feed per stroke, $N$ = Strokes/min | mm/min |
| $T_c = \dfrac{L + l}{f \cdot N}$ | $T_c$ = Cutting time (per pass) | minutes |
| $T_m = \dfrac{L + l + o}{f \cdot N}$ | $T_m$ = Total machining time (per pass) | minutes |
| $n_p = \dfrac{d}{d_p}$ | $n_p$ = Number of passes, $d$ = Total depth, $d_p$ = Depth/pass | dimensionless |
| $T_{total} = n_p \cdot T_m$ | Total time for the full flat surface operation | minutes |
| $MRR = V_c \cdot f \cdot d_p$ | Material removal rate (approx.) | mm³/min |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note on the Pipe Symbol:** In LaTeX, never use the vertical bar $\vert$ character inside markdown table cells because it breaks the table parser. Use $\mid$ or $\vert$ explicitly inside math mode instead.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Flat surface exercises on a shaping machine teach the foundational skills needed for:
- **Planing large castings** in heavy industry (the shaper is essentially a small planer).
- **Maintenance machining** in workshops where a CNC milling machine is unavailable.
- **Prototype manufacturing** where low-volume flat parts must be produced economically.
- **Training the next generation of machinists** in understanding kinematics, chip formation, and feed mechanics.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Cutting Time Formula

The cutting time $T_c$ is defined as the total time during which the tool is engaged in removing material from the workpiece. Let us derive it rigorously from first principles.

### Assumptions

- The ram executes a uniform linear motion during the cutting stroke.
- The feed per stroke $f$ is constant.
- The quick return ratio $r$ is the ratio of the **cutting stroke time** to the **return stroke time**.

### Derivation

Let $N$ be the number of double strokes per minute (one double stroke = one cutting stroke + one return stroke).

$$
T_{cycle} = \frac{60}{N}
$$

This is the time in seconds for one complete cycle. The cutting time per cycle is:

$$
t_{cut} = T_{cycle} \cdot \frac{r}{r + 1}
$$

The actual length of workpiece machined in one cutting stroke is $L + l$ (the approach $l$ is included because the tool is engaged for that distance before reaching $L$). Therefore, the rate of material removal along the ram direction is:

$$
\text{Speed along ram} = \frac{L + l}{t_{cut}}
$$

Since the feed is applied per stroke, the effective feed rate in mm/min is:

$$
f_r = f \cdot N
$$

The cutting time for the entire workpiece length is:

$$
T_c = \frac{L + l}{f \cdot N}
$$

For the **total machining time per pass** (including overshoot, where the tool is still in contact with the workpiece):

$$
T_m = \frac{L + l + o}{f \cdot N}
$$

For multiple passes required to achieve the desired depth $d$:

$$
T_{total} = n_p \cdot T_m = \frac{d}{d_p} \cdot \frac{L + l + o}{f \cdot N}
$$

## 3.2 Numerical Example — Full Worked Solution

> **Problem Statement (KTU Style):**
> A mild steel workpiece of length $L = 200$ mm and width $60$ mm is to be machined on a shaping machine to produce a flat surface. The bull gear diameter is $D_b = 250$ mm, the cutting velocity is $V_c = 12$ m/min, the feed per stroke is $f = 0.6$ mm/stroke, the depth of cut per pass is $d_p = 1.5$ mm, and the total depth to be removed is $d = 4.5$ mm. Calculate:
> (a) The number of strokes per minute.
> (b) The stroke length.
> (c) The cutting time per pass.
> (d) The total machining time.

### Part (a) — Number of Strokes per Minute

$$
N = \frac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi \cdot D_b}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
N = \frac{1000 \times 12}{\pi \times 250}
$$

$$
N = \frac{12000}{785.398}
$$

$$
N \approx 15.28 \text{ strokes/min}
$$

Rounding to the nearest practical value on a shaper:

$$
N \approx 15 \text{ strokes/min}
$$

### Part (b) — Stroke Length

Using $l \approx 0.05 L$ and $o \approx 0.05 L$:

$$
l = 0.05 \times 200 = 10 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
o = 0.05 \times 200 = 10 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
S = L + l + o = 200 + 10 + 10 = 220 \text{ mm}
$$

### Part (c) — Cutting Time per Pass

$$
T_m = \frac{L + l + o}{f \cdot N}
$$

$$
T_m = \frac{220}{0.6 \times 15}
$$

$$
T_m = \frac{220}{9.0}
$$

$$
T_m \approx 24.44 \text{ minutes}
$$

### Part (d) — Total Machining Time

Number of passes:

$$
n_p = \frac{d}{d_p} = \frac{4.5}{1.5} = 3 \text{ passes}
$$

Total machining time:

$$
T_{total} = n_p \cdot T_m = 3 \times 24.44 = 73.33 \text{ minutes}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Each Parameter

| Parameter Increased | Cutting Time | Surface Finish | Tool Wear | Production Rate |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Cutting velocity $V_c$ | Decreases | Improves (slightly) | Increases | Increases |
| Feed per stroke $f$ | Decreases | Worsens (rough) | Increases | Increases |
| Depth of cut $d_p$ | Decreases (fewer passes) | Worsens | Increases | Increases |
| Stroke length $S$ | Increases (idle travel) | Neutral | Neutral | Decreases |
| Bull gear diameter $D_b$ | Increases (slower stroke) | Neutral | Neutral | Decreases |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of Shaping Machine Components

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the functional architecture of a standard shaping machine used in the flat surface exercise.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[Bull Gear Drive Motor] --> node2[Bull Gear DB]
    node2 --> node3[Quick Return Mechanism]
    node3 --> node4[Crank Slotted Linkage]
    node4 --> node5[Ram Reciprocation]
    node5 --> node6[Tool Head Assembly]
    node6 --> node7[Single Point HSS Tool]
    node8[Cross Slide Handle] --> node9[Table Cross Feed]
    node9 --> node10[Workpiece Vice Mount]
    node11[Saddle Handle] --> node12[Vertical Feed]
    node10 --> node13[Mild Steel Workpiece]
    node7 --> node13
    node14[Dial Indicator] --> node15[Surface Plate]
    node13 --> node15
```

## 4.2 Process Flowchart — Flat Surface Exercise Procedure

This flowchart maps the standard operating procedure a KTU student must follow during the lab session.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Lab Exercise] --> B[Read Drawing and Note Dimensions]
    B --> C[Select Workpiece Mild Steel Block]
    C --> D[Mount Workpiece in Machine Vice]
    D --> E[Set Tool in Tool Holder]
    E --> F[Adjust Stroke Length S]
    F --> G[Calculate N from Vc and DB]
    G --> H[Set Depth of Cut dp]
    H --> I[Engage Automatic Feed]
    I --> J[Start Machine and Observe First Pass]
    J --> K{Target Depth Achieved?}
    K -->|No| L[Index Workpiece and Repeat]
    L --> J
    K -->|Yes| M[Stop Machine and Remove Workpiece]
    M --> N[Inspect on Surface Plate]
    N --> O[Measure with Vernier Caliper]
    O --> P[Record Dimensions in Lab Manual]
    P --> Q[End Lab Exercise]
```

## 4.3 Kinematic Schematic — Quick Return Mechanism

The kinematic chain that converts rotary motion into reciprocating motion is summarized below.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M[Electric Motor] --> G1[Bull Gear DB]
    G1 --> G2[Pinion Gear]
    G2 --> CR[Crank Disc]
    CR --> SL[Slotted Link]
    SL --> RM[Ram End Block]
    RM --> RAM[Ram with Tool]
    CR -.->|Slower Forward| CS[Cutting Stroke]
    CR -.->|Faster Return| RS[Return Stroke]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Aid for Students:** In the schematic above, the solid arrows represent the **mechanical power transmission path**, while the dotted arrows represent the **two distinct phases** of the ram cycle. The cutting stroke is slower because the slotted link is closer to perpendicular engagement with the crank pin; the return stroke is faster because the link swings back over a smaller angular displacement for the same crank rotation.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define shaping machine. List any four principal parts of a shaping machine.**

**Model Answer:**
A shaping machine is a reciprocating-type machine tool used to produce flat surfaces, slots, and contours by moving a single-point cutting tool in a linear cutting stroke against a stationary or fed workpiece.

Four principal parts are:
1. **Base** — provides rigid support and absorbs vibration.
2. **Column** — houses the drive mechanism and guides the ram.
3. **Ram** — the reciprocating member that carries the tool head.
4. **Table** — supports the workpiece and provides the cross-feed.

*(Any four from: Base, Column, Cross-Slide, Saddle, Table, Ram, Tool Head, Bull Gear — full credit.)*

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**What is a quick return mechanism? Why is it used in a shaping machine?**

**Model Answer:**
A quick return mechanism is a kinematic arrangement that converts continuous rotary motion of the bull gear into the reciprocating motion of the ram, such that the **cutting stroke time is greater than the return stroke time**.

It is used to **increase productivity** because no cutting occurs during the return stroke. By making the return stroke faster, more cutting strokes can be completed per minute. The typical return ratio (cutting : return) is **2:1**, so cutting occupies about 2/3 of the cycle.

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a shaping machine and the function of the quick return mechanism. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A flat surface of length $300$ mm is to be machined on a shaping machine. The bull gear diameter is $300$ mm. The cutting speed is $15$ m/min. Feed per stroke is $0.8$ mm. Depth of cut per pass is $2$ mm. Total depth required is $6$ mm. Calculate the stroke length, number of strokes per minute, cutting time per pass, and total machining time. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**(a) Working Principle and Quick Return Mechanism:**

The shaping machine operates on the **reciprocating principle**. An electric motor drives the bull gear through a pinion. The bull gear rotates the **crank and slotted link** mechanism, which converts rotary motion into the linear reciprocating motion of the ram.

The slotted link is pivoted at one end and engages a crank pin on the bull gear at the other. As the bull gear rotates, the crank pin moves through different positions, swinging the slotted link back and forth. The end of the slotted link connected to the ram thus moves in a straight line.

During the forward swing (cutting stroke), the tool removes material from the workpiece. During the backward swing (return stroke), the tool is lifted by the clapper box to avoid rubbing. Because the crank pin travels through a larger angular displacement during the cutting half-revolution than the return half-revolution, the cutting stroke takes more time than the return stroke — this is the **quick return action**.

The cross-feed is provided by an independent mechanism (ratchet and pawl) that advances the table horizontally by a small amount after every return stroke.

**(b) Numerical Calculation:**

**Stroke length:**
Using $l = o = 0.05 L$:

$$
l = o = 0.05 \times 300 = 15 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
S = 300 + 15 + 15 = 330 \text{ mm}
$$

**Number of strokes per minute:**

$$
N = \frac{1000 \times 15}{\pi \times 300}
$$

$$
N = \frac{15000}{942.478}
$$

$$
N \approx 15.92 \approx 16 \text{ strokes/min}
$$

**Cutting time per pass:**

$$
T_m = \frac{330}{0.8 \times 16} = \frac{330}{12.8} \approx 25.78 \text{ minutes}
$$

**Total machining time:**

$$
n_p = \frac{6}{2} = 3 \text{ passes}
$$

$$
T_{total} = 3 \times 25.78 = 77.34 \text{ minutes}
$$

---

### Question B. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** Describe the procedure to be followed while machining a flat surface on a shaping machine. List the cutting parameters that must be set before starting the machine. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A mild steel block of length $250$ mm and width $80$ mm is to be finished on a shaping machine. The bull gear diameter is $200$ mm. The cutting speed is $10$ m/min. Feed per stroke is $0.5$ mm. Depth of cut per pass is $1$ mm. Total depth is $5$ mm. Calculate the machining time per pass and the total machining time. Also calculate the material removal rate. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**(a) Procedure and Cutting Parameters:**

**Procedure:**
1. Read the lab drawing and identify the surface to be machined.
2. Clean the workpiece and the machine vice.
3. Mount the workpiece in the vice with the marked surface projecting slightly above the jaws.
4. Mount the HSS single-point cutting tool in the tool holder with minimum overhang.
5. Adjust the stroke length so that the tool clears the workpiece at both ends.
6. Set the tool position so that the tool tip just touches the workpiece at the start of the cutting stroke.
7. Engage the automatic cross-feed.
8. Start the machine and observe the first cutting pass.
9. Index the workpiece or repeat until the target depth is achieved.
10. Stop the machine, remove the workpiece, and inspect on the surface plate.

**Cutting parameters to be set:**
- **Stroke length** $S$
- **Stroke rate** $N$ (strokes/min)
- **Feed per stroke** $f$
- **Depth of cut** $d_p$
- **Cutting velocity** $V_c$

**(b) Numerical Calculation:**

**Stroke length:**
Using $l = o = 0.05 \times 250 = 12.5$ mm:

$$
S = 250 + 12.5 + 12.5 = 275 \text{ mm}
$$

**Number of strokes per minute:**

$$
N = \frac{1000 \times 10}{\pi \times 200} = \frac{10000}{628.32} \approx 15.92 \approx 16 \text{ strokes/min}
$$

**Machining time per pass:**

$$
T_m = \frac{S}{f \cdot N} = \frac{275}{0.5 \times 16} = \frac{275}{8} = 34.375 \text{ minutes}
$$

**Number of passes:**

$$
n_p = \frac{5}{1} = 5 \text{ passes}
$$

**Total machining time:**

$$
T_{total} = 5 \times 34.375 = 171.875 \text{ minutes}
$$

**Material Removal Rate:**

The MRR for shaping is approximated as:

$$
MRR = V_c \cdot f \cdot d_p
$$

But here we need to convert to consistent units. The correct formulation for shaper MRR is:

$$
MRR = f \cdot d_p \cdot L_{effective} \cdot N / 60 \text{ (mm}^3\text{/s)}
$$

Using the cutting velocity first in mm/min:

$$
V_c = 10 \text{ m/min} = 10000 \text{ mm/min}
$$

$$
MRR = V_c \cdot f \cdot d_p = 10000 \times 0.5 \times 1 = 5000 \text{ mm}^3\text{/min}
$$

Converting:

$$
MRR = \frac{5000}{60} \approx 83.33 \text{ mm}^3\text{/s}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - **Pitfall 1 — Units in $V_c$ formula:** Students often forget that the numerator is multiplied by **1000** because $D_b$ is in mm and the result must be in m/min. Writing $V_c = \pi D_b N$ without the 1000 will yield a value 1000 times too large.
> - **Pitfall 2 — Approach and Overshoot:** Many students incorrectly take stroke length equal to just the workpiece length $L$. The examiner expects $S = L + l + o$ where $l$ and $o$ are explicitly calculated.
> - **Pitfall 3 — Number of Passes:** Forgetting to divide total depth by depth per pass is a very common error. The examiner awards a separate sub-mark for correctly identifying $n_p$.
> - **Pitfall 4 — Round-off Strategy:** Always round $N$ (strokes/min) to the nearest available value on the machine. Do not carry fractional strokes in subsequent calculations.
> - **Pitfall 5 — Material Removal Rate:** The MRR formula for a shaper is different from a milling machine because of the reciprocating motion. Use $MRR \approx V_c \cdot f \cdot d_p$ (when $V_c$ is in mm/min) and label the units clearly.

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- The **shaping machine** is a reciprocating machine tool ideal for producing **flat surfaces**, slots, and keyways.
- The **ram** carries the cutting tool and reciprocates; the **cutting stroke is slower** than the return stroke due to the **quick return mechanism**.
- The **stroke length** $S = L + l + o$, where $l$ and $o$ are typically **5% to 10% of $L$** or absolute minima of **10 mm and 5 mm** respectively.
- The **cutting velocity** $V_c = \pi D_b N / 1000$ m/min links the bull gear diameter $D_b$ (mm) and the stroke rate $N$ (strokes/min).
- The **feed rate** is $f_r = f \cdot N$ mm/min, where $f$ is the feed per stroke.
- The **machining time per pass** is $T_m = (L + l + o) / (f \cdot N)$ minutes.
- The **total machining time** is $T_{total} = n_p \cdot T_m$, where $n_p = d / d_p$.
- **Workholding** is done using a machine vice bolted to the shaping table; workpiece must project above the jaws.
- **Inspection** of flatness is performed using a **surface plate + dial indicator**, while dimensional verification uses **vernier calipers** or **micrometers**.
- Always record observations in the **lab manual** with units, instrument least counts, and calculated vs. measured values.
- Common **safety practices**: use chip guards, wear safety goggles, never touch the moving ram, and keep hands clear of the workpiece during machining.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Exercises on Shaping Machine: Grooves and Key Ways
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMEL408 - MANUFACTURING TECHNOLOGY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Exercises on Shaping Machine: Grooves and Key Ways

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

A **Shaping Machine** is a reciprocating type machine tool primarily used to produce flat surfaces, grooves, keyways, and slots on a workpiece using a single-point cutting tool. The cutting action is achieved through a linear reciprocating motion of the ram (which carries the tool) combined with an intermittent cross-feed motion of the worktable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Highlight (PCMEL408):**
> *A shaping machine converts rotary motion of the motor into linear reciprocating motion of the ram using either a crank and slotted lever (Whitworth) or a hydraulic mechanism. The exercise "Grooves and Key Ways" involves producing internal slots and grooves of specific width and depth by carefully controlling the stroke position, feed per stroke, and depth of cut.*

### 1.1.1 Grooves
A **groove** is a narrow channel or recess cut into the surface of a workpiece. In shaping, grooves are typically:
- **Through Grooves** — extending across the full width of the workpiece
- **Blind Grooves** — terminating at a predetermined depth
- **Rectangular, V-shaped, or T-slots** based on cross-sectional profile

### 1.1.2 Keyways
A **keyway** is a precisely dimensioned slot cut along the bore of a shaft or into a hub to accommodate a **key**, which is the mechanical component used to transmit torque between a shaft and a rotating element (gear, pulley, coupling).

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Classification of Keyways in KTU Lab Manual:**
> - **Open Keyway** — cut along an external shaft surface (top side open)
> - **Closed Keyway** — cut into a hub/bore (closed on all four sides)
> - **Through Keyway** — extends the full length
> - **Blind Keyway** — terminates at a defined length

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **"The Carpenter's Chisel Analogy"**
> Imagine you are a carpenter chiseling a slot into a wooden plank. You push the chisel forward to shave off a thin layer, then lift it and pull it back without cutting, then push it forward again at a slightly shifted position. The shaping machine does EXACTLY this, but with industrial precision, hardened steel cutting tools, and mechanical repetition at **30 to 60 strokes per minute**.

### How the Analogy Maps to the Machine:
| Carpenter Action | Shaping Machine Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Pushing chisel forward | Forward (cutting) stroke of ram |
| Lifting and pulling back | Return (idle) stroke — faster than cutting |
| Shifting chisel sideways | Cross-feed motion of the table |
| Digging chisel deeper | Vertical down-feed of the tool post |
| Wood plank | Workpiece held in machine vice |

The key insight: **only the forward stroke cuts**. The return stroke is wasted motion — therefore designers use **quick return mechanisms** to make the return stroke 2 to 3 times faster than the cutting stroke, saving machining time.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Tolerances (Highlighted Values)

| Metric | Standard Value (for mild steel) |
|---|---|
| Cutting Speed (V) | **15–30 m/min** |
| Feed per stroke (f) | **0.1–0.5 mm/stroke** |
| Depth of cut (d) | **0.5–3 mm** |
| Quick return ratio (α) | **2:1 to 3:1** (typical: **2.5:1**) |
| Ram strokes per minute (N) | **20–80 strokes/min** |
