| Keyway tolerance (width) | **±0.025 mm (H7 fit)** |
| Surface roughness achieved | **1.6–3.2 µm Ra** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Shaping Machine Working Principle

The shaping machine transforms **rotary motion → linear reciprocating motion** using a **crank and slotted lever (Whitworth quick return) mechanism**.

### 2.1.1 Step-by-Step Logic of the Mechanism

1. **Electric motor** drives a bull gear at constant RPM.
2. The bull gear carries a **crank pin** set at an adjustable radius (determines stroke length).
3. The crank pin slides inside a **slotted lever** (Whitworth lever) pivoted at one end.
4. As the crank rotates, the slotted lever oscillates, driving the **ram** in reciprocating motion.
5. The position of the crank pin relative to the slotted lever pivot determines whether the ram is in **forward (cutting)** or **return (idle)** phase.
6. A **ratchet and pawl** mechanism on the cross-slide provides **intermittent cross-feed** to the table after each return stroke.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Cutting Cycle Has Two Phases:**
> - **Forward (Cutting) Stroke:** Ram moves from rear dead center to front dead center. Tool engages workpiece.
> - **Return Stroke:** Ram moves from front dead center back to rear dead center. Tool is lifted (via clapper box) to avoid dragging.

---

## 2.2 Why and How — The Mechanics Behind Keyway Cutting

### The "Why" Behind Tool Geometry

A **keyway tool** is not a regular turning/shaping tool. It has a very specific geometry:
- **Narrow cutting edge** (equal to the desired keyway width)
- **Zero rake angle** (to prevent digging into the narrow slot walls)
- **Slight clearance angle (6°–8°)** on the sides
- **High side relief** to prevent rubbing against the groove walls

### The "How" Behind the Machining Sequence

1. **Roughing:** A parting/grooving tool wider than the keyway is used to remove bulk material in multiple passes.
2. **Semifinishing:** A tool equal to the keyway width with sharp edges is used.
3. **Finishing:** A precision-ground keyway tool makes the final 1–2 passes to achieve dimensional accuracy and surface finish.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:**
> Students often confuse the **width of the groove** with the **width of the tool**. They are EQUAL in shaping because the tool must be exactly the width of the desired groove — you cannot take a "side wall cut" in shaping since the tool only moves linearly.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This formula sheet covers 90% of numerical questions asked in PCMEL408 viva and written exams.**

### Table 2.1 — Core Machining Formulas for Shaping

| Symbol | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| $V$ | $V = \dfrac{2 \cdot L \cdot N \cdot (1+\alpha)}{1000 \cdot \alpha}$ | Average cutting velocity during cutting stroke | m/min |
| $T_m$ | $T_m = \dfrac{(L + \ell)}{f \cdot N}$ | Machining time per pass | minutes |
| $N$ | $N = \dfrac{V \cdot 1000}{2 \cdot L \cdot (1+\alpha)} \cdot \alpha$ | Strokes per minute (from cutting speed) | strokes/min |
| $\alpha$ | $\alpha = \dfrac{t_c}{t_r} = \dfrac{180+\beta}{180-\beta}$ | Quick return ratio (cutting time / return time) | dimensionless |
| $f$ | $f = \dfrac{V_f}{N}$ | Feed per stroke (from table feed rate) | mm/stroke |
| $V_f$ | $V_f = f \cdot N$ | Table feed rate (per minute) | mm/min |
| $P$ | $P = \dfrac{F_c \cdot V}{60 \cdot 1000 \cdot \eta}$ | Power required (cutting force × velocity) | kW |
| $\text{MRR}$ | $\text{MRR} = L \cdot d \cdot f \cdot N$ | Material removal rate (approximation for shaping) | mm³/min |
| $R_a$ | $R_a \approx \dfrac{f^2}{8r}$ | Theoretical surface roughness (r = nose radius) | µm |

Where:
- $L$ = Length of stroke (mm)
- $N$ = Ram strokes per minute
- $\alpha$ = Quick return ratio
- $\beta$ = Angle of advance of the crank at cutting stroke start/end (degrees)
- $f$ = Feed per stroke (mm)
- $\ell$ = Over-travel / approach allowance (~ 10–20 mm)
- $d$ = Depth of cut (mm)
- $F_c$ = Cutting force (N)
- $\eta$ = Mechanical efficiency (~ 0.7–0.85)

### Table 2.2 — Recommended Cutting Parameters for Keyway Cutting on Mild Steel

| Operation | Tool Material | V (m/min) | f (mm/stroke) | d (mm) | Coolant |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Roughing | HSS | 18–22 | 0.30–0.50 | 1.5–3.0 | Cutting oil |
| Finishing | HSS (ground) | 25–30 | 0.10–0.15 | 0.20–0.50 | Dry or oil |
| HSS → Carbide | Carbide P20 | 60–90 | 0.20–0.40 | 1.0–2.0 | Dry |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Sector | Application of Shaped Grooves/Keyways |
|---|---|
| **Automotive** | Keyways on crankshafts, transmission shafts, propeller shafts |
| **Aerospace** | Dovetail grooves in turbine disk assemblies |
| **Machine Tool Building** | T-slots on machine tables, slideways |
| **Heavy Machinery** | Splines and keyways in gearbox shafts |
| **Tool & Die Making** | Dovetail slides in press tools |
| **Shipbuilding** | Long through-keyways on rudder stock and propeller shafts |

> [!TIP]
> **Industrial Relevance:** Despite the advent of CNC milling, shaping machines remain widely used in Indian small-scale industries (SSI) and in maintenance workshops due to their **low cost, simplicity, and ability to produce internal slots** that are otherwise difficult to make on lathes.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Quick Return Ratio (α) for Crank-Slotted Lever Mechanism

### Given
- The crank rotates at uniform angular velocity $\omega$.
- Let $\beta$ be the angle of the crank at the **start** of the cutting stroke (measured from the inner dead center).
- Let $\beta$ be the angle of the crank at the **end** of the cutting stroke (measured from the outer dead center).
- By symmetry of the slotted lever geometry, both angles are equal to $\beta$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Total angle traversed by the crank in one complete revolution:
$$\theta_{total} = 360°$$

**Step 2:** Angle traversed during cutting (forward) stroke:
$$\theta_c = (180° + \beta) - \beta = 180°$$

Wait — let me correct this using the standard Whitworth formulation.

**Step 1 (Corrected Setup):** Place the crank at the position where the ram is at its **rear dead center** (start of cutting stroke). Define the angle of the crank pin position **beyond this dead center** as $\beta$.

**Step 2:** The crank must rotate through angle $(180° + \beta)$ to bring the ram to the front dead center (end of cutting stroke).

$$\theta_{cutting} = 180° + \beta$$

**Step 3:** The remaining angle for the return stroke is:
$$\theta_{return} = 360° - (180° + \beta) = 180° - \beta$$

**Step 4:** Since the crank rotates at uniform angular velocity, time is proportional to angle traversed. Hence:
$$\frac{t_c}{t_r} = \frac{\theta_{cutting}}{\theta_{return}} = \frac{180° + \beta}{180° - \beta}$$

**Step 5:** Define the quick return ratio:
$$\alpha = \frac{t_c}{t_r}$$

**Final Result:**
$$\alpha = \frac{180° + \beta}{180° - \beta}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** As $\beta \to 0°$, $\alpha \to 1$ (no quick return). As $\beta \to 90°$, $\alpha \to \infty$ (return is instantaneous). Typical practical values keep $\alpha$ between **2 and 3**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Average Cutting Velocity During Cutting Stroke

### Given
- Length of stroke = $L$ mm
- Quick return ratio = $\alpha$
- Ram strokes per minute = $N$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Time for one complete cycle (forward + return) of the ram:
$$t_{cycle} = \frac{60}{N} \quad \text{seconds}$$

**Step 2:** Since $\alpha = t_c / t_r$ and $t_c + t_r = t_{cycle}$:
$$t_c = \frac{\alpha \cdot t_{cycle}}{1 + \alpha} = \frac{60 \cdot \alpha}{N \cdot (1+\alpha)}$$

**Step 3:** During the cutting stroke, the ram travels a distance $L$ mm in time $t_c$.

**Step 4:** Average velocity during cutting stroke:
$$\bar{V} = \frac{L}{t_c} = \frac{L \cdot N \cdot (1+\alpha)}{60 \cdot \alpha} \quad \text{mm/s}$$

**Step 5:** Convert to m/min:
$$\bar{V} = \frac{L \cdot N \cdot (1+\alpha)}{60 \cdot \alpha} \times \frac{60}{1000}$$

**Final Result:**
$$\boxed{\bar{V} = \frac{L \cdot N \cdot (1+\alpha)}{1000 \cdot \alpha} \quad \text{m/min}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Machining Time for a Keyway

### Given
- Length of keyway = $l$ mm
- Feed per stroke = $f$ mm/stroke
- Number of strokes per minute = $N$
- Approach allowance = $\ell_0$ mm

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** The tool must traverse the keyway length PLUS an over-travel/approach distance on each side.
$$L_{effective} = l + \ell_0$$

**Step 2:** In each stroke, the tool advances laterally by $f$ mm (cross-feed).

**Step 3:** Number of strokes required to complete one full pass across the width $w$:
$$n_w = \frac{w}{f}$$

**Step 4:** Each stroke takes $\frac{60}{N}$ seconds. So time for one pass over length $L_{effective}$:
$$t_{one\,pass} = \frac{L_{effective}}{N \cdot f} \quad \text{minutes}$$

**Step 5:** Total time for $n_w$ strokes (assuming tool width = keyway width, only 1 pass needed laterally):
$$T_m = \frac{(l + \ell_0) \cdot n_w}{f \cdot N}$$

**Step 6:** For a single-pass keyway where tool width = keyway width, $n_w = 1$:
$$\boxed{T_m = \frac{(l + \ell_0)}{f \cdot N} \quad \text{minutes}}$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Standard)

### Problem Statement
> A keyway of length 80 mm and width 10 mm is to be cut on a mild steel shaft using a shaping machine. The cutting speed recommended is **20 m/min**. The quick return ratio is **2:1**. The feed per stroke is **0.3 mm**. Calculate:
> (a) The number of strokes per minute.
> (b) The time required to machine the keyway.
> Take over-travel = 15 mm.

### Given Data
- $L$ (stroke length) = 80 + 15 + 15 = **110 mm** (assuming stroke = keyway length + over-travel on both sides)

Actually, let me use $L$ = stroke length set on machine = 110 mm.

- $V$ = 20 m/min
- $\alpha$ = 2
- $f$ = 0.3 mm/stroke
- $l$ = 80 mm
- $\ell_0$ = 15 mm (over-travel at one end)

### Part (a): Number of Strokes per Minute

**Step 1:** Rearrange the cutting speed formula:
$$V = \frac{L \cdot N \cdot (1+\alpha)}{1000 \cdot \alpha}$$

**Step 2:** Solve for N:
$$N = \frac{V \cdot 1000 \cdot \alpha}{L \cdot (1+\alpha)}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute values:
$$N = \frac{20 \cdot 1000 \cdot 2}{110 \cdot (1+2)}$$

**Step 4:** Compute numerator:
$$20 \cdot 1000 \cdot 2 = 40000$$

**Step 5:** Compute denominator:
$$110 \cdot 3 = 330$$

**Step 6:** Final division:
$$N = \frac{40000}{330} = 121.21 \approx \mathbf{121 \text{ strokes/min}}$$

### Part (b): Machining Time

**Step 1:** Total effective length:
$$L_{eff} = l + \ell_0 = 80 + 15 = 95 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the formula:
$$T_m = \frac{L_{eff}}{f \cdot N}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute:
$$T_m = \frac{95}{0.3 \cdot 121}$$

**Step 4:** Compute denominator:
$$0.3 \cdot 121 = 36.3$$

**Step 5:** Final answer:
$$T_m = \frac{95}{36.3} = 2.617 \approx \mathbf{2.62 \text{ minutes}}$$

### Final Answers
- **(a) N ≈ 121 strokes/min**
- **(b) Tₘ ≈ 2.62 minutes**

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Numerical Traps

| Mistake Type | Common Error | Correct Approach |
|---|---|---|
| Unit confusion | Using $L$ in meters instead of mm | Convert $L$ to mm; $V$ to m/min; ensure 1000 factor |
| Stroke length confusion | Setting $L = l$ (workpiece length) | $L = l + 2\ell_0$ (over-travel both sides) |
| Quick return misuse | Forgetting the $(1+\alpha)/\alpha$ factor | Always include the quick return correction |
| Feed direction | Confusing feed with depth of cut | Feed = lateral (per stroke); Depth = vertical (per pass) |
| Tool width assumption | Assuming tool is narrower than keyway | In shaping, tool width = final keyway width |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Shaping Machine Kinematic Block Architecture

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram type:** Functional block-flow showing how rotary motion is converted to linear reciprocating cutting action and how the feed system operates.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M1[Electric Motor 3 Phase AC] --> M2[Bull Gear Drive Pulley]
    M2 --> M3[Crank Pin on Bull Gear]
    M3 --> M4[Slotted Lever Whitworth Mechanism]
    M4 --> M5[Oscillating Lever Drives Ram]
    M5 --> M6[Ram Carries Tool Holder and Tool]
    M6 --> M7[Linear Reciprocating Cutting Stroke]
    M7 --> M8[Workpiece Held in Machine Vice on Table]
    M8 --> M9[Cutting Action on Workpiece]
    M9 --> M10[Clapper Box Lifts Tool on Return]
    P1[Ratchet and Pawl Feed System] --> P2[Cross Slide of Worktable]
    P2 --> P3[Intermittent Cross Feed per Stroke]
    V1[Stroke Adjustment Knob] --> V2[Sets Crank Pin Radius]
    V2 --> V3[Determines Stroke Length L]
    S1[Feed Dial Setting] --> S2[Sets Feed per Stroke f]
    S2 --> P1
    S3[Depth Stop Dial] --> S4[Limits Vertical Downfeed]
    S4 --> S5[Controls Depth of Cut d]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Step-by-Step Procedure for Cutting a Keyway

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Step 1 Receive Job Drawing and Workpiece] --> A2[Step 2 Mark Keyway Centerlines Using Vernier Height Gauge]
    A2 --> A3[Step 3 Clamp Workpiece in Machine Vice on Shaper Table]
    A3 --> A4[Step 4 Align Marking with Ram Tool Axis]
    A4 --> A5[Step 5 Set Stroke Length L on Machine]
    A5 --> A6[Step 6 Adjust Stroke Position Front Rear Dead Center]
    A6 --> A7[Step 7 Mount Keyway Tool in Tool Holder]
    A7 --> A8[Step 8 Set Tool Clearance Angle 6 to 8 Degrees]
    A8 --> A9[Step 9 Set Feed Dial to 0.3 mm per stroke]
    A9 --> A10[Step 10 Engage Ram and Perform Rough Pass]
    A10 --> A11[Step 11 Inspect with Vernier Caliper]
    A11 --> A12{Dimensions within Limit}
    A12 -- No --> A10
    A12 -- Yes --> A13[Step 12 Reduce Feed to 0.1 mm per stroke]
    A13 --> A14[Step 13 Perform Finishing Pass]
    A14 --> A15[Step 14 Final Inspection with Depth Gauge and Caliper]
    A15 --> A16[Step 15 Check Surface Roughness with Comparator]
    A16 --> A17[Step 16 Record Observations and Submit]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Block Architecture of Cutting Parameter Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[Identify Workpiece Material] --> B2[Refer Machinability Chart]
    B2 --> B3[Select Tool Material HSS or Carbide]
    B3 --> B4[Choose Cutting Speed V from Table]
    B4 --> B5[Determine Stroke Length L from Drawing]
    B5 --> B6[Calculate Strokes per Minute N]
    B6 --> B7[Choose Feed f based on Finish Quality]
    B7 --> B8[Determine Depth of Cut d from Stock Allowance]
    B8 --> B9[Verify Power Availability on Machine]
    B9 --> B10[Generate Machining Time Estimate T]
    B10 --> B11[Setup Machine and Begin Cutting]
```

---

## 4.4 Schematic Description — Keyway Tool Geometry (Text-Based)

Since Mermaid cannot render orthographic engineering drawings, the following text-block describes the standard keyway tool geometry:

```
        Top View                    Side View                Front View
       ___________                ___________              ___________
      |           |              |           |            |           |
      |  /        |              |  /        |            |     ____ |
      | /  W=10mm |              | /  W=10mm |            |    |    ||
      |/__________|              |/__________|            |____|____||
      |\\          |              |           |            |  Clearance
      | \\  W=10mm |              |  Rake=0°  |            |  Angle 6°
      |__\\________|              |___________|            |___________

      Width = Keyway Width         Nose Radius = 0.4 mm     Side Relief = 8°
      (equal to groove)            Relief Angle = 12°        Cutting Edge: Sharp
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Q: Define quick return ratio as applied to a shaping machine. What is its typical value?**

**Model Answer:**

The quick return ratio (α) of a shaping machine is defined as the ratio of the time taken for the cutting (forward) stroke to the time taken for the return (idle) stroke of the ram.

$$\alpha = \frac{t_c}{t_r}$$

It is achieved using a crank and slotted lever (Whitworth) mechanism, where the crank rotates at uniform angular velocity but the lever oscillates non-uniformly — making the return stroke faster. The typical value of α for a standard shaping machine is **2:1 to 3:1** (commonly **2.5:1**).

This mechanism reduces idle time and increases productivity.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: Differentiate between an open keyway and a closed keyway. List two applications each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Open Keyway | Closed Keyway |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Keyway cut along the outer surface of a shaft | Keyway cut inside a bore or hub |
| Top surface | Open along the length | Fully enclosed on all 4 sides |
| Cutting method | Easy — single-sided tool access | Difficult — requires special tool or slotting |
| Application 1 | Shaft-key-pulley assembly | Gear-bore-key-coupling assembly |
| Application 2 | Cylindrical shaft for gear | Hub of a pulley mounted on shaft |
| Tool used in shaping | Standard keyway tool | Slotting tool or end-mill (in milling) |

---

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **As per KTU 2024 Scheme, Part B questions from lab modules carry internal choice. Both options A and B must be attempted structure-wise.**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Q: A rectangular groove of length 60 mm, width 12 mm and depth 8 mm is to be cut on a mild steel workpiece using a shaping machine. The cutting speed is 18 m/min, quick return ratio is 2.5, feed per stroke is 0.25 mm. Calculate:**
**(a) [7 marks] The number of strokes per minute and the average cutting velocity.**
**(b) [7 marks] The machining time required. Also, describe the procedure to cut the groove step by step.**

#### Solution (a):

**Step 1:** Identify given values:
- Length of groove $l = 60$ mm
- Stroke length set on machine $L = l + 2\ell_0 = 60 + 20 = 80$ mm (assuming $\ell_0 = 10$ mm on each side)
- Cutting speed $V = 18$ m/min
- Quick return ratio $\alpha = 2.5$
- Feed per stroke $f = 0.25$ mm

**Step 2:** Rearrange formula for strokes per minute:
$$N = \frac{V \cdot 1000 \cdot \alpha}{L \cdot (1+\alpha)}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute:
$$N = \frac{18 \cdot 1000 \cdot 2.5}{80 \cdot (1+2.5)} = \frac{45000}{80 \cdot 3.5} = \frac{45000}{280}$$

$$\boxed{N = 160.71 \approx 161 \text{ strokes/min}}$$

**Step 4:** Verify average cutting velocity (sanity check):
$$V_{avg} = \frac{L \cdot N \cdot (1+\alpha)}{1000 \cdot \alpha} = \frac{80 \cdot 161 \cdot 3.5}{1000 \cdot 2.5} = \frac{45080}{2500}$$

$$V_{avg} = 18.03 \text{ m/min} \checkmark \text{ (matches input)}$$

#### Solution (b):

**Step 1:** Machining time:
$$T_m = \frac{L_{eff}}{f \cdot N} = \frac{80}{0.25 \cdot 161} = \frac{80}{40.25}$$

$$\boxed{T_m = 1.99 \approx 2 \text{ minutes}}$$

**Step 2: Procedure to cut the groove:**

1. **Job preparation:** Receive the mild steel workpiece with job drawing. Clean and deburr the workpiece.

2. **Marking:** Apply marking blue (engineer's blue) on the workpiece surface. Using a vernier height gauge and scriber, mark the boundaries of the groove (length and width) precisely.

3. **Work holding:** Mount the workpiece in the machine vice such that the marked groove area projects above the vice jaws by at least 10 mm. Tighten the vice firmly.

4. **Setup:** Align the marking center line with the path of the ram tool using a dial indicator or by visual inspection.

5. **Stroke adjustment:** Set the stroke length on the machine using the stroke adjustment knob. Position the stroke such that the tool enters and exits beyond the groove boundaries by 5–10 mm on each side.

6. **Tool mounting:** Mount a grooving/keyway tool (width = 12 mm) in the tool holder with proper clearance angle (6°–8°).

7. **Feed setting:** Set the feed dial to 0.25 mm/stroke.

8. **Depth setting:** Set the depth stop on the vertical slide to 8 mm.

9. **Rough cut:** Start the machine, engage the ram, and take the first roughing pass. Check the depth after one pass.

10. **Inspection:** Use a vernier caliper to check the width (12 mm) and a depth micrometer to check depth (8 mm).

11. **Finishing pass:** Take the final light pass (0.05 mm depth) for surface finish.

12. **Final inspection:** Verify all dimensions as per drawing tolerance (±0.05 mm).

13. **Cleaning:** Remove the workpiece, clean, deburr, and apply preservative oil.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q: With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a shaping machine. Also explain how the crank and slotted lever mechanism produces quick return motion.**

#### Solution Outline:

**(a) [7 marks] Working Principle:**

The shaping machine operates on the principle of converting **rotary motion** into **linear reciprocating motion**. The main components are:

| S.No. | Component | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Base | Supports the entire machine, provides rigidity |
| 2 | Column | Vertical housing for ram and mechanism |
| 3 | Cross-rail | Horizontal beam carrying the ram |
| 4 | Ram | Reciprocating slide carrying tool holder |
| 5 | Tool head | Holds the cutting tool; can swivel and downfeed |
| 6 | Clapper box | Lifts tool on return stroke |
| 7 | Worktable | Supports the vice and workpiece; provides cross-feed |
| 8 | Vice | Holds the workpiece |
| 9 | Drive motor | Provides rotary power |
| 10 | Crank and slotted lever | Converts rotary to reciprocating motion |

**Working:** The motor drives the bull gear → crank pin rotates → slotted lever oscillates → ram reciprocates → tool cuts on forward stroke and lifts on return stroke → after each return, the ratchet-pawl advances the worktable by `f` mm laterally.

**(b) [7 marks] Quick Return Mechanism:**

The crank and slotted lever (Whitworth mechanism) produces quick return through **non-uniform oscillation of the lever** despite uniform rotation of the crank.

- The crank pin engages a slot in the lever.
- The lever is pivoted at one end.
- As the crank rotates 360°, the lever oscillates between two extremes.
- The geometry is designed such that the lever takes a **larger angle to move in one direction (forward stroke)** and a **smaller angle to return (return stroke)**.
- Since crank speed is uniform, time ∝ angle, hence return stroke takes less time.

**Mathematical relation:**
$$\alpha = \frac{t_c}{t_r} = \frac{180° + \beta}{180° - \beta}$$

**Derivation (already in SECTION 3).** Typical value α = 2 to 3.

**Block diagram (textual representation):**

```
Motor → Bull Gear → Crank Pin (uniform ω)
                       ↓
              Slotted Lever (oscillating)
                       ↓
                   Connecting Link
                       ↓
                      RAM (reciprocates)
                       ↓
              ┌────────┴────────┐
              ↓                 ↓
        Cutting Stroke    Return Stroke
        (slower, lower)   (faster, lifted)
              ↓                 ↓
           Tool cuts       Clapper box lifts tool
```

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Deduction Areas in PCMEL408 Lab Exam (Examiner's Perspective):**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency (-1 to -2 marks):** Students mix mm and m in cutting speed formulas. ALWAYS write units explicitly.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the Quick Return Factor (-2 marks):** Using $V = L \cdot N / 1000$ instead of the corrected formula. The (1+α)/α factor is **mandatory** in shaping.
>
> 3. **Wrong Tool Width Concept (-1 mark):** Writing that the tool width is "less than keyway width and side cutting is done." This is FALSE for shaping — only TRUE for milling.
>
> 4. **Missing Clapper Box Explanation (-1 mark):** Any answer on shaping MUST mention the clapper box — it is a signature feature.
>
> 5. **Procedure Without Safety (-1 mark):** Steps must include safety precautions: wearing safety goggles, ensuring proper tool clamping, checking vice tightness, keeping hands away from cutting zone.
>
> 6. **Confusing Feed Direction (-1 mark):** Feed is LATERAL (cross-feed of table). Depth of cut is VERTICAL (downfeed of tool). Do not interchange.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Use this as a 5-minute revision flashcard before entering the exam hall.**

### ⚡ Must-Know Formulas
- ✅ $N = \dfrac{V \cdot 1000 \cdot \alpha}{L \cdot (1+\alpha)}$ — Strokes per minute
- ✅ $T_m = \dfrac{(l + \ell_0)}{f \cdot N}$ — Machining time
- ✅ $\alpha = \dfrac{180° + \beta}{180° - \beta}$ — Quick return ratio
- ✅ $V = \dfrac{2 \cdot L \cdot N \cdot (1+\alpha)}{1000 \cdot \alpha}$ — Cutting velocity

### 🔧 Must-Know Components
- ✅ Crank and slotted lever (Whitworth mechanism)
- ✅ Ratchet and pawl (cross-feed mechanism)
- ✅ Clapper box (lifts tool on return stroke)
- ✅ Stroke adjustment knob (sets $L$)
- ✅ Feed dial (sets $f$)
- ✅ Depth stop (limits vertical downfeed)

### 📐 Must-Know Terminology
- ✅ Open vs Closed keyway
- ✅ Through vs Blind groove
- ✅ Quick return ratio
- ✅ Cutting vs Return stroke
- ✅ Cross-feed vs Downfeed

### ⚠️ Must-Avoid Mistakes
- ❌ Don't confuse shaping with milling — tool width = groove width in shaping
- ❌ Don't forget the $(1+\alpha)/\alpha$ factor in cutting speed
- ❌ Don't omit clapper box in mechanism explanation
- ❌ Don't use $V = \pi D N / 1000$ — that's for rotary tools, NOT shaping
- ❌ Don't forget to add over-travel $\ell_0$ when computing machining time

### 🎯 Viva-Voce Quick Answers
- **Q: Why is return stroke faster?** → Quick return mechanism saves idle time.
- **Q: Why is shaping preferred over planing for small jobs?** → Lower cost, smaller footprint, suitable for small workshops.
- **Q: Why can't we cut tapered keyways in shaping?** → Tool moves only linearly; taper requires angular tool setting (possible but limited).
- **Q: What is the typical surface finish in shaping?** → 1.6 to 3.2 µm Ra.
- **Q: Why use HSS over carbide for keyway tools?** → HSS can be ground to narrow widths easily; carbide is brittle at sharp edges.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Exercises on Drilling Machine: Drilling, Boring, Reaming, Tapping and Counter Sinking
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMEL408 - MANUFACTURING TECHNOLOGY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Exercises on Drilling Machine: Drilling, Boring, Reaming, Tapping and Counter Sinking

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition — The Drilling Machine (Drill Press)

A **drilling machine** is a powered, stationary or portable machine tool designed to produce **cylindrical holes** in solid workpieces, enlarge existing holes, finish bored surfaces, or cut internal threads using a rotating, multi-point (or single-point) cutting tool held in a spindle. The standard KTU classification recognises it as a *non-portable, power-driven, vertical-spindle machine tool* with reciprocating feed motion in the Z-axis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 2 Scope:** Five chip-producing operations are grouped under one experiment because they share a *common machine base* and *common kinematic chain*. Students must record the procedure, tool nomenclature, and feed/ speed chart values for **each** sub-experiment in the lab record.

### 1.2 The Five Operations — One-Liner Definitions

| # | Operation | Formal Definition |
|---|-----------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Drilling** | Producing a *new* cylindrical hole in a solid workpiece using a multi-point rotating tool called a **twist drill**. |
| 2 | **Boring** | *Enlarging* an already existing hole (cast, forged, or drilled) using a **single-point cutting tool** held on a boring bar. |
| 3 | **Reaming** | A *finishing* operation that improves the dimensional accuracy, geometric form, and surface finish of an already-drilled or bored hole using a **multi-tooth reamer**. |
| 4 | **Tapping** | Cutting **internal threads** inside a pre-drilled hole using a multi-tooth, self-leading tool called a **tap**. |
| 5 | **Counter Sinking** | Producing a *conical recess* at the entrance of a hole so that a flat-head fastener (screw, rivet, bolt) sits flush with the surface. |

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Carpenter" Analogy — picture yourself assembling a wooden chair:**
>
> 1. **Drilling** → Using a hand drill (or cordless drill) to create a *fresh* hole in a wooden plank. The drill bit rotates while you push it forward.
> 2. **Boring** → You already have a small hole, but the chair's wooden dowel is thicker. So you take a *lathe-like* enlarging bar and open up the existing hole.
> 3. **Reaming** → The hole is *almost* the correct size, but slightly rough. You pass a finishing tool through it to make the diameter exact and the surface smooth — like sanding the inside of a hole.
> 4. **Tapping** → You want to screw a bolt into the hole. So you cut helical grooves (threads) on the inside of the hole, just like the spiral on a screw itself.
> 5. **Counter Sinking** → You want the flat-head screw to sit *flush* with the wood surface. So you make a shallow, funnel-shaped dip at the hole entrance so the screw head disappears inside the wood.

### 1.4 Classification Snapshot (for the Record File)

- **Bench Drilling Machine** — small, mounted on a workbench (most common in KTU labs).
- **Pillar/Upright Drilling Machine** — column-mounted, taller work envelope.
- **Radial Drilling Machine** — spindle moves on a radial arm; for large workpieces.
- **Gang Drilling Machine** — multiple spindles in a row.
- **Sensitive Drilling Machine** — hand-feed only, for very small holes / precision.

> [!TIP]
> **Sensible mnemonic for the five operations' sequence in any given workpiece:**
> **D**rill → **B**ore (if needed) → **R**eam (if needed) → **T**ap (if threaded) → **C**ounter-Sink (if flush screw).
> *"**D**esigning **B**ridges **R**equires **T**hinking, **C**alling-out."*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Operations — Structured Theoretical Breakdown

#### 2.1.1 DRILLING (Twist Drill Operation)

**The Kinematic Chain:** The drill rotates about its own axis (primary motion) AND advances linearly into the workpiece (secondary feed motion). Both motions are concurrent — this is what distinguishes drilling from turning.

**Anatomy of a Twist Drill (must know for the viva):**
- **Point / Lip** — the cutting edge that initiates the chip.
- **Flutes** — helical grooves that evacuate chips.
- **Body** — the cylindrical shank that has the cutting lips.
- **Shank** — the gripping portion (straight or tapered Morse taper).
- **Margin / Land** — the narrow outer strip that guides the drill.
- **Web / Core** — the central column that provides rigidity.
- **Neck** — relief between body and shank (for regrinding identification).

**Drill Material (KTU must-know):** **High-Speed Steel (HSS)** for general lab work; **carbide (WC-Co)** tipped for CNC/high-speed production. HSS can be heat-treated to **62–65 HRC**.

**Standard Drill Angle:** Conventional point angle = **118°** (general purpose); 135° for hardened steels; 90° for soft materials like brass/ aluminium.

#### 2.1.2 BORING (Enlargement of an Existing Hole)

- Boring is performed when the desired diameter is **larger** than the largest standard drill, or when the hole must be **concentric, accurate, and true**.
- Tool = **Single-point Boring Bar** (rigid) or **Boring Head** (adjustable diameter).
- The boring tool does NOT drill — it *only removes material radially* (it has no axial drilling point).
- The pre-existing hole is called the **pilot hole** or **parent hole**.
- **L/D ratio** of the boring bar should be ≤ 4:1 to avoid chatter.

> [!NOTE]
> **Boring vs. Drilling — the Big Difference:**
> | Aspect | Drilling | Boring |
> |---|---|---|
> | Starting condition | Solid metal | Existing hole |
> | Tool | Multi-point (twist drill) | Single-point (boring bar) |
> | Diameter | Fixed (drill size) | Variable (adjustable insert position) |
> | Production of initial hole? | Yes | No |
> | Can correct misalignment? | Limited | Excellent |

#### 2.1.3 REAMING (Sizing & Finishing)

- Reaming is **NOT** a roughing operation. It removes only a tiny **reaming allowance** (typically **0.1 mm to 0.4 mm** for holes up to 25 mm, and **0.2 mm to 0.5 mm** for larger holes).
- Surface finish: **Ra 0.4 to 1.6 μm** achievable.
- Tolerance: **IT7 to IT8** (one grade better than drilling).
- **Reamer clearance angle** is small (5°–8°) to prevent chatter and ensure smooth finish.
- **Cannot correct misalignment** — the parent hole must already be concentric.

#### 2.1.4 TAPPING (Internal Thread Cutting)

- A **tap** is essentially a hardened screw with axial cutting flutes.
- Standard **pitch** for KTU lab work (M10 × 1.5, M12 × 1.75, M16 × 2.0) must be memorised.
- **Tap drill diameter** rule: $D_{td} = D_{maj} - P$, where $D_{maj}$ = nominal major thread diameter, $P$ = pitch.
- The tap is held in a **Tap Holder / Tap Wrench** with a sliding socket that permits axial float — this prevents tap breakage when the spindle feeds faster than the thread pitch demands.
- **Tapping cannot be done on a drill press without a tap holder** (rigid tap = broken tap every time on blind holes).

> [!WARNING]
> A common lab mistake is to **feed the tap manually** instead of using the power feed. Manual tapping on a drill press produces a 60% scrap rate due to broken taps. **Always use a floating tap holder.**

#### 2.1.5 COUNTER SINKING (Conical Recess)

- Tool = **Countersink** with a pilot that fits inside the drilled hole.
- Standard included angles: **60°** (socket-head cap screws), **82°** (wood / counter-bore screws), **90°** (flat-head rivets).
- The drill press spindle speed for countersinking is **30–50% lower** than the equivalent drilling speed, because the cross-sectional area of engagement is much higher (conical surface area).
- The depth of countersink = (head height) of the fastener + safety margin of **0.5 mm**.

### 2.2 The Cutting Parameters — Universal Drilling Formulas

These formulas apply to **drilling, boring, reaming, countersinking, and tapping** with the appropriate substitution of cutting speed and feed.

**Cutting Speed** ($V_c$) — relative velocity between tool and workpiece at the cutting edge.

$$V_c = \frac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000} \quad \left[ \frac{m}{min} \right]$$

**Spindle Speed** ($N$) — rotational speed of the spindle.

$$N = \frac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi \cdot D} \quad \left[ rpm \right]$$

**Feed Rate** ($v_f$) — linear feed per minute.

$$v_f = f_r \cdot N \quad \left[ \frac{mm}{min} \right]$$

**Feed per Revolution** ($f_r$) — depends on the tool and material; tabulated in machinability handbooks.

**Material Removal Rate** (MRR) — volume of metal removed per unit time.

$$\text{MRR} = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^{2} \cdot v_f \quad \left[ \frac{mm^{3}}{min} \right]$$

**Machining Time** (Drilling / Boring / Reaming / Counter Sinking) — for a depth $L$:

$$T_{m} = \frac{L + 0.3 \, D}{v_f} \quad \left[ min \right]$$

The **+0.3 D** term accounts for the **drill point approach distance** (conical tip must clear the workpiece bottom). For a **through hole**, use **+D** instead of **+0.3 D** to ensure full breakthrough.

**Machining Time (Tapping)** — special case: feed per revolution equals the thread pitch.

$$T_{m,\text{tap}} = \frac{L + P}{N \cdot P} \quad \left[ min \right]$$

> Where $P$ is the thread pitch in mm and $N$ is the spindle rpm.

**Thrust Force** on the drill (empirical) — useful for power calculation.

$$F_{t} = C_{F} \cdot D \cdot f_r \quad \left[ N \right]$$

> Where $C_F$ is the **specific cutting force** in N/mm², tabulated for each work material (e.g., mild steel ≈ 2000 N/mm², aluminium ≈ 750 N/mm²).

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet (All-In-One)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorise this **one-page table** — it covers 90% of the numerical questions asked in KTU 2024 Scheme lab viva and ESE.

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Unit | Used For |
|---|----------|--------|---------|------|----------|
| 1 | Cutting Speed | $V_c$ | $V_c = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000}$ | m/min | All 5 operations |
| 2 | Spindle Speed | $N$ | $N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi \cdot D}$ | rpm | All 5 operations |
| 3 | Feed Rate | $v_f$ | $v_f = f_r \cdot N$ | mm/min | All 5 operations |
| 4 | MRR (Drilling) | MRR | $\text{MRR} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^{2} \cdot v_f$ | mm³/min | Drilling, Boring |
| 5 | Drilling Time | $T_m$ | $T_m = \dfrac{L + 0.3 \, D}{v_f}$ | min | Drilling, Boring, Reaming, CS |
| 6 | Tapping Time | $T_m$ | $T_m = \dfrac{L + P}{N \cdot P}$ | min | Tapping only |
| 7 | Tap Drill Dia. | $D_{td}$ | $D_{td} = D_{maj} - P$ | mm | Tapping only |
| 8 | Thrust Force | $F_t$ | $F_t = C_F \cdot D \cdot f_r$ | N | Drilling power |
| 9 | Power | $P$ | $P = \dfrac{F_t \cdot v_c}{60 \cdot 1000 \cdot \eta}$ | kW | Drilling motor sizing |
| 10 | Spindle Pitch | $P$ | $P = 1 / \text{TPI}$ | mm | Imperial tapping |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Recommended Cutting Speeds (HSS tool on Mild Steel, KTU lab typical):**
> - **Drilling:** $V_c$ = 25 to 35 m/min
> - **Boring:** $V_c$ = 20 to 30 m/min
> - **Reaming:** $V_c$ = 8 to 15 m/min (LOW — finishing)
> - **Tapping:** $V_c$ = 4 to 8 m/min (LOWEST — chip evacuation critical)
> - **Counter Sinking:** $V_c$ = 15 to 25 m/min

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> The five drilling operations form the **backbone of component interchangeability**:
> 1. **Drilling + Boring** = large, accurate holes (engine cylinder blocks, bearing seats).
> 2. **Drilling + Reaming** = precision pins and dowel fits (jigs and fixtures).
> 3. **Drilling + Tapping** = fastener assembly (every machine you can imagine).
> 4. **Drilling + Counter Sinking** = aerodynamics, aesthetics, and snag-free panels (aircraft fuselages, consumer electronics).
> 5. **Boring + Reaming** = hydraulic cylinder bores, where seal performance depends on Ra ≤ 0.8 μm.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From the Definition of Cutting Speed to the Spindle-Speed Formula

**Starting Definition (textbook, axiomatic):**

Cutting speed is defined as the *instantaneous linear velocity* of a point on the cutting edge relative to the workpiece surface. For a rotating tool of diameter $D$ turning at $N$ revolutions per minute, the circumference traced in one revolution is $\pi \cdot D$, and the linear distance covered in one minute is therefore $\pi \cdot D \cdot N$.

However, since the diameter $D$ is in **millimetres** and the desired unit is **metres per minute**, we must divide by **1000** to convert mm to m.

$$V_c = \frac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000} \quad \left[ \frac{m}{min} \right]$$

**Rearranging for Spindle Speed $N$ (algebraic isolation step-by-step):**

**Step 1.** Start from the definition.

$$V_c = \frac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000}$$

**Step 2.** Multiply both sides by 1000.

$$1000 \cdot V_c = \pi \cdot D \cdot N$$

**Step 3.** Divide both sides by $\pi \cdot D$.

$$\frac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi \cdot D} = N$$

**Step 4.** Express with the quantity of interest on the LHS (standard form).

$$N = \frac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi \cdot D} \quad \left[ rpm \right]$$

> **Sanity Check:** If $D$ increases (larger drill), $N$ must decrease to maintain the same $V_c$. This is consistent with workshop practice and validates the formula.

### 3.2 Derivation: Drilling Machining Time $T_m$

**Step 1.** The drill must travel an effective axial distance $L_{\text{eff}}$.

For a **blind hole**, the drill point (cone) must travel past the cylindrical portion by an amount equal to the drill's **chisel-edge approach**. For a 118° standard point drill, this is empirically **≈ 0.3 D**.

$$L_{\text{eff}} = L + 0.3 \, D \quad \text{(blind hole)}$$

For a **through hole**, the drill must emerge entirely, so we add a full diameter.

$$L_{\text{eff}} = L + D \quad \text{(through hole)}$$

**Step 2.** The feed rate is $v_f$ in mm/min, so the time required to traverse $L_{\text{eff}}$ is:

$$T_m = \frac{L_{\text{eff}}}{v_f} = \frac{L + 0.3 \, D}{v_f} \quad \left[ min \right]$$

**Step 3.** Substituting $v_f = f_r \cdot N$:

$$T_m = \frac{L + 0.3 \, D}{f_r \cdot N} \quad \left[ min \right]$$

### 3.3 Derivation: Tapping Machining Time $T_m$

**Step 1.** In tapping, the feed per revolution is **NOT** an arbitrary $f_r$ — it is locked to the **pitch** $P$ of the thread. The tap must advance exactly $P$ mm for each revolution to keep the thread geometry correct.

$$v_{f,\text{tap}} = P \cdot N \quad \left[ \frac{mm}{min} \right]$$

**Step 2.** The effective axial distance to be cut is $L$ (thread length) plus the pitch $P$ to ensure a full thread at the entry and exit.

$$L_{\text{eff,tap}} = L + P$$

**Step 3.** Time = Distance / Feed Rate.

$$T_{m,\text{tap}} = \frac{L + P}{P \cdot N} \quad \left[ min \right]$$

> **Why the floating tap holder is non-negotiable:** The spindle feed of the drill press is fixed at $f_r \cdot N$. If this does not exactly equal $P \cdot N$ (the geometric requirement), the tap is either *pulled* (tap breaks) or *pushed* (stripped thread). The floating socket decouples the two feed rates, ensuring the tap self-feeds at exactly the pitch rate.

### 3.4 Worked Example 1 — Drilling Time (Board-Exam Style)

**Problem:** A Ø20 mm hole is to be drilled to a depth of 50 mm in a mild-steel plate. The cutting speed is 30 m/min and the feed per revolution is 0.20 mm/rev. The hole is **through** the plate. Calculate the spindle speed, feed rate, MRR, and machining time.

**Given Data:**
- $D = 20$ mm
- $L = 50$ mm (through hole)
- $V_c = 30$ m/min
- $f_r = 0.20$ mm/rev

**Step A — Spindle Speed $N$:**

$$N = \frac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi \cdot D} = \frac{1000 \cdot 30}{\pi \cdot 20} = \frac{30000}{62.832} \approx 477.5 \text{ rpm}$$

Take the **nearest standard speed** = **480 rpm** (drill press dials usually have steps: 60, 100, 160, 240, 320, 480, 640, 800, 1000, 1280, 2000).

**Step B — Feed Rate $v_f$:**

$$v_f = f_r \cdot N = 0.20 \cdot 480 = 96 \text{ mm/min}$$

**Step C — Material Removal Rate:**

$$\text{MRR} = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^{2} \cdot v_f = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 20^{2} \cdot 96 = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 400 \cdot 96 \approx 30159 \text{ mm}^{3}/min \approx 30.16 \text{ cm}^{3}/min$$

**Step D — Machining Time (through hole):**

$$T_m = \frac{L + D}{v_f} = \frac{50 + 20}{96} = \frac{70}{96} \approx 0.729 \text{ min} \approx 43.75 \text{ s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Common error in the answer sheet:** Students use $L$ alone in the numerator, forgetting the +D term for a through hole. The drill point must fully clear the bottom — that adds a full diameter of travel.

### 3.5 Worked Example 2 — Tap Drill Diameter & Tapping Time

**Problem:** An M16 × 2.0 mm internal thread is required. The blind hole depth is 30 mm. The cutting speed for tapping in mild steel is 6 m/min. Calculate (a) tap drill diameter, (b) spindle speed, (c) tapping time.

**Step (a) — Tap Drill Diameter:**

$$D_{td} = D_{maj} - P = 16 - 2.0 = 14.0 \text{ mm}$$

Select the **nearest standard fractional drill** = **Ø14.0 mm** (lucky coincidence) or Ø13.75 mm if the standard set is imperial.

**Step (b) — Spindle Speed:**

$$N = \frac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi \cdot D_{maj}} = \frac{1000 \cdot 6}{\pi \cdot 16} = \frac{6000}{50.265} \approx 119.4 \text{ rpm}$$

Round to **120 rpm**.

**Step (c) — Tapping Time:**

$$T_{m,\text{tap}} = \frac{L + P}{N \cdot P} = \frac{30 + 2.0}{120 \cdot 2.0} = \frac{32}{240} \approx 0.133 \text{ min} \approx 8.0 \text{ s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For a **blind hole**, the tap cannot bottom out completely. The thread depth is **2.5 P to 3 P short of the drilled depth** to allow chip accumulation and to provide clearance for the tap's tapered lead-in. The above calculation assumes the full $L$ is threaded; subtract 2 P to 3 P for a realistic blind-hole thread length.

### 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Operation When?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The KTU lab record expects a "selection of operation" justification for every step of the experiment. Use this matrix as your rationale template.**

| Engineering Need | Best Operation | Reason |
|------------------|----------------|--------|
| Make a new Ø12 mm hole | **Drilling** | Ø12 ≤ Ø15 → standard drill exists |
| Make a new Ø80 mm hole | **Drill + Bore** | No Ø80 drill; cast/forge a pilot, then bore |
| Achieve IT7 tolerance on Ø20 mm | **Drill + Ream** | Reaming brings IT8 → IT7 with low Ra |
| Achieve Ra 0.4 μm on Ø40 mm | **Drill + Bore + Ream** | Roughing + finishing stack-up |
| Cut M12 internal thread | **Drill (Ø10.2) + Tap** | Tap drill = 12 - 1.75 = 10.25 mm |
| Flush-mount a flat-head screw | **Drill + Counter Sink (82°)** | Provides conical recess for screw head |
| True up a misaligned cast hole | **Bore** | Boring can correct axial offset |
| Through hole for a clevis pin (precise) | **Drill + Ream** | Pin fits with < 25 μm clearance |

### 3.7 The "Three Standard Drill Angles" — Quick Reference

| Drill Point Angle | Workpiece Material | Why |
|-------------------|-------------------|-----|
| **118°** | Mild steel, cast iron, general purpose | Balanced rake, strong cutting edge |
| **135°** | Hardened steel, stainless, Inconel | Negative effective rake → less heat |
| **90°** | Brass, aluminium, soft plastics, copper | Sharper edge cuts through soft gummy metal without grabbing |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Bench-Type Drilling Machine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[MOTOR 3 PHASE AC SUPPLY] --> B[SPINDLE PULLEY ASSEMBLY]
    B --> C[SPINDLE SHAFT WITH BEARING BLOCK]
    C --> D[SPINDLE NOSE MORTISE OR CHUCK]
    D --> E[DRILL BIT OR TAP OR REAMER]
    E --> F[WORKTABLE WITH T SLOTS]
    F --> G[WORKPIECE CLAMPED IN VICE]
    G --> H[FEED LEVER QUILL PINION]
    H --> I[QUILL SLEEVE WITH Z AXIS TRAVEL]
    I --> C
    J[FEED HANDLE OPERATOR] --> I
    K[BASE COLUMN STRUCTURE] --> A
    K --> F

```

> The **arrows** show the *power-flow path* (top) and the *mechanical feed path* (bottom). The quill (I) translates the rotational motion of the spindle into a vertical feed, which is the heart of all five operations.

### 4.2 Operation-Selection Decision Tree (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[START REQUIREMENT ANALYSIS] --> P1{IS THE HOLE ALREADY EXISTING?}
    P1 -- NO --> P2[DRILLING WITH TWIST DRILL]
    P1 -- YES --> P3{DOES DIAMETER NEED TO INCREASE?}
    P3 -- NO --> P4{IS TOLERANCE TIGHTER THAN IT9?}
    P3 -- YES --> P5[BORING WITH SINGLE POINT TOOL]
    P4 -- NO --> P6[NO OPERATION REAMING SKIP]
    P4 -- YES --> P7[REAMING WITH REAMER]
    P5 --> P8{IS TOLERANCE TIGHTER THAN IT8?}
    P8 -- YES --> P9[BORING PLUS REAMING STACK]
    P8 -- NO --> P5
    P7 --> P10{IS INTERNAL THREAD REQUIRED?}
    P9 --> P10
    P10 -- YES --> P11[TAPPING WITH TAP HOLDER]
    P10 -- NO --> P12{IS FLUSH HEAD SCREW USED?}
    P12 -- YES --> P13[COUNTER SINKING]
    P12 -- NO --> P14[FINAL INSPECTION GAUGE]
    P11 --> P14
    P13 --> P14
    P2 --> P15{IS THREAD OR FLUSH SCREW NEEDED?}
    P15 -- YES --> P10
    P15 -- NO --> P14

    style P5 fill:#C2E0F0,stroke:#222
    style P7 fill:#C2E0F0,stroke:#222
    style P11 fill:#C2E0F0,stroke:#222
    style P13 fill:#C2E0F0,stroke:#222
```

### 4.3 Chip Flow and Tool Engagement Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ROTATING_TOOL
        T1[POINT AND LIPS]
        T2[FLUTE HELICAL GROOVE]
        T3[MARGIN AND LAND]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU lab:** A blocked flute = drill breakage. The **chip-breaker geometry** of a drill point, the **feed rate** (too low → no chip, too high → chip jam), and the **cutting fluid** all influence flute evacuation. Document this in the lab record.

### 4.4 Comparative Cross-Section of the Five Operations

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define "drilling" as a machining operation. With a neat sketch, list the major parts of a twist drill and state the standard point angle for general-purpose drilling on mild steel.**

**Model Answer:**

Drilling is a chip-producing machining operation in which a **multi-point rotating cutting tool** (the twist drill) is fed axially into a stationary (or slowly rotating) workpiece to produce a **new cylindrical hole**.

**Major parts of a twist drill (refer to §2.1.1 of these notes):**
1. **Point / Cutting Lip** – the wedge-shaped cutting edges that initiate the chip.
2. **Flute** – the helical groove that evacuates chips and admits cutting fluid.
3. **Body / Land / Margin** – the cylindrical land that guides the drill and provides clearance.
4. **Web / Core** – the central column connecting the two flutes; provides rigidity.
5. **Neck** – the relief provided for regrinding identification marks.
6. **Shank** – straight (cylindrical) or tapered (Morse taper) gripping portion.

**Standard point angle** for general-purpose mild-steel drilling = **118°**.

> A neat sketch in the answer sheet should label all six parts.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Distinguish between Drilling, Boring, and Reaming. State one industrial application of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Drilling | Boring | Reaming |
|--------|----------|--------|---------|
| **Purpose** | Produce a *new* hole | Enlarge an *existing* hole | Improve *accuracy and finish* of a hole |
| **Tool** | Multi-point twist drill | Single-point boring bar | Multi-tooth reamer |
| **Material removed** | Bulk (full diameter) | Bulk (radial stock) | Tiny allowance (0.1–0.4 mm) |
| **Tolerance** | IT10 to IT12 | IT7 to IT8 | IT7 to IT8 |
| **Surface finish Ra** | 1.6 to 6.3 μm | 0.8 to 3.2 μm | 0.4 to 1.6 μm |

**Industrial Applications:**
- **Drilling:** Pilot hole for tapping a wooden cabinet.
- **Boring:** Cylinder bore of an IC engine block (rough boring + finish boring).
- **Reaming:** Final sizing of dowel-pin holes in a machine tool fixture.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module-Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> Per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern: Module 2 is a single 14-mark question with **internal choice**. Attempt **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B.

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Comprehensive Drill Operation Problem

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Dec 2023 style]**

**(a)** A Ø18 mm hole is to be drilled to a depth of 60 mm in a mild-steel workpiece. The recommended cutting speed is 28 m/min and the feed is 0.18 mm/rev. The hole is **blind**. Calculate:
- (i) Spindle speed $N$
- (ii) Feed rate $v_f$
- (iii) Material removal rate MRR
- (iv) Drilling time $T_m$ **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **procedure for tapping an M12 × 1.75 internal thread** in the same mild steel workpiece, with a labelled sketch of the tap and tap holder. State the tap drill diameter. **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution to Question A(a)

**Given Data:**
- $D = 18$ mm
- $L = 60$ mm (blind hole)
- $V_c = 28$ m/min
- $f_r = 0.18$ mm/rev

**(i) Spindle Speed $N$:**

$$N = \frac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi \cdot D} = \frac{1000 \cdot 28}{\pi \cdot 18} = \frac{28000}{56.549} \approx 495.2 \text{ rpm}$$

Select the **nearest standard speed** = **480 rpm** (or 500 rpm if a stepless drive is available).

**(ii) Feed Rate $v_f$:**

$$v_f = f_r \cdot N = 0.18 \cdot 480 = 86.4 \text{ mm/min}$$

**(iii) Material Removal Rate MRR:**

$$\text{MRR} = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^{2} \cdot v_f = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 18^{2} \cdot 86.4 = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 324 \cdot 86.4 \approx 21991 \text{ mm}^{3}/\text{min} \approx 21.99 \text{ cm}^{3}/\text{min}$$

**(iv) Drilling Time (blind hole):**

$$T_m = \frac{L + 0.3 \, D}{v_f} = \frac{60 + 0.3 \cdot 18}{86.4} = \frac{60 + 5.4}{86.4} = \frac{65.4}{86.4} \approx 0.757 \text{ min} \approx 45.4 \text{ s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Pitfall 1:** Forgetting the +0.3 D term.
> **Pitfall 2:** Failing to round $N$ to a *standard spindle speed* — the examiner awards a half-mark for showing this awareness.

---

##### Model Solution to Question A(b)

**Tap Drill Diameter:**

$$D_{td} = D_{maj} - P = 12 - 1.75 = 10.25 \text{ mm}$$

Select **standard Ø10.2 mm** drill (or the imperial Ø10.3 mm "R" letter drill).

**Procedure for Tapping:**

1. **Pre-drill the tap hole** at Ø10.2 mm to a depth ≥ Thread depth + 3 P (to allow chip clearance and lead-in).
2. **Mount the tap** in a **floating tap holder / tap wrench** — never in the drill chuck directly.
3. **Set spindle speed** to the value calculated for $V_c$ ≈ 5 m/min.
4. **Align the tap axis** with the hole axis; tap holder's axial float ensures this.
5. **Engage the spindle** with cutting fluid (cutting oil for steel, kerosene for aluminium, none for cast iron).
6. **Cut the thread** by advancing the tap; the floating holder allows the tap to self-feed at the correct pitch.
7. **Reverse** the spindle at the end of the thread run; the tap unscrews from the freshly cut thread.
8. **Inspect** the thread with a **GO / NO-GO plug gauge**.

**Labelled Sketch of a Taper Tap + Tap Holder (to be drawn in the answer script):**
- Tap features: **tapered lead-in (first 7–10 threads)**, **chamfered cutting edges**, **straight flutes**, **square drive tang** at the shank.
- Tap holder: **sliding socket** with **drive dogs** engaging the tap tang; **tension/compression spring** to allow axial float; **drive pins** to transmit torque.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-loss areas for the KTU Examiner (Question A):**
> 1. Not stating that the **tap must be used in a *floating* tap holder** (loses 1 mark).
> 2. Using the **wrong** tap-drill formula for through-pitch (e.g., writing $D_{td} = D_{maj}$).
> 3. Drawing the tap without the **drive tang** — full sketch is required.
> 4. Quoting tapping speed of 30 m/min (drilling speed) — speed is ~6× lower.

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Counter Sinking + Boring + Reaming Application

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / May 2024 alternative]**

**(a)** A cast-iron gearbox housing has a **pre-cast pilot hole of Ø40 mm**, which must be enlarged and finished to **Ø42 mm H7** with a **surface finish of Ra 0.8 μm**. The hole depth is 100 mm. The workpiece is mounted on a drilling machine (boring attachment available).
- (i) Identify the **sequence of operations** required to achieve the final dimension.
- (ii) Calculate the **MRR** if boring is done at $V_c$ = 25 m/min and $f_r$ = 0.15 mm/rev, given a final bore diameter of 42 mm and a spindle speed rounded to the nearest 10 rpm.
- (iii) State the **reaming allowance** and the **typical reaming speed range** in m/min. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **counter sinking** as an operation. With a neat sketch, describe the three standard countersink angles and their applications. A Ø10 mm hole is to be counter-sunk to receive a **flat-head wood screw**. Calculate the **countersink diameter at the surface** if the screw head angle is 82° and the screw head height is 4 mm. **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution to Question B(a)

**(i) Sequence of Operations:**

1. **Rough Boring** from Ø40 → Ø41.5 mm (remove bulk stock, relieve the cast skin).
2. **Finish Boring** from Ø41.5 → Ø42.0 mm (achieve IT7 tolerance).
3. **Reaming** with a Ø42 H7 reamer (achieve Ra 0.8 μm finish and final form accuracy).

**Why not skip boring and directly ream?** Reaming cannot remove the cast skin or correct a Ø2 mm radial offset; it would only size the *minimum* diameter. Boring first centres and sizes; reaming only finishes.

**(ii) MRR Calculation:**

**Spindle Speed $N$ (for final boring at Ø42 mm):**

$$N = \frac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi \cdot D} = \frac{1000 \cdot 25}{\pi \cdot 42} = \frac{25000}{131.95} \approx 189.5 \text{ rpm}$$

Round to **190 rpm**.

**Feed Rate:**

$$v_f = f_r \cdot N = 0.15 \cdot 190 = 28.5 \text{ mm/min}$$

**MRR (final boring):**

$$\text{MRR} = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^{2} \cdot v_f = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 42^{2} \cdot 28.5 = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 1764 \cdot 28.5 \approx 39483 \text{ mm}^{3}/\text{min} \approx 39.48 \text{ cm}^{3}/\text{min}$$

**(iii) Reaming Allowance:**

$$\text{Reaming allowance} = 0.2 \text{ to } 0.4 \text{ mm on diameter} \quad (\text{for Ø42, take } 0.3 \text{ mm})$$

**Reaming speed range (HSS, mild/cast steel):** $V_c$ = **8 to 15 m/min** (a *finishing* operation — must be slow).

> [!NOTE]
> If the speed is set to 30 m/min during reaming, the reamer burns, chatters, and produces a *worse* finish than the boring operation it is supposed to improve.

---

##### Model Solution to Question B(b)

**Definition:** Counter sinking is a **secondary drilling operation** that produces a *conical (funnel-shaped) recess* at the entrance of a previously drilled hole. The purpose is to allow a **flat-head fastener** (screw, rivet, or bolt) to sit flush with or below the surface of the parent material.

**Three Standard Countersink Angles:**

| Angle | Application | Example Fastener |
|-------|-------------|------------------|
| **60°** | Machine screws, socket-head cap screws (ISO 7721) | Hex-socket cap screw |
| **82°** | Wood screws, counter-bore screws (most common) | Slotted/Phillips wood screw |
| **90°** | Rivets (aerospace, sheet metal) | Countersunk rivet |

**Countersink Diameter Calculation (Wood Screw, 82°, 4 mm head):**

**Geometry (right-triangle cross-section):**

The countersink is a cone with included half-angle = 82°/2 = 41° from the axis, i.e., 49° from the surface plane.

Wait — the **included angle of the screw head** = 82°. The half-angle from the axis = 41°.

**Trigonometric relationship:**

$$\tan(41°) = \frac{D_{cs}/2}{h}$$

where $D_{cs}$ = countersink diameter at the surface and $h$ = screw head height (depth of countersink) = 4 mm.

**Rearranging:**

$$D_{cs} = 2 \cdot h \cdot \tan(41°) = 2 \cdot 4 \cdot 0.8693 \approx 6.95 \text{ mm}$$

**Verification:** For a Ø10 mm drilled hole, the countersink must be at least Ø6.95 mm at the top of the screw. The countersink is a *cone* — so at the screw-head depth (4 mm below the surface), the cone has zero diameter (the screw tip). At the surface, the cone is widest (Ø6.95 mm). This matches the geometry of a flat-head wood screw.

**Labelled Sketch (to be drawn):**
- 82° included angle at the cone apex.
- Screws shown seated flush with the workpiece surface.
- Ø10 mm drilled hole continuing below the countersink depth.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-loss areas for the KTU Examiner (Question B):**
> 1. Forgetting the **reaming allowance** requirement (loses 1 mark).
> 2. Confusing the **82° included angle** with the **41° half-angle** in the trig calculation.
> 3. Using a $90°$ countersink for a wood screw (wrong standard — wood screws are 82°).
> 4. Drawing the countersink as a *cylinder* instead of a *cone*.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 10 reasons students lose marks in the Module 2 lab exam:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $L$ with $L + 0.3 D$** in the drilling-time formula.
> 2. **Using $V_c = 30$ m/min for tapping** (it should be 4–8 m/min).
> 3. **Drawing a twist drill without labelling the flutes, lips, web, and shank.**
> 4. **Forgetting the floating tap holder** when describing the tapping procedure.
> 5. **Not rounding spindle speed** to a standard dial value.
> 6. **Confusing the tap-drill formula** for fine-pitch vs coarse-pitch threads.
> 7. **Calculating countersink diameter** with the wrong trigonometric half-angle.
> 8. **Ignoring the through-hole** vs **blind-hole** distinction in the time formula.
> 9. **Stating that reaming corrects misalignment** — it does NOT.
> 10. **Mixing up $V_c$ (m/min) and $v_f$ (mm/min)** units in the final answer.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-density rapid-revision checklist — read this 5 minutes before the exam.**

- **Drilling** = new hole, multi-point twist drill, point angle **118°** for mild steel.
- **Boring** = enlarge existing hole, single-point tool, cannot drill solid metal.
- **Reaming** = finishing operation, removes only **0.1–0.4 mm** allowance.
- **Tapping** = internal thread, requires **floating tap holder**, $D_{td} = D_{maj} - P$.
- **Counter sinking** = conical recess, standard angles **60° / 82° / 90°**.

- **Universal formula:** $V_c = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000}$ m/min (all 5 operations).
- **Feed formula:** $v_f = f_r \cdot N$ mm/min.
- **MRR formula:** $\text{MRR} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^{2} \cdot v_f$ mm³/min.
- **Drilling time (blind):** $T_m = \dfrac{L + 0.3 D}{v_f}$.
- **Drilling time (through):** $T_m = \dfrac{L + D}{v_f}$.
- **Tapping time:** $T_m = \dfrac{L + P}{N \cdot P}$.
- **Tap drill diameter:** $D_{td} = D_{maj} - P$.

- **Standard recommended cutting speeds (HSS, mild steel):**
  Drilling 25–35 m/min | Boring 20–30 m/min | Reaming 8–15 m/min | Tapping 4–8 m/min | Counter Sinking 15–25 m/min.

- **Tolerance hierarchy (coarsest → finest):** Drilling (IT10–IT12) → Boring (IT7–IT8) → Reaming (IT7–IT8 with better Ra) → Honing/Grinding (IT5–IT6).

- **Surface finish hierarchy (worst → best):** Drilling Ra 1.6–6.3 μm → Boring Ra 0.8–3.2 μm → Reaming Ra 0.4–1.6 μm → Honing Ra 0.05–0.4 μm.

- **Tool materials (KTU must-know):** HSS (general lab use, 62–65 HRC) vs Carbide (WC-Co) (production, 5× faster).

- **Countersink angle selection rule:** 60° = machine screw, 82° = wood screw, 90° = rivet.

- **Why the floating tap holder is mandatory:** Decouples spindle feed from thread-pitch feed; prevents tap breakage on blind holes.

- **Three drill point angles:** 118° (general), 135° (hardened steel), 90° (soft metals).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Exercises on Drilling Machine: Measurement of Cutting Forces in Drilling Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMEL408 - MANUFACTURING TECHNOLOGY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Exercises on Drilling Machine: Measurement of Cutting Forces in Drilling Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Drilling** is a **material removal machining process** that uses a multi-point cutting tool known as a **twist drill** to create cylindrical holes in a solid workpiece. The **cutting forces** generated during this process are measured to evaluate **machinability**, **tool life**, **power consumption**, and **workpiece deflection**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *"Cutting forces in drilling are the resistance offered by the workpiece material against the rotation and axial feed of the drill bit. These forces are primarily resolved into two orthogonal components: the **Axial Thrust Force (F<sub>z</sub>)** along the drill axis, and the **Cutting Torque (M<sub>z</sub> or T)** about the drill axis."*

The two principal cutting force components are:

| Symbol | Component | Direction | Cause |
|--------|-----------|-----------|-------|
| **F<sub>z</sub>** | Axial Thrust Force | Along drill axis | Engagement of chisel edge and cutting lips |
| **M<sub>z</sub>** | Cutting Torque | About drill axis | Rotational resistance at the cutting lips |

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Screwdriver and the Cork"**
>
> Imagine pushing a **screwdriver** into a wine cork with both hands while twisting it. The **downward push** required to make the screwdriver penetrate the cork is exactly like the **thrust force (F<sub>z</sub>)** in drilling. The **twisting effort** your wrist applies against the cork's resistance is analogous to the **cutting torque (M<sub>z</sub>)**.
>
> - **Harder cork (higher material strength)** → More push needed (higher F<sub>z</sub>) and harder twisting (higher M<sub>z</sub>).
> - **Larger screwdriver (larger drill diameter D)** → More cork is being cut per revolution (higher cross-sectional area A) → More force.
> - **Twisting faster (higher spindle speed N)** → Material is removed more quickly, so total force changes with the volume of chips.
>
> The drill behaves like a **mechanical resistor**: it resists penetration (thrust) and resists rotation (torque), and the machinist must supply both work inputs to make a hole.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard KTU-Lab Metrics to Memorize:**
> - **Cutting speed (V<sub>c</sub>)** — measured in **m/min**
> - **Spindle speed (N)** — measured in **rev/min (rpm)**
> - **Feed (f)** — measured in **mm/rev**
> - **Feed rate (v<sub>f</sub>)** — measured in **mm/min**
> - **Thrust force (F<sub>z</sub>)** — measured in **Newtons (N)**
> - **Cutting torque (M<sub>z</sub>)** — measured in **N·m**
> - **Specific cutting energy (k<sub>s</sub>)** — measured in **N/mm²** or **J/mm³**
> - **Spindle power (P)** — measured in **Watts (W)** or **Kilowatts (kW)**
>
> Standard workpiece materials in this lab: **Mild Steel (EN8)**, **Aluminium (Al6061)**, **Cast Iron**, **Brass**.

## 1.4 Significance of Force Measurement in Manufacturing

> [!NOTE]
> **Why KTU expects you to measure cutting forces:**
> 1. **Tool wear monitoring** — Force signals rise as the drill wears.
> 2. **Machine tool sizing** — Ensures the available motor power exceeds the calculated power.
> 3. **Deflection control** — Excessive F<sub>z</sub> causes hole taper, poor surface finish, and tool breakage.
> 4. **FEM/CNC parameter optimization** — Empirical force coefficients feed directly into CAM libraries.
> 5. **Validation of theoretical models** — KTU lab viva questions test the gap between theory and experiment.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposition of Drilling Forces

Drilling is geometrically complex because the **two cutting lips** and the **chisel edge** of the drill engage the workpiece simultaneously, and the cutting velocity varies radially from zero at the drill center to a maximum at the outer corner.

The total **resultant force** on the drill can be decomposed as follows:

- **F<sub>z</sub> (Thrust Force):** Acts **axially downward** along the drill axis. It is the sum of:
  - F<sub>z,lips</sub> — Force from the two main cutting lips (≈ 50–60% of total thrust)
  - F<sub>z,chisel</sub> — Force from the chisel edge pushing metal aside (≈ 40–50% of total thrust)
- **F<sub>c</sub> (Cutting Force):** Acts **tangentially** at each cutting lip; its moment about the drill axis generates the torque.
- **F<sub>a</sub> (Radial / Passive Force):** Acts perpendicular to the cutting edge; ideally **self-cancels** in a perfectly symmetric drill but produces vibration in practice.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:**
> In an **ideal symmetric drill**, the **radial forces on the two lips cancel** each other out. This is why KTU numericals assume F<sub>a</sub> = 0 and consider only **F<sub>z</sub>** and **M<sub>z</sub>**.

## 2.2 Theoretical Foundation — Specific Cutting Energy

The Specific Cutting Energy (**k<sub>s</sub>**) is the **work done per unit volume of material removed** and is a fundamental property of the workpiece-tool pair.

For drilling, the **uncut chip cross-sectional area (A)** is:

$$A = \frac{\pi \cdot D^2}{4} \quad \text{[mm}^2\text{]}$$

The **material removal rate (MRR)** is:

$$Q = A \cdot v_f = \frac{\pi \cdot D^2}{4} \cdot f \cdot N \quad \text{[mm}^3\text{/min]}$$

The axial thrust force is theoretically:

$$F_z = k_s \cdot A = k_s \cdot \frac{\pi \cdot D^2}{4}$$

The cutting torque is related to the **tangential force component** integrated over the drill radius:

$$M_z = \int_0^{R} F_c(r) \cdot r \, dr$$

## 2.3 Empirical Force Models (KTU Frequently Tested)

In practice, forces are **not** purely proportional to A because of the **chisel edge**, **point angle effects**, and **strain-rate sensitivity**. KTU expects the following **empirical power-law models**:

$$F_z = C_F \cdot D^{p} \cdot f^{q} \quad \text{[N]}$$

$$M_z = C_M \cdot D^{x} \cdot f^{y} \quad \text{[N·m]}$$

Where:
- **C<sub>F</sub>, C<sub>M</sub>** are **material-tool constants** (determined experimentally).
- **p, q, x, y** are **exponents** (typically p ≈ 1, q ≈ 0.7–0.8 for mild steel).

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Lab Note:** During the KTU lab session, you will turn the **feed handle** to vary **f** at a constant **N**, record **F<sub>z</sub>** from the dynamometer, then take a few more readings at different N values. A **log-log plot** of F<sub>z</sub> vs f gives slope **q** and intercept **log(C<sub>F</sub>)**.

## 2.4 Spindle Power and Energy Equations

The **cutting power (P<sub>c</sub>)** required at the spindle is:

$$P_c = M_z \cdot \omega = M_z \cdot \frac{2 \pi N}{60} \quad \text{[W]}$$

Where the angular velocity is:

$$\omega = \frac{2 \pi N}{60} \quad \text{[rad/s]}$$

The **cutting speed** at the drill periphery is:

$$V_c = \frac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000} \quad \text{[m/min]}$$

The **specific cutting pressure** k<sub>s</sub> is also expressed as:

$$k_s = \frac{F_z}{A} = \frac{4 F_z}{\pi D^2} \quad \text{[N/mm}^2\text{]}$$

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Description |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | Cutting speed | $V_c = \dfrac{\pi D N}{1000}$ | m/min | Surface speed at drill periphery |
| 2 | Spindle speed | $N = \dfrac{1000 \, V_c}{\pi D}$ | rpm | Spindle rotational speed |
| 3 | Feed rate | $v_f = f \cdot N$ | mm/min | Axial feed velocity |
| 4 | MRR | $Q = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot f \cdot N$ | mm³/min | Volume of material removed per minute |
| 5 | Thrust Force (empirical) | $F_z = C_F \cdot D^{p} \cdot f^{q}$ | N | Empirical axial force |
| 6 | Thrust Force (theoretical) | $F_z = k_s \cdot \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$ | N | Energy-based axial force |
| 7 | Cutting Torque (empirical) | $M_z = C_M \cdot D^{x} \cdot f^{y}$ | N·m | Empirical torque |
| 8 | Spindle Power | $P_c = M_z \cdot \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60}$ | W | Mechanical power consumed |
| 9 | Specific cutting energy | $k_s = \dfrac{4 F_z}{\pi D^2}$ | N/mm² | Material machinability index |
| 10 | Torque–Thrust relation | $M_z = \mu \cdot F_z \cdot \dfrac{D}{2}$ | N·m | Where $\mu$ is force ratio (≈ 0.5–0.7) |
| 11 | Angular velocity | $\omega = \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60}$ | rad/s | Spindle angular velocity |
| 12 | Feed force ratio | $\mu = \dfrac{2 M_z}{F_z \cdot D}$ | – | Dimensionless force ratio |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Numerical Tip:** Whenever the problem gives **F<sub>z</sub>** and asks for **M<sub>z</sub>** (or vice versa) without empirical constants, use the **force ratio model**: $M_z = \mu \cdot F_z \cdot \dfrac{D}{2}$, assuming $\mu = 0.5$ for mild steel unless stated.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> These equations are not just textbook artefacts:
> - **CNC drilling path planners** use k<sub>s</sub> values from standard handbooks (e.g., **MACHINING DATA HANDBOOK**) to predict thrust force and avoid exceeding the machine's **maximum allowable spindle load**.
> - **Aerospace drilling of CFRP/Titanium stacks** uses F<sub>z</sub> signals for **real-time feed override** to prevent delamination.
> - **Industry 4.0 smart drills** monitor M<sub>z</sub> via spindle current to detect **tool wear** and trigger **automatic tool changes (ATC)**.
> - In KTU's **Design Project (B.Tech main project)**, drilling force data validates the design of **workholding fixtures** to ensure clamping force > thrust force.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Thrust Force from Specific Cutting Energy

**Statement:** Show that $F_z = k_s \cdot A$ where $A = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$.

### Step 1 — Define Specific Cutting Energy

By definition, the specific cutting energy is the **force per unit uncut chip cross-sectional area**:

$$k_s = \frac{F_z}{A}$$

### Step 2 — Express the Uncut Chip Area

The drill removes a cylindrical plug of diameter **D**, so the cross-sectional area is:

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2$$

### Step 3 — Substitute into the Definition

$$F_z = k_s \cdot A = k_s \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

### Step 4 — Real-World Interpretation

> A drill of **D = 10 mm** in **mild steel** with **k<sub>s</sub> = 2000 N/mm²** produces:
> $$F_z = 2000 \cdot \frac{\pi \cdot 10^2}{4} = 2000 \cdot 78.54 = 157{,}080 \text{ N}$$
> This is enormous; in practice k<sub>s</sub> for drilling is much lower (≈ 1500–2500 N/mm² in soft steels) because only a fraction of the area is cut by the lips. The **empirical model** in Section 2.3 corrects this.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Torque from Distributed Tangential Cutting Forces

**Statement:** Derive $M_z = \dfrac{F_c \cdot D}{2}$ for a single cutting lip.

### Step 1 — Geometry of the Cutting Lip

The **main cutting lip** engages the workpiece along a line at radius **r** from the drill axis. The **tangential cutting force F<sub>c</sub>(r)** acts in the direction of rotation at every point on the lip.

### Step 2 — Elemental Torque Contribution

For a small radial element at radius **r** with width **dr**, the elemental force is:

$$dF_c = \tau \cdot dr$$

where $\tau$ is the tangential force per unit radial length. The elemental torque is:

$$dM_z = r \cdot dF_c = \tau \cdot r \, dr$$

### Step 3 — Integrate Across the Cutting Lip

Integrating from **0** to **R** (where $R = D/2$ is the drill radius):

$$M_z = \int_0^{R} \tau \cdot r \, dr = \tau \cdot \frac{R^2}{2}$$

### Step 4 — Express in Terms of Total Tangential Force

The total tangential force is:

$$F_c = \int_0^{R} \tau \, dr = \tau \cdot R$$

Therefore, solving for $\tau$:

$$\tau = \frac{F_c}{R}$$

### Step 5 — Substitute Back

$$M_z = \frac{F_c}{R} \cdot \frac{R^2}{2} = \frac{F_c \cdot R}{2} = \frac{F_c \cdot D}{4}$$

> **Correction with Two Lips:** Since a standard twist drill has **two symmetric cutting lips**, the total tangential force doubles, giving:
> $$M_z = \frac{F_c \cdot D}{2}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Relation Between Thrust Force, Torque, and Power

**Statement:** Derive the energy-conservation relation $P_c = F_z \cdot v_f$ and reconcile it with $P_c = M_z \cdot \omega$.

### Step 1 — Mechanical Power from the Axial Feed

Power equals force times velocity along the direction of force. The thrust force **F<sub>z</sub>** moves axially at **v<sub>f</sub>** (mm/min):

$$P_z = F_z \cdot v_f = F_z \cdot f \cdot N \quad \text{[N·mm/min]}$$

Convert to SI units (W = N·m/s):

$$P_z = \frac{F_z \cdot f \cdot N}{60{,}000} \quad \text{[W]}$$

### Step 2 — Mechanical Power from Spindle Torque

The torque **M<sub>z</sub>** rotates at angular velocity $\omega = \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60}$ rad/s:

$$P_c = M_z \cdot \omega = M_z \cdot \frac{2 \pi N}{60} \quad \text{[W]}$$

### Step 3 — Equate the Two Expressions

For a given instant in the steady-state drilling process, both must equal the same cutting power:

$$\frac{F_z \cdot f \cdot N}{60{,}000} = M_z \cdot \frac{2 \pi N}{60}$$

### Step 4 — Cancel N and Solve for the Force Ratio

$$F_z \cdot \frac{f}{60{,}000} = M_z \cdot \frac{2 \pi}{60}$$

$$M_z = \frac{F_z \cdot f}{2000 \pi}$$

This gives a feed-based relation. In practice, a **dimensionless force ratio** $\mu$ is preferred:

$$M_z = \mu \cdot F_z \cdot \frac{D}{2}$$

with $\mu \approx 0.5$ for mild steel and $\mu \approx 0.3$ for cast iron.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A **10 mm diameter** HSS drill cuts **mild steel** at a **spindle speed N = 800 rpm** and **feed f = 0.2 mm/rev**. The measured **thrust force F<sub>z</sub> = 1800 N**. The force ratio is **$\mu = 0.5$**. Calculate:
(a) The cutting speed.
(b) The cutting torque.
(c) The spindle power.

### Solution

**(a) Cutting Speed:**

$$V_c = \frac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000} = \frac{\pi \cdot 10 \cdot 800}{1000} = 25.13 \text{ m/min}$$

**(b) Cutting Torque:**

$$M_z = \mu \cdot F_z \cdot \frac{D}{2} = 0.5 \cdot 1800 \cdot \frac{10}{2} = 0.5 \cdot 1800 \cdot 5 = 4500 \text{ N·mm} = 4.5 \text{ N·m}$$

**(c) Spindle Power:**

$$\omega = \frac{2 \pi N}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \cdot 800}{60} = 83.78 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$P_c = M_z \cdot \omega = 4.5 \cdot 83.78 = 377.0 \text{ W} \approx 0.377 \text{ kW}$$

### Verification via Feed Power

$$P_z = \frac{F_z \cdot f \cdot N}{60{,}000} = \frac{1800 \cdot 0.2 \cdot 800}{60{,}000} = \frac{288{,}000}{60{,}000} = 4.8 \text{ W}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the mismatch?** $P_z$ represents **only the thrust work** (work against axial resistance), while $P_c$ represents the **total cutting work** (including the torsional shear work at the cutting lips). The difference — $377 - 4.8 \approx 372$ W — is dissipated as **chip-formation shear energy**. This is the dominant energy term in drilling.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Process Parameters on Forces

| Parameter | Increase Action | Effect on F<sub>z</sub> | Effect on M<sub>z</sub> | Effect on P<sub>c</sub> | Engineering Consequence |
|-----------|-----------------|------------------|------------------|------------------|--------------------------|
| **D (diameter)** ↑ | Larger hole | **Strong ↑** (∝ D^2) | **Strong ↑** (∝ D²·5) | **Strong ↑** | Smaller D preferred for thin sheets |
| **f (feed/rev)** ↑ | Faster drilling | **Moderate ↑** (∝ f^0.7) | **Moderate ↑** (∝ f^0.8) | **↑** | Reduces cycle time, raises tool wear |
| **V<sub>c</sub> (cutting speed)** ↑ | Higher N | **Slight ↓** (thermal softening) | **Slight ↓** | **↑** (∝ Vc) | Limits chip evacuation |
| **Workpiece hardness ↑** | Harder material | **Strong ↑** | **Strong ↑** | **Strong ↑** | May require coolant + coated HSS |
| **Point angle ↑** | Flatter drill | **F<sub>z</sub> ↑**, M<sub>z</sub> ↓ | **F<sub>z</sub> ↑**, M<sub>z</sub> ↓ | ≈ constant | Standard 118° for general steel |
| **Coolant applied** | Flood vs dry | **↓ 15–30%** | **↓ 10–25%** | **↓** | Essential for deep holes |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define thrust force and cutting torque in drilling. Why is radial force neglected in standard analysis?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Thrust force (F<sub>z</sub>)** is the **axial force** acting along the drill axis, resisting the penetration of the drill into the workpiece. It arises mainly from the **chisel edge pushing material aside** and the **axial component of lip cutting force**.
>
> **Cutting torque (M<sub>z</sub>)** is the **rotational moment** about the drill axis required to shear the material at the cutting lips. It is generated by the **tangential force component F<sub>c</sub>** acting at the drill periphery.
>
> The **radial force (F<sub>a</sub>)** is neglected because in a **geometrically symmetric twist drill**, the radial components on the **two opposing cutting lips are equal and opposite**, cancelling each other out. Therefore, only F<sub>z</sub> and M<sub>z</sub> need to be measured for force analysis.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is specific cutting energy in drilling? How is it determined experimentally?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Specific cutting energy (k<sub>s</sub>)** is defined as the **cutting force per unit uncut chip cross-sectional area**, expressed in **N/mm²**. It represents the **energy required to remove a unit volume of material** and is an indicator of **machinability**.
>
> **Experimental determination:**
