> 1. Measure **F<sub>z</sub>** using a dynamometer for a known drill diameter **D** and feed **f**.
> 2. Calculate uncut chip area $A = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$.
> 3. Compute $k_s = \dfrac{4 F_z}{\pi D^2}$.
>
> For mild steel, k<sub>s</sub> is typically in the range **1500–2500 N/mm²**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part-B question has an **internal choice** between two alternative sub-questions. You must answer **either** Choice A **or** Choice B in full. Each choice carries **two sub-parts of 7 marks each**.

---

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — **CHOICE A**

**A (a) [7 marks]** — With a neat block diagram, explain the **functional architecture of a drilling dynamometer** used to measure cutting forces. List the components and describe the **signal conditioning chain**.

**Model Solution:**

A **drill press dynamometer** is a force-measuring transducer mounted between the workpiece and the machine table. It is designed to capture the **three orthogonal force components** during drilling.

**1. Mechanical Mounting (1 mark)**
The dynamometer is bolted to the drill press table. The **workpiece is clamped on top** of the dynamometer so that **all reaction forces pass through the sensing element**.

**2. Sensing Element (2 marks)**
- Four **strain gauges** are bonded to a **cylindrical or octagonal ring** in a **Wheatstone bridge** configuration.
- Two gauges measure **tangential strain** (torque), two measure **axial strain** (thrust).
- When force is applied, the ring deforms elastically, gauges change resistance, and the bridge outputs a **millivolt signal** proportional to the force.

**3. Signal Conditioning Chain (3 marks)**
- **Amplification:** Differential amplifier with gain ≈ 1000 boosts the mV signal to volts.
- **Filtering:** Low-pass filter (cutoff ≈ 100 Hz) removes spindle-noise artefacts.
- **ADC:** 16-bit analog-to-digital converter samples at 1 kHz.
- **Software:** LabVIEW/MATLAB computes **F<sub>z</sub>, M<sub>z</sub>** from the calibration matrix.

**4. Calibration (1 mark)**
- Apply **known dead weights** along the dynamometer axis.
- Record the voltage output and build a **calibration curve** (V vs N).
- Verify with a **known torque arm and weight** for the torque channel.

---

**A (b) [7 marks]** — A **12 mm HSS drill** is used on **mild steel** at **N = 600 rpm** and **f = 0.15 mm/rev**. The measured **thrust force F<sub>z</sub> = 2200 N**. Given the **force ratio μ = 0.5**, calculate: **(i) cutting speed V<sub>c</sub>**, **(ii) cutting torque M<sub>z</sub>**, **(iii) spindle power P<sub>c</sub>**, and **(iv) specific cutting energy k<sub>s</sub>**.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** D = 12 mm, N = 600 rpm, f = 0.15 mm/rev, F<sub>z</sub> = 2200 N, μ = 0.5

**(i) Cutting Speed (1.5 marks)**

$$V_c = \frac{\pi D N}{1000} = \frac{\pi \cdot 12 \cdot 600}{1000} = \frac{22{,}619.5}{1000} = 22.62 \text{ m/min}$$

**(ii) Cutting Torque (2 marks)**

$$M_z = \mu \cdot F_z \cdot \frac{D}{2} = 0.5 \cdot 2200 \cdot \frac{12}{2} = 0.5 \cdot 2200 \cdot 6 = 6600 \text{ N·mm} = 6.6 \text{ N·m}$$

**(iii) Spindle Power (2 marks)**

$$\omega = \frac{2 \pi N}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \cdot 600}{60} = 62.83 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$P_c = M_z \cdot \omega = 6.6 \cdot 62.83 = 414.7 \text{ W} \approx 0.415 \text{ kW}$$

**(iv) Specific Cutting Energy (1.5 marks)**

$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \cdot 12^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \cdot 144}{4} = 113.10 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$k_s = \frac{F_z}{A} = \frac{2200}{113.10} = 19.45 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

> **Note:** This very low k<sub>s</sub> value reflects the fact that the **empirical thrust force** is much smaller than the **theoretical chip-area-based value** because the chisel edge does not cut in shear; it indents and extrudes.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — **CHOICE B**

**B (a) [7 marks]** — Explain the **empirical force model** used in drilling. Discuss the significance of the **exponents p, q** and the **constant C<sub>F</sub>**. How are they determined from a lab experiment?

**Model Solution:**

**1. Empirical Model (2 marks)**
The empirical force model captures the **non-linear dependence** of cutting force on process parameters:

$$F_z = C_F \cdot D^{p} \cdot f^{q}$$

$$M_z = C_M \cdot D^{x} \cdot f^{y}$$

**2. Significance of Terms (3 marks)**
- **C<sub>F</sub> (N/mm<sup>p+q</sup>):** A **material-tool constant** that combines the effects of workpiece strength, tool geometry, and cutting conditions. It is determined by linear regression on log-transformed data.
- **Exponent p (≈ 1):** Indicates that **doubling the diameter doubles the thrust force** linearly. In practice, p ∈ [0.8, 1.2] depending on whether the chisel-edge effect dominates.
- **Exponent q (≈ 0.7–0.8):** Indicates that **thrust force rises sub-linearly with feed**. A small increase in f gives a moderate rise in F<sub>z</sub>, which is why high-feed drilling is efficient for soft materials.

**3. Experimental Determination (2 marks)**
- Conduct a **calibration drilling test series** at **3 different feeds** and **2 different speeds** (n = 6 readings minimum).
- Plot **log F<sub>z</sub>** vs **log f** at constant D.
- The **slope = q**, the **intercept = log(C<sub>F</sub>) + p·log(D)**.
- Repeat for **multiple diameters** to extract **p** and **C<sub>F</sub>** independently.

---

**B (b) [7 marks]** — During a drilling experiment on **EN8 mild steel** with a **10 mm drill** at **N = 1000 rpm** and **f = 0.1 mm/rev**, the cutting torque was found to be **M<sub>z</sub> = 3.5 N·m**. Calculate: **(i) the spindle power**, **(ii) the cutting speed**, **(iii) the feed rate**, and **(iv) the specific cutting pressure** assuming the force ratio μ = 0.5.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** D = 10 mm, N = 1000 rpm, f = 0.1 mm/rev, M<sub>z</sub> = 3.5 N·m, μ = 0.5

**(i) Spindle Power (2 marks)**

$$P_c = M_z \cdot \frac{2 \pi N}{60} = 3.5 \cdot \frac{2 \pi \cdot 1000}{60} = 3.5 \cdot 104.72 = 366.5 \text{ W}$$

**(ii) Cutting Speed (1.5 marks)**

$$V_c = \frac{\pi D N}{1000} = \frac{\pi \cdot 10 \cdot 1000}{1000} = 31.42 \text{ m/min}$$

**(iii) Feed Rate (1.5 marks)**

$$v_f = f \cdot N = 0.1 \cdot 1000 = 100 \text{ mm/min}$$

**(iv) Specific Cutting Pressure (2 marks)**

Using $M_z = \mu \cdot F_z \cdot \dfrac{D}{2}$:

$$F_z = \frac{2 M_z}{\mu \cdot D} = \frac{2 \cdot 3.5 \cdot 1000}{0.5 \cdot 10} = \frac{7000}{5} = 1400 \text{ N}$$

$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \cdot 100}{4} = 78.54 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$k_s = \frac{F_z}{A} = \frac{1400}{78.54} = 17.83 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in this Experiment:**
>
> 1. **Unit Conversion Error:** Failing to convert **N from rpm to rad/s** before computing power. Use $\omega = \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60}$, not $\pi N$.
> 2. **Confusing f (mm/rev) and v<sub>f</sub> (mm/min):** Many students write $v_f = f$ instead of $v_f = f \cdot N$. This causes a 10×–1000× error.
> 3. **Forgetting the 1/1000 factor in V<sub>c</sub>:** $V_c = \pi D N$ gives mm/min, not m/min. Divide by 1000.
> 4. **Neglecting the chisel-edge contribution:** Reporting k<sub>s</sub> in the thousands of N/mm² because they used the full $\pi D^2/4$ area. Always state the assumption clearly.
> 5. **Using the wrong force ratio μ:** μ is **not** the coefficient of friction. For mild steel, μ ≈ 0.5; for cast iron, μ ≈ 0.3. State it explicitly.
> 6. **Lab Record Pitfall:** Forgetting to record the **drill point angle** (118° standard) and the **workpiece material grade** in the observation table. Examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark for incomplete experimental setup documentation.
> 7. **Plotting Errors:** In the log-log graph of F<sub>z</sub> vs f, students often miss marking the **slope value (q)** explicitly on the graph. Always annotate the slope.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**
>
> - **F<sub>z</sub>** = Axial thrust force (along drill axis), in **N**.
> - **M<sub>z</sub>** = Cutting torque (about drill axis), in **N·m**.
> - **F<sub>a</sub>** = Radial/passive force, **ideally zero** in symmetric drills.
> - **Cutting speed:** $V_c = \dfrac{\pi D N}{1000}$ m/min.
> - **Spindle speed:** $N = \dfrac{1000 V_c}{\pi D}$ rpm.
> - **Feed rate:** $v_f = f \cdot N$ mm/min.
> - **MRR:** $Q = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot f \cdot N$ mm³/min.
> - **Thrust force (empirical):** $F_z = C_F \cdot D^{p} \cdot f^{q}$.
> - **Thrust force (theoretical):** $F_z = k_s \cdot \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$.
> - **Spindle power:** $P_c = M_z \cdot \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60}$ W.
> - **Force ratio:** $\mu = \dfrac{2 M_z}{F_z D} \approx 0.5$ for mild steel.
> - **Specific cutting energy:** $k_s = \dfrac{4 F_z}{\pi D^2}$ N/mm².
> - **Dynamometer components:** Strain gauge → Wheatstone bridge → Amplifier → Filter → ADC → PC.
> - **Lab instruments:** Dial indicator, vernier, HSS drills, mild steel workpiece, cutting fluid, drill press with dynamometer.
> - **Standard drill point angle:** **118°** for general-purpose steel; 135° for hardened steels and stainless.
> - **Two cutting lips** → radial forces cancel; one chisel edge → significant axial thrust.
> - **Cutting fluid** reduces F<sub>z</sub> by 15–30% and improves surface finish.
> - **Verification relation:** $P_c$ from torque must equal the sum of feed power and chip-formation power.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Exercises on Cylindrical Grinding Machine: Grinding of a Plain Cylindrical Surface
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMEL408 - MANUFACTURING TECHNOLOGY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Exercises on Cylindrical Grinding Machine: Grinding of a Plain Cylindrical Surface

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

**Cylindrical Grinding** is a precision machining process in which material is removed from the external or internal surface of a cylindrical workpiece using a rotating **multi-point abrasive grinding wheel**, while the workpiece is itself rotated at a controlled, slower speed. The process aims to produce components with very high dimensional accuracy (tolerances of the order of **±0.005 mm** to **±0.01 mm**), excellent surface finish (Ra **0.2 µm to 1.6 µm**), and superior geometric form (roundness, cylindricity).

When the operation is restricted to producing a simple, straight, non-contoured cylindrical (journal) surface — as on a shaft, pin, or spindle — the experiment is specifically termed **"Grinding of a Plain Cylindrical Surface"** under the KTU 2024 Scheme PCMEL408 lab syllabus.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme – PCMEL408, Module 2):**
> Students are required to perform plain cylindrical grinding on a given mild steel workpiece, measure diameter and surface roughness before and after grinding, calculate Material Removal Rate (MRR), Spark-out time, and observe the effect of depth of cut, traverse rate, and work speed on surface finish and dimensional accuracy.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are shaping a wooden log into a perfectly smooth rolling pin. The fastest way is **not** to use a single sharp knife (turning) but to use a spinning sanding drum that is slightly wider than the log. As the drum spins at **thousands of RPM**, thousands of tiny abrasive grains act like microscopic chisels — each removing an infinitesimal chip. The log itself is turned slowly against the drum, and the drum is fed sideways, gradually peeling off the entire surface to a uniform diameter.

Cylindrical grinding is **exactly this**, but with industrial precision:

- The **sandpaper drum** = **Grinding wheel** (Al₂O₃ or SiC abrasive, vitrified bond).
- The **wooden log** = **Workpiece** (held between centers, rotated by a dog and faceplate).
- The **sideways feeding motion** = **Traverse feed**.
- The **inward pressing motion** = **Infeed / depth of cut**.

The genius of the process is **statistical averaging**: although every single grain cuts a slightly different path, the *combined effect* of millions of grains produces a surface that is **rounder, straighter, and smoother** than the original — and often smoother than what turning can achieve.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Cylindrical Grinding?**
> Turning leaves tool marks, requires rigid tools, and cannot easily reach hardness above **HRC 55–60** with conventional HSS. Grinding uses **abrasive grains (hardness ≈ 2000–2500 HV)** held by a bond, so it can machine **hardened steels, carbides, ceramics, and nitride layers** that no other conventional process can finish economically.

## 1.3 Classification of Cylindrical Grinding (Lab-Relevant)

| Type | Description | Typical Use |
|------|-------------|-------------|
| **Plain Cylindrical (External) Grinding** | Workpiece rotates between centers; wheel grinds external journal surface | Shafts, pins, rollers |
| **Internal Cylindrical Grinding** | Small wheel enters bore of workpiece | Bushing, sleeve finishing |
| **Centerless Grinding** | No centers; workpiece supported on blade, rotated by contact with regulating wheel | High-volume production |
| **Plunge Grinding** | Wheel is fed radially (no traverse); for short, rigid surfaces | Shoulders, grooves |
| **Traverse (Through-Feed) Grinding** | Wheel is stationary radially; workpiece traverses past it | Long shafts |

> The **plain cylindrical surface grinding** exercise in PCMEL408 belongs to the **first category**, performed on a **universal cylindrical grinder** using a **between-centers** work-holding setup with **traverse grinding** as the predominant mode.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Cylindrical Grinding Machine (Lab Focus)

A **plain cylindrical grinder** consists of the following major sub-systems, each of which the KTU examiner expects the student to identify and label in the lab record.

### A. Bed / Base
Heavy **cast iron** bed providing vibration damping. Houses the **table slideways** that carry the workhead and tailstock. Rigidity is paramount — any deflection causes **diametral error and chatter marks**.

### B. Wheelhead
Mounts the **grinding wheel spindle**, driven by a separate high-speed motor (typically **1500–2000 RPM motor with 1:3 to 1:5 belt ratio → wheel speed 4500–10000 SFPM**). Houses the **wheel guard** (mandatory safety feature) and **coolant nozzle**.

### C. Workhead
Holds the workpiece via a **chuck** or **faceplate + dog carrier**, and rotates it at **variable speeds (typically 30–500 RPM)** through a geared drive. The spindle axis must be precisely **coaxial** with the tailstock axis (verified by a **test bar** or **dial indicator sweep**).

### D. Tailstock
Provides the **dead center** support at the opposite end of the workpiece. Has a **spindle travel** (≈ 25–50 mm) to accommodate different workpiece lengths and thermal expansion during grinding.

### E. Table (Saddle)
Carries workhead + tailstock. Provides:
- **Longitudinal traverse** (left–right) at controlled feed rates.
- **Cross-slide / Infeed** (in/out toward the wheel) for depth of cut.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Always mention that the **workpiece axis**, **wheel axis**, and **table slideway** must be set **parallel and coplanar** for true cylindrical grinding; otherwise the surface will be **barrel-shaped** or **tapered**.

## 2.2 The Grinding Wheel — Specification Decoding

Every grinding wheel is **stamped with a 5–6 character code** that is universal. KTU expects students to **read and select** the correct wheel for a given job.

| Symbol | Meaning | Example (for HRC 50 mild steel shaft) |
|--------|---------|----------------------------------------|
| **Abrasive Type** | A = Aluminium Oxide, C = Silicon Carbide, S = Sol Gel, D = Diamond, B = CBN | **A** (Al₂O₃) |
| **Grain Size (Mesh)** | Coarse (10–24), Medium (30–60), Fine (80–220) | **46** (Medium) |
| **Grade (Hardness)** | Soft (A–H) → Hard (I–P) | **K** (Medium-soft) |
| **Structure** | 0 (dense) → 12 (open) | **5** (medium open) |
| **Bond Type** | V = Vitrified, R = Rubber, B = Resin, M = Metal | **V** (Vitrified) |
| **Manufacturer's Mark** | Free text | Norton / CUMI |

**Example code:** `A 46 K 5 V 23` → Aluminium oxide, 46-grit, K-grade, structure 5, vitrified bond, 23-inch OD.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Selection Logic for Plain Cylindrical Grinding of Mild Steel:**
> - **Abrasive:** Aluminium Oxide (A) — tougher, suited for low-to-medium hardness ferrous metals.
> - **Grain Size:** **46–60** for roughing, **80–100** for finishing.
> - **Grade:** **J–L** (medium); harder grade for finishing to retain form.
> - **Bond:** Vitrified (V) — rigid, heat-resistant, easily dressed.

## 2.3 Grinding Parameters — The Three Principal Kinematics

Cylindrical grinding has three independent motions whose proper selection distinguishes a finished journal from a scrapped one.

### 1. Primary Motion (Wheel Speed, V)
The **peripheral surface speed** of the grinding wheel.

$$V_w = \frac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{60 \times 1000} \quad \text{(m/s)}$$

Where $D$ = wheel outer diameter (mm), $N$ = wheel RPM.

**Typical value for external cylindrical grinding:** **V = 25 to 35 m/s (5000–7000 SFPM)**.

### 2. Secondary Motion (Work Speed, v)
The **peripheral surface speed** of the workpiece.

$$v = \frac{\pi \cdot d \cdot n}{60 \times 1000} \quad \text{(m/min)}$$

Where $d$ = workpiece diameter (mm), $n$ = workpiece RPM.

**Typical value:** **v = 15 to 30 m/min** for external grinding of steel.

### 3. Tertiary Motion (Infeed & Traverse)
- **Infeed (depth of cut, $a_p$):** Radial advance per pass. For finishing, $a_p$ = **0.005 to 0.02 mm/pass**.
- **Traverse feed ($f_t$):** Longitudinal table feed per workpiece revolution, expressed as a **fraction of wheel width (B)**, typically **0.2 to 0.6 of B**.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Exam-Ready)

> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All `|x|` style absolute-value or absolute-diameter notations below are rendered as `\vert x \vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Typical Range (Plain Cyl. Grinding, Steel) |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|--------------------------------------------|
| 1 | Wheel surface speed | $V = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60000}$ | m/s | 25 – 35 |
| 2 | Workpiece surface speed | $v = \dfrac{\pi d n}{1000}$ | m/min | 15 – 30 |
| 3 | Spindle power available check | $P = \dfrac{F_c \cdot V}{1000}$ | kW | 2 – 10 kW |
| 4 | MRR – Traverse grinding | $Z_w = \pi \cdot d \cdot l \cdot f_t \cdot n$ | mm³/min | 1000 – 8000 |
| 5 | MRR – Plunge grinding | $Z_w = \pi \cdot d \cdot a_p \cdot v$ | mm³/min | 500 – 4000 |
| 6 | Material removed per pass (radial) | $\Delta d = 2 \cdot a_p$ | mm | 0.01 – 0.04 |
| 7 | Number of roughing passes | $n_r = \dfrac{d_{initial} - d_{final}}{2 \, a_{p,rough}}$ | — | 3 – 10 |
| 8 | Traverse feed per rev | $f_t = (0.2 \text{ to } 0.6) \cdot B$ | mm/rev | 5 – 25 |
| 9 | Spark-out passes | Empirical, until no sparks visible | passes | 2 – 6 |
| 10 | Specific energy (steel) | $U = \dfrac{F_c \cdot V}{Z_w}$ | J/mm³ | 20 – 60 |
| 11 | G-ratio (wheel performance) | $G = \dfrac{V_{work}}{V_{wheel}}$ | — | 10 – 100 |
| 12 | Specific volumetric feed rate | $Q' = \dfrac{Z_w}{V \cdot b}$ | mm²/s | — |
| 13 | Average chip thickness | $h_{cu} \approx \sqrt{\dfrac{v \cdot a_p}{V \cdot C \cdot r}}$ | µm | 0.1 – 2 |
| 14 | Wheel grit count on contact arc | $C$ (active cutting points per mm²) | — | 0.5 – 4 |
| 15 | Theoretical Ra (Snoeys model) | $R_a \approx \dfrac{h_{cu}}{4}$ | µm | 0.1 – 1.6 |

Where:
- $D$ = wheel diameter (mm), $N$ = wheel RPM
- $d$ = workpiece diameter (mm), $n$ = work RPM
- $B$ = wheel width (mm), $l$ = workpiece length (mm)
- $f_t$ = traverse feed (mm/rev)
- $a_p$ = radial infeed (mm)
- $F_c$ = tangential cutting force (N)
- $C$ = active grit density, $r$ = average chip ratio, $b$ = contact width

## 2.5 Spark-Out — The Hidden Phase of Precision

Once the micrometer dial reaches the target diameter, the wheel is **not retracted immediately**. The infeed is held constant and the table continues to traverse for **2 to 6 additional revolutions** until sparks cease to appear from the contact zone. This phase, called **Spark-Out**, allows:

- Elastic recovery of the machine–wheel–workpiece system.
- Removal of any residual peaks (improving roundness by **30–50%**).
- Stabilization of temperature (preventing post-grind thermal distortion).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Viva Question (Very Common):** *"Why is spark-out necessary?"*
> **Answer:** To eliminate spring-back error, remove the last few microns left by elastic deflection, and improve roundness. Skipping spark-out typically leaves a **barrel-shaped or lobed** surface even if the average diameter is correct.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive crankshaft journals:** Finished by cylindrical grinding to Ra **0.4 µm**.
- **Hydraulic pump shafts:** Tolerances of **±0.005 mm** are mandatory for seal life.
- **Bearing rings** (SKF, NTN): Ground to **ISO tolerance class 4 or 2**.
- **Aerospace turbine shafts** (Inconel 718): Cylindrical grinding with CBN wheels achieves Ra **0.1 µm**.
- **Defence:** Tank gun barrel bore finishing (a specialized internal cylindrical grinding variant).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Material Removal Rate (MRR) for Traverse Cylindrical Grinding

The MRR in cylindrical grinding is derived from the fundamental volumetric definition: the **volume of material swept by the wheel per unit time**.

### Step 1: Define the geometry of one traverse pass

Consider the wheel of width $B$ traversing past a workpiece of diameter $d$ and length $l$. In **one revolution** of the workpiece, every point on its circumference has been swept by the wheel once. The radial depth removed per revolution is the **infeed $a_p$**, so the **diametral decrement per rev** is $\Delta d = 2 a_p$.

### Step 2: Identify the material swept per revolution

In one revolution, a thin cylindrical shell of thickness $\Delta d$ is removed from the entire circumference:

$$\text{Volume per rev} = \pi \cdot d \cdot l \cdot \frac{\Delta d}{2} = \pi \cdot d \cdot l \cdot a_p$$

This is the **volume removed in one full revolution of the work**.

### Step 3: Convert to per-minute basis

The work rotates at $n$ rev/min, so MRR becomes:

$$Z_w = n \times (\text{Volume per rev}) = \pi \cdot d \cdot l \cdot a_p \cdot n \quad \text{(mm}^3\text{/min)}$$

This is the **plunge-grinding MRR formula**, valid when the wheel is fed radially with no longitudinal motion (i.e., the wheel width $B$ covers the entire grinding length).

### Step 4: Extend to traverse grinding

In traverse grinding, the **infeed per revolution** is not the full $a_p$ but only a fraction, because the wheel's effective feed per rev is governed by the **traverse feed $f_t$** (mm/rev of work) and the wheel width $B$ (mm). The effective infeed per revolution becomes:

$$a_{p,\text{eff}} = a_p \times \frac{f_t}{B}$$

Substituting:

$$\boxed{Z_w = \pi \cdot d \cdot l \cdot n \cdot a_p \cdot \frac{f_t}{B} \quad \text{(mm}^3\text{/min)}}$$

This is the **universal traverse-grinding MRR formula** used in KTU laboratory calculations.

### Step 5: Algebraic check with limiting case

If $f_t = B$ (wheel advances its full width per rev), then $a_{p,\text{eff}} = a_p$, and the formula reduces to the **plunge case** — which is the correct limiting behaviour. ✓

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A mild steel shaft of initial diameter **52.0 mm** and length **150 mm** is to be ground to a final diameter of **50.0 mm** on a plain cylindrical grinder. The wheel diameter is **300 mm**, wheel width **25 mm**, wheel speed **1500 RPM**, work speed **120 RPM**, radial infeed per pass **0.02 mm**, and traverse feed is set at **0.5 × wheel width per work revolution**. Calculate:
> (a) Wheel surface speed, (b) Workpiece surface speed, (c) MRR during steady-state grinding, (d) Total material to be removed, (e) Approximate time for roughing (ignoring spark-out).

### Given Data
- $d_{initial} = 52.0$ mm, $d_{final} = 50.0$ mm, $l = 150$ mm
- $D = 300$ mm, $B = 25$ mm, $N = 1500$ RPM, $n = 120$ RPM
- $a_p = 0.02$ mm, $f_t = 0.5 \times B = 12.5$ mm/rev

### Solution

**(a) Wheel surface speed:**

$$V = \frac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{60000} = \frac{\pi \times 300 \times 1500}{60000}$$

$$V = \frac{1{,}413{,}716.69}{60000} = 23.56 \text{ m/s}$$

**(b) Workpiece surface speed:**

$$v = \frac{\pi \cdot d \cdot n}{1000} = \frac{\pi \times 50 \times 120}{1000} = 18.85 \text{ m/min}$$

**Note:** We use $d = d_{final} = 50$ mm as the reference for an average.

**(c) MRR during steady-state grinding:**

$$Z_w = \pi \cdot d \cdot l \cdot n \cdot a_p \cdot \frac{f_t}{B}$$

$$Z_w = \pi \times 50 \times 150 \times 120 \times 0.02 \times \frac{12.5}{25}$$

$$Z_w = \pi \times 50 \times 150 \times 120 \times 0.02 \times 0.5$$

$$Z_w = \pi \times 50 \times 150 \times 1.2$$

$$Z_w = \pi \times 9000 = 28{,}274.33 \text{ mm}^3\text{/min}$$

$$Z_w = 28{,}274.33 \text{ mm}^3\text{/min} = 28.27 \text{ cm}^3\text{/min}$$

**(d) Total material to be removed:**

$$\Delta r = \frac{d_{initial} - d_{final}}{2} = \frac{52.0 - 50.0}{2} = 1.0 \text{ mm (radial)}$$

$$V_{removed} = \pi \cdot d_{avg} \cdot l \cdot \Delta r = \pi \times 51 \times 150 \times 1.0 = 24{,}031.4 \text{ mm}^3$$

**(e) Approximate time for roughing (ignoring spark-out):**

$$t_{rough} = \frac{V_{removed}}{Z_w} = \frac{24{,}031.4}{28{,}274.33} = 0.85 \text{ min} \approx 51 \text{ seconds}$$

> **Interpretation:** The shaft is roughed in under a minute. In practice, an additional **2–3 spark-out passes** (≈ 4–6 seconds each) plus **dressing** the wheel between roughing and finishing will push the total grinding cycle to **~2–3 minutes** including all setup.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing Grinding Parameters

| Goal | Work Speed (v) | Infeed ($a_p$) | Traverse ($f_t$) | Wheel Grit | Coolant Flow |
|------|---------------|----------------|------------------|------------|--------------|
| **Maximum MRR (roughing)** | High (25–30 m/min) | High (0.03–0.05 mm) | 0.5–0.6 B | Coarse (36–46) | High |
| **Best surface finish (finishing)** | Low (10–15 m/min) | Very low (0.005 mm) | 0.2–0.3 B | Fine (80–100) | Very high |
| **Dimensional accuracy** | Moderate | Spark-out mandatory | ≤ 0.3 B | Fine | Steady, filtered |
| **Heat-sensitive alloy (e.g., Inconel)** | Low | Low | 0.2 B | CBN, fine | Flood, chilled |
| **Hardened tool steel (HRC 60)** | Moderate | Low | 0.3 B | Al₂O₃ / CBN | Abundant |

## 3.4 Derived Relationship — Average Chip Thickness (Snoeys–Malkin Model)

A useful formula for predicting **surface roughness** $R_a$ from grinding parameters:

$$h_{cu} = \left[ \frac{4 \cdot v \cdot a_p}{V \cdot C \cdot r} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + \frac{2 \cdot v \cdot a_p}{V \cdot C \cdot r}} \right]^{1/2}$$

A simplified engineering approximation widely used in KTU labs:

$$\boxed{h_{cu} \approx \sqrt{\frac{v \cdot a_p}{V \cdot C \cdot r}}}$$

And the resulting theoretical surface roughness:

$$\boxed{R_a \approx \frac{h_{cu}}{4}}$$

> **Take-away:** To reduce $R_a$ (improve finish), **decrease $v$ and $a_p$**, or **increase $V$** (faster wheel). This is why finishing is done at very low infeed with a freshly dressed fine-grit wheel.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Plain Cylindrical Grinder

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph POWER["POWER AND DRIVE SUBSYSTEM"]
        M1[MOTOR WHEEL 1500 RPM]
        M2[MOTOR WORKHEAD 30 TO 500 RPM]
        M3[MOTOR COOLANT PUMP]
    end

    subgraph WHEELH["WHEELHEAD ASSEMBLY"]
        WS[GRINDING WHEEL SPINDLE]
        WG[GRINDING WHEEL A46KV]
        GD[WHEEL GUARD AND DRESSER]
        CN[COOLANT NOZZLE]
    end

    subgraph TABLE["TABLE AND SLIDE SYSTEM"]
        TS[TABLE SLIDEWAYS]
        IF[CROSS SLIDE INFEED HANDWHEEL]
        TF[LONGITUDINAL TRAVERSE HANDWHEEL]
    end

    subgraph WORKH["WORKHOLDING ASSEMBLY"]
        WH[WORKHEAD SPINDLE]
        CH[CHUCK OR FACEPLATE]
        DG[DOG CARRIER]
        TS2[TAILSTOCK]
        DC[DEAD CENTER]
    end

    subgraph CONTROL["CONTROL AND METROLOGY"]
        DC2[DIAL INDICATOR ON CROSS SLIDE]
        TC[TEST BAR FOR ALIGNMENT]
        MM[OUTSIDE MICROMETER]
        RA[SURFACE ROUGHNESS TESTER]
    end

    M1 --> WS
    WS --> WG
    M2 --> WH
    WH --> CH
    CH --> DG
    WG ---|grinds| DG
    TS2 --> DC
    DC ---|supports| DG
    M3 --> CN
    CN ---|floods| WG
    TS --> IF
    TS --> TF
    IF -->|radial feed| WG
    TF -->|longitudinal feed| CH
    DC2 -.measures.-> WH
    TC -.verifies.-> CH
    MM -.final check.-> DG
    RA -.final check.-> DG
```

> **How to read this diagram:** The **power subsystem** (top) drives the **wheelhead** and **workhead** (middle). The workpiece is held between the **chuck and dead center**. Coolant floods the contact zone. The **table system** provides two orthogonal feeds (infeed & traverse). The **control block** lists the metrology instruments a student must use during the experiment.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Workflow of the Lab Exercise

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[STEP 1 READ WORK ORDER AND INSPECT JOB] --> B[STEP 2 MEASURE INITIAL DIAMETER AND LENGTH]
    B --> C[STEP 3 SELECT WHEEL A46KV AND MOUNT IT]
    C --> D[STEP 4 DRESS THE WHEEL WITH DIAMOND DRESSER]
    D --> E[STEP 5 MOUNT WORKPIECE BETWEEN CENTERS]
    E --> F[STEP 6 SET TABLE TRAVERSE AND WORK SPEED]
    F --> G[STEP 7 ENGAGE WHEEL WITH LIGHT INFEED FOR TEST CUT]
    G --> H{STEP 8 DIAMETER REACHED TARGET}
    H -->|NO| I[STEP 9 APPLY RADIAL INFEED 0.02 MM PER PASS]
    I --> J[STEP 10 PERFORM SPARK OUT 2 TO 6 REVS]
    J --> H
    H -->|YES| K[STEP 11 RETRACT WHEEL AND STOP TABLE]
    K --> L[STEP 12 MEASURE FINAL DIAMETER AT 3 SECTIONS]
    L --> M[STEP 13 MEASURE SURFACE ROUGHNESS RA]
    M --> N[STEP 14 CALCULATE MRR GRINDING TIME AND G RATIO]
    N --> O[STEP 15 RECORD RESULTS IN LAB MANUAL]
```

## 4.3 Schematic of Wheel–Work Contact Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph WHEEL_CIRC["GRINDING WHEEL"]
        A[WHEEL CENTER]
        A -->|RADIUS R| B[CONTACT TOP]
        A -->|RADIUS R| C[CONTACT BOTTOM]
    end
    subgraph WORK_CIRC["WORKPIECE"]
        D[WORKPIECE CENTER]
        D -->|RADIUS r| E[CONTACT BOTTOM]
        D -->|RADIUS r| F[CONTACT TOP]
    end
    B ---|TANGENCY| F
    C ---|TANGENCY| E
    G[DEPTH OF CUT ap] ---|RADIAL DIRECTION| A
    G ---|RADIAL DIRECTION| D
    H[CONTACT ARC LENGTH la] --- B
    H --- C
```

> **Explanation:** The two circles (wheel of radius $R$ and workpiece of radius $r$) are externally tangent. The contact arc length $l_a$ is small but contains **hundreds of active grits** cutting simultaneously. The depth of cut $a_p$ is the radial penetration of the wheel below the original workpiece center.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define cylindrical grinding. Mention any two advantages of the process. [3 Marks] [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Model Answer:**
Cylindrical grinding is a precision abrasive machining process in which material is removed from the external cylindrical surface of a workpiece using a rotating multi-point grinding wheel, while the workpiece rotates between centers at a controlled speed.

**Two advantages:**
1. It can machine **hardened steels (HRC 60 and above)** that cannot be finished by turning.
2. It produces **very high dimensional accuracy (±0.005 mm)** and excellent surface finish (Ra **0.2–1.6 µm**), which turning cannot achieve economically.

### Q2. What is meant by "spark-out" in cylindrical grinding? Why is it performed? [3 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Model Answer:**
**Spark-out** is the phase of cylindrical grinding in which, after the micrometer dial indicates the target diameter, the radial infeed is held constant and the table continues to traverse for **2 to 6 additional revolutions** without further infeed, until sparks cease to emerge from the contact zone.

**Purpose:**
1. To eliminate the effect of **elastic deflection** (spring-back) of the machine, wheel, and workpiece — the true diameter is reached only after spring-back is overcome.
2. To improve **roundness and surface finish** by removing the last few microns of peak material.
3. To allow **thermal stabilization** before measurement, avoiding post-grind distortion.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Choice-Based Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]

**Q.A (a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the major parts of a plain cylindrical grinding machine. State the function of each part. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

| Part | Function |
|------|----------|
| **Bed** | Heavy cast-iron base; provides rigidity and houses slideways; absorbs vibrations. |
| **Wheelhead** | Mounts the grinding wheel spindle, driven at high speed; carries the wheel guard and dresser. |
| **Workhead** | Holds the workpiece (chuck / faceplate + dog); rotates the work at variable speed. |
| **Tailstock** | Provides dead-center support at the opposite end of the workpiece. |
| **Table (Saddle)** | Carries workhead and tailstock; provides longitudinal traverse and cross-slide infeed. |
| **Coolant System** | Floods the grinding zone to reduce heat, wash away chips, and prevent wheel loading. |
| **Wheel Dresser** | Diamond tool used to true and dress the wheel — restores sharpness and form. |
| **Control Panel** | Houses the start/stop, speed selectors, and emergency stop. |

**Geometrical conditions for plain cylindrical grinding:**
- Wheel axis and work axis must be set **parallel** (otherwise taper).
- Both axes must lie in the **same horizontal plane** (otherwise barrel or hourglass form).

**Q.A (b)** A cylindrical grinder is used to finish a shaft from an initial diameter of **48.0 mm to a final diameter of 45.0 mm**. The workpiece length is **100 mm**, the wheel diameter is **250 mm**, wheel speed is **1800 RPM**, work speed is **150 RPM**, radial infeed per pass is **0.015 mm**, and traverse feed is **0.4 times the wheel width (B = 20 mm)**. Calculate:
- (i) Wheel surface speed in m/s.
- (ii) Workpiece surface speed in m/min.
- (iii) Material removal rate (MRR).
- (iv) Total material removed in mm³.
- (v) Approximate roughing time in minutes. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:** $D = 250$ mm, $B = 20$ mm, $N = 1800$ RPM, $n = 150$ RPM, $l = 100$ mm, $a_p = 0.015$ mm, $f_t = 0.4 \times 20 = 8$ mm/rev, $d_{initial} = 48.0$ mm, $d_{final} = 45.0$ mm.

**(i) Wheel surface speed:**

$$V = \frac{\pi D N}{60000} = \frac{\pi \times 250 \times 1800}{60000} = \frac{1{,}413{,}716.7}{60000} = 23.56 \text{ m/s}$$

**(ii) Workpiece surface speed:**

$$v = \frac{\pi d_{avg} n}{1000} = \frac{\pi \times 46.5 \times 150}{1000} = 21.91 \text{ m/min}$$

**(iii) MRR:**

$$Z_w = \pi \cdot d \cdot l \cdot n \cdot a_p \cdot \frac{f_t}{B}$$

$$Z_w = \pi \times 46.5 \times 100 \times 150 \times 0.015 \times \frac{8}{20}$$

$$Z_w = \pi \times 46.5 \times 100 \times 150 \times 0.015 \times 0.4$$

$$Z_w = \pi \times 46.5 \times 100 \times 0.9 = \pi \times 4185 = 13{,}147.6 \text{ mm}^3\text{/min}$$

**(iv) Total material removed (radial decrement = 1.5 mm):**

$$V_{removed} = \pi \cdot d_{avg} \cdot l \cdot \Delta r = \pi \times 46.5 \times 100 \times 1.5 = 21{,}912.4 \text{ mm}^3$$

**(v) Roughing time:**

$$t_{rough} = \frac{V_{removed}}{Z_w} = \frac{21{,}912.4}{13{,}147.6} = 1.67 \text{ min} \approx 100 \text{ seconds}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]

**Q.B (a)** Explain the standard marking system of a grinding wheel. State the recommended wheel specification for plain cylindrical grinding of a hardened (HRC 55) EN24 steel shaft. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Standard 5/6-symbol marking system (ISO 525):**

1. **Abrasive type:** A (Aluminium Oxide), C (Silicon Carbide), S (Sol Gel Alumina), B (Cubic Boron Nitride), D (Diamond).
2. **Grain size:** Numeric, e.g., 46, 60, 80 (mesh per inch). Coarse → Roughing, Fine → Finishing.
3. **Grade (wheel hardness):** A (softest) → Z (hardest). Soft grade for hard work material, hard grade for soft material.
4. **Structure:** 0 (most dense) → 12 (most open). Open structure for soft/tough materials (better chip clearance).
5. **Bond type:** V (vitrified), B (resin), R (rubber), M (metal), E = shellac.
6. **Manufacturer's symbol** (optional).

**Recommended wheel for EN24 (HRC 55) plain cylindrical grinding:**

| Symbol | Choice | Reason |
|--------|--------|--------|
| Abrasive | **A** (Al₂O₃, regular) or **SG** (Sol-Gel) for HRC 55 | Toughness adequate, cost-effective |
| Grain | **60** (medium) for roughing, **80** (fine) for finishing | Balanced MRR and finish |
| Grade | **J or K** (medium) | Self-sharpening action on hardened steel |
| Structure | **5 or 6** (medium open) | Adequate chip clearance for long chips of steel |
| Bond | **V** (Vitrified) | Rigid, heat-resistant, easily dressed |

**Example code:** `A 60 K 5 V` for roughing; `A 80 J 6 V` for finishing.

**Q.B (b)** A mild steel workpiece of **50 mm diameter** is ground on a cylindrical grinder. The wheel diameter is **350 mm** running at **1500 RPM**, and the work rotates at **100 RPM**. A traverse feed of **0.5 × wheel width (B = 30 mm)** is used with a radial infeed of **0.02 mm/pass**. Calculate:
- (i) Wheel surface speed in m/s.
- (ii) Workpiece surface speed in m/min.
- (iii) MRR in mm³/min.
- (iv) If the average chip thickness is 0.5 µm and the active grit density is 2.0 grits/mm², estimate the theoretical surface roughness $R_a$. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:** $D = 350$ mm, $B = 30$ mm, $N = 1500$ RPM, $n = 100$ RPM, $d = 50$ mm, $l$ not needed (not given — assume $l = 100$ mm for MRR), $a_p = 0.02$ mm, $f_t = 0.5 \times 30 = 15$ mm/rev, $h_{cu} = 0.5$ µm, $C = 2.0$ grits/mm².

**(i) Wheel surface speed:**

$$V = \frac{\pi D N}{60000} = \frac{\pi \times 350 \times 1500}{60000} = 27.49 \text{ m/s}$$

**(ii) Workpiece surface speed:**

$$v = \frac{\pi d n}{1000} = \frac{\pi \times 50 \times 100}{1000} = 15.71 \text{ m/min}$$

**(iii) MRR (taking $l = 100$ mm):**

$$Z_w = \pi \cdot d \cdot l \cdot n \cdot a_p \cdot \frac{f_t}{B}$$

$$Z_w = \pi \times 50 \times 100 \times 100 \times 0.02 \times 0.5 = \pi \times 5000 = 15{,}708 \text{ mm}^3\text{/min}$$

**(iv) Theoretical $R_a$:**

$$R_a \approx \frac{h_{cu}}{4} = \frac{0.5}{4} = 0.125 \text{ µm}$$

> **Engineering interpretation:** This $R_a$ value is in the **mirror-finish range** — typical of precision bearing journals. To achieve it in practice, the wheel must be **freshly dressed, balanced, and the machine must be thermally stable**; theoretical $R_a$ is the *lower bound* — actual measured $R_a$ is typically 2–3 times higher due to grit irregularities, vibration, and grit pull-out.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2:** Many students compute material removed as $\Delta d$ (diametral) instead of $\Delta r = \Delta d / 2$ (radial) for volume calculations. KTU examiners **deduct 1 mark** for this.
> 2. **Mixing up units:** Wheel speed in m/s vs m/min. The wheel surface speed formula has different constants ($60000$ for m/s, $6000$ for m/min). Mixing them up is a **1–2 mark penalty**.
> 3. **Ignoring spark-out:** A common viva trap — "Why is the actual grinding time longer than calculated?" — answer must mention **spark-out, wheel dressing between roughing and finishing, and measurement time**.
> 4. **Reading the wheel code backwards:** A common Part-A error. Always read **Abrasive → Grain → Grade → Structure → Bond** (e.g., A 46 K 5 V). Reversing the order shows a lack of understanding.
> 5. **Writing MRR in cm³ without converting** mm³ to cm³ properly. Standard KTU answer key expects **mm³/min** unless otherwise stated.
> 6. **Confusing plunge and traverse grinding formulas** — the traverse formula has the additional factor $f_t / B$ which students often omit.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

> **Quick-Recall Bullet Box for Last-Minute Revision:**

- **Cylindrical grinding** = abrasive machining between centers, for accurate cylindrical surfaces.
- **Three principal motions:** Wheel rotation (V, m/s), Work rotation (v, m/min), Traverse feed ($f_t$, mm/rev).
- **Wheel surface speed formula:** $V = \pi D N / 60000$ (m/s). Typical value **25–35 m/s**.
- **Work surface speed formula:** $v = \pi d n / 1000$ (m/min). Typical **15–30 m/min**.
- **MRR – Plunge:** $Z_w = \pi \cdot d \cdot l \cdot a_p \cdot n$.
- **MRR – Traverse:** $Z_w = \pi \cdot d \cdot l \cdot a_p \cdot n \cdot (f_t / B)$.
- **Radial decrement = half the diametral decrement.** Always convert carefully.
- **Wheel code (ISO 525):** Abrasive → Grain → Grade → Structure → Bond (e.g., **A 46 K 5 V**).
- **For hardened steel (HRC ≥ 50):** use Al₂O₃ (A), medium-fine grit (46–80), vitrified bond (V).
- **For finishing:** low infeed (0.005 mm), low traverse (0.2 B), fine grit, mandatory spark-out.
- **Spark-out:** 2–6 additional revolutions after reaching target — improves roundness and finish.
- **G-ratio** = Volume of work removed / Volume of wheel worn; higher is better (good wheel economy).
- **Average chip thickness** $h_{cu} \approx \sqrt{(v \cdot a_p)/(V \cdot C \cdot r)}$ — controls $R_a$.
- **Theoretical** $R_a \approx h_{cu} / 4$; **actual** $R_a$ is 2–3× higher.
- **Coolant:** Must flood the contact zone; reduces thermal damage, prevents wheel loading, improves finish.
- **Wheel dressing:** Use diamond dresser between roughing and finishing to restore grit sharpness and form.
- **Safety:** Eye protection, no loose clothing, wheel guard must be in place, work must rotate freely between centers before wheel engagement.
- **Commonly confused pair:** *Plunge vs Traverse* — distinguish by the $(f_t / B)$ factor in MRR.
- **Examiner's favourite viva question:** *"Why spark-out?"* — Always answer with **elastic recovery, roundness, and thermal stabilization**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Exercises on Surface Grinding Machine: Grinding of a Flat Surface
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMEL408 - MANUFACTURING TECHNOLOGY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Exercises on Surface Grinding Machine: Grinding of a Flat Surface

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Surface Grinding** is a precision abrasive machining process in which material is removed from the surface of a workpiece using a **rotating grinding wheel** as the cutting tool. The workpiece is mounted on a reciprocating or rotating table, and the wheel is traversed across the workpiece surface to produce a flat, smooth, and dimensionally accurate surface finish.

The **Surface Grinding Machine** is a specialized machine tool designed exclusively for producing flat surfaces by abrasive action. The grinding wheel acts as a multi-point cutting tool where each abrasive grain functions as an individual micro-cutting edge. The process is governed by the **BUE (Built-Up Edge) free** principle and falls under the broad classification of **Abrasive Machining Processes (AMP)** as defined in the KTU 2024 Scheme.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Surface grinding is classified under **"Finishing Operations"** in manufacturing technology. It is the most common method used to obtain flat surfaces with a surface roughness typically ranging from **0.2 µm to 1.6 µm Ra** and dimensional accuracy of **±0.005 mm to ±0.025 mm**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of surface grinding like using an **electric sander on a wooden plank**, but at a microscopic, industrial level:

- The **wooden plank** = Your metal workpiece (e.g., a mild steel block).
- The **sanding pad** = The grinding wheel (made of abrasive grains bonded together).
- The **back-and-forth motion** of your hand = The **reciprocating table** movement.
- The **dust extraction system** = The **coolant supply system** (which actually cools AND washes away chips).
- The **fine grit sandpaper** = A **finer grinding wheel** (produces smoother surface, just like finer sandpaper).

Unlike a milling cutter where you can *see* the discrete teeth removing material, in grinding, you are relying on **thousands of tiny, randomly-oriented abrasive points** all hitting the surface at the same time. This is why grinding can achieve finishes that are practically **impossible with conventional cutting tools**.

## 1.3 Classification of Surface Grinding Machines

| Type | Description | Application |
|------|-------------|-------------|
| **Horizontal Spindle Reciprocating Table** | Wheel axis horizontal; table moves back and forth | Most common; general flat grinding |
| **Horizontal Spindle Rotary Table** | Wheel axis horizontal; table rotates | High-volume production of small parts |
| **Vertical Spindle Reciprocating Table** | Wheel axis vertical (cup wheel) | Heavy stock removal, large flat surfaces |
| **Vertical Spindle Rotary Table** | Wheel axis vertical; rotary table | Bearing races, piston rings |

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Peripheral wheel speed (Vs)** for grinding: typically **20–35 m/s** for conventional surface grinding.
- **Depth of cut (d)** per pass: **0.005 mm to 0.05 mm**.
- **Table traverse speed (Vw)**: **6 m/min to 30 m/min**.
- **Spark-out passes**: 2–5 passes with no in-feed (for dimensional stability).
- **Grinding ratio (G)**: typically **10 to 50** for soft workpieces, can exceed **100** for hard materials.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Principal Parts of a Horizontal Spindle Reciprocating Table Surface Grinder

> [!NOTE]
> The following 9 components form the **core anatomy** tested in KTU viva voce. Memorize both their function and location.

### 2.1.1 Base (Bed)
The base is the **heavy cast iron foundation** that supports the entire machine. It absorbs vibrations and provides rigidity. It houses the **coolant reservoir** and pump at its base.

### 2.1.2 Column
The vertical column supports the **wheel head assembly** and provides the vertical guideways for the wheel head's up-and-down movement. It is made of heavily ribbed cast iron to dampen vibrations.

### 2.1.3 Table
The **worktable** is mounted on the bed. It holds the workpiece via a magnetic chuck or vise. It performs the **reciprocating motion** (back-and-forth) under the grinding wheel. The table can be hand-driven or power-driven via hydraulic mechanism.

### 2.1.4 Saddle
The saddle sits on the bed and carries the table. It enables **cross-feed movement** (perpendicular to the table travel) so the wheel can traverse across the entire width of the workpiece.

### 2.1.5 Wheel Head
The **wheel head** houses the electric spindle motor and the grinding wheel spindle. It moves **vertically** on the column guideways and provides the **in-feed** (downward feed for depth of cut).

### 2.1.6 Grinding Wheel
The **cutting tool** of the machine. Standard designations follow the **FEPA (Federation of European Producers of Abrasives)** or **ISO 525** standard.

### 2.1.7 Wheel Guard
A **safety enclosure** around the grinding wheel to protect the operator from sparks, coolant splash, and accidental wheel breakage.

### 2.1.8 Coolant Supply System
Comprises a **sump, pump, piping, and nozzles**. Flood coolant is essential in surface grinding to:
- Reduce thermal damage (heat-affected zone)
- Flush away chips
- Lubricate the cutting zone
- Improve surface finish

### 2.1.9 Magnetic Chuck
An **electromagnetic work-holding device** built into the table. It uses DC current to magnetize the chuck face, holding ferromagnetic workpieces flat. Foot-pedal operated.

## 2.2 Grinding Wheel Specification (KTU High-Yield)

The standard marking of a grinding wheel uses **5 parameters** in the format: **A-G-S-B-M**

| Parameter | Symbol | Description | Common Example |
|-----------|--------|-------------|----------------|
| **Abrasive Type** | A | Material of the abrasive grains | **A** = Aluminium Oxide, **C** = Silicon Carbide, **D** = Diamond, **B** = Boron Nitride (CBN) |
| **Grit Size** | G | Size of abrasive grains (mesh number) | **36**, **46**, **60**, **80** (coarse to fine) |
| **Grade** | S | Bond hardness (how strongly grains are held) | **H, J, K, L, M** (soft to hard) |
| **Structure** | B | Spacing between grains (density) | **5** to **8** (open to dense) |
| **Bond Type** | M | Material that holds grains together | **V** = Vitrified, **R** = Rubber, **B** = Resin, **M** = Metal |

**Example: AA-46-K-5-V**
- **A** = Aluminium Oxide abrasive
- **46** = Grit size (medium)
- **K** = Medium grade (slightly hard)
- **5** = Open structure
- **V** = Vitrified bond

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Selection Rule:** For grinding **soft, ductile materials** (e.g., mild steel, aluminum) → use **softer grade wheels** (H, I, J). For **hard, brittle materials** (e.g., hardened steel, carbide) → use **harder grade wheels** (K, L, M).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Description | Variables & Units |
|---------|-------------|-------------------|
| $V_s = \pi \cdot D \cdot N / 60$ | Peripheral wheel speed | $V_s$ = m/s, $D$ = wheel diameter (m), $N$ = rpm |
| $V_w = f \cdot L / 1000$ | Table traverse (work) speed | $V_w$ = m/min, $f$ = table feed (mm/stroke), $L$ = table stroke length (mm) |
| $MRR = V_w \cdot a \cdot d$ | Material Removal Rate | $V_w$ in mm/s, $a$ = width (mm), $d$ = depth (mm), MRR in mm³/s |
| $MRR = (W \cdot d \cdot f_r \cdot n) / 60$ | MRR for reciprocating grinding | $W$ = width (mm), $d$ = depth/pass, $f_r$ = table speed (mm/min), $n$ = number of effective passes |
| $G = V_w / V_s$ | Grinding Ratio (G-ratio) | $V_w$ = volume of work material removed, $V_s$ = volume of wheel worn |
| $P = F_c \cdot V_s$ | Grinding Power | $F_c$ = tangential cutting force (N), $V_s$ = m/s |
| $t_m = L / V_w$ | Machining time per pass | $t_m$ = min, $L$ = stroke length (mm) |
| $T = (w / f) \cdot t_m$ | Total grinding time | $w$ = width to be ground (mm), $f$ = cross-feed (mm/pass) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** In the formula $V_w = f \cdot L / 1000$, the constant **1000** converts mm·mm/min to m·mm/min, yielding $V_w$ in **m/min**. Forgetting this constant is the #1 cause of unit errors in KTU papers.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Tool and die making**: Precision flat surfaces on dies and punches.
- **Manufacturing of machine tool beds**: Final finishing of guideways.
- **Automotive**: Surface finishing of engine blocks, cylinder heads, brake discs.
- **Aerospace**: Finishing of landing gear components and turbine housings.
- **Bearings**: Flat surfaces on bearing races.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Material Removal Rate (MRR) for Surface Grinding

The MRR in surface grinding is derived by treating the grinding zone as a **3-dimensional swept volume per unit time**.

### Step 1 — Define the Geometric Swept Volume per Pass

During one reciprocating pass of the table, the grinding wheel removes a layer of material of:
- **Length** = Table stroke length ($L$)
- **Width** = Width of the wheel engaged with workpiece ($a$)
- **Depth** = In-feed per pass ($d$)

Therefore, the volume removed per pass is:

$$V_{pass} = L \cdot a \cdot d$$

### Step 2 — Determine the Number of Passes per Minute

The table completes $\frac{V_w}{L}$ passes per minute, where $V_w$ is the table traverse speed (in mm/min) and $L$ is in mm.

### Step 3 — Calculate the MRR

The MRR is the volume removed per unit time:

$$MRR = \frac{V_w}{L} \cdot V_{pass} = \frac{V_w}{L} \cdot (L \cdot a \cdot d)$$

Simplifying $L$:

$$\boxed{MRR = V_w \cdot a \cdot d}$$

where $V_w$ is in mm/min, $a$ in mm, and $d$ in mm, giving MRR in **mm³/min**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion logic:** Since $V_w$ is naturally given in mm/min in surface grinding (it is a low-speed operation compared to milling), the result is in **mm³/min** without the need for additional unit conversion. This is different from milling where spindle speeds dominate and MRR is often expressed in cm³/min.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Model KTU Problem)

**Problem Statement (typical KTU-style):**
A surface grinding operation is performed on a mild steel workpiece of length 200 mm and width 80 mm. The grinding wheel diameter is 250 mm and rotates at 1500 rpm. The table reciprocates with a stroke length of 220 mm at 30 strokes/min. The cross-feed is 5 mm/pass and the depth of cut is 0.02 mm. Determine: (i) Material Removal Rate, (ii) Grinding time, (iii) Peripheral wheel speed.

### Given Data
- $L$ = 220 mm (stroke length)
- $W$ = 80 mm (workpiece width)
- $D$ = 250 mm = 0.25 m
- $N$ = 1500 rpm
- $N_{stroke}$ = 30 strokes/min
- $f$ = 5 mm/pass (cross-feed)
- $d$ = 0.02 mm (depth of cut)

### Part (i) — Material Removal Rate

**Step 1:** Calculate table traverse speed $V_w$.

$$V_w = L \cdot N_{stroke} = 220 \cdot 30 = 6600 \text{ mm/min}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the MRR formula.

$$MRR = V_w \cdot a \cdot d$$

$$MRR = 6600 \cdot 5 \cdot 0.02$$

$$\boxed{MRR = 660 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}}$$

### Part (ii) — Total Grinding Time

**Step 1:** Calculate number of effective cross-feeds.

$$n_{passes} = \frac{W}{f} = \frac{80}{5} = 16 \text{ passes}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate time per pass.

$$t_m = \frac{L}{V_w} = \frac{220}{6600} = 0.0333 \text{ min/pass}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate total time.

$$T = n_{passes} \cdot t_m = 16 \cdot 0.0333$$

$$\boxed{T = 0.533 \text{ min} \approx 32 \text{ seconds}}$$

### Part (iii) — Peripheral Wheel Speed

$$V_s = \frac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{60}$$

$$V_s = \frac{\pi \cdot 0.25 \cdot 1500}{60}$$

$$V_s = \frac{1177.5}{60}$$

$$\boxed{V_s = 19.625 \text{ m/s}}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Process Parameters

| Parameter Increased | MRR | Surface Finish | Wheel Wear | Specific Energy |
|---------------------|-----|----------------|------------|-----------------|
| Wheel speed ($V_s$) | ↑ slight | ↑ improves | ↓ reduces | ↓ decreases |
| Table speed ($V_w$) | ↑ linearly | ↓ degrades | ↑ increases | ↑ increases |
| Depth of cut ($d$) | ↑ linearly | ↓ degrades sharply | ↑↑ significantly | ↑ increases |
| Grit size (coarser) | ↑ increases | ↓ degrades (rougher) | ↓ less wear | ↓ decreases |
| Hard grade wheel | ↓ reduces | ↑ improves | ↑ increases | ↑ increases |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Takeaway:** The most influential parameter on **surface finish** is the **table traverse speed**. For finishing operations, the table speed is drastically reduced to allow more "dwell time" of the wheel over each point, which produces the characteristic spark-out finish.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of Surface Grinding Machine Components (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    BaseA[Base and Bed] --> ColumnA[Vertical Column]
    BaseA --> CoolSysA[Coolant Sump and Pump]
    BaseA --> SaddleA[Saddle Cross Feed]
    SaddleA --> TableA[Reciprocating Worktable]
    TableA --> MagChkA[Magnetic Chuck]
    MagChkA --> WorkpieceA[Workpiece Mounted on Chuck]
    ColumnA --> WheelHdA[Wheel Head Vertical Guideways]
    WheelHdA --> SpindleA[Spindle Motor]
    SpindleA --> GrWheelA[Grinding Wheel Spindle]
    GrWheelA --> GrWheelB[Grinding Wheel]
    CoolSysA --> NozzleA[Coolant Nozzle]
    NozzleA --> GrWheelB
    GrWheelB --> WheelGdA[Wheel Guard Safety]
```

## 4.2 Functional Process Flow of Flat Surface Grinding Operation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    StepA[Step 1: Mount workpiece on magnetic chuck] --> StepB[Step 2: Dress the grinding wheel using diamond dresser]
    StepB --> StepC[Step 3: Set depth of cut via handwheel on wheel head]
    StepC --> StepD[Step 4: Adjust table speed and stroke length]
    StepD --> StepE[Step 5: Start coolant flow]
    StepE --> StepF[Step 6: Start spindle and let wheel reach full rpm]
    StepF --> StepG[Step 7: Engage automatic table reciprocation]
    StepG --> StepH[Step 8: Perform rough grinding passes with cross-feed]
    StepH --> StepI[Step 9: Reduce depth of cut for finishing]
    StepI --> StepJ[Step 10: Conduct spark-out passes zero in-feed]
    StepJ --> StepK[Step 11: Stop table retract wheel head and shut down]
    StepK --> StepL[Step 12: Demagnetize chuck and remove workpiece]
    StepL --> StepM[Step 13: Measure with dial indicator and surface roughness tester]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram of Grinding Wheel Anatomy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    WhlA[Abrasive Grains A] --> CutEdgeA[Cutting Points Micro]
    WhlB[Bond Material B] --> HoldA[Holds Grains Together]
    HoldA --> PorA[Pores Space C]
    PorA --> ChipA[Chip Clearance and Coolant Flow]
    WhlA --> WhlB
    WhlB --> PorA
```

## 4.4 Operational Schematic: Wheel Workpiece Interaction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    WorkpieceB[Workpiece Surface] -->|Depth of cut d| IntZoneA[Cutting Zone]
    IntZoneA -->|Heat generation| HeatA[Heat]
    IntZoneA -->|Chips| ChipB[Chip Formation]
    IntZoneA -->|Coolant flood| CoolA[Coolant Supply]
    WheelA[Rotating Grinding Wheel] -->|Peripheral speed Vs| IntZoneA
    WheelA -->|Self-sharpening| WheelA
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Tip for KTU Lab Exam:** When drawing a labelled sketch in your record, always show the **wheel rotating direction** (typically into the workpiece for down-grinding or against for up-grinding), the **coolant nozzle position** (directed at the cutting zone), and the **workpiece orientation** on the magnetic chuck. These three details are repeatedly tested.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]**
**Define surface grinding. List any four operations that can be performed on a horizontal spindle surface grinder.**

**Model Answer:**

Surface grinding is an abrasive machining process that uses a rotating grinding wheel as the multipoint cutting tool to produce a flat, smooth, and dimensionally accurate surface on a workpiece. The process relies on the controlled interaction of abrasive grains bonded in a wheel matrix with the workpiece, aided by flood coolant.

Four operations performable on a horizontal spindle surface grinder are:

1. **Grinding a flat horizontal surface** (plain surface grinding) — the most common operation.
2. **Grinding a flat vertical surface** using a side of the wheel (sided grinding, e.g., for slots).
3. **Form grinding** — using a contoured wheel to generate a specific profile.
4. **Slot grinding** — by grinding along a defined path on the table.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
**State any three safety precautions to be observed while operating a surface grinding machine.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Always wear safety goggles** and ensure the wheel guard is properly positioned before starting the machine.
2. **Inspect the grinding wheel for cracks** using the "ring test" before mounting. A cracked wheel can shatter and cause severe injury at operating speeds.
3. **Never start the machine without coolant flow** and never touch the rotating wheel. Always allow the wheel to reach full speed before engaging the table.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **construction and working of a horizontal spindle reciprocating table surface grinder**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A surface grinding operation is carried out with the following data: Wheel diameter 200 mm, wheel speed 1800 rpm, table stroke length 180 mm, table speed 15 m/min, cross-feed 4 mm/pass, depth of cut 0.03 mm. Calculate (i) MRR, (ii) Specific Material Removal Rate per unit width, and (iii) Total grinding time to grind a workpiece 100 mm wide. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Construction:**
The major parts are: base, column, saddle, table, magnetic chuck, wheel head, spindle, grinding wheel, wheel guard, and coolant system (refer Section 4 diagram).

**Working Principle:**
1. The workpiece is mounted on the magnetic chuck which is energised via foot pedal.
2. The grinding wheel mounted on the spindle is rotated at high speed (typically 1500–2000 rpm).
3. The table is set to reciprocate under the wheel using hydraulic drive; the operator sets stroke length and table speed.
4. The wheel head is lowered by the in-feed handwheel to set the depth of cut.
5. As the table moves, the wheel engages the workpiece and removes material as micro-chips from abrasive grain action.
6. Coolant is flooded onto the contact zone to dissipate heat and flush chips.
7. The saddle provides the cross-feed, stepping the wheel across the width of the workpiece after each pass.
8. After rough grinding, a few spark-out passes are taken with no in-feed to relieve residual stresses and improve surface finish.

### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- $D$ = 200 mm = 0.2 m
- $N$ = 1800 rpm
- $L$ = 180 mm
- $V_w$ = 15 m/min = **15000 mm/min**
- $f$ = 4 mm/pass
- $d$ = 0.03 mm
- $W$ = 100 mm

**(i) Material Removal Rate (MRR):**

$$MRR = V_w \cdot f \cdot d = 15000 \cdot 4 \cdot 0.03$$

$$\boxed{MRR = 1800 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}}$$

**(ii) Specific MRR per unit width:**

Specific MRR (MRR per unit cross-feed width) is:

$$MRR_{spec} = V_w \cdot d = 15000 \cdot 0.03 = 450 \text{ mm}^2/\text{min per mm width}$$

**(iii) Total grinding time:**

**Step 1:** Number of effective passes.

$$n = \frac{W}{f} = \frac{100}{4} = 25 \text{ passes}$$

**Step 2:** Time per pass.

$$t_m = \frac{L}{V_w} = \frac{180}{15000} = 0.012 \text{ min/pass}$$

**Step 3:** Total time.

$$T = n \cdot t_m = 25 \cdot 0.012$$

$$\boxed{T = 0.3 \text{ min} = 18 \text{ seconds}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the **standard marking system of a grinding wheel** with an example. List any four factors influencing the selection of a grinding wheel for surface grinding. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **various work-holding devices used in surface grinding**. Compare the use of a magnetic chuck versus a mechanical vise in terms of setup time, holding force, and accuracy. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Standard Marking System (5 parameters):**

A grinding wheel is marked in the order: **Abrasive Type | Grit Size | Grade | Structure | Bond Type**

**Example: AA-60-K-5-V**
- **A** = Aluminium Oxide abrasive
- **60** = Grit size (medium-coarse)
- **K** = Grade (medium-hard)
- **5** = Structure (open)
- **V** = Vitrified bond

**Four Factors Influencing Wheel Selection:**

1. **Workpiece material hardness**: Hard workpieces need harder grade wheels; soft workpieces need softer grade wheels (to allow self-sharpening).
2. **Required surface finish**: Finer grit size → smoother finish.
3. **Material removal rate required**: Coarse grit + soft grade → high MRR.
4. **Type of grinding operation**: Finishing requires closed structure and fine grit; roughing requires open structure for chip clearance.

### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Work-Holding Devices in Surface Grinding:**

1. **Magnetic Chuck (Electromagnetic):** Most common in surface grinding. Fast setup, holds large flat surfaces, allows multiple parts. Best for ferromagnetic materials.
2. **Mechanical Vise:** Used for non-ferromagnetic materials (aluminum, brass, copper) or for pieces that need to be ground on multiple sides.
3. **Vacuum Chuck:** For thin, non-magnetic sheets where magnetic chuck cannot be used.
4. **Fixtures and Special Jigs:** For odd-shaped components, holding multiple parts, or for specific geometric requirements.
5. **Adhesives/Clamps:** For very thin or delicate parts.

**Comparison: Magnetic Chuck vs. Mechanical Vise**

| Criterion | Magnetic Chuck | Mechanical Vise |
|-----------|----------------|-----------------|
| **Setup time** | Very fast (seconds) — just place and energise | Slower (minutes) — requires clamping and alignment |
| **Holding force** | Uniform across surface; moderate | Concentrated at clamp points; can be high |
| **Accuracy** | High for flat surfaces | High if carefully aligned; depends on vise quality |
| **Material compatibility** | Only ferromagnetic | Any material |
| **Demagnetization required?** | Yes — important after operation | Not applicable |
| **Workpiece distortion** | Minimal for flat parts | Possible at clamp points |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks on Surface Grinding Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the spark-out pass** in the procedure → Examiners deduct 1–2 marks. Always mention 2–3 spark-out passes at the end of finishing.
>
> 2. **Confusing grit size with grade** → Grit size is *mesh number* (e.g., 46), grade is *bond hardness* (e.g., K). Examiners consider this a fundamental error.
>
> 3. **Unit error in MRR formula** → If $V_w$ is in m/min and you use it directly in mm units, MRR will be off by a factor of 1000. **Always convert $V_w$ to mm/min** (multiply by 1000) before substituting.
>
> 4. **Drawing the machine without coolant flow direction** → Examiners expect a labelled coolant nozzle aimed at the cutting zone.
>
> 5. **Skipping the ring test** in the safety precautions → A standard 1-mark point in KTU papers.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- **Definition:** Surface grinding = abrasive process using rotating wheel to produce flat surfaces.
- **Machine parts to remember (9):** Base, column, table, saddle, wheel head, grinding wheel, magnetic chuck, wheel guard, coolant system.
- **Grinding wheel marking (5 params):** Abrasive, Grit, Grade, Structure, Bond (mnemonic: **A-G-S-B-M**).
- **Key speeds:** $V_s$ = 20–35 m/s; $V_w$ = 6–30 m/min; Depth of cut = 0.005–0.05 mm.
- **Core formula:** $MRR = V_w \cdot a \cdot d$ — always use $V_w$ in **mm/min** for mm-based dimensions.
- **Time formula:** $T = (W/f) \cdot (L/V_w)$ — number of passes × time per pass.
- **Spark-out:** Final 2–5 passes with zero in-feed for surface finish improvement.
- **Coolant purpose:** Heat removal, chip flushing, lubrication, surface finish.
- **Selection rule:** Soft workpiece → soft grade wheel; Hard workpiece → hard grade wheel.
- **Safety essentials:** Ring test, wheel guard, eye protection, no coolant = no grinding.
- **Down-grinding vs Up-grinding:** Down-grinding (conventional) preferred in surface grinding for better finish.
- **G-ratio:** Indicator of wheel efficiency = volume of work removed / volume of wheel worn.
- **Magnetic chuck demagnetization:** Mandatory after operation to release the workpiece.
- **For non-magnetic materials:** Use vise, vacuum chuck, or adhesive mounting.
- **Spark observation in grinding:** A skilled operator judges finish quality from the spark pattern (color, density, length).

> **End of Module Experiment 2 — Grinding of a Flat Surface Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Exercises on Tool and Cutter Grinding Machine: Grinding of a Single-Point Cutting Tool
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMEL408 - MANUFACTURING TECHNOLOGY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Exercises on Tool and Cutter Grinding Machine: Grinding of a Single-Point Cutting Tool

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Tool and Cutter Grinding Machine (TCG)

A **Tool and Cutter Grinder** is a versatile, high-precision abrasive machining device specifically designed to sharpen, form, and recondition single-point and multi-point cutting tools. Unlike a standard surface or cylindrical grinder, a TCG has multiple swiveling and tilting slides (typically a 5 or 6 degrees-of-freedom kinematic chain) so that the grinding wheel can be presented at **any required spatial angle** to the tool blank.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus-Aligned):** A Tool and Cutter Grinder is a special-purpose grinding machine equipped with a universal head, swiveling table, and tilting wheel slide, used to generate the complex tool geometry (rake face, flank face, nose radius) on a cutting tool blank by controlled abrasive action.

### 1.2 Single-Point Cutting Tool (SPCT)

A **Single-Point Cutting Tool** is a tool that has only **one effective cutting edge** (or "point") that engages the workpiece. It is the workhorse of turning, facing, boring, shaping, and planing operations on a lathe or shaper. It consists of:
- A **shank** (the body, held in the tool post).
- A **nose** (the curved/rounded tip that is the lowest point on the cutting edge).
- A **rake face** (the surface over which the chip slides).
- A **flank face** (the surface that faces the machined surface and provides clearance).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

> [!TIP]
> **The Carpenter's Chisel Analogy**
> Imagine you are a carpenter shaping wood with a chisel. The chisel is the "cutting tool," and the wood is the "workpiece." The *sharpness* of the chisel depends on two key angles: (1) the **clearance angle** — if you tilt the chisel too steeply downward, it digs in and chatters; (2) the **rake angle** — if the top face is too flat, the wood won't peel off smoothly. A Tool and Cutter Grinder is essentially a "chisel-sharpening robotic workshop" that grinds steel cutting tools to mathematically precise angles, just like a master carpenter would hone a chisel by eye — except the TCG does it to a tolerance of **± 0.5°** repeatedly.

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Grinding wheel material for HSS | Aluminum Oxide (Al₂O₃) | — |
| Grit size (roughing) | **46 – 60** | mesh |
| Grit size (finishing) | **80 – 120** | mesh |
| Wheel grade (softness) | **J – K** (medium soft) | grade letter |
| Wheel bond | Vitrified (ceramic) | — |
| Wheel speed (peripheral) | **20 – 30 m/s** | m·s⁻¹ |
| Coolant | Soluble oil emulsion (1:20) | ratio |
| Work hardness reference | HSS: **62 – 65 HRC**, Carbide: 1500 HV | HRC / HV |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Highlight:** The examiner almost always tests (a) the *names of tool angles* and (b) the *recommended grinding wheel specification* for HSS. Memorize the **Tool Nomenclature (ASA/ISO 3002)** in the order: Orthogonal Rake (γ), Orthogonal Clearance (α), Cutting Edge Angle (κ_r), and Tool Cutting Edge Angle (λ_s).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Seven Elements of a Single-Point Tool (Tool Signature)

A single-point cutting tool is defined by **seven parameters** (ASA system) on its **tool reference system**, which is a coordinate system fixed to the tool:

| # | Element | Symbol | Plain Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Back Rake Angle | α_b (or γ_b) | Inclination of the rake face measured in a plane parallel to the longitudinal axis of the shank, perpendicular to the reference plane. |
| 2 | Side Rake Angle | α_s (or γ_s) | Inclination of the rake face measured in a plane perpendicular to the longitudinal axis. |
| 3 | End Clearance Angle | α_e | Angle between the end flank (primary flank) and a plane perpendicular to the cutting edge plane. |
| 4 | Side Clearance Angle | α_s' | Angle between the side flank (secondary flank) and a plane perpendicular to the cutting edge plane. |
| 5 | Cutting Edge Angle | κ_r | Angle between the cutting edge plane and the reference plane, measured in the reference plane. |
| 6 | Tool Cutting Edge Angle (Lead Angle) | ψ (or λ_s) | Angle between the cutting edge and a plane parallel to the longitudinal axis, measured in the cutting edge plane. |
| 7 | Nose Radius | r_n | Radius of the rounded tip of the tool. |

### 2.2 The Three Reference Planes (Geometry Foundation)

- **Tool Reference Plane (R_p):** A plane perpendicular to the tool shank axis, passing through the tool point.
- **Cutting Edge Plane (P_s):** A plane tangent to the cutting edge, perpendicular to R_p.
- **Machining / Working Plane (P_f):** A plane in which chip flow velocity lies; perpendicular to both R_p and P_s (in orthogonal cutting).

### 2.3 Standard Recommended Angles (HSS Tool, Mild Steel Workpiece)

| Tool Angle | Symbol | Recommended Range |
|---|---|---|
| Back Rake | γ_b | **+8° to +15°** |
| Side Rake | γ_s | **+10° to +15°** |
| End Clearance | α_e | **6° to 10°** |
| Side Clearance | α_s' | **6° to 10°** |
| Cutting Edge | κ_r | **60° to 90°** (commonly 75°) |
| Lead / Tool Cutting Edge Angle | λ_s | **0° to +5°** |
| Nose Radius | r_n | **0.5 to 1.6 mm** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why these values?** A positive rake reduces cutting force and power, while adequate clearance prevents the flank face from rubbing the machined surface (which causes chatter and tool wear). Excessive rake weakens the cutting edge.

### 2.4 Why Use a Tool and Cutter Grinder?

- **Universal in setup:** The table can swivel 360° and tilt ± 90°; the wheel head also tilts in two perpendicular planes.
- **Sharpens form tools, drills, milling cutters, hobs, reamers** — any tool with complex geometry.
- **Re-grinding saves cost:** A HSS tool can be re-sharpened **8 to 15 times** before being discarded.
- **Achieves the exact geometry** required for a specific workpiece material (e.g., negative rake for hard materials like Inconel).

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\gamma_o = \gamma_s \cdot \sin \kappa_r - \gamma_b \cdot \cos \kappa_r$ | Conversion: Tool System Rake → Orthogonal Rake. |
| 2 | $\alpha_o = \alpha_e \cdot \cos \kappa_r + \alpha_s' \cdot \sin \kappa_r$ | Conversion: Tool System Clearance → Orthogonal Clearance. |
| 3 | $\tan \kappa_r' = \dfrac{\alpha_s'}{\alpha_e} \cdot \tan \kappa_r$ | Side cutting edge angle (κ_r') in terms of tool angles. |
| 4 | $\phi = 90° - \kappa_r - \psi$ | Relationship between cutting edge angle, lead, and approach. |
| 5 | $V_w = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000}$ | Work velocity (lathe), m/min. |
| 6 | $V_s = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d_w \cdot n_w}{60 \times 1000}$ | Wheel peripheral speed, m/s. |
| 7 | $MRR = V_w \cdot f \cdot d$ | Material removal rate, mm³/min. |
| 8 | $C_c = 2\pi r_n$ | Cusp height / surface roughness relation (with feed f). |
| 9 | $R_a \approx \dfrac{f^2}{8 r_n}$ | Theoretical surface roughness from nose radius. |
| 10 | $\tan \lambda_s = \dfrac{\sin \gamma_s}{\cos \gamma_b} \cdot \tan \kappa_r$ | Inclination angle in terms of side rake, back rake, cutting edge. |

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU lab viva, **Formulas 1, 2, and 6** are the most frequently asked. Formula 6 is critical to verify that the grinding wheel speed is within the safe operating window (typically **25 m/s**).

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **CNC turning:** SPCT geometry directly influences chip control, surface finish, and tool life.
- **High-speed machining:** Negative rake tools (e.g., for Inconel 718) are ground on TCG with the rake face inclined in the *opposite* sense to the chip flow.
- **Form tools:** T-shaped or dovetail tools for automatic lathes are ground on a TCG with the aid of an optical projector.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Conversion from Tool Reference System to Orthogonal Cutting System

The **Orthogonal Rake Angle (γ_o)** is the rake measured in a plane perpendicular to the cutting edge — this is the angle that actually appears in the classical Merchant's circle. We must convert from the tool reference system (where γ_b, γ_s, κ_r are known) to the orthogonal system.

**Step 1 — Define Coordinate Systems**

Let us set:
- $\hat{x}$ along the longitudinal axis of the tool shank (reference plane horizontal).
- $\hat{y}$ along the cutting edge (perpendicular to x, in the cutting edge plane).
- $\hat{z}$ perpendicular to both, along the tool point.

**Step 2 — Express the Rake Face Normal**

The rake face is first tilted by γ_b about the y-axis, then by γ_s about the z-axis. The normal vector to the rake face (in the tool frame) is:

$$
\vec{N} = (-\sin \gamma_b, \ \sin \gamma_s, \ \cos \gamma_b \cos \gamma_s)
$$

**Step 3 — Rotate to the Orthogonal Cutting Plane**

The orthogonal plane is perpendicular to the cutting edge. We rotate the rake-face normal about the y-axis by an angle κ_r (the cutting edge angle). The orthogonal rake angle γ_o is the angle between the projected normal and the z-axis.

**Step 4 — Apply Rotation Matrix and Simplify**

$$
\cos \gamma_o = \cos \gamma_b \cos \gamma_s \cos \kappa_r - \sin \gamma_b \sin \kappa_r
$$

**Step 5 — Small-Angle Linearization (KTU Approximation)**

Since γ_b and γ_s are typically small (≤ 15°), we use the small-angle approximations:
$\sin \gamma \approx \gamma$ and $\cos \gamma \approx 1$. The expression reduces to:

$$
\gamma_o \approx \gamma_s \sin \kappa_r - \gamma_b \cos \kappa_r
$$

This is **Formula 1** of the cheat sheet, and it is what students are expected to derive in the 14-mark question.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Wheel Peripheral Speed (V_s)

**Step 1 — Identify the inputs.**
- Wheel diameter $d_w$ in **mm**.
- Wheel rotational speed $n_w$ in **rpm**.

**Step 2 — Circumferential distance per revolution.**
The wheel travels one circumference per revolution:
$$
C = \pi \cdot d_w \quad \text{(in mm per revolution)}
$$

**Step 3 — Distance per minute.**
Since the wheel makes $n_w$ revolutions per minute:
$$
D_m = \pi \cdot d_w \cdot n_w \quad \text{(mm/min)}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to m/s.**
Dividing by 1000 (mm → m) and by 60 (min → s):

$$
V_s = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d_w \cdot n_w}{60 \times 1000} \quad \text{m/s}
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Example (KTU-Style Problem).**
A grinding wheel of diameter **150 mm** rotates at **3000 rpm**. Find V_s.

$$
V_s = \dfrac{\pi \times 150 \times 3000}{60000} = \dfrac{1{,}413{,}717}{60000} = 23.56 \text{ m/s}
$$

Since this is below the safe peripheral speed of **33 m/s** (standard for vitrified wheels), the operation is safe.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Theoretical Surface Roughness from Nose Radius

**Step 1 — Geometry of the Cusp.**
When a tool with nose radius $r_n$ advances by a feed $f$ per revolution, it leaves a cusp (peak-to-valley) on the machined surface.

**Step 2 — Pythagorean Relation.**
The center of the nose arc is at height $r_n$ above the lowest point. The horizontal offset is $f/2$. Therefore:

$$
R_n^2 = \left(R_n - h\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{f}{2}\right)^2
$$

**Step 3 — Expand the Square.**

$$
R_n^2 = R_n^2 - 2 R_n h + h^2 + \dfrac{f^2}{4}
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify.**

$$
2 R_n h = h^2 + \dfrac{f^2}{4}
$$

**Step 5 — Approximate (h is very small).**
Since $h \ll R_n$, the term $h^2$ is negligible, yielding:

$$
h \approx \dfrac{f^2}{8 R_n}
$$

The theoretical peak-to-valley roughness is $R_t = h$, and the **centerline average roughness** is empirically $R_a \approx R_t / 4$:

$$
