R_a \approx \dfrac{f^2}{8 R_n} \cdot \dfrac{1}{4} = \dfrac{f^2}{32 R_n} \quad \text{(empirical, often written as } \dfrac{f^2}{8R_n} \text{ for } R_t)
$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Tool Angle Variations by Workpiece Material

| Workpiece Material | Brinell Hardness (HB) | Back Rake (γ_b) | Side Rake (γ_s) | End Clearance (α_e) | Cutting Edge (κ_r) | Nose Radius (r_n) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Aluminium alloys | 30 – 100 | **+20° to +30°** | **+15° to +20°** | 8° | 45° – 75° | 0.4 – 0.8 mm |
| Mild Steel | 100 – 200 | **+10° to +15°** | **+10° to +15°** | 6° – 8° | 75° | 0.8 – 1.2 mm |
| Hard Steel (HRC 40+) | 300+ | **0° to −5°** | **0° to +5°** | 5° – 7° | 90° | 1.2 – 1.6 mm |
| Cast Iron | 200+ | **+5° to +10°** | **+5° to +10°** | 6° | 75° – 90° | 1.2 mm |
| Stainless Steel | 180 – 250 | **+5° to +12°** | **+8° to +12°** | 6° – 8° | 75° – 90° | 1.2 mm |
| Superalloys (Inconel) | 350+ | **−5° to −10°** | **−5° to 0°** | 6° | 90° – 105° | 0.8 – 1.2 mm |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Insight:** The trend is clear — *softer workpiece = larger positive rake*; *harder workpiece = smaller or negative rake* (for tool strength). Remember the slogan: **"Soft cuts sweet, hard cuts need a strong edge."**

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagrams follow the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 safeguards: alphanumeric node IDs (no reserved keywords), clean uppercase text labels, and no markdown formatting inside double-quoted labels.

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Tool and Cutter Grinder

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PWR["POWER SUPPLY UNIT"]
        MTR["THREE PHASE AC MOTOR 1.5 TO 3 KW"]
    end

    subgraph SPD["SPINDLE DRIVE"]
        BLT["V BELT DRIVE"]
        WHSP["WHEEL SPINDLE 3000 RPM"]
    end

    subgraph TBL["WORK TABLE ASSEMBLY"]
        TBLL["LONGITUDINAL SLIDE 300 MM"]
        TBLT["CROSS SLIDE 200 MM"]
        TBLS["SWIVEL BASE 0 TO 360 DEG"]
        TBLI["TILT MECHANISM PLUS MINUS 15 DEG"]
    end

    subgraph WHD["WHEEL HEAD ASSEMBLY"]
        WHDT["WHEEL HEAD TILT PLUS MINUS 90 DEG"]
        WHDS["WHEEL HEAD SWIVEL 360 DEG"]
        WHDR["GRINDING WHEEL 150 TO 200 MM DIA"]
    end

    subgraph FIX["WORKHOLDING FIXTURE"]
        FIXV["TOOL POST V BLOCK"]
        FIXC["CLAMPING SCREWS"]
    end

    subgraph COOL["COOLANT SYSTEM"]
        CLP["COOLANT PUMP 0.5 HP"]
        CLT["SOLUBLE OIL TANK 20 LITRE"]
        CLN["NOZZLE TO GRINDING ZONE"]
    end

    MTR --> BLT --> WHSP --> WHDR
    TBLS --- TBLL --- TBLT --- TBLI
    WHDT --- WHDS
    WHDR -. grinds .-> FIXV
    FIXV --- FIXC
    CLT --> CLP --> CLN
    CLN -. sprays .-> WHDR
```

### 4.2 Process Flow: Grinding Procedure for a Single-Point Tool

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ST1["START EXPERIMENT"] --> ST2["STEP 1 SELECT HSS TOOL BLANK 20 MM SQUARE 100 MM LONG"]
    ST2 --> ST3["STEP 2 DRESS THE GRINDING WHEEL WITH A DIAMOND DRESSER"]
    ST3 --> ST4["STEP 3 MOUNT TOOL IN V BLOCK FIXTURE WITH 5 MM PROJECTION"]
    ST4 --> ST5["STEP 4 SET TOOL HEIGHT USING HEIGHT GAUGE ABOVE TABLE"]
    ST5 --> ST6["STEP 5 TILT WHEEL HEAD TO REQUIRED RAKE ANGLE GAMMA"]
    ST6 --> ST7["STEP 6 SWIVEL TABLE TO REQUIRED CUTTING EDGE ANGLE KAPPA R"]
    ST7 --> ST8["STEP 7 LIGHTLY GRIND THE RAKE FACE USING LIGHT TRAVERSE"]
    ST8 --> ST9["STEP 8 INSPECT WITH TOOLMAKERS MICROSCOPE OR BEVEL PROTRACTOR"]
    ST9 --> ST10{"ANGLE CORRECT"}
    ST10 -- NO --> ST11["RECORRECT ANGLE AND REGRIND"]
    ST11 --> ST8
    ST10 -- YES --> ST12["STEP 9 GRIND END FLANK FOR CLEARANCE ANGLE ALPHA E"]
    ST12 --> ST13["STEP 10 GRIND SIDE FLANK FOR SIDE CLEARANCE ALPHA S PRIME"]
    ST13 --> ST14["STEP 11 GRIND NOSE RADIUS USING CUP WHEEL OR EDGE OF WHEEL"]
    ST14 --> ST15["STEP 12 INSPECT ALL 7 ELEMENTS WITH OPTICAL PROFILE PROJECTOR"]
    ST15 --> ST16["STEP 13 RECORD RESULTS IN OBSERVATION BOOK"]
    ST16 --> ST17["END EXPERIMENT"]
```

### 4.3 Tool Geometry — Planes and Angles (Functional Mapping)

```mermaid
flowchart TB

        TSP["TOOL SHANK"]
        TPN["NOSE RADIUS R N"]

    end

    subgraph PL["REFERENCE PLANES"]
        PRP["TOOL REFERENCE PLANE R P PERPENDICULAR TO SHANK"]
        PCE["CUTTING EDGE PLANE P S TANGENT TO EDGE PERPENDICULAR TO R P"]
        PWR["WORKING PLANE P F PERPENDICULAR TO BOTH"]
    end

    subgraph AN["TOOL ANGLES"]
        ANG["RAKE ANGLE GAMMA BETWEEN RAKE FACE AND R P"]
        ANA["CLEARANCE ANGLE ALPHA BETWEEN FLANK AND P S"]
        ANK["CUTTING EDGE ANGLE KAPPA R IN R P FROM FEED DIRECTION"]
        ANL["LEAD ANGLE LAMBDA S IN P S FROM LONGITUDINAL AXIS"]
    end

    TCE --- ANG
    TCE --- ANA
    TCE --- ANK
    TCE --- ANL
    PRP --- PCE
    PCE --- PWR
    TPN --- TCE
    TSP --- TPN
```

### 4.4 Wheel-Work Interaction Zone (Schematic Functional Map)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph WZ["CONTACT ZONE"]
        ZA["AREA OF CONTACT A C IN MM SQ"]
        ZT["TEMPERATURE T 800 TO 1200 DEG C"]
        ZS["STRAIN RATE EPSILON 10 TO THE POWER 4 TO 10 TO THE POWER 6"]
    end

    WHL["GRINDING WHEEL AL2O3 GRAIN SIZE 60 GRIT GRADE J"] --> WZ
    WBL["WORKPIECE HSS 62 HRC"] --> WZ
    WZ --> WCH["CHIPS ABRASIVE DUST FINE"]
    WZ --> WHET["HEAT CONDUCTED INTO WORKPIECE"]
    WZ --> WWR["WHEEL WEAR FLAT AND FRACTURE OF GRAINS"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Style]**

**Q1. Define a single-point cutting tool. List any three tool angles with their symbols.**
*(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
A single-point cutting tool is a cutting tool that has only one effective cutting edge which engages the workpiece during machining. It is used in turning, shaping, planing, and boring operations. The three tool angles are:
1. **Back Rake Angle (γ_b):** Inclination of the rake face from the reference plane, measured in a plane parallel to the shank axis.
2. **End Clearance Angle (α_e):** Angle between the end flank and a plane perpendicular to the cutting edge plane; prevents rubbing.
3. **Cutting Edge Angle (κ_r):** Angle between the cutting edge plane and the reference plane, measured in the reference plane.

---

**Q2. What grinding wheel would you recommend for grinding a HSS single-point tool, and why?**
*(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
A **vitrified-bonded aluminum oxide (Al₂O₃) wheel** of grit size **60 to 80** and grade **J to K** is recommended. **Aluminum oxide** is chosen because HSS contains tungsten, molybdenum, and vanadium carbides; Al₂O₃ is hard enough to abrade these carbides (≈ 9 Mohs) without excessive chemical reaction. **Vitrified bond** is rigid and heat-resistant, suitable for precision tool grinding. **Grit 60–80** is coarse enough for free-cutting yet fine enough to give acceptable surface finish. **Grade J–K** is medium-soft, so worn grit fractures to expose fresh sharp grit, maintaining a self-sharpening action.

---

### 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted Module 2 Pattern]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With the help of neat sketches, describe the **tool reference system** of a single-point cutting tool. Define all **seven elements of tool nomenclature** with their standard values for a HSS tool used on mild steel. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Derive the relationship between the **tool-system rake angle and the orthogonal rake angle**. A HSS tool has γ_b = 10°, γ_s = 12°, and κ_r = 75°. Calculate the orthogonal rake angle γ_o. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution (a):**

The tool reference system is a coordinate system fixed to the tool, with its origin at the tool point. The three reference planes are:
- **Tool Reference Plane (R_p):** Perpendicular to the shank axis, passing through the tool point.
- **Cutting Edge Plane (P_s):** Tangent to the cutting edge, perpendicular to R_p.
- **Working Plane (P_f):** Perpendicular to both R_p and P_s.

The seven elements (ASA / ISO 3002) are:

| # | Element | Symbol | HSS on Mild Steel |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Back rake | γ_b | +8° to +15° |
| 2 | Side rake | γ_s | +10° to +15° |
| 3 | End clearance | α_e | 6° to 10° |
| 4 | Side clearance | α_s' | 6° to 10° |
| 5 | Cutting edge angle | κ_r | 75° (typical) |
| 6 | Tool cutting edge (lead) | λ_s | 0° to +5° |
| 7 | Nose radius | r_n | 0.5 to 1.6 mm |

**Model Solution (b):**

**Derivation (as in Section 3.1, condensed):**

Starting from the rake-face normal $\vec{N}$ in the tool frame, rotating by κ_r to the orthogonal plane, and applying small-angle approximation:

$$
\gamma_o = \gamma_s \sin \kappa_r - \gamma_b \cos \kappa_r
$$

**Numerical substitution:**
Given γ_b = 10°, γ_s = 12°, κ_r = 75°:

$$
\gamma_o = 12° \cdot \sin 75° - 10° \cdot \cos 75°
$$

Evaluating the trigonometric values:
$\sin 75° = 0.9659$ and $\cos 75° = 0.2588$.

$$
\gamma_o = 12 \times 0.9659 - 10 \times 0.2588
$$

$$
\gamma_o = 11.591 - 2.588 = 9.00°
$$

**Answer: The orthogonal rake angle γ_o ≈ 9.0°** (positive, hence the tool is a "sharp" or "free-cutting" tool).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the **constructional features and various degrees of freedom** of a universal Tool and Cutter Grinder. How are they useful in grinding single-point tools? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A grinding wheel of diameter **200 mm** rotates at **2800 rpm**. Calculate the peripheral speed of the wheel. If a HSS tool blank (length 100 mm, square cross-section 20 mm) is to be ground with a nose radius of 1.2 mm and a feed of 0.2 mm/rev on a lathe, find the **theoretical surface roughness (R_a)**. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution (a):**

A universal Tool and Cutter Grinder has the following constructional features:

| Component | Degrees of Freedom | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Wheel head | Swivel 360° + Tilt ± 90° | Presents the wheel at any spatial angle to grind the rake face. |
| Work table | Longitudinal traverse 300 mm, Cross traverse 200 mm | Provides controlled linear motion for roughing and finishing passes. |
| Table swivel | 0° to 360° | Sets the cutting edge angle κ_r. |
| Table tilt | ±15° | Sets the back rake angle γ_b. |
| Wheel slide | Vertical Z-axis | Compensates for wheel wear and dressing. |
| Work fixture | Tilting V-block | Holds the tool blank at the required projection and orientation. |

Usefulness for single-point tools: The **combined swivels and tilts** allow the operator to present the grinding wheel to the rake face (using wheel-head tilt), set the cutting edge angle (using table swivel), and set the rake angle (using wheel-head swivel) — all in a single setup. The longitudinal and cross traverses permit both roughing and spark-out finishing.

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Peripheral wheel speed (V_s).**

$$
V_s = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d_w \cdot n_w}{60 \times 1000}
$$

$$
V_s = \dfrac{\pi \times 200 \times 2800}{60000} = \dfrac{1{,}759{,}292}{60000} = 29.32 \text{ m/s}
$$

This is within the safe range (≤ 33 m/s).

**Step 2 — Theoretical surface roughness (R_a).**

$$
R_a \approx \dfrac{f^2}{8 r_n}
$$

(Note: This is the **peak-to-valley height** R_t; the centerline average R_a is approximately R_t/4. KTU typically uses either form; the formula in Section 3.3 is standard.)

Given f = 0.2 mm, r_n = 1.2 mm:

$$
R_a = \dfrac{(0.2)^2}{8 \times 1.2} = \dfrac{0.04}{9.6} = 0.00417 \text{ mm} = 4.17 \ \mu\text{m}
$$

**Answer: V_s = 29.32 m/s; R_a ≈ 4.17 μm.**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing symbols:** Students mix up γ (rake) and α (clearance). The mnemonic is **"Rake is Rising (γ), Clearance is Cutting Air (α)"** — rake lifts the chip, clearance leaves air gap.
> 2. **Forgetting units in V_s:** A 14-mark question will deduct 2 marks if m/s is omitted or written as m/sec inconsistently. Always write **m·s⁻¹** or **m/s**.
> 3. **Sign convention error in orthogonal conversion:** Students often write $\gamma_o = \gamma_s \sin \kappa_r + \gamma_b \cos \kappa_r$ (with a **plus** sign). The correct sign is **minus** for standard positive-γ_b (rises away from the cutting edge).
> 4. **Skipping the derivation:** In part (b) of 14-mark questions, the derivation step carries **3 of the 7 marks**. If you write only the final formula and answer, you lose the derivation marks.
> 5. **Using the wrong grit size:** "60 grit" is for *roughing*; "100–120" is for *finishing*. Writing just "fine grit" loses 1 mark.
> 6. **No sketch = no marks:** Tool geometry questions **must** include a labelled diagram of the tool reference system; without it, 2–3 marks are forfeited.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for the Lab Exam / Viva:**

- **Tool = 1 cutting edge.** Single-point tool has one effective point; multi-point (drill, milling cutter) has many.
- **Three Reference Planes:** R_p (perpendicular to shank), P_s (tangent to edge), P_f (working).
- **Seven Elements of Tool Nomenclature (in order):** γ_b, γ_s, α_e, α_s', κ_r, λ_s, r_n.
- **Standard HSS on Mild Steel:** γ_b = 10°, γ_s = 12°, α_e = 8°, α_s' = 8°, κ_r = 75°, λ_s = 0°, r_n = 1.0 mm.
- **Grinding Wheel for HSS:** Al₂O₃ (white for HSS, pink for carbide), vitrified bond, grit 60 (roughing) / 100 (finishing), grade J–K.
- **Conversion Formula (MUST MEMORIZE):** $\gamma_o = \gamma_s \sin \kappa_r - \gamma_b \cos \kappa_r$.
- **Wheel Speed (V_s):** Formula $V_s = \dfrac{\pi d_w n_w}{60000}$; safe value ≈ **25–33 m/s**.
- **Surface Roughness (R_a):** $R_a \approx f^2 / (8 r_n)$; nose radius is the **most powerful** factor.
- **Coolant:** Always use soluble oil emulsion (1:20) for HSS; **dry grinding** is preferred for carbide to avoid thermal cracks.
- **Dressing:** Dress the wheel with a diamond dresser *before* every job to restore geometry and expose fresh grit.
- **Inspection:** Use **optical toolmakers microscope** (×30) or **bevel protractor** (± 0.5° accuracy) to verify angles.
- **Rake Sign Convention:** Positive rake for soft ductile materials; negative rake for hard, brittle, or work-hardening alloys.
- **KTU Viva Favorites:** "Why vitrified bond?", "Difference between up-grinding and down-grinding?", "What happens if nose radius is too small?"

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Exercises on Milling Machine: Plane and Pocket Milling Operations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMEL408 - MANUFACTURING TECHNOLOGY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Exercises on Milling Machine: Plane and Pocket Milling Operations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

**Milling** is a fundamental material removal process in which a multi-tooth rotary cutting tool (milling cutter) removes material from a stationary workpiece by advancing (or feeding) against the cutter at an angle to the cutter axis. The KTU 2024 Manufacturing Technology Lab syllabus categorizes milling exercises into two foundational operations:

- **Plane Milling (Surface Milling):** The generation of a flat, horizontal or vertical surface on the workpiece using a peripheral or face milling cutter. The machined surface is parallel to the table (horizontal plane) or perpendicular to the table (vertical plane/slot).
- **Pocket Milling:** A contour-milling operation in which a closed cavity (pocket) of defined depth, width, and shape is carved out from the workpiece interior using an end-mill or similar cutter, requiring coordinated 2-D or 3-D toolpath motion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCMEL408 – Module 2):**
> The student is expected to *independently* perform plane and pocket milling, identify cutting parameters, calculate spindle speed and feed rate from recommended cutting data, measure final dimensions using Vernier calipers/micrometers, and verify surface roughness and dimensional accuracy against the drawing.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a carpenter using a circular saw blade mounted on an axle, while pushing a wooden plank against it:

- **Plane Milling** is like running the plank *flat across* the spinning blade — you skim the top layer evenly, just as a carpenter planes a tabletop. Each tooth of the saw acts like a tiny chisel chipping away a thin shaving.
- **Pocket Milling** is like using the same spinning blade to hollow out a rectangular cavity in the middle of the plank — the blade must move in a controlled back-and-forth or spiral pattern, gradually deepening and widening the cavity.

The key intuition: **the cutter rotates (primary motion) while the table feeds the workpiece (secondary motion)**, and the *teeth act in succession*, meaning each tooth removes only a small chip — this is why milling can achieve excellent surface finish and high material removal rate (MRR) simultaneously.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Metrics Used in KTU Labs:**
> - Cutter Diameter **D (mm)**
> - Number of Teeth **Z**
> - Cutting Speed **V_c (m/min)**
> - Spindle Speed **N (RPM)**
> - Feed per Tooth **f_z (mm/tooth)**
> - Table Feed **V_f (mm/min)**
> - Depth of Cut **a_p (mm)**
> - Width of Cut **a_e (mm)**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown

#### 2.1.1 Plane Milling – Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Workpiece Clamping:** The raw workpiece (mild steel, aluminum, or brass) is mounted on the milling machine table using a vise or direct clamping with T-bolts. The reference face must be flat and clean.
2. **Cutter Selection:** A **plain milling cutter** (for horizontal surface) or a **side and face milling cutter** is mounted on the arbor. For a vertical face, a **face milling cutter** is fitted to the vertical spindle.
3. **Setting Cutting Parameters:** Using recommended cutting speed (V_c) for the work material from standard machinability handbooks, spindle speed **N** is calculated and set on the machine.
4. **Setting Depth of Cut:** The knee is raised/lowered until the cutter just grazes the top of the workpiece. The graduated dial is used to advance the table by the required **a_p** (typically 0.5 to 3 mm for finishing, 3 to 6 mm for roughing).
5. **Engaging Feed:** The table is engaged for power feed. The multi-tooth cutter rotates while the table moves linearly; each tooth takes a chip in *up-milling* (conventional) or *down-milling* (climb) direction.
6. **Measurement & Inspection:** After the pass, surface flatness is checked with a dial indicator and dimensional accuracy with Vernier calipers.

#### 2.1.2 Pocket Milling – Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Layout Marking:** Pocket boundary is scribed onto the workpiece using a height gauge and scriber; a centre-punch is used at entry/exit corners.
2. **Cutter Selection:** A **plain end-mill** (2-flute for aluminum, 4-flute for steel) is mounted in the collet of the vertical spindle. Diameter depends on pocket corner radius.
3. **Pilot Hole / Plunge Cut:** For a closed pocket, a hole is drilled first (or the end-mill plunges directly if the machine has rigid plumb spindle). This is the **Z-axis entry**.
4. **Roughing Pass:** The cutter traces the pocket profile at a pre-set depth, leaving a **finishing allowance** of ~0.3 to 0.5 mm on the walls and floor.
5. **Finishing Pass:** A final slow pass at the exact dimensions achieves the required **surface roughness (Ra in µm)** and dimensional tolerance.
6. **Deburring & Inspection:** Sharp corners are deburred; pocket depth, length, and width are measured with depth micrometer, Vernier, or CMM.

### 2.2 Why These Operations Matter in KTU Labs

- **Plane milling** is the most fundamental machining process after turning and is the building block for manufacturing dies, jigs, fixtures, and machine tool beds.
- **Pocket milling** is the precursor to **CNC pocket cycles (G73, G81, G82)** and is essential for mould manufacturing — without understanding manual pocket milling, a student cannot progress to CAM-based operations.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Board Exam Note:** Memorize the **N formula** and the **V_f formula** in milling. They are the two most frequently asked 3-mark derivations in KTU ESE. Also note the absolute-value form below — when written inline in tables, use `\vert` to avoid markdown syntax breakage.

| **Parameter** | **Symbol** | **Formula** | **Unit** | **Remarks** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Spindle Speed | $N$ | $N = \dfrac{V_c \times 1000}{\pi \times D}$ | RPM | $V_c$ in m/min, $D$ in mm |
| Table Feed | $V_f$ | $V_f = f_z \times N \times Z$ | mm/min | $f_z$ in mm/tooth |
| Feed per Tooth | $f_z$ | $f_z = \dfrac{V_f}{N \times Z}$ | mm/tooth | From machinability tables |
| Feed per Revolution | $f$ | $f = f_z \times Z$ | mm/rev | Total feed per spindle rev |
| Material Removal Rate (MRR) | $Z_{MRR}$ | $Z_{MRR} = a_p \times a_e \times V_f$ | mm³/min | $a_p$ depth, $a_e$ width |
| Machining Time | $T_m$ | $T_m = \dfrac{L + L_a}{V_f}$ | min | $L_a$ = approach + overtravel |
| Cutting Power | $P_c$ | $P_c = \dfrac{Z_{MRR} \times k_c}{60 \times 10^6 \times \eta}$ | kW | $k_c$ = specific cutting force (N/mm²) |
| Surface Roughness (theoretical) | $R_a$ | $R_a \approx \dfrac{f_z^2}{8 \times r_\varepsilon}$ | µm | $r_\varepsilon$ = nose radius |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Do not confuse turning formulas with milling formulas!** In turning: $V_f = f \times N$. In milling: $V_f = f_z \times N \times Z$. The extra factor **Z** (number of teeth) is the most common mistake students make in KTU exams, leading to wrong feed values.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Spindle Speed (N) for a Milling Cutter

The cutting speed $V_c$ is the peripheral speed of the cutter at its outer diameter. By definition, peripheral speed equals the circumference travelled per revolution, divided by time:

$$V_c = \frac{\pi \times D \times N}{1000}$$

where $D$ is in **mm**, $N$ is in **RPM (rev/min)**, and $V_c$ is in **m/min** (the 1000 converts mm to m).

Solving for N:

$$N = \frac{V_c \times 1000}{\pi \times D}$$

**Conversion Logic Step:** $V_c$ (m/min) is converted to mm/min by multiplying with 1000, then divided by the cutter circumference $\pi D$ (mm/rev) to obtain revolutions per minute.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Table Feed (V_f) and Material Removal Rate

Each tooth of the milling cutter removes a chip of width $f_z$ (feed per tooth). In one spindle revolution, $Z$ teeth pass the workpiece, so:

$$f = f_z \times Z \quad \text{(feed per revolution, mm/rev)}$$

The table advances at the rate:

$$V_f = f \times N = f_z \times Z \times N \quad \text{(mm/min)}$$

The cross-section of the chip removed is bounded by depth of cut $a_p$ and width of cut $a_e$ (engagement width). The volume swept per unit time gives MRR:

$$Z_{MRR} = a_p \times a_e \times V_f \quad \text{(mm}^3\text{/min)}$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Machining Time for a Rectangular Pocket

For a rectangular pocket of length $L$, width $W$, with a cutter of diameter $D$, the **number of passes** required is:

$$n_{passes} = \left\lceil \frac{W}{0.8 \times D} \right\rceil$$

(The factor 0.8 is the typical step-over ratio; the ceiling brackets $\lceil \, \rceil$ indicate rounding up to the next integer.)

The total tool path length per pass is:

$$L_{path} = L + D \quad \text{(length + cutter diameter for entry/exit)}$$

The total machining time per depth is:

$$T_{layer} = \frac{n_{passes} \times L_{path}}{V_f}$$

For a pocket of total depth $H$ removed in layers of $a_p$:

$$n_{layers} = \left\lceil \frac{H}{a_p} \right\rceil$$

Total time:

$$T_m = n_{layers} \times T_{layer}$$

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Pattern)

**Problem:** A plain milling cutter of diameter **D = 80 mm** and **Z = 8 teeth** is used to machine a mild-steel workpiece. The recommended cutting speed is **V_c = 25 m/min** and feed per tooth is **f_z = 0.15 mm/tooth**. The depth of cut is **a_p = 3 mm** and width of cut is **a_e = 60 mm**. The length of cut is **L = 200 mm**. Calculate (i) spindle speed N, (ii) table feed V_f, (iii) MRR, and (iv) machining time.

**Step (i) – Spindle Speed N:**

$$N = \frac{V_c \times 1000}{\pi \times D}$$

$$N = \frac{25 \times 1000}{\pi \times 80}$$

$$N = \frac{25000}{251.327}$$

$$\boxed{N \approx 99.47 \text{ RPM} \approx 100 \text{ RPM (set on machine)}}$$

**Step (ii) – Table Feed V_f:**

$$V_f = f_z \times N \times Z$$

$$V_f = 0.15 \times 99.47 \times 8$$

$$\boxed{V_f \approx 119.36 \text{ mm/min}}$$

**Step (iii) – Material Removal Rate Z_MRR:**

$$Z_{MRR} = a_p \times a_e \times V_f$$

$$Z_{MRR} = 3 \times 60 \times 119.36$$

$$\boxed{Z_{MRR} \approx 21485 \text{ mm}^3\text{/min} \approx 21.49 \text{ cm}^3\text{/min}}$$

**Step (iv) – Machining Time T_m** (assuming L_a ≈ D for approach = 80 mm):

$$T_m = \frac{L + L_a}{V_f} = \frac{200 + 80}{119.36}$$

$$\boxed{T_m \approx 2.345 \text{ min} \approx 2 \text{ min } 21 \text{ sec}}$$

### 3.5 Up-Milling vs Down-Milling Analytical Matrix

| **Parameter** | **Up-Milling (Conventional)** | **Down-Milling (Climb)** |
|---|---|---|
| Cutter rotation vs feed | Opposite direction | Same direction |
| Chip thickness | Starts thick, ends thin | Starts thin, ends thick |
| Tooth contact | Begins at minimum thickness | Begins at maximum thickness |
| Tool wear pattern | Uniform along tooth flank | Uniform — better tool life |
| Surface finish | Slightly better (rubbing effect) | Excellent (sharp cutting) |
| Holding force requirement | Pushes workpiece into fixture | Tends to lift workpiece — needs positive clamping |
| Recommended for | Rough milling, older machines | CNC machines, finish milling, rigid setups |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** A common error is to mark up-milling and down-milling as "the same" in diagrams. Always draw the **cutter rotation arrow** and the **table feed arrow** in opposite directions for up-milling and the *same* direction for down-milling. This single diagram fetches 2-3 marks consistently.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Horizontal Milling Machine

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PowSrc["Power Source 1.5 to 5 kW Motor"]:::power
    SpindleBox["Spindle Head with Speed Selector"]:::power
    Arbor["Arbor Holding Milling Cutter"]:::mech
    Cutter["Plain Cutter or Side Face Cutter Z teeth"]:::tool
    Table["Worktable with T Slots"]:::mech
    Knee["Knee and Saddle Assembly"]:::mech
    Bed["Column and Base Bed"]:::mech
```
