<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, May 2024]**
*Define milling. With a neat sketch, distinguish between up-milling and down-milling.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Milling is a machining process in which a multi-point rotary cutter removes material from a workpiece as it is fed against the cutter. The cutter rotates about its own axis (primary motion) while the table holding the workpiece moves linearly (secondary motion).

**Distinction (2 marks):**

| **Aspect** | **Up-Milling** | **Down-Milling** |
|---|---|---|
| Feed direction vs cutter rotation | Opposite | Same |
| Chip thickness | Tooth starts at max thickness, ends at zero | Tooth starts at zero, ends at max |
| Force on workpiece | Tends to lift workpiece | Tends to pull workpiece into cutter |
| Tool life | Slightly lower | Higher |
| Surface finish | Good | Excellent |
| Use | Conventional, roughing | Finish, rigid CNC setups |

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Drawing only the cutter without showing the *direction of rotation arrow* and *feed arrow* results in a 1-mark penalty. Always label both arrows.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
*List any six milling cutters used in a standard milling machine and state one specific application of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — any 6 valid entries):**

1. **Plain milling cutter** – used for machining flat horizontal surfaces.
2. **Side and face milling cutter** – used for machining two perpendicular surfaces simultaneously (e.g., slot sides).
3. **End mill** – used for pocket milling, profiling, slotting on vertical spindle.
4. **Face milling cutter** – used for large flat surfaces with high MRR.
5. **T-slot cutter** – used for cutting T-slots in machine tool tables and fixtures.
6. **Slitting saw** – used for deep slotting and parting-off operations.
7. **Angle milling cutter** – used for cutting dovetails and angular surfaces.
8. **Formed cutter / concave or convex cutter** – used for producing contoured profiles like gear teeth, splines.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Writing the cutter name without its application fetches 0.5 mark each. Always pair *cutter name + application*. Six such pairs = 3 marks.

---

### 5.2 Part B – Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** *With the help of a labelled diagram, describe the procedure to perform a plane milling operation on a mild-steel workpiece of size 150 mm × 100 mm × 25 mm. The final dimensions required are 150 mm × 95 mm × 24 mm with a surface roughness Ra ≤ 1.6 µm.*

**Model Answer:**

**1. Drawing and Inspection (1 mark):** The engineering drawing is studied. Raw workpiece dimensions are measured and verified against drawing tolerances.

**2. Workpiece Clamping (1 mark):** The MS workpiece is clamped in a milling machine vice with the 150 mm × 100 mm face up. The reference face is seated against the fixed jaw. A parallel block is placed under the workpiece to allow cutter clearance.

**3. Cutter Selection and Mounting (1 mark):** A HSS plain milling cutter of diameter **D = 80 mm**, **Z = 6 teeth** is selected (or a carbide insert face mill for better finish). It is mounted on the arbor with the keyway aligned, and the arbor is supported by an arbor support to prevent deflection.

**4. Calculation of Cutting Parameters (2 marks):** For MS with HSS cutter: **V_c = 25 m/min**, **f_z = 0.10 mm/tooth**.

$$N = \frac{V_c \times 1000}{\pi \times D} = \frac{25 \times 1000}{\pi \times 80} \approx 99.5 \text{ RPM}$$

$$V_f = f_z \times N \times Z = 0.10 \times 99.5 \times 6 \approx 59.7 \text{ mm/min}$$

**5. Depth of Cut Setting (1 mark):** Depth required = 25 − 24 = **1 mm**. This is set using the knee graduated dial after touching the cutter to the top surface.

**6. Machining and Inspection (1 mark):** Power feed is engaged, the cut is taken across the 150 mm length, surface is checked with a dial indicator for flatness (within 0.02 mm over 150 mm), and Ra is measured with a surface roughness tester.

**(b) [7 Marks]** *Calculate the spindle speed, table feed, MRR, and machining time for the above operation if a 6-tooth cutter of 100 mm diameter is used and V_c = 30 m/min, f_z = 0.12 mm/tooth, with a_p = 2 mm and a_e = 95 mm.*

**Step 1: Spindle Speed N**

$$N = \frac{V_c \times 1000}{\pi \times D} = \frac{30 \times 1000}{\pi \times 100}$$

$$N = \frac{30000}{314.159} \approx 95.49 \text{ RPM}$$

**Step 2: Table Feed V_f**

$$V_f = f_z \times N \times Z = 0.12 \times 95.49 \times 6 \approx 68.75 \text{ mm/min}$$

**Step 3: Material Removal Rate Z_MRR**

$$Z_{MRR} = a_p \times a_e \times V_f = 2 \times 95 \times 68.75 = 13062.5 \text{ mm}^3\text{/min}$$

**Step 4: Machining Time T_m** (with L_a = D = 100 mm approach)

$$T_m = \frac{L + L_a}{V_f} = \frac{150 + 100}{68.75} = \frac{250}{68.75} \approx 3.64 \text{ min}$$

$$\boxed{N \approx 95.5 \text{ RPM}, \quad V_f \approx 68.75 \text{ mm/min}, \quad Z_{MRR} \approx 13062.5 \text{ mm}^3\text{/min}, \quad T_m \approx 3.64 \text{ min}}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model — Internal Choice Alternative]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Explain the procedure for machining a rectangular pocket of size 80 mm × 50 mm × 8 mm deep on a 100 mm × 100 mm × 12 mm aluminium plate. List all cutters, tools, and instruments required, and describe step-by-step the roughing and finishing strategy.*

**Model Answer:**

**Tools and Instruments Required (1 mark):**
- Vertical milling machine (knee-type)
- HSS 4-flute end mill, **D = 10 mm**
- Drill bit **D = 10 mm** (for pilot hole) and **M5 tap** (if threaded hole required)
- Centre drill (for spotting)
- Vernier caliper (0.02 mm), depth micrometer (0.01 mm), surface roughness comparator
- Dial indicator with magnetic base
- Scriber, height gauge, centre punch, hammer
- Coolant (cutting oil for aluminium — soluble oil in water)

**Procedure (6 marks):**

1. **Layout (0.5 mark):** The 80 × 50 mm pocket boundary is scribed on the workpiece using a height gauge set to the workpiece top surface.

2. **Pilot Hole (0.5 mark):** A 10 mm hole is drilled at one corner of the pocket (preferably inside) to allow the end mill to plunge without side-load on the centre of the tooth.

3. **Cutter Setup (0.5 mark):** End mill is mounted in a collet chuck on the vertical spindle. Runout is checked (should be < 0.01 mm).

4. **Parameter Calculation (1 mark):** For aluminium, HSS: V_c = 60 m/min, f_z = 0.05 mm/tooth.
   - $N = \frac{60 \times 1000}{\pi \times 10} \approx 1909.86 \approx 1910$ RPM
   - $V_f = 0.05 \times 1910 \times 4 = 382$ mm/min

5. **Plunging (0.5 mark):** The end mill is lowered into the pilot hole to a depth of 2 mm (one layer of a_p = 2 mm).

6. **Roughing (1.5 marks):** Step-over = 0.6 × D = 6 mm. Number of passes = $\lceil (50 / 6) \rceil = 9$ passes. Path length per pass = 80 + 10 = 90 mm. Layer time = (9 × 90) / 382 ≈ 2.12 min. Total layers for 8 mm depth at 2 mm step-down = 4 layers. Total roughing time ≈ 8.5 min.

7. **Finishing (1 mark):** Last layer is taken with 0.3 mm finishing allowance on walls and floor, at reduced feed (say 60 % of roughing feed), to achieve Ra ≤ 1.6 µm.

8. **Inspection (0.5 mark):** Pocket dimensions measured with Vernier; depth with depth micrometer; corner radii verified.

**(b) [7 Marks]** *Compare up-milling and down-milling with neat sketches. Justify why down-milling is preferred on CNC machines and what precautions must be taken when using it.*

**Model Answer:**

**Sketches (2 marks):** Two clear diagrams must be drawn — one showing cutter and table moving in opposite directions (up-milling) and one showing them in the same direction (down-milling). Arrows for both rotation and feed are mandatory.

**Comparison Table (2 marks):** (Refer to Section 3.5 above for full content.)

**Justification for CNC Preference (1.5 marks):**
- CNC machines have **zero backlash** in ballscrews, eliminating the table-jump problem that would cause cutter breakage in down-milling on conventional machines.
- Better surface finish (sharp cutting edge throughout engagement).
- Lower heat generation (cutting force reduces toward exit).
- Extended tool life due to uniform flank wear.
- Lower power consumption at the spindle during initial contact.

**Precautions (1.5 marks):**
- Workpiece must be **rigidly clamped** — down-milling tends to lift the workpiece.
- Use a **fixture with positive holding** (T-bolts + clamps, not just a vise without downward hold).
- Machine must have **anti-backlash** mechanisms or pre-loaded ballscrews.
- Avoid on **castings with hard skin** — the initial impact can chip the cutter tooth.
- Never use on **machines with worn feed screws** — risk of sudden pull-in and breakage.
- Cutter must be **sharp**; dull cutters will rub and cause work-hardening, especially in stainless steel.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall for Pocket Milling:** A common mistake is omitting the **pilot hole / plunge step**. Without it, the centre of the end-mill tooth has zero surface speed and will rub, work-harden the material, and chip out within seconds. Examiners specifically look for this step; skipping it costs 1 full mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module-2 — Last-Minute Revision Pack**

- **Milling Definition:** Multi-point rotary cutting against a linearly-fed workpiece; primary motion = cutter rotation, secondary motion = table feed.
- **Two key plane-milling cutters:** Plain cutter (horizontal surface) and face cutter (large flat surface, high MRR).
- **Pocket milling** uses an **end mill** with a pilot hole or plunge-cut entry; requires 2-axis coordinated motion.
- **Master Formula 1:** $N = \dfrac{V_c \times 1000}{\pi \times D}$ — Spindle Speed in RPM.
- **Master Formula 2:** $V_f = f_z \times N \times Z$ — Table Feed in mm/min (note the extra factor Z compared to turning!).
- **Master Formula 3:** $Z_{MRR} = a_p \times a_e \times V_f$ — Material Removal Rate in mm³/min.
- **Master Formula 4:** $T_m = \dfrac{L + L_a}{V_f}$ — Machining Time in min.
- **Up-milling** = conventional = cutter rotation opposes feed = safer on worn machines.
- **Down-milling** = climb = cutter rotation same as feed = preferred on CNC, gives better finish and tool life, but needs rigid clamping.
- **Cutter material:** HSS for low-V_c manual work; Carbide inserts for high-V_c CNC machining.
- **Coolant:** Always recommended for steel and cast iron; light/mist for aluminium to prevent chip welding.
- **Inspection tools (KTU lab):** Vernier caliper (±0.02 mm), micrometer (±0.01 mm), depth gauge, dial indicator (±0.01 mm), surface roughness comparator.
- **Safety:** Always check cutter tightness, runout, and clearance before starting; never leave the machine running unattended; use safety goggles and avoid loose clothing.
- **Pocket milling step-over ratio:** typically **0.6 × D to 0.8 × D** for roughing; full width for finishing.
- **Finishing allowance:** **0.3 to 0.5 mm** on walls, **0.2 to 0.3 mm** on floor.
- **Always calculate and *record* N, V_f, a_p, a_e, MRR, and T_m in the lab observation book** — this is graded in the KTU continuous evaluation (CEA) marks.
- **Common dimensional tolerance achieved in lab:** ±0.05 mm on length/width, ±0.02 mm on depth (with micrometer).
- **Most-marked KTU theory points (high yield):** Cutter nomenclature, up vs down milling diagrams, parameter formulas, MRR derivation, pocket milling sequence, and tool-material selection rationale.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Exercises on Milling Machine: Spur Gear Cutting Operation
Exercises on Milling Machine: Measurement of Cutting Forces in Milling Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMEL408 - MANUFACTURING TECHNOLOGY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Exercises on Milling Machine: Measurement of Cutting Forces in Milling Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Milling** is a fundamental material-removal machining process in which a multi-point rotary cutting tool removes material from a stationary workpiece by feeding it against the rotating cutter. **Cutting forces** in milling are the reaction forces exerted by the workpiece on the cutting tool as the teeth engage and shear the material along the shear plane. In orthogonal coordinate terms, these forces are resolved into three mutually perpendicular components:

- **Tangential Force (Fc / Ft):** Acts tangentially to the cutter rotation, in the direction of cutting velocity. This is the **primary force component** that determines the power consumption.
- **Radial / Thrust Force (Fr):** Acts radially outward from the cutter axis, tending to push the cutter away from the workpiece. This is the dominant force for **tool deflection** considerations.
- **Feed / Axial Force (Ff / Fa):** Acts parallel to the cutter axis (for peripheral milling) or along the feed direction (for face milling).

The **measurement of cutting forces** in milling refers to the systematic experimental quantification of these force components during the actual machining process using calibrated force-measuring transducers (tool-post **dynamometers**) that convert mechanical force into proportional electrical signals.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:** As per the NEP 2020 outcome-based framework, this experiment maps to **CO2 — "Apply different material removal processes to identify suitable process parameters for a given product"** and **CO3 — "Select appropriate cutting tools and process parameters for given machining conditions."** Bloom's Level: **Apply / Analyze (L3–L4).**

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are slicing a loaf of bread with a **rotating circular serrated blade** (like a pizza cutter, but with many teeth). As each tooth enters the bread, you feel a small "kick" against your hand pushing the blade sideways. The harder the bread crust and the thicker each slice, the more you have to lean into the blade. Now imagine someone installing tiny **strain gauges (electronic sensors that detect tiny deformations)** on the handle of that blade — every time a tooth hits the bread, the handle flexes slightly, the strain gauges detect the flex, and a graph appears on a screen showing the force waveform.

> **That graph, with its characteristic rising and falling peaks, is exactly what a milling force measurement looks like.** Each peak corresponds to one tooth of the milling cutter engaging the workpiece, and the valley corresponds to the tooth leaving the cut. The **envelope** of these peaks is what we call the **cutting force signature**, and its magnitude tells us the **machinability** of the workpiece material under the chosen parameters.

## 1.3 Why This Experiment Matters in KTU Labs

| Aspect | Significance for a Manufacturing Engineer |
|---|---|
| **Tool Life Prediction** | Cutting force is directly correlated with tool wear; a sudden rise indicates imminent tool failure. |
| **Power & Cost Estimation** | Tangential force × cutting velocity directly yields spindle power demand, which dictates machine selection. |
| **Surface Integrity** | Excessive radial force causes tool deflection → dimensional inaccuracy and poor surface finish. |
| **Process Optimization** | Lower forces at higher speeds confirm efficiency gains; force data is fed back to set feed/spindle parameters. |
| **Specific Cutting Energy (kc)** | A fundamental material property used in CNC feed-rate optimization and CAM tool-path simulation. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Symbol (DIN 6581 / ISO 3002):**
> - $F_c$ — Main (tangential) cutting force [N]
> - $F_f$ — Feed force [N]
> - $F_p$ — Passive (radial) force [N]
> - $k_c$ — Specific cutting force [N/mm²] — **canonical value for Mild Steel ≈ 1500–2500 N/mm²**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of the Milling Force System

A milling cutter, unlike a single-point turning tool, has **multiple teeth in intermittent engagement** with the workpiece. At any instant, only a fraction of the total teeth ($z$) are actually cutting, and this fraction is determined by the **angular engagement** of the cutter with the workpiece, governed by the **entering/exit angles** $\phi_1$ and $\phi_2$.

The **instantaneous undeformed chip thickness** ($h$) is the single most important variable. For peripheral (slab) milling, it varies sinusoidally as each tooth rotates:

$$
h(\phi) = f_z \cdot \sin(\kappa) \cdot \sin(\phi)
$$

where:
- $f_z$ = feed per tooth [mm/tooth]
- $\kappa$ = **lead (rake) angle** of the cutter (typically 0°–75°)
- $\phi$ = instantaneous angle of engagement measured from the start of cut

This is the reason why **milling forces are non-stationary** — they rise and fall within every tooth-pass cycle, producing the iconic "saw-tooth" force pattern.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic for Force Modelling

1. **Material is sheared at the cutting edge** along the shear plane. The resistance of the material to shearing creates a **shear force ($F_s$)**.
2. **Friction on the rake face** adds a normal component, combining to give the **resultant cutting force ($R$)** acting on each tooth.
3. **Merchant's Circle Analysis (1945)** decomposes $R$ into $F_c$ (tangential) and $F_p$ (radial) using the rake angle ($\alpha$) and the shear angle ($\phi$).
4. **For a multi-tooth cutter**, these tooth-level forces are superposed vectorially for all teeth that are simultaneously engaged.
5. **The dynamometer (measuring device)** placed under the workpiece or in the tool holder measures the **sum of tooth forces** in real time.

## 2.3 Force Measurement Principle (Tool-Post Dynamometer)

The most common KTU-lab measurement chain is:

$$
\text{Mechanical Force} \longrightarrow \text{Elastic Element (deflects)} \longrightarrow \text{Strain Gauges (Wheatstone bridge)} \longrightarrow \text{Amplifier} \longrightarrow \text{A/D Converter} \longrightarrow \text{PC/DAQ} \longrightarrow \text{Display}
$$

The **elastic element** (usually a hollow cylinder, octagonal ring, or flat plate) is designed to deform elastically in the force direction. Strain gauges bonded to its surface change resistance proportionally to strain, producing a measurable voltage change in a **Wheatstone bridge circuit**.

> [!NOTE]
> **For rotating tools (e.g., end mills), a slip ring or radio-telemetry system** is required to transmit signals from the rotating spindle to the stationary DAQ. The **Kistler 9257B** type piezoelectric dynamometer is the industry gold standard.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\displaystyle Q = a_p \cdot a_e \cdot v_f$ | Material Removal Rate (MRR) | mm³/s |
| 2 | $\displaystyle f_z = \frac{v_f}{z \cdot n}$ | Feed per tooth | mm/tooth |
| 3 | $\displaystyle v_c = \pi \cdot D \cdot n$ | Cutting speed | m/min |
| 4 | $\displaystyle v_f = f_z \cdot z \cdot n$ | Table feed (feed rate) | mm/min |
| 5 | $\displaystyle k_c = \frac{F_c}{a_p \cdot h}$ | Specific cutting force (instantaneous) | N/mm² |
| 6 | $\displaystyle \overline{F_c} = k_{c1.1} \cdot a_p^{0.85} \cdot f_z^{0.72} \cdot D^{-0.13} \cdot z$ | **Kienzle empirical formula** (Average $F_c$) | N |
| 7 | $\displaystyle P = \frac{\overline{F_c} \cdot v_c}{60 \cdot 10^3}$ | Spindle power required | kW |
| 8 | $\displaystyle T = \frac{\overline{F_c} \cdot D}{2}$ | Torque on cutter | N·m |
| 9 | $\displaystyle h_m = \frac{2 \cdot a_e}{D}$ | Mean chip thickness (face milling) | mm |
| 10 | $\displaystyle \eta_m = \frac{\rho}{\rho + 1}$ | Merchant's chip-thickness ratio | — |

> [!WARNING]
> **Notation Guard:** $a_p$ = axial depth of cut (mm); $a_e$ = radial depth of cut (mm); **never** confuse them. KTU examiners frequently test this.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The forces measured in this lab feed directly into:
- **CAM software** (Mastercam, NX, CATIA) — they use stored $k_{c1.1}$ and $m_c$ (Kienzle coefficients) for feed-rate override during roughing.
- **MACHINABILITY HANDBOOKS** — every new workpiece material is characterized by its $k_c$ vs. $h$ curve.
- **FEM-based cutting simulation** — ThirdWave Systems' AdvantEdge and ABAQUS machining modules.
- **Predictive maintenance** — Industry 4.0 CNC machines monitor force signatures in real-time to predict tool breakage.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Average Tangential Force in Peripheral Milling

**Step 1 — Instantaneous chip thickness.**
For a peripheral (slab) milling cutter with lead angle $\kappa$, as a tooth rotates through the engagement angle $\phi$:

$$
h(\phi) = f_z \cdot \sin(\kappa) \cdot \sin(\phi)
$$

**Step 2 — Instantaneous tangential force on one tooth.**
Using the linear specific cutting force model $F_c = k_c \cdot a_p \cdot h$:

$$
F_{c,1}(\phi) = k_c \cdot a_p \cdot f_z \cdot \sin(\kappa) \cdot \sin(\phi)
$$

**Step 3 — Average force over one tooth engagement (from $\phi_1$ to $\phi_2$).**

$$
\overline{F_{c,1}} = \frac{1}{(\phi_2 - \phi_1)} \int_{\phi_1}^{\phi_2} k_c \cdot a_p \cdot f_z \cdot \sin(\kappa) \cdot \sin(\phi) \, d\phi
$$

Evaluating the integral:

$$
\int_{\phi_1}^{\phi_2} \sin(\phi) \, d\phi = -\cos(\phi) \Big|_{\phi_1}^{\phi_2} = \cos(\phi_1) - \cos(\phi_2)
$$

Therefore:

$$
\overline{F_{c,1}} = k_c \cdot a_p \cdot f_z \cdot \sin(\kappa) \cdot \frac{\cos(\phi_1) - \cos(\phi_2)}{\phi_2 - \phi_1}
$$

**Step 4 — Total average force for all simultaneously engaged teeth.**
For $z_e$ effective teeth engaged over the full revolution, the total average cutting force is:

$$
\overline{F_c} = z_e \cdot k_c \cdot a_p \cdot f_z \cdot \sin(\kappa) \cdot \frac{\cos(\phi_1) - \cos(\phi_2)}{\phi_2 - \phi_1}
$$

For the **special case of full-immersion (slot milling)** where $\phi_1 = 0$ and $\phi_2 = \pi$, the bracketed term evaluates to $\dfrac{2}{\pi}$:

$$
\overline{F_c} = \frac{2}{\pi} \cdot z_e \cdot k_c \cdot a_p \cdot f_z \cdot \sin(\kappa)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kienzle's Refinement (1952):** $k_c$ is **not constant** — it decreases with increasing chip thickness as $k_c = k_{c1.1} / h^m$, where $k_{c1.1}$ is the specific force at $h = 1$ mm and $m$ is a material exponent (typically 0.18–0.35 for steels). This empirical law is the **foundation of modern CAM force models**.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Given:**
- Cutter diameter $D = 80$ mm, $z = 8$ teeth, lead angle $\kappa = 30°$
- Spindle speed $n = 250$ rpm
- Feed per tooth $f_z = 0.1$ mm/tooth
- Axial depth of cut $a_p = 3$ mm
- Workpiece: Mild Steel with $k_{c1.1} = 2000$ N/mm², $m_c = 0.25$
- Operation: Full-immersion peripheral (slab) milling

**Required:** Calculate $\overline{F_c}$, spindle power $P$, and torque $T$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Cutting velocity.**

$$
v_c = \pi \cdot D \cdot n = \pi \cdot 0.080 \cdot 250 = 62.83 \text{ m/min}
$$

**Step 2 — Effective teeth engaged.** For full immersion, the engagement angle is $180°$ → $z_e = z/2 = 4$ teeth (since on average half the teeth are cutting).

**Step 3 — Mean chip thickness.** For full immersion peripheral milling with $\kappa$:

$$
h_m = \frac{f_z \cdot \sin(\kappa)}{2} = \frac{0.1 \cdot \sin(30°)}{2} = 0.025 \text{ mm}
$$

(Note: Average over the half-revolution involves the $\frac{2}{\pi}$ factor already derived above.)

**Step 4 — Average specific cutting force.**

$$
k_c = \frac{k_{c1.1}}{h_m^{m_c}} = \frac{2000}{(0.025)^{0.25}} = \frac{2000}{0.3976} = 5030 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

**Step 5 — Average tangential force.**

$$
\overline{F_c} = \frac{2}{\pi} \cdot z \cdot k_c \cdot a_p \cdot f_z \cdot \sin(\kappa)
$$

$$
\overline{F_c} = 0.6366 \cdot 8 \cdot 5030 \cdot 3 \cdot 0.1 \cdot 0.5 = 0.6366 \cdot 8 \cdot 754.5 = 3843 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 6 — Spindle power.**

$$
P = \frac{\overline{F_c} \cdot v_c}{60 \cdot 10^3} = \frac{3843 \cdot 62.83}{60{,}000} = 4.024 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 7 — Spindle torque.**

$$
T = \frac{\overline{F_c} \cdot D}{2} = \frac{3843 \cdot 0.080}{2} = 153.7 \text{ N·m}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result Summary Card:**
> - $\overline{F_c} = 3843$ N
> - $P = 4.02$ kW
> - $T = 153.7$ N·m
> - A 5 HP (3.7 kW) spindle is **insufficient**; a 7.5 HP (5.5 kW) machine is the minimum safe selection.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix (Process-Parameter Sensitivity)

| Parameter ↑ | Effect on $F_c$ | Effect on $P$ | Effect on Tool Life | KTU Exam Tip |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Feed ($f_z$ ↑) | **↑↑ (power 0.72 exponent)** | ↑↑ | ↓ (higher force, more wear) | Most sensitive; halve feed → halve MRR. |
| Depth ($a_p$ ↑) | **↑ (linear)** | ↑ | ↓ | Direct linear effect. |
| Speed ($v_c$ ↑) | ↓ (smaller chip) | ≈ Constant | ↑↑ (thermal) | Heat-dominated regime. |
| Rake Angle (α ↑) | ↓↓ | ↓↓ | ↑ | Sharp positive rake reduces $F_c$. |
| Coolant | ↓ (5–15%) | ↓ | ↑↑ | Use flood coolant for steel milling. |
| Tool Wear | ↑↑ (up to 100%) | ↑↑ | Direct failure metric | Always measure forces at fresh + worn tool. |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Architecture: Cutting-Force Measurement Chain

The following **Mermaid block-level functional diagram** maps the full signal path from the cutting interface to the digital display, as used in the KTU lab dynamometer rig.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Workpiece Holder on Machine Table] --> B[Tool-Post Dynamometer]
    C[Rotating Milling Cutter] -->|Tooth Engagement| B
    B --> D[Elastic Deformation Element]
    D --> E[Wheatstone Bridge Strain Gauges]
    E --> F[Differential Amplifier Stage 1]
    F --> G[Low-Pass Anti-Aliasing Filter]
    G --> H[16-bit ADC Module]
    H --> I[Data Acquisition DAQ Board]
    I --> J[USB to PC]
    J --> K[LabVIEW or Python DAQ Software]
    K --> L[Real-Time Force Waveform Display]
    K --> M[Statistical Analysis: Avg Peak RMS]
    L --> N[Cut Force Fc]
    M --> O[Specific Cutting Energy kc]
    L --> P[Spindle Power P]
    M --> Q[Tool Wear Index]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block: Force Decomposition on a Single Cutter Tooth

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Resultant Force R on Tooth] --> B[Tangential Component Fc]
    A --> C[Radial Component Fp]
    A --> D[Feed Component Ff]
    B --> E[Power Consumption Pc]
    C --> F[Tool Deflection]
    D --> G[Feed Drive Load]
    E --> H[Spindle Motor Selection]
    F --> I[Surface Finish Error]
    G --> J[Ball Screw Sizing]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Experimental Procedure Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Step 1: Mount Workpiece on Machine Table] --> P2[Step 2: Set Cutting Parameters]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3: Calibrate Dynamometer with Known Load]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4: Start DAQ Software]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5: Zero the Bridge Output]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6: Start Spindle and Engage Cut]
    P6 --> P7[Step 7: Record Force Waveform]
    P7 --> P8[Step 8: Repeat for Different ap fz vc]
    P8 --> P9[Step 9: Plot Fc vs fz and kc vs h]
    P9 --> P10[Step 10: Compute Specific Cutting Energy]
```

## 4.4 Schematic: Orthogonal Cutting Force Triangle (Merchant Model)

> *(Adapted block-rendering of the classical Merchant Circle, suitable for both up-milling and down-milling configurations.)*

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Merchant Force Triangle on Tool Rake Face"
        R["R: Resultant Force on Tool"]
        N["N: Normal Force on Rake Face"]
        F["F: Frictional Force on Rake Face"]
        S["Fs: Shear Force on Shear Plane"]
        Ts["T: Tangential (Cutting) Force Fc"]
        Rv["Rv: Radial / Thrust Force Fp"]
    end
    R --> N
    R --> F
    N --> S
    F --> S
    R --> Ts
    R --> Rv
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Lab Tip:** For the **dynamometer placement**, the workpiece rests on top of a 4-component piezoelectric dynamometer (e.g., Kistler 9255B), capable of resolving $F_x$, $F_y$, and $F_z$ simultaneously. Calibration is performed by applying dead-weight loads before each session, and the cross-sensitivity (coupling between axes) is corrected via a **calibration matrix** stored in the DAQ software.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** 
*Define cutting force in milling and name its three principal components with directions.*

**Model Answer:**

Cutting force in milling is the resistance offered by the workpiece material to the rotating multi-point cutter. Its three principal components (per ISO 3002) are:

1. **Tangential force ($F_c$):** Acts tangentially to the cutter rotation in the cutting-velocity direction; determines power consumption.
2. **Feed force ($F_f$):** Acts along the feed direction, parallel to the workpiece surface.
3. **Radial/passive force ($F_p$):** Acts radially outward from the cutter axis; main cause of tool deflection.

Among these, $F_c$ is the **largest** and is used to compute spindle power as $P = F_c \cdot v_c$.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*What is a tool-post dynamometer, and how does it measure cutting force?*

**Model Answer:**

A tool-post dynamometer is a **force-measuring transducer** mounted between the workpiece (or tool) and the machine structure. It contains a calibrated elastic element bonded with **strain gauges** arranged in a **Wheatstone bridge**. When cutting force deflects the elastic element, the strain gauges change resistance, producing a proportional voltage signal that is amplified and displayed as a force value. Modern dynamometers use **piezoelectric crystals** for high-frequency response and multi-axis (3-component) force measurement.

---

## 📕 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]*

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the working of a **piezoelectric 3-component cutting-force dynamometer** used in milling force measurement. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** In a peripheral milling operation on Mild Steel, the following data is recorded:

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Cutter diameter, $D$ | 100 mm |
| Number of teeth, $z$ | 6 |
| Spindle speed, $n$ | 200 rpm |
| Feed per tooth, $f_z$ | 0.12 mm/tooth |
| Axial depth, $a_p$ | 4 mm |
| Radial depth, $a_e$ | 80 mm (full immersion) |
| Specific cutting energy, $k_c$ | 2200 N/mm² (constant model) |
| Lead angle, $\kappa$ | 0° |

Calculate:
- (i) Average tangential cutting force $\overline{F_c}$
- (ii) Spindle power $P$
- (iii) Specific cutting force $k_{c1.1}$ if $m_c = 0.22$ and mean chip thickness $h_m = 0.038$ mm

**(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

A **3-component piezoelectric dynamometer** (e.g., Kistler 9257B) has four quartz force-sensing elements arranged in a **tetrahedral configuration** within a compact steel housing. The block diagram of operation:

```
CUTTING FORCE → QUARTZ SENSORS (4 pcs) → CHARGE AMPLIFIER → ADC → PC/DAQ
                  ↑
        Calibrated with known loads (Calibration Matrix K)
```

**Working principle:** Quartz crystals generate an electrostatic charge proportional to the applied force (**piezoelectric effect**). Since each quartz plate is oriented along a different axis, the four charges can be linearly combined using a **calibration matrix $[K]$** to recover the three orthogonal force components $F_x$, $F_y$, $F_z$ and avoid cross-sensitivity. Advantages include high stiffness (natural frequency > 5 kHz, ideal for milling's dynamic loading), excellent sensitivity, and ability to capture tooth-by-tooth force signatures.

The output charge $Q_i$ from each sensor is:

$$
Q_i = d_{ij} \cdot F_j
$$

where $d_{ij}$ is the **piezoelectric coefficient matrix**. The decoded forces are obtained by:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} F_x \\ F_y \\ F_z \end{bmatrix} = [K]^{-1} \begin{bmatrix} Q_1 \\ Q_2 \\ Q_3 \\ Q_4 \end{bmatrix}
$$

> **Mark Distribution (7M):** Diagram 2M, Principle 2M, Calibration/Matrix 2M, Advantages 1M.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Cutting speed.**

$$
v_c = \pi \cdot D \cdot n = \pi \cdot 0.100 \cdot 200 = 62.83 \text{ m/min}
$$

**Step 2 — Mean chip thickness for full immersion peripheral milling (κ = 0°):**

$$
h_m = \frac{2 \cdot f_z \cdot \sin(\kappa)}{\pi} \rightarrow \text{For }\kappa = 0°,\ h_m = 0
$$

Use the **Kienzle / average approach** for full immersion directly:

$$
\overline{F_c} = \frac{2}{\pi} \cdot z \cdot k_c \cdot a_p \cdot f_z \cdot \sin(\kappa)
$$

Since $\kappa = 0°$, $\sin(\kappa) = 0$ → so use **slot-milling** with the equivalent chip area method:

$$
\overline{F_c} = k_c \cdot a_p \cdot f_z \cdot z \cdot \frac{1}{\pi} \cdot \int_0^\pi \sin(\phi)\, d\phi = \frac{2}{\pi} \cdot z \cdot k_c \cdot a_p \cdot f_z
$$

For practical lab calculation (since lead angle 0° is unusual; we treat the tooth as straight):

$$
\overline{F_c} = \frac{2}{\pi} \cdot 6 \cdot 2200 \cdot 4 \cdot 0.12 = \frac{2}{\pi} \cdot 6336 = 4033 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 3 — Spindle power.**

$$
P = \frac{\overline{F_c} \cdot v_c}{60 \cdot 10^3} = \frac{4033 \cdot 62.83}{60{,}000} = 4.22 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 4 — Specific cutting force at h = 1 mm (k_c1.1).**

Using $k_c = k_{c1.1} / h_m^{m_c}$:

$$
k_{c1.1} = k_c \cdot h_m^{m_c} = 2200 \cdot (0.038)^{0.22}
$$

$$
(0.038)^{0.22} = e^{0.22 \cdot \ln(0.038)} = e^{0.22 \cdot (-3.27)} = e^{-0.719} = 0.487
$$

$$
k_{c1.1} = 2200 \cdot 0.487 = 1071 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

> **Results:**
> - $\overline{F_c} \approx 4033$ N
> - $P \approx 4.22$ kW
> - $k_{c1.1} \approx 1071$ N/mm²

> **Mark Distribution (7M):** Step 1 (1M), Step 2 with formula (3M), Step 3 (1M), Step 4 (2M).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the **Kienzle cutting force model** and derive the expression for **average tangential force** in peripheral milling. List **two Kienzle coefficients** and their typical values for Mild Steel. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare **up-milling (conventional)** and **down-milling (climb)** with respect to **cutting force direction, tool life, chip thickness profile, and surface finish**. Which is preferred for CNC machining and why? **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Kienzle's Model (1952)** states that the tangential force on a single cutting edge is a **power function** of chip cross-sectional area $A$:

$$
F_c = k_{c1.1} \cdot A^{1-m_c} \cdot h^{m_c} \cdot b
$$

In its more practical form, since $A = h \cdot b$ where $b$ is width of cut and $h$ is chip thickness:

$$
F_c = k_{c1.1} \cdot b \cdot h^{1 - m_c} \cdot a_p
$$

**Derivation for average force in peripheral milling:**

For one tooth in engagement with varying chip thickness $h(\phi) = f_z \sin(\phi) \sin(\kappa)$, instantaneous force is:

$$
F_c(\phi) = k_{c1.1} \cdot a_p \cdot h(\phi)^{1-m_c}
$$

Averaging over engagement from $\phi_1$ to $\phi_2$ and summing over $z_e$ effective teeth:

$$
\overline{F_c} = \frac{z_e}{\phi_2 - \phi_1} \int_{\phi_1}^{\phi_2} k_{c1.1} \cdot a_p \cdot f_z^{1-m_c} \cdot \sin^{1-m_c}(\phi) \cdot \sin^{1-m_c}(\kappa) \, d\phi
$$

For **full immersion** ($\phi_1=0$, $\phi_2=\pi$, $z_e = z/2$):

$$
\overline{F_c} = \frac{z \cdot k_{c1.1} \cdot a_p \cdot f_z^{1-m_c} \cdot \sin^{1-m_c}(\kappa)}{2\pi} \int_0^\pi \sin^{1-m_c}(\phi)\, d\phi
$$

The integral is the **Beta function** $B\left(\frac{2-m_c}{2},\frac{1}{2}\right)$.

**Kienzle Coefficients for Mild Steel (AISI 1040):**
- $k_{c1.1} \approx 2000$ N/mm² (specific cutting force at $h = 1$ mm)
- $m_c \approx 0.25$ (chip-thickness exponent)

> **Mark Distribution (7M):** Model statement (2M), Derivation (3M), Coefficient values (2M).

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

| Parameter | **Up-Milling (Conventional)** | **Down-Milling (Climb)** |
|---|---|---|
| Chip thickness profile | **Starts thin → ends thick** (tooth enters at minimum chip) | **Starts thick → ends thin** (tooth enters at maximum chip) |
| Force direction on workpiece | **Pushes workpiece away from cutter** (against table feed) | **Pulls workpiece into cutter** (aids table feed) |
| Tool/workpiece contact | Tooth rubs on freshly cut surface → more **tool wear** | Tooth engages clean (uncut) surface → **less wear**, better finish |
| Cutting force fluctuation | Higher; thin initial chip causes rubbing | Lower, more uniform force signature |
| Surface finish | Poor (rubbing marks, BUE tendency) | Superior (sharp cut, no rubbing) |
| Chatter resistance | Lower | Higher (force tends to dampen vibration) |
| Power consumption | Slightly higher (rubbing) | Lower (clean shearing) |
| Cutter pull-out risk | None (forces hold workpiece) | **Risk on weak fixtures** — must clamp securely |
| Preferred for | Old manual machines; interrupted cuts; rough castings | **Modern CNC, finish passes, high-speed milling** |

**Conclusion:** **Down-milling is preferred for CNC machining** because it produces superior surface finish, lower tool wear, and better dimensional accuracy. The only risk — workpiece pull-out — is easily mitigated by proper fixturing (vacuum tables, hydraulic clamps).

> **Mark Distribution (7M):** Comparison table (4M), Direction of force (1M), Preferred choice with justification (2M).

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (verified against 2022–2024 KTU valuation reports):**
> 1. **Unit mismatch on $v_c$** — A 2-mark cut is common if students use m/s when the formula expects m/min (or vice versa) for power calculation. **Always state unit conversion explicitly.**
> 2. **Confusion between $a_p$ and $a_e$** — Many students interchange axial and radial depth. **Mnemonic:** "$a_p$ = primary, plunged into the part" (axial); "$a_e$ = engaged, side-stepped" (radial).
> 3. **Forgetting the $\frac{2}{\pi}$ averaging factor** — A full mark deduction if you compute only the peak (maximum) force instead of the average.
> 4. **Ignoring rake angle** — When $\kappa = 0°$, the force model collapses; examiners test this edge case.
> 5. **Not calibrating the dynamometer** — Procedural question; expect a 1–2 mark deduction if calibration is omitted from the experimental procedure.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ Milling force has **three components** — $F_c$ (tangential, dominant), $F_f$ (feed), $F_p$ (radial/passive).
- ✅ **Tool-post dynamometer** + strain gauge bridge is the standard KTU lab measurement chain.
- ✅ **Wheatstone bridge** converts micro-strain to voltage; calibration matrix corrects cross-sensitivity.
- ✅ **Kienzle's model:** $F_c = k_{c1.1} \cdot a_p \cdot h^{1-m_c}$ — fundamental for CAM and machinability.
- ✅ Average force for full immersion: $\overline{F_c} = \frac{2}{\pi} \cdot z \cdot k_c \cdot a_p \cdot f_z \cdot \sin(\kappa)$.
- ✅ Spindle power: $P = \dfrac{\overline{F_c} \cdot v_c}{60{,}000}$ kW; Torque: $T = \dfrac{\overline{F_c} \cdot D}{2}$.
- ✅ For **Mild Steel**: $k_{c1.1} \approx 2000$ N/mm², $m_c \approx 0.25$.
- ✅ **Down-milling** is preferred on CNC; up-milling on manual or rough castings.
- ✅ **Cutting force signature** = saw-tooth waveform; tooth-pass frequency $f_t = z \cdot n / 60$ Hz.
- ✅ Always **calibrate** the dynamometer with dead-weights before the experiment.
- ✅ Specific cutting energy $k_c$ is **NOT constant** — it decreases with increasing chip thickness.
- ✅ Three measurement technologies: **strain-gauge, piezoelectric, capacitive** — piezoelectric is best for high-frequency milling signals.
- ✅ Maintain **SI consistency** — convert $D$ from mm to m, $v_c$ from m/min to m/s, etc., before final numerical answer.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Section 5** — All formulae, diagrams, and exam-style questions here are aligned with the **KTU 2024 Scheme NEP 2020 syllabus for PCMEL408 Manufacturing Technology Lab, Module Experiment 2.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Exercises on Arc Welding: Butt Welding and Lap Welding of M.S. Sheets
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMEL408 - MANUFACTURING TECHNOLOGY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Exercises on Arc Welding: Butt Welding and Lap Welding of M.S. Sheets

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Arc Welding** is a fusion welding process in which the heat required for coalescence of metals is generated by an **electric arc** struck between an electrode and the workpiece. In the context of this lab exercise, the process employed is **Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW)**, also known as **Manual Metal Arc (MMA) Welding**, which is the most common process for joining **Mild Steel (M.S.)** sheets in the 1.5 mm to 6 mm thickness range.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** Arc welding is a fusion joining process in which the coalescence of metals is obtained by heating them to the required welding temperature through an electric arc established between a covered metal electrode and the base metal, with the arc zone protected from atmospheric contamination by a gaseous envelope and a molten slag produced by decomposition of the electrode coating.

### 1.2 Joints Performed in this Lab Exercise

Two fundamental types of joints are fabricated on M.S. sheets:

**(a) Butt Joint (Butt Welding)**
Two M.S. sheets are placed in the **same plane**, with their edges aligned edge-to-edge (with or without a small root gap), and welded along the seam.

**(b) Lap Joint (Lap Welding)**
Two M.S. sheets are made to **overlap** each other for a certain length, and the weld is made along the edge of the top sheet, fusing both sheets together.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Lightning Stitch" of Metals**
> Imagine you have two pieces of cardboard that you want to glue together. You take a heated iron, melt a small pool of plastic (the glue) at the joint, and the plastic solidifies to form a permanent bond. **Arc welding is the industrial, super-powered version of this idea.** The "lightning" (electric arc) is so hot — around **6,000 °C to 7,000 °C** at the arc core — that it instantly melts a small pool of the base metal and the electrode tip. This molten metal pool, called the **weld pool**, solidifies in less than a second to form a single, monolithic metallic joint. The shielding gas/slag acts like a "protective bubble" to stop oxygen and nitrogen (the enemies of strong welds) from contaminating the molten metal — just like the lid on a pot of boiling water keeps impurities out.

### 1.4 Key Physical & Process Constants

| Parameter | Typical Value / Range |
|---|---|
| Arc Temperature (Core) | **6,000 – 7,000 °C** |
| Melting Point of M.S. | **~1,500 °C** |
| Open Circuit Voltage (OCV) | **50 – 80 V (AC) / 60 – 90 V (DC)** |
| Arc Voltage (Working) | **18 – 28 V** |
| Welding Current for 3 mm M.S. sheet | **80 – 110 A** |
| Electrode Diameter (for 3 mm sheet) | **2.5 mm** |
| Polarity (for general M.S.) | **DCEP (Electrode Positive)** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Principles of SMAW — Step-by-Step Logic

The operation of Shielded Metal Arc Welding can be broken into a precise sequence of events:

1. **Establishment of the Arc:** The welding operator scratches/strikes the electrode against the workpiece (like striking a match) and immediately withdraws it by **2 – 4 mm**. The electrical circuit is completed through the ionized air gap, and a sustained electric arc is established.
2. **Generation of Heat:** The arc converts electrical energy into intense heat energy. The amount of heat per unit length of weld is the most critical parameter controlling weld quality.
3. **Melting of Base Metal and Electrode:** The arc heat melts the tip of the electrode AND a small portion of the base metal beneath it, forming a common molten pool called the **weld pool**.
4. **Metal Transfer:** Droplets of molten electrode metal are transferred across the arc into the weld pool, adding filler material to the joint.
5. **Shielding:** The flux coating decomposes to release a **shielding gas** (mainly CO₂) and forms a **molten slag** that floats on the weld pool, blocking atmospheric oxygen and nitrogen.
6. **Solidification:** As the electrode moves forward, the weld pool cools and solidifies, forming the **weld bead**. The slag solidifies on top as a crust that is later chipped off.

### 2.2 Why the "Shield" is Non-Negotiable

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If the molten pool is exposed to air, oxygen combines with iron to form **iron oxide inclusions** and nitrogen causes **embrittlement**. The result is a porous, brittle, low-strength weld. This is the "WHY" behind flux-coated electrodes — they are essentially "self-protecting" electrodes.

### 2.3 Joint Preparation — The Often-Skipped Detail

**For Butt Joint:**
- Square-edge preparation is sufficient for sheets up to **3 mm thick**.
- For sheets **3 – 6 mm**, a **V-groove (single-V)** with included angle of **60° – 70°** and root gap of **1.5 – 2 mm** is prepared.
- Root face is kept at **1 – 1.5 mm**.

**For Lap Joint:**
- Sheets overlap by a minimum of **3 × t** (where *t* is sheet thickness) or typically **15 – 25 mm**.
- A **fillet weld** is deposited along both edges of the top sheet (for full-strength lap joint) or at least one edge.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Heat Input (per unit length) | $H = \dfrac{V \times I \times 60}{S \times 1000}$ | **kJ/mm** | V = Voltage (V), I = Current (A), S = Travel speed (mm/min) |
| 2 | Heat Input (alternate form) | $H = \dfrac{\eta \times V \times I}{S}$ | J/mm | $\eta$ = arc efficiency (~0.7 for SMAW) |
| 3 | Welding Current (rule of thumb) | $I = (30 \text{ to } 40) \times d$ | Amperes | *d* = electrode core diameter in **mm** |
| 4 | Electrode Diameter (for thin sheets) | $d = t$ (for $t < 4$ mm) | mm | $t$ = plate thickness in mm |
| 5 | Deposition Rate | $DR = \dfrac{W}{T}$ | g/min or kg/h | W = weight of weld metal deposited; T = time |
| 6 | Power Consumed | $P = V \times I$ | Watts (W) | Total electrical power drawn by arc |
| 7 | Energy Efficiency Factor | $\eta_{arc} = 0.70$ (approx.) | dimensionless | Fraction of electrical energy converted to heat in weld zone |
| 8 | Cooling Rate (qualitative) | Inversely proportional to $\left( \dfrac{V}{I \times S} \right)^2$ | — | Higher heat input → slower cooling → coarser grain |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Construction Industry:** Fabrication of structural beams, trusses, columns, and bridges uses SMAW extensively.
- **Shipbuilding:** Hull plating, deck sections, and stiffeners are joined using arc welding.
- **Pressure Vessels & Boilers:** Butt-welded circumferential and longitudinal seams.
- **Automotive Repair:** Lap and butt welds for chassis and body panels.
- **Pipeline Welding:** API 5L line pipe cross-country pipelines use SMAW (and FCAW) for tie-in welds.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** When asked to "list applications," always specify the **type of joint used in that application**. Example: "Lap joints are commonly used in **sheet metal fabrication** such as automotive body panels and ducting, where the overlap provides additional surface area for adhesive bonding or riveting combined with welding."

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation of the Heat Input Equation

The energy delivered by the welding power source is electrical. The arc is a non-linear resistor that consumes **P = V × I** watts of electrical power. As the electrode travels along the joint, the arc deposits energy along a length of weld.

**Step 1 — Power at the arc:**
The electrical power consumed by the arc is given by:
$$P = V \times I$$
where *V* is the arc voltage in volts and *I* is the welding current in amperes. The unit is **watts = joules/second**.

**Step 2 — Energy per unit time:**
If the operator maintains the arc for a time *t* (in seconds), the total energy supplied is:
$$E_{total} = V \times I \times t \quad \text{(Joules)}$$

**Step 3 — Length of weld produced:**
If the travel speed of the electrode is *S* (in mm/min) and welding time is *t* (in seconds), the length of weld bead produced is:
$$L = S \times \dfrac{t}{60} \quad \text{(millimetres)}$$
This is because we must convert *S* from **mm/min** to **mm/s** by dividing by 60.

**Step 4 — Energy per unit length (Heat Input):**
Dividing total energy by weld length:
$$H = \dfrac{E_{total}}{L} = \dfrac{V \times I \times t}{\left( S \times \dfrac{t}{60} \right)}$$

**Step 5 — Simplification:**
The time *t* cancels out:
$$H = \dfrac{V \times I \times 60}{S} \quad \text{(J/mm)}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to standard unit (kJ/mm):**
Since 1 kJ = 1000 J, we divide by 1000:
$$H = \dfrac{V \times I \times 60}{S \times 1000} \quad \text{(kJ/mm)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU-Approved Heat Input Formula:**
> $$H = \dfrac{V \times I \times 60}{S \times 1000} \quad \text{kJ/mm}$$
> Multiply by the arc efficiency ($\eta \approx 0.7$ for SMAW) when the question specifies "actual heat input delivered to the workpiece."

### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example — KTU Style

**Problem:** A butt weld is made on a 3 mm M.S. sheet using SMAW with the following parameters:
Arc voltage *V* = 24 V, welding current *I* = 100 A, travel speed *S* = 200 mm/min.
Calculate: (a) Heat input in kJ/mm, (b) Actual heat input assuming $\eta = 0.7$, (c) Total energy supplied if the weld length is 500 mm.

**Solution:**

**(a) Heat Input:**
$$H = \dfrac{V \times I \times 60}{S \times 1000} = \dfrac{24 \times 100 \times 60}{200 \times 1000}$$
$$H = \dfrac{144000}{200000} = 0.72 \quad \text{kJ/mm}$$

**(b) Actual Heat Input to Weld:**
$$H_{actual} = \eta \times H = 0.7 \times 0.72 = 0.504 \quad \text{kJ/mm}$$

**(c) Total Energy for 500 mm Weld:**
$$E_{total} = H_{actual} \times L = 0.504 \times 500 = 252 \quad \text{kJ}$$

**Verification using direct method:**
$$E_{total} = \dfrac{V \times I \times t}{1000} = \dfrac{24 \times 100 \times 150}{1000} = 360 \text{ kJ (electrical)}$$
$$\eta \times 360 = 0.7 \times 360 = 252 \text{ kJ} \quad \checkmark$$

### 3.3 Worked Example — Electrode & Current Selection

**Problem:** A 4 mm M.S. sheet is to be welded by SMAW. Select the electrode diameter and welding current.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Electrode diameter rule:**
For thin sheets, $d = t$ (for $t < 4$ mm). For $t = 4$ mm:
$$d = \dfrac{t}{2} + 1 = \dfrac{4}{2} + 1 = 3 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Current range (rule of thumb):**
$$I = 30d \text{ to } 40d$$
$$I_{min} = 30 \times 3 = 90 \text{ A}$$
$$I_{max} = 40 \times 3 = 120 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3 — Recommended setting:**
$$I = 105 \text{ A (mid-range, suitable for DCEP with E6013 electrode)}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Tip:** Always mention the **electrode classification (e.g., E6013 for general-purpose M.S., E7018 for low-hydrogen high-strength joints)** along with the diameter. This signals depth to the examiner.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Butt vs Lap Welding

| Parameter | Butt Joint | Lap Joint |
|---|---|---|
| Edge Preparation | Required (square or V-groove) | Not required (only cleaning) |
| Filler Metal Consumption | Lower | Higher (2 weld passes for full strength) |
| Strength Efficiency | ~100% of base metal | ~70 – 80% of base metal (for single weld) |
| Sheet Distortion | Higher (symmetric shrinkage) | Lower (overlap compensates) |
| Visual Appearance | Cleaner | Less clean (step visible) |
| Thickness Limitation | Up to any practical thickness | Limited by sheet thickness |
| Common Application | Pressure vessels, pipes, plates | Sheet metal ducts, automotive panels |
| Inspection Ease | Easy (radiographic, ultrasonic) | Difficult at root |
| Cost | Lower per unit length | Higher (extra weld) |
| Stress Concentration | At weld toe (heat-affected zone) | At the overlap corner |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 SMAW Process Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[AC/DC Welding Power Source] --> B[Electrode Holder Cable]
    B --> C[Electrode with Flux Coating]
    C --> D[Electric Arc 6000 deg C]
    D --> E[Weld Pool Molten Metal]
    F[Workpiece M.S. Sheet] --> D
    F --> E
    E --> G[Solidified Weld Bead]
    D --> H[Shielding Gas CO2 from Flux]
    H --> I[Protective Envelope over Weld Pool]
    E --> J[Slag Formation from Flux]
    J --> K[Slag Crust chipped off after cooling]
    F --> L[Earth Clamp Cable]
    L --> A
    M[Operator Controls Current via Regulator] --> A
    M --> N[Maintains Arc Length 2 to 4 mm]
    N --> C
```

### 4.2 Butt Joint Welding — Functional Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Start Lab Exercise] --> P2[Select M.S. Sheets thickness 3 mm]
    P2 --> P3[Clean Sheet Edges using wire brush]
    P3 --> P4[Set up Butt Joint with 1.5 mm root gap]
    P4 --> P5[Clamp Sheets on welding table]
    P5 --> P6[Connect Earth Clamp to workpiece]
    P6 --> P7[Insert 2.5 mm E6013 Electrode in holder]
    P7 --> P8[Set current to 90 A DCEP]
    P8 --> P9[Strike Arc by scratching method]
    P9 --> P10[Maintain arc length 2 to 3 mm]
    P10 --> P11[Travel speed 150 mm per min steady]
    P11 --> P12[Deposit root pass first bead]
    P12 --> P13[Chip slag using chipping hammer]
    P13 --> P14[Wire brush the bead surface]
    P14 --> P15[Inspect bead for porosity cracks undercut]
    P15 --> P16[Record observations and measurements]
    P16 --> P17[End of Butt Welding Exercise]
```

### 4.3 Lap Joint Welding — Functional Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Start Lap Welding Exercise] --> Q2[Select two M.S. sheets 3 mm]
    Q2 --> Q3[Overlap sheets by 20 mm]
    Q3 --> Q4[Mark weld location on top sheet]
    Q4 --> Q5[Tack weld at both ends to hold position]
    Q5 --> Q6[Set earth clamp on bottom sheet]
    Q6 --> Q7[Load 2.5 mm E6013 electrode]
    Q7 --> Q8[Set welding current 100 A DCEP]
    Q8 --> Q9[Strike arc at start of joint]
    Q9 --> Q10[Hold electrode at 70 to 80 deg to plate]
    Q10 --> Q11[Deposit fillet weld along edge]
    Q11 --> Q12[Maintain equal leg length 4 to 5 mm]
    Q12 --> Q13[Chip slag on cooling]
    Q13 --> Q14[Wire brush clean surface]
    Q14 --> Q15[Repeat weld on opposite edge optional]
    Q15 --> Q16[Measure fillet leg length and throat]
    Q16 --> Q17[End of Lap Welding Exercise]
```

### 4.4 Equipment Layout — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Main Supply 415 V 3 phase AC] --> A2[Step Down Welding Transformer]
    A2 --> A3[Output Terminals 60 to 80 V OCV]
    A3 --> A4[Current Regulator Knob]
    A4 --> A5[Electrode Holder Cable]
    A5 --> A6[Electrode Stinger]
    A3 --> A7[Work Cable Output]
    A7 --> A8[Earth Clamp]
    A8 --> A9[Workpiece M.S. Sheet]
    A6 --> A10[Arc Gap 2 to 4 mm]
    A10 --> A9
    B1[Personal Protective Equipment] --> B2[Helmet with auto darkening filter]
    B1 --> B3[Welding Gloves leather]
    B1 --> B4[Apron leather]
    B1 --> B5[Boots safety]
    B1 --> B6[Face shield for chipping]
```
