## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, 3 Marks]**
*Define Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW). List any four safety precautions to be observed during arc welding.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 1 + 2):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
SMAW is a manual arc welding process in which the heat for welding is produced by an electric arc between a flux-coated consumable electrode and the workpiece. The flux decomposes to provide a shielding gas and a molten slag that protects the weld pool from atmospheric contamination.

**Safety Precautions (2 marks — any four of the following):**
1. Always wear a welding helmet with the correct shade filter lens (shade 10–13) to protect the eyes from UV/IR radiation.
2. Use leather welding gloves and an apron to protect skin from sparks and spatter.
3. Ensure the workplace is well-ventilated to remove welding fumes (especially important with cellulose-coated electrodes).
4. Keep a fire extinguisher (Class ABC) nearby because sparks can travel up to 10 metres and ignite flammable materials.
5. Ensure the welding machine is properly earthed and cables are insulated.
6. Never weld in damp conditions or on containers that previously held flammable substances.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, 3 Marks]**
*Differentiate between a butt joint and a lap joint with neat sketches (mention any three points of difference).*

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 3 × 1):**

| S.No. | Butt Joint | Lap Joint |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Two plates are placed edge-to-edge in the same plane. | Two plates overlap each other by a certain length. |
| 2 | Requires edge preparation (square or V-groove). | No edge preparation is required. |
| 3 | Weld is a full-penetration groove weld. | Weld is a fillet weld (triangular cross-section). |
| 4 | Strength efficiency is nearly 100% of base metal. | Strength efficiency is 70–80% (single weld). |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, split as **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**, with internal choice between two question options.

---

#### **OPTION A — QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** With the help of a labelled block diagram, describe the **Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW) process** for joining M.S. sheets. Explain the functions of the **flux coating** and the **electric arc** in the process. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 Marks — 1 + 3 + 2 + 1):**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
SMAW is the most widely used manual arc welding process. An electric arc is struck between a flux-coated consumable electrode and the M.S. workpiece. The arc melts both the electrode tip and the base metal, forming a common weld pool that solidifies to form the joint.

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**
*(Refer to Section 4.1 — Equipment Layout Diagram)*

The key components in functional order are:
1. **Welding Power Source** (transformer/rectifier) — converts mains supply to low-voltage, high-current welding output (60–80 V OCV, 18–28 V arc voltage).
2. **Electrode Holder** — insulated handle that grips the electrode and connects it to the welding cable.
3. **Electrode with Flux Coating** — provides filler metal and shielding.
4. **Electric Arc** — established across a 2–4 mm gap; temperature ~6,000 °C.
5. **Workpiece + Earth Clamp** — completes the electrical circuit.
6. **Weld Pool + Slag Cover** — molten metal protected from atmosphere.

**Functions of Flux Coating (2 marks):**
1. Generates a **shielding gas** (CO and CO₂) that displaces atmospheric O₂ and N₂ from the arc zone.
2. Forms a **molten slag** that floats on the weld pool, blocking atmospheric contamination and slowing cooling.
3. Adds **deoxidizers and alloying elements** (Si, Mn) to the weld metal, improving mechanical properties.
4. Stabilises the arc (e.g., potassium compounds in the coating).
5. Provides a **viscous coating** that aids in striking and maintaining the arc.

**Functions of Electric Arc (1 mark):**
The arc converts electrical energy into intense concentrated heat, melting the base metal and electrode to form the weld pool. It also creates the ionized path for current flow and provides the energy needed to decompose the flux coating.

---

**(b)** Calculate the **heat input per unit length** for an arc welding operation with the following data: arc voltage = 25 V, welding current = 120 A, travel speed = 150 mm/min. If the arc efficiency is 0.7, what is the **actual heat input** delivered to the workpiece? Also, calculate the **total energy supplied** to make a 400 mm long butt weld. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 Marks — 2 + 2 + 1 + 2):**

**Given Data:**
- Arc Voltage $V = 25$ V
- Welding Current $I = 120$ A
- Travel Speed $S = 150$ mm/min
- Arc efficiency $\eta = 0.7$
- Weld length $L = 400$ mm

**Step 1 — Heat Input Formula (2 marks):**
$$H = \dfrac{V \times I \times 60}{S \times 1000} \quad \text{(kJ/mm)}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Values (2 marks):**
$$H = \dfrac{25 \times 120 \times 60}{150 \times 1000} = \dfrac{180000}{150000} = 1.2 \quad \text{kJ/mm}$$

**Step 3 — Actual Heat Input (1 mark):**
$$H_{actual} = \eta \times H = 0.7 \times 1.2 = 0.84 \quad \text{kJ/mm}$$

**Step 4 — Total Energy for 400 mm Weld (2 marks):**
$$E_{total} = H_{actual} \times L = 0.84 \times 400 = 336 \quad \text{kJ}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch in travel speed:** Many students forget to convert S from mm/min to mm/s. The "×60" in the numerator handles this — do not double-convert.
> 2. **Forget to divide by 1000 for kJ:** Without this, your answer will be in **J/mm**, not kJ/mm. The examiner will deduct **1 mark** for wrong units.
> 3. **Heat input vs. Actual heat input:** The formula gives *gross* heat input. Only multiply by $\eta$ when the question asks for "actual/true/net heat delivered to workpiece."
> 4. **Decimals vs. fractions:** KTU examiners accept both, but decimals are easier to verify — use them.

---

#### **OPTION B — QUESTION B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 / Model Paper, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Explain the **step-by-step procedure** to perform a **butt joint** on two M.S. sheets (3 mm thick) using SMAW in the workshop. Mention the **electrode specification**, **welding parameters**, and **post-weld inspection** steps. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 Marks — 1 + 2 + 2 + 1 + 1):**

**Step 1 — Material & Equipment Preparation (1 mark):**
Select two M.S. sheets of 3 mm thickness (100 mm × 50 mm each). Clean edges using a wire brush to remove rust, oil, and mill scale. Arrange the sheets edge-to-edge on the welding table with a 1.5 mm root gap and clamp them using C-clamps or tack welds.

**Step 2 — Electrode Selection (2 marks):**
- **Electrode type:** E6013 (rutile-coated, general-purpose M.S. electrode, all-position).
- **Electrode diameter:** 2.5 mm (rule: $d = t$ for $t < 4$ mm).
- **Current setting:** $I = 30d$ to $40d$ → **75 A to 100 A**; recommend **90 A**.
- **Polarity:** DCEP (Direct Current Electrode Positive) for deeper penetration on M.S.

**Step 3 — Welding Procedure (2 marks):**
1. Connect earth clamp firmly to the workpiece.
2. Hold the electrode holder at 70°–80° to the plate (push angle for rutile electrodes).
3. Strike the arc using the **scratch (striking) method** — drag the electrode tip across the plate and quickly lift it to a 2–3 mm gap.
4. Maintain a steady travel speed of **150 mm/min** along the seam.
5. Use a slight weaving motion (if gap > 2 mm) or straight bead (stringer bead) for a tight root.
6. Stop the arc at the crater end by reversing the travel direction briefly to fill the crater.

**Step 4 — Post-Weld Slag Removal (1 mark):**
After the bead cools to black (room temperature, ~5–10 min), use a **chipping hammer** to remove the slag crust, followed by a **wire brush** to clean the bead surface for inspection.

**Step 5 — Inspection (1 mark):**
Visual inspection for **porosity** (pin-holes), **cracks**, **undercut** (groove at weld toe), **incomplete penetration**, and **excess reinforcement**. Measure the bead width and reinforcement height. Note the absence of spatter, uniformity, and toe smoothness.

---

**(b)** With a neat diagram, describe the **lap joint** welding procedure on M.S. sheets. Compare the **strength and filler-metal consumption** of a lap joint with a butt joint. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 Marks — 2 + 2 + 2 + 1):**

**Diagram (2 marks):**
*(Refer to Section 4.5 — Lap Joint Cross-Section Diagram)*

A lap joint is formed by overlapping two M.S. sheets by a minimum of **3 × t** (typically 20 mm for 3 mm sheets) and depositing a **fillet weld** along the edge(s) of the top sheet. The fillet weld has a triangular cross-section with two equal legs of length *a* and a throat thickness of $0.707a$.

**Lap Joint Welding Procedure (2 marks):**
1. Clean both sheets along the overlap zone using a wire brush.
2. Place one sheet over the other with 20 mm overlap.
3. Tack weld at both ends of the overlap to hold position.
4. Set DCEP, 2.5 mm E6013 electrode, current 100 A.
5. Strike the arc at the start, hold electrode at 45° to the vertical plate (inclined towards the direction of travel).
6. Deposit the fillet weld using a slight weaving motion to ensure both sheets fuse with the weld pool.
7. Repeat on the opposite edge if full-strength double-fillet lap is required.
8. Chip slag and clean bead for inspection.

**Strength & Filler Metal Comparison (2 marks):**
- **Strength:** A properly executed butt joint (full-penetration groove weld) achieves nearly **100% strength efficiency** of the base metal. A single-fillet lap joint typically achieves only **70 – 80% strength efficiency** because the load path is eccentric (creates a bending moment). A double-fillet lap joint approaches **~90%** of base-metal strength.
- **Filler metal consumption:** A lap joint consumes **roughly 1.5 – 2 times** more filler metal per unit length of joint than a butt joint, because the triangular fillet has a larger cross-sectional area than a typical butt weld reinforcement.

**Concluding Statement (1 mark):**
Hence, although lap joints are easier to fabricate and require no edge preparation, butt joints are preferred in **critical load-bearing and pressure-retaining** applications due to superior strength efficiency and lower filler-metal consumption.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Losing Mistakes in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Confusing heat input units:** Writing the answer in J/mm instead of kJ/mm — **deduct 1 mark**.
> 2. **Forgetting polarity:** Always specify DCEP or DCEN; examiners specifically look for this.
> 3. **No mention of slag removal or post-weld inspection:** The lab manual expects you to demonstrate process knowledge beyond the arc itself.
> 4. **Skipping safety precautions:** KTU 2024 scheme now awards marks for safety/environment awareness even in Part A answers.
> 5. **Wrong electrode classification:** E6013 ≠ E7018. E6013 is rutile (general purpose); E7018 is low-hydrogen (high strength). Mixing them up costs **0.5–1 mark**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Points — KTU 2024 Scheme**

- [x] **SMAW = Shielded Metal Arc Welding** = Manual Metal Arc Welding (MMA). Synonymous terms.
- [x] **Arc temperature = 6,000 – 7,000 °C**; M.S. melts at ~1,500 °C — heat is far in excess, so the limiting factor is travel speed and current.
- [x] **Open Circuit Voltage (OCV) = 50 – 80 V AC**; **Arc working voltage = 18 – 28 V**.
- [x] **Standard Electrode for M.S. lab work = E6013** (rutile, 2.5 mm dia, 75–100 A).
- [x] **Polarity = DCEP (Electrode Positive, Work Negative)** gives deeper penetration — preferred for M.S. butt welds.
- [x] **Electrode diameter rule for thin sheets:** $d = t$ for $t < 4$ mm.
- [x] **Current rule:** $I = 30d$ to $40d$ (in Amperes).
- [x] **Arc length maintained = 2 – 4 mm** during welding.
- [x] **Electrode tilt angle = 70° – 80°** for butt welds; **45°** for fillet (lap) welds.
- [x] **Heat input formula (must memorize):** $H = \dfrac{V \times I \times 60}{S \times 1000}$ kJ/mm.
- [x] **Arc efficiency of SMAW = 0.70 (70%)** — multiply gross heat input to get actual heat input.
- [x] **Flux functions:** Shielding gas + Slag cover + Deoxidation + Arc stabilisation + Alloy addition.
- [x] **Butt joint:** Higher strength (100%), edge prep required, lower filler consumption.
- [x] **Lap joint:** Easier fabrication, no edge prep, but only 70–80% single-weld strength, more filler consumed.
- [x] **Overlapping length for lap joint = 3 × t** (minimum).
- [x] **Post-weld cleanup:** Chipping hammer (slag) → Wire brush (loose particles) → Visual inspection.
- [x] **Common weld defects to mention in answers:** Porosity, undercut, cracks, lack of penetration, slag inclusion, spatter, crater cracks.
- [x] **Safety:** Helmet shade 10–13, leather gloves, apron, fire extinguisher, ventilation, earthing of machine.
- [x] **KTU frequently asked topics:** Heat input numerical (every exam!), electrode selection logic, SMAW diagram with all blocks labeled.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Exercises on Gas Welding: Butt Welding and Lap Welding of M.S. Sheets
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMEL408 - MANUFACTURING TECHNOLOGY LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Exercises on Gas Welding: Butt Welding and Lap Welding of M.S. Sheets # SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ## 1.1 What is Gas Welding? **Gas Welding** is a fusion welding process in which the joint between two metal pieces is produced by heating them to their melting temperature using a flame generated by the combustion of a fuel gas (typically **acetylene**, **hydrogen**, **propane**, or **natural gas**) with **oxygen**. The molten pool fuses the parent metal, and a filler rod (optional) is added to strengthen the joint. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus Standard):** > *Gas welding is a fusion welding process where coalescence of metals is achieved by heating with a gas flame (oxy-acetylene being the most common), with or without the application of pressure and with or without the use of filler metal.* The most widely used variant in KTU lab experiments is **Oxy-Acetylene Welding (OAW)**, where the fuel gas is **acetylene (C₂H₂)** and the oxidizer is **pure oxygen (O₂)**. The flame produced reaches a temperature of approximately **3,200 °C to 3,500 °C**, which is more than sufficient to melt mild steel (M.S.) sheets. > [!NOTE] > **Why Acetylene?** > Acetylene is the preferred fuel because: > - It has the **highest flame temperature** among commercially used fuel gases (~3,160 °C in pure O₂). > - It produces a **concentrated, clean, and easily controllable** flame. > - It is available in **dissolved acetone** form in cylinders, making it safe to transport and store. ## 1.2 The Joints Under Study ### (a) Butt Joint — A Face-to-Face Union A **Butt Joint** is a type of edge joint where two metal plates are placed in the same plane, edge-to-edge, with their square-edged (or beveled) faces touching each other, and the weld is made along the seam. **Conceptual Analogy:** Think of two slices of bread placed side by side on a plate, touching at their crusts. If you "fuse" those touching edges with molten cheese, you create a butt joint. The bread represents the M.S. sheet, the cheese represents the molten weld pool, and the crust-to-crust contact is the butt configuration. > [!NOTE] > **In KTU Lab Context:** > For M.S. sheets of thickness **1.5 mm to 3 mm**, the edges are kept **square (no beveling)**. For thicker sheets (> 3 mm), a **V-groove** is prepared. The lab experiment in Module 2 deals with sheets ≤ 3 mm thickness, so square butt joints are the standard practice. ### (b) Lap Joint — An Overlapping Union A **Lap Joint** is a type of joint where two metal plates overlap each other over a certain length, and the weld is made along the edges of the overlap region, typically on **both top and bottom sides** for full strength. **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine two paper sheets, one placed on top of the other with a 10–15 mm overlap (like a roof tile). If you apply a strong glue line along the edges of the overlap on both faces, you have essentially created a lap joint. The overlap length in welding is called the **lap length** or **weld pitch**. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Lab Specification:** > For M.S. sheets of **2 mm to 3 mm thickness**, the recommended overlap is **3T to 5T** (where T = sheet thickness). For example, a 2 mm sheet would have a **6 mm to 10 mm** overlap. Two parallel fillet welds (one on each side of the overlap) are deposited to complete the lap joint. ## 1.3 M.S. Sheets — The Work Material **Mild Steel (M.S.)** is a low-carbon steel containing **0.15% to 0.25% carbon**. It is the standard work material in the KTU Manufacturing Technology Lab for gas welding exercises because: - It has a **low melting point** (~1,500 °C), making it easy to weld with oxy-acetylene flame. - It is **ductile and forgiving** for beginner students. - It is **inexpensive and readily available** in sheet form (typically 1.5 mm, 2 mm, 2.5 mm, 3 mm thicknesses in the KTU lab). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard KTU Lab Sheet Specification:** > - **Thickness:** 1.5 mm to 3 mm (commonly 2 mm) > - **Size:** 100 mm × 50 mm or 150 mm × 50 mm > - **Edge Preparation:** Sheared or saw-cut, deburred > - **Cleaning:** Wire-brushed and degreased with acetone before welding ## 1.4 KTU Examiner's Snapshot of Key Terms | Term | Definition | Standard Value | |------|------------|----------------| | Acetylene (C₂H₂) | Fuel gas used in OAW | Stored dissolved in acetone at 15–16 atm | | Oxygen (O₂) | Combustion supporter | Stored at 120–150 atm in blue cylinders | | Neutral Flame | O₂ : C₂H₂ ratio = 1 : 1 | Used for welding M.S. | | Carburizing Flame | Excess C₂H₂ (ratio < 1) | Used for hard surfacing | | Oxidizing Flame | Excess O₂ (ratio > 1.5) | Used for brass and bronze | | Filler Rod | ER70S / MS filler | Ø 1.6 mm to 3.15 mm for thin sheets | # SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 Oxy-Acetylene Welding (OAW) — System Architecture The OAW setup consists of the following components connected in series: **Cylinder → Regulator → Hose → Torch (with Nozzle)** ### Component 1: Acetylene Cylinder - **Color coding:** Maroon (Red in some standards) - **Pressure when full:** ~15–16 atm - **Dissolved in:** Acetone (which absorbs ~25 volumes of acetylene per volume of acetone) - **Safety:** Never lay horizontally; always keep upright ### Component 2: Oxygen Cylinder - **Color coding:** Black (or Blue in India: BS standard) - **Pressure when full:** ~120–150 atm - **Form:** Compressed gaseous oxygen - **Safety:** Avoid contact with oil/grease (explosion hazard) ### Component 3: Regulators (Pressure Reducing Valves) - **Oxygen regulator:** Reduces cylinder pressure to working pressure of **2–3 bar** (delivered to torch). - **Acetylene regulator:** Reduces cylinder pressure to working pressure of **0.5–1.0 bar** (delivered to torch). - Each regulator has **two gauges:** cylinder pressure and working pressure. ### Component 4: Hoses - **Oxygen hose:** Black, single-braided - **Acetylene hose:** Red, double-braided (for safety identification) - Connected to torch via **flashback arrestors** (mandatory in KTU labs). ### Component 5: Welding Torch (Blowpipe) - The torch mixes O₂ and C₂H₂ in a **mixing chamber** and delivers them through a **nozzle** (also called a **jet** or **tip**). - Nozzle sizes are numbered **0 to 8**; for M.S. sheets of 2 mm, nozzle size **1 or 2** is used. ## 2.2 Flame Chemistry — The Three Sacred Flames The OAW flame's character depends on the **O₂ : C₂H₂ volume ratio**: ### (a) Neutral Flame - **O₂ : C₂H₂ = 1 : 1** (slight excess acetylene; actually ~1.1 to 1.2 acetylene) - **Temperature:** ~3,160 °C (core); ~1,260 °C (outer envelope) - **Structure:** Three zones — bright white inner cone, feather-like intermediate zone, blue outer envelope - **Use:** Universal flame for welding M.S., stainless steel, cast iron, copper - **KTU Exam Point:** *"The neutral flame is the standard flame for gas welding mild steel."* ### (b) Carburizing (Reducing) Flame - **O₂ : C₂H₂ = 0.8 : 1 to 0.9 : 1** (excess acetylene) - **Visual clue:** A long, feathery, white acetylene "feather" extends 2–3 times the length of the inner cone - **Use:** Hard-facing, welding high-carbon steel, welding non-ferrous alloys (to prevent oxide formation) - **Drawback:** Excess carbon dissolves into weld pool → **hard, brittle weld** ### (c) Oxidizing Flame - **O₂ : C₂H₂ = 1.5 : 1 to 2 : 1** (excess oxygen) - **Visual clue:** Short, pointed, sharp inner cone; hissing sound - **Use:** Welding brass, bronze, zinc - **Drawback:** Burns away carbon → **porous, oxidized weld** > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 High-Yield Question Pattern:** > *"With neat sketches, describe the three types of flames used in oxy-acetylene welding. Which flame is used for welding mild steel and why?"* — This is a 7-mark standard question. ## 2.3 Process Parameters for Gas Welding M.S. Sheets | Parameter | Typical Range for 2 mm M.S. Sheet | |-----------|------------------------------------| | **Nozzle size** | 1 or 2 | | **Oxygen working pressure** | 1.0 – 1.5 bar | | **Acetylene working pressure** | 0.3 – 0.7 bar | | **Filler rod diameter** | 1.6 mm | | **Flame type** | Neutral | | **Welding position** | Flat (1G / 1F) | | **Torch angle** | 45° to 60° from plate surface | | **Filler rod angle** | 30° to 45° from plate surface | | **Travel speed (S)** | 100 – 150 mm/min | ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL KTU FORMULAS FOR GAS WELDING EXPERIMENTS** | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---------|-------------|-------| | 1 | $Q_{ac} = k \cdot t \cdot L$ | Acetylene consumption (k = constant ≈ 70–100 L/hr) | Liters | | 2 | $Q_{o2} = 1.1 \cdot Q_{ac}$ | Oxygen consumption (neutral flame, O₂:C₂H₂ = 1.1:1) | Liters | | 3 | $W = \pi \cdot d^2 / 4 \cdot \rho \cdot L$ | Filler rod weight (d = dia, ρ = steel density 7.85 g/cm³) | grams | | 4 | $t = L / v$ | Welding time (v = travel speed) | minutes | | 5 | $H = (V \cdot I \cdot 60) / (v \cdot 1000)$ | Heat input (arc welding form, for reference) | kJ/mm | | 6 | $P_{th} = (Q_{ac} \cdot HHV) / 3600$ | Thermal power (HHV of C₂H₂ = 55 MJ/m³) | kW | | 7 | $\eta = P_{eff} / P_{th}$ | Heat transfer efficiency (≈ 0.6 to 0.8 for OAW) | dimensionless | | 8 | $E = V \cdot t \cdot I \cdot k$ | Total energy (alternative form) | Joules | > [!WARNING] > **Markdown Table Safety Note:** > All absolute value bars in the above formulas have been written using the standard parenthesis notation `( )` to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax. In LaTeX rendering, vertical bars $\vert$ or $\mid$ should be used. ## 2.5 Engineering Utility of Gas Welding - **Aerospace:** Repair of thin aluminum and magnesium alloy components (despite being largely replaced by TIG/MIG). - **Automobile:** Sheet metal repair, body work, exhaust system repair. - **Plumbing:** Joining copper and brass pipes. - **Maintenance & Repair Work (MRO):** Field repairs where electricity is unavailable. - **Art & Sculpture:** Bronze and copper sculptures. - **HVAC:** Brazing of refrigeration copper tubes. # SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Gas Consumption Calculation **Problem Statement (KTU Style):** A butt joint is to be made on a 2 mm thick M.S. sheet of length **150 mm** using oxy-acetylene welding. Calculate the **total acetylene and oxygen consumption** given: - Nozzle size: 2 - Acetylene flow rate: **k = 90 L/hr** - Flame type: Neutral (O₂ : C₂H₂ = 1.1 : 1) - Travel speed: **v = 120 mm/min** ### Step 1: Identify the Governing Formula The acetylene consumption is given by: $$Q_{ac} = k \cdot t$$ where **t** is the total welding time in hours. ### Step 2: Calculate the Welding Time The welding time depends on the joint length **L** and the travel speed **v**: $$t = \frac{L}{v}$$ Substituting **L = 150 mm** and **v = 120 mm/min**: $$t = \frac{150 \text{ mm}}{120 \text{ mm/min}}$$ $$t = 1.25 \text{ min}$$ Converting to hours: $$t = \frac{1.25}{60} \text{ hr} = 0.02083 \text{ hr}$$ ### Step 3: Calculate Acetylene Consumption $$Q_{ac} = k \cdot t = 90 \text{ L/hr} \times 0.02083 \text{ hr}$$ $$Q_{ac} = 1.875 \text{ L}$$ ### Step 4: Calculate Oxygen Consumption For a **neutral flame**, the volume ratio is: $$\frac{O_2}{C_2H_2} = 1.1$$ Therefore: $$Q_{O_2} = 1.1 \cdot Q_{ac} = 1.1 \times 1.875 \text{ L}$$ $$Q_{O_2} = 2.0625 \text{ L}$$ ### Step 5: Final Answer (Engineering Format) | Quantity | Value | |----------|-------| | Acetylene consumption (Q_ac) | **1.875 L** | | Oxygen consumption (Q_O₂) | **2.0625 L** | | Total gas consumption | **3.94 L** | > [!NOTE] > **Practical Insight:** > The actual cylinder pressure drop is negligible for such a short weld. However, for industrial-scale gas welding planning, this calculation is essential to determine the number of cylinders required for a day's work. --- ## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Filler Rod Weight Calculation **Problem Statement:** For the same 2 mm M.S. butt joint, a filler rod of diameter **1.6 mm** is used along the entire 150 mm weld length, with a **30% wastage factor** (overlap of root, spatter, and wastage). Calculate the **total filler rod weight required**. ### Step 1: Volume of Filler Rod Consumed The volume of a cylindrical rod is: $$V = \frac{\pi \cdot d^2}{4} \cdot L$$ Substituting **d = 1.6 mm** and **L = 150 mm** (note: length in mm, diameter in mm): $$V = \frac{\pi \cdot (1.6)^2}{4} \times 150$$ $$V = \frac{\pi \cdot 2.56}{4} \times 150$$ $$V = \frac{8.0425}{4} \times 150$$ $$V = 2.0106 \times 150$$ $$V = 301.59 \text{ mm}^3$$ ### Step 2: Convert to cm³ (for density compatibility) $$V = 301.59 \text{ mm}^3 \times \frac{1 \text{ cm}^3}{1000 \text{ mm}^3} = 0.30159 \text{ cm}^3$$ ### Step 3: Calculate Mass Without Wastage Using density of steel $\rho = 7.85 \text{ g/cm}^3$: $$m_{net} = V \cdot \rho = 0.30159 \times 7.85$$ $$m_{net} = 2.3675 \text{ g}$$ ### Step 4: Apply 30% Wastage Factor The actual rod weight to be kept ready: $$m_{actual} = m_{net} \times (1 + 0.30)$$ $$m_{actual} = 2.3675 \times 1.30$$ $$m_{actual} = 3.078 \text{ g}$$ ### Step 5: Final Answer $$\boxed{m_{actual} \approx 3.08 \text{ grams of filler rod}}$$ --- ## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Thermal Power of the Flame **Problem Statement:** Calculate the **thermal power** delivered by the flame in the above weld, given the **Higher Heating Value (HHV)** of acetylene is **55 MJ/m³**, and the heat transfer efficiency of gas welding is **η = 0.70**. ### Step 1: Convert Acetylene Volume to m³ $$Q_{ac} = 1.875 \text{ L} = 1.875 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$$ ### Step 2: Calculate the Theoretical Thermal Power $$P_{th} = \frac{Q_{ac} \cdot HHV}{t}$$ where **t = 0.02083 hr = 75 seconds = 75/3600 hr** (using hours for HHV consistency): $$P_{th} = \frac{(1.875 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3) \times (55 \times 10^6 \text{ J/m}^3)}{75 \text{ s}}$$ $$P_{th} = \frac{103,125 \text{ J}}{75 \text{ s}}$$ $$P_{th} = 1,375 \text{ W} = 1.375 \text{ kW}$$ ### Step 3: Calculate the Effective Power (Heat Actually Used) $$P_{eff} = \eta \cdot P_{th} = 0.70 \times 1.375 \text{ kW}$$ $$P_{eff} = 0.9625 \text{ kW} \approx 963 \text{ W}$$ ### Step 4: Final Interpretation > The flame delivers about **963 W of useful heat** to the joint. For a 2 mm M.S. sheet, this is more than adequate to reach the melting point (1500 °C) within seconds. A typical 60W incandescent bulb consumes the same power, but here the heat is **localized** to a tiny spot (the inner cone). --- ## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Joint When? | Criterion | Butt Joint | Lap Joint | |-----------|------------|-----------| | **Joint strength requirement** | Moderate | High (larger weld area) | | **Sheet thickness** | Any (≤ 3 mm in lab) | Usually thin sheets (≤ 3 mm) | | **Material wastage** | Low (no overlap) | High (overlap = wastage) | | **Edge preparation** | Required (square/bevel) | Not required | | **Distortion** | More | Less (overlap provides rigidity) | | **Loading type suited** | Tension, compression | Shear, bending | | **Common applications** | Plates, sheets, pipe longitudinal seam | Sheet metal fabrication, tanks, panels | | **Filler metal required** | Yes | Yes | | **Number of weld passes** | 1 (for thin sheets) | 2 (top and bottom fillets) | # SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Oxy-Acetylene Welding Equipment Layout ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[OXYGEN CYLINDER

Which flame is preferred for welding mild steel and why?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The three types of OAW flames are: 1. **Neutral Flame** — O₂ : C₂H₂ = 1 : 1 (slight excess acetylene for clean combustion). Inner cone is bright white, with a blue outer envelope. Temperature ≈ 3,160 °C. 2. **Carburizing Flame** — Excess acetylene, O₂ : C₂H₂ = 0.8 to 0.9 : 1. Has a long acetylene feather extending from the inner cone. 3. **Oxidizing Flame** — Excess oxygen, O₂ : C₂H₂ = 1.5 to 2 : 1. Short, sharp, pointed inner cone with a hissing sound. **Preferred Flame for M.S.:** The **neutral flame** is preferred for welding mild steel because it neither adds carbon (carburizing) nor burns away carbon (oxidizing), producing a **clean, ductile, and crack-free weld** with proper mechanical properties. **(1 mark for listing, 1 mark for preferred flame, 1 mark for reason)** --- ### Question A2 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PCMEL408]** **Q: What is the difference between a butt joint and a lap joint? Give one industrial application of each.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Feature | Butt Joint | Lap Joint | |---------|------------|-----------| | Configuration | Two plates placed edge-to-edge in the same plane | Two plates overlap each other | | Edge preparation | Required (square cut or beveled) | Not required | | Weld passes | Usually one | Two (top and bottom fillet) | | Strength | Moderate (depends on throat) | Higher (larger weld area) | | Material wastage | Low | High (due to overlap) | - **Application of Butt Joint:** Longitudinal seam welding of **pressure vessels, storage tanks, and pipes**. (1.5 marks) - **Application of Lap Joint:** Fabrication of **sheet metal panels, automobile body panels, and aircraft skin splices**. (1.5 marks) --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### Question Choice A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PCMEL408]** **Q: With a neat sketch, describe the oxy-acetylene welding (OAW) process. List the equipment used and explain the procedure to perform a butt joint on a 2 mm M.S. sheet.** #### (a) Description of OAW Process and Equipment (7 Marks) **Oxy-Acetylene Welding (OAW)** is a gas welding process in which the heat required for fusion is obtained from the combustion of **acetylene (C₂H₂)** with **oxygen (O₂)** in a welding torch. The flame temperature reaches approximately **3,200 °C**, which is sufficient to melt most engineering metals including M.S. (1.5 marks) **Equipment Used (with descriptions):** 1. **Oxygen Cylinder** — Black/blue colored, high-pressure (120–150 atm) storage of pure O₂. (0.5 marks) 2. **Acetylene Cylinder** — Maroon/red colored, lower pressure (15–16 atm) storage of C₂H₂ dissolved in acetone for safety. (0.5 marks) 3. **Pressure Regulators** — Two regulators (one for O₂, one for C₂H₂) reduce cylinder pressure to safe working pressures. (0.5 marks) 4. **Hoses** — Red hose for acetylene, black hose for oxygen, with flashback arrestors at the torch inlet. (0.5 marks) 5. **Welding Torch (Blowpipe)** — Has a handle with control valves (O₂ valve, C₂H₂ valve), a mixing chamber, and an interchangeable nozzle (tip). The nozzle size depends on sheet thickness. (0.5 marks) 6. **Filler Rod** — Low-carbon steel filler rod (ER70S grade), Ø 1.6 mm for 2 mm sheet. (0.5 marks) 7. **Spark Lighter** — A special friction lighter to ignite the gas mixture safely (no matches/lighters allowed in lab). (0.5 marks) 8. **Personal Protective Equipment (PPE)** — Welding goggles (shade 4–6 for OAW), leather gloves, apron, and safety shoes. (0.5 marks) 9. **Welding Jig / Fixture** — Holds the sheets in position during tack welding and welding. (0.5 marks) 10. **Wire Brush and Chipping Hammer** — For post-weld cleaning of slag and spatter. (0.5 marks) 11. **Chipping Hammer and Chisel** — For removing hardened slag. (0.5 marks) #### (b) Procedure for Butt Joint on 2 mm M.S. Sheet (7 Marks) **Step 1 — Preparation (1 mark):** - Cut M.S. sheets to size (e.g., 100 mm × 50 mm × 2 mm). - Square-cut the edges using a shear or hacksaw. - Clean edges with a wire brush to remove mill scale, rust, and oil. - Degrease with acetone. **Step 2 — Setup and Edge Alignment (1 mark):** - Place the two sheets edge-to-edge in a welding jig. - Maintain a **root gap of 1 to 1.5 mm** to allow penetration. - Set root face (no bevel needed for 2 mm thickness). **Step 3 — Tack Welding (1 mark):** - Apply 2–3 small tack welds (~5 mm long) at intervals along the joint to hold the sheets. - Allow each tack to cool and clean before proceeding. **Step 4 — Flame Adjustment (1 mark):** - Open acetylene valve first, ignite with spark lighter. - Slowly open oxygen valve and adjust to get a **neutral flame** (sharp inner cone with no feather). - Set working pressures: O₂ = 1.0 bar, C₂H₂ = 0.5 bar. **Step 5 — Welding (2 marks):** - Hold the torch at **45° to 60°** to the plate surface, leading direction of travel. - Hold the filler rod at **30° to 45°** to the plate. - Move the torch in a **slight circular or side-to-side motion** to distribute heat evenly. - Dip the filler rod into the molten pool at regular intervals. - Maintain a **uniform bead height and width** along the seam. - Travel speed: 100–150 mm/min. **Step 6 — Post-Weld Operations (1 mark):** - Allow the welded joint to **cool in still air** (do not quench). - Wire-brush the bead to remove oxide and spatter. - Visually inspect for defects (cracks, porosity, undercuts). - Measure the **bead width, height, and penetration** (in cross-section if destructive test allowed). --- ### Question Choice B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PCMEL408]** **Q: Explain the procedure to perform a lap joint on 2 mm M.S. sheets using gas welding. Compare the lap joint and butt joint in terms of strength, wastage, and applications.** #### (a) Procedure for Lap Joint on 2 mm M.S. Sheets (7 Marks) **Step 1 — Material Preparation (1 mark):** - Cut two M.S. sheets to 100 mm × 50 mm × 2 mm. - Clean all surfaces with a wire brush and acetone. **Step 2 — Overlap Setup (1 mark):** - Place one sheet on top of the other with an **overlap of 3T to 5T** (6 to 10 mm for 2 mm sheets). - Clamp or tack-weld at the corners to maintain the overlap. **Step 3 — Tack Welding (1 mark):** - Apply a small tack weld (~5 mm) at the center of the overlap on **one side only** to lock the assembly. **Step 4 — First Fillet Weld (Top Side) (1.5 marks):** - Adjust torch to a **neutral flame**. - Position torch at **45° to 60°** along the top edge of the overlap. - Move torch from **left to right**, depositing a **fillet weld** along the upper edge of the lap. - Maintain equal weld legs (leg length ≈ 4–5 mm for 2 mm sheet). - Use a slight weaving motion to ensure fusion with both sheets. **Step 5 — Cooling and Flipping (0.5 mark):** - Allow the top fillet to cool naturally to below 100 °C. - Flip the assembly to expose the bottom overlap edge. **Step 6 — Second Fillet Weld (Bottom Side) (1.5 marks):** - Deposit the second fillet weld along the bottom edge of the lap, moving in the **opposite direction** (right to left) to balance heat input. - This second pass is **mandatory** for full strength in KTU lab evaluation. **Step 7 — Inspection and Cleaning (0.5 mark):** - Inspect both fillet welds for uniform leg length, smooth profile, and absence of undercut. - Wire-brush and clean. Measure **throat thickness** with a weld gauge. #### (b) Comparison: Lap Joint vs Butt Joint (7 Marks) | Comparison Parameter | Butt Joint (3.5 marks) | Lap Joint (3.5 marks) | |----------------------|------------------------|------------------------| | **Joint Strength** | Lower for the same sheet thickness (single line weld) | Higher (two fillet welds = double weld area) | | **Shear Strength** | Limited by throat area of the single weld | Excellent (resists shear across two welds) | | **Material Wastage** | Minimal (no overlap; edges trimmed only) | High (overlap = 3T to 5T of material per joint) | | **Edge Preparation** | Mandatory (square or bevel cut) | Not required (sheets overlap as-cut) | | **Distortion Risk** | Higher (heat concentrated on one line) | Lower (overlap area provides built-in rigidity) | | **Fit-up Tolerance** | Strict (gap must be controlled) | Loose (overlap hides misalignment) | | **Number of Welds** | One (one side only) | Two (top and bottom fillets) | | **Labor / Time** | Less (single weld) | More (two welds) | | **Applications** | Pipe seams, pressure vessels, plate girders | Sheet metal panels, automotive bodies, tanks, aircraft skin | | **Loading Type Best Suited** | Tension, compression along axis | Shear, bending, peel | **Conclusion (in 1 line for KTU answer):** *The lap joint is preferred for thin sheet assemblies requiring higher shear strength, while the butt joint is preferred for thicker sections and where material economy matters.* --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **COMMON MISTAKES THAT COST MARKS IN KTU EXAMS:** > > 1. **Confusing the flame ratio:** Students often write O₂ : C₂H₂ = 1 : 1 for neutral, but technically it is **1.1 : 1 (slight excess acetylene)** for *true* neutral combustion. Writing 1:1 is acceptable in KTU answers but writing 2:1 for neutral loses marks. > > 2. **Ignoring PPE:** OAW uses **shade 4 to 6 goggles**, NOT the dark shade 10–13 used for arc welding. Writing shade 13 goggles for gas welding will be marked wrong. > > 3. **Forgetting flashback arrestors:** Flashback arrestors at the torch inlet are **mandatory** and examiners often test this as a 2-mark question. Never omit them in an equipment list. > > 4. **Confusing nozzle and tip:** Nozzle and tip refer to the same component. Do not list them as two separate items. > > 5. **Lap joint = one weld:** Many students weld only one side of the lap and skip the second pass. KTU expects **two fillet welds** (one on each side). Skipping the second weld is a common reason for losing 3–4 marks. > > 6. **Wrong order of gas valve operation:** Always **open acetylene first, then oxygen** while lighting. To shut off, **close oxygen first, then acetylene**. Reversing this order causes a soot-laden (carbon-rich) flame and a safety hazard. > > 7. **Calculation mistakes in unit conversion:** When calculating gas consumption, students forget to convert minutes to hours. Always write: $t_{hr} = t_{min} / 60$ explicitly. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — KTU PCMEL408 MODULE 2** ### 🔹 Equipment Checklist - [ ] Oxygen cylinder: Black/Blue, 120–150 atm - [ ] Acetylene cylinder: Maroon/Red, 15–16 atm, dissolved in acetone - [ ] Two pressure regulators (one per gas) - [ ] Hoses: Black for O₂, Red for C₂H₂ - [ ] Flashback arrestors (mandatory safety item) - [ ] Welding torch with control valves, mixing chamber, and nozzle - [ ] Nozzle size 1–2 for 2 mm M.S. sheet - [ ] Filler rod: ER70S, Ø 1.6 mm - [ ] Spark lighter, wire brush, PPE (shade 4–6 goggles, gloves) ### 🔹 Flame Mastery - [ ] **Neutral** = O₂ : C₂H₂ = 1 : 1, sharp white inner cone, **for M.S.** - [ ] **Carburizing** = O₂ : C₂H₂ < 1, feather visible, for hard-facing - [ ] **Oxidizing** = O₂ : C₂H₂ > 1, short hissing cone, for brass/bronze ### 🔹 Butt Joint Key Points - [ ] Square-cut edges for ≤ 3 mm thickness - [ ] Root gap = 1 to 1.5 mm - [ ] Tack welds at both ends - [ ] Single-pass weld, left to right - [ ] Torch angle = 45° to 60° - [ ] Filler rod angle = 30° to 45° ### 🔹 Lap Joint Key Points - [ ] Overlap = 3T to 5T (6–10 mm for 2 mm sheet) - [ ] **Two fillet welds** (top + bottom) - [ ] Second pass after cooling - [ ] Equal leg length on both fillets ### 🔹 Key Formulas to Memorize - [ ] $t = L / v$ (welding time) - [ ] $Q_{ac} = k \cdot t$ (acetylene consumption) - [ ] $Q_{O_2} = 1.1 \cdot Q_{ac}$ (oxygen for neutral flame) - [ ] $m = (\pi d^2 / 4) \cdot L \cdot \rho$ (filler rod mass) - [ ] $P_{th} = Q_{ac} \cdot HHV / t$ (thermal power) - [ ] $P_{eff} = \eta \cdot P_{th}$, with $\eta \approx 0.7$ for OAW ### 🔹 Safety Non-Negotiables - [ ] Acetylene cylinder **always upright** (acetone can leak if horizontal) - [ ] Never use oil/grease near oxygen fittings - [ ] Flashback arrestors on **both** gas lines - [ ] **Open acetylene first, oxygen second** while lighting - [ ] **Close oxygen first, acetylene second** while shutting off - [ ] Wear **shade 4–6 goggles** (NOT arc-welding shade 10–13) - [ ] Ensure **proper ventilation** in the welding bay ### 🔹 High-Yield KTU Question Hooks - [ ] "Why is acetylene preferred over other fuel gases?" → Highest flame temperature + clean flame - [ ] "Why is neutral flame used for M.S.?" → Balanced chemistry, no carbon pickup or loss - [ ] "Why two fillet welds in lap joint?" → Full strength + balanced heat input - [ ] "Why square-cut edges for 2 mm sheet?" → 2 mm is thin; beveling wastes material and risks burn-through - [ ] "Why is acetylene stored in acetone?" → Acetone safely absorbs 25× its volume of acetylene, preventing unstable gas-phase decomposition --- > **END OF MODULE EXPERIMENT 2 NOTES — PCMEL408** > *Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 Aligned) — Outcome-Based Education Standards*

topics.title: Study and Preparation of Program, Simulation and Exercise on CNC Lathe: Turning, Step Turning, Taper Turning, Thread Cutting, Ball and Cup Turning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMEL408 - MANUFACTURING TECHNOLOGY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Study and Preparation of Program, Simulation and Exercise on CNC Lathe: Turning, Step Turning, Taper Turning, Thread Cutting, Ball and Cup Turning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU Definition of CNC Lathe & Part Programming

A **CNC (Computer Numerical Control) Lathe** is a programmable machine tool that performs turning, facing, threading, contouring and other rotary-symmetric machining operations through coordinated motion of the spindle (C-axis) and the cross slides (X and Z axes), all governed by a numeric control unit that interprets a written **part program** (a sequence of G-codes and M-codes) generated either manually or via CAM software.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — PCMEL408, Module Experiment 2**
> The student must *study the machine*, *prepare a manual part program*, *simulate it on CNC simulator software* (e.g., FANUC, SinuTrain, or CNCSimulator Pro), and *execute it on the actual CNC lathe*. The five canonical exercise profiles assessed are: **Plain Turning, Step Turning, Taper Turning, Thread Cutting, and Ball & Cup Turning**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The CNC Lathe as a "Robotic Potter's Wheel"

Imagine a **traditional potter's wheel** (chuck + spindle) where the clay spins (workpiece rotates). A potter uses his **fingers and a wooden rib** to shape the spinning clay — every subtle push of the rib is an *incremental cut*. Now replace the potter with a **computer-controlled cutting tool** that moves in precise, mathematically defined X and Z increments every millisecond.

- The **workpiece (clay)** is held in a 3-jaw / 4-jaw / collet chuck.
- The **tool (rib/fingers)** is a single-point cutting tool (SPCT) mounted on a turret or cross-slide.
- The **computer (potter's brain)** interprets the *part program* (set of instructions) line by line.
- The **feed rate (F)** controls how fast the tool moves radially (X) or axially (Z).
- The **spindle speed (S)** controls how fast the clay (workpiece) spins.

Where a human potter can only make approximate shapes, the CNC lathe can produce **sub-micron accurate threads, perfect tapers, and complex ball profiles** — the same shape, thousands of times, identically.

### 1.3 Standard Coordinate System for CNC Lathe (FANUC Convention)

| Axis | Direction | Positive Direction | Used For |
|------|-----------|-------------------|----------|
| **X** | Radial (cross-slide) | Away from spindle centreline (OD turning increases diameter) | Diameter / radial infeed |
| **Z** | Longitudinal (carriage) | Away from chuck (towards tailstock) | Lengthwise feed / facing |
| **+X, +Z** | Tool motion away from workpiece, no cutting | — | Rapid / approach |
| **−X, −Z** | Tool motion into workpiece | — | Cutting direction |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Point:** On a CNC lathe, **X-axis values represent DIAMETER (not radius)**. This is the single most common error committed by beginners trained on CNC milling machines, where X represents radius. Forgetting this leads to a workpiece machined to **half the intended diameter** — a 40 mm part becomes 20 mm.

### 1.4 Essential G-Codes (Preparatory Functions) — Lathe Subset

| G-Code | Function | Modal/Non-Modal |
|--------|----------|------------------|
| **G00** | Rapid positioning (no cutting) | Modal |
| **G01** | Linear interpolation (straight cut at feed F) | Modal |
| **G02** | Circular interpolation — Clockwise (CW) | Modal |
| **G03** | Circular interpolation — Counter-Clockwise (CCW) | Modal |
| **G04** | Dwell (P-address for time in seconds) | Non-modal |
| **G20** | Input in inches | Modal |
| **G21** | Input in millimetres | Modal |
| **G28** | Return to reference point via intermediate | Non-modal |
| **G32** | Single-point thread cutting | Modal |
| **G90** | Outer diameter / inner diameter turning cycle (longitudinal) | Modal |
| **G92** | Thread cutting cycle (simple) | Modal |
| **G94** | Facing cycle (chamfering/taper cycles) | Modal |
| **G96** | Constant surface speed (CSS) ON | Modal |
| **G97** | Constant spindle speed (RPM) ON | Modal |
| **G98** | Feed per minute (mm/min) | Modal |
| **G99** | Feed per revolution (mm/rev) | Modal |

### 1.5 Essential M-Codes (Miscellaneous Functions)

| M-Code | Function |
|--------|----------|
| **M00** | Program stop (operator intervention) |
| **M01** | Optional stop (only when OPT STOP switch ON) |
| **M03** | Spindle ON, Clockwise (CW) |
| **M04** | Spindle ON, Counter-Clockwise (CCW) |
| **M05** | Spindle stop |
| **M08** | Coolant ON |
| **M09** | Coolant OFF |
| **M30** | Program end & rewind |

### 1.6 Standard Part Program Frame (FANUC Lathe)

```
O[NNNN];                          → Program number (mandatory)
N10 G21 G99 G97 G90;              → Safety block (metric, feed/rev, RPM mode, absolute)
N20 M08;                          → Coolant ON
N30 M03 S[nnn];                   → Spindle CW at given RPM
N40 G00 X[xs] Z[zs];              → Rapid to start point
N50 G01 X... Z... F[fff];         → Cutting moves
... (machining blocks)
Nxx G00 X[safe] Z[safe];          → Rapid retract
Nyy M09;                          → Coolant OFF
Nzz M05;                          → Spindle stop
Nww M30;                          → End + rewind
%
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Lab Exam Tip:** Always begin with a **safety block** (`G21 G99 G97 G90` or `G21 G98 G97`) and end with **M30** followed by `%` (when saved to a file). A missing safety block is a 1-mark deduction per the KTU valuation key.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Plain (Straight) Turning — Theoretical Foundation

**Plain turning** reduces the diameter of a cylindrical workpiece over a defined length using **G01** with an X-coordinate equal to the final diameter and a Z-coordinate equal to the cut length. The tool moves **axially (along Z)** while the workpiece rotates — this is **longitudinal turning**.

#### Operational Logic (Step-by-Step)

1. **Workpiece set-up:** The raw blank (e.g., Ø50 × 60 mm mild steel) is mounted in a 3-jaw self-centring chuck with adequate protrusion (≈ 5 mm beyond the tool tip's required travel).
2. **Tool set-up:** A CNMG-type turning tool (rhombic 80° insert) is mounted on the tool post with its **nose radius** (typically 0.4 or 0.8 mm) and **tool offset** (H + D values) registered in the offset table.
3. **Reference establishment:** The tool is jogged to a known position (often the work face at Ø50), and that position is assigned as the **program zero (X0, Z0)** — called the *floating zero* convention.
4. **Approach:** The tool rapids to `G00 X[final-diameter + 2 mm] Z2` — staying 2 mm axially clear of the face and 1–2 mm radially clear of the cut diameter to avoid sudden engagement.
5. **Cutting pass:** `G01 X[final] Z[−length] F[feed]` — tool moves longitudinally at the commanded feed (typically 0.15–0.30 mm/rev for mild steel).
6. **Retract:** `G00 X[clear] Z[clear]` to a safe index position.
7. **Repeat** for multiple passes reducing X by 1–2 mm per pass.

### 2.2 Step Turning — Theoretical Foundation

**Step turning** produces **multiple cylindrical diameters on the same workpiece**, separated by **shoulders** (radial faces perpendicular to the Z-axis). Each step has its own diameter and length.

#### Operational Logic

1. The tool first **faces the work** to establish Z0.
2. It then **turns the largest diameter** along the longest length.
3. For each subsequent **smaller diameter**, the tool:
   - **Rapids radially** (`G00 X[smaller-Dia] Z[axis position of shoulder]`)
   - **Feeds longitudinally** (`G01 Z[end position of step]`)
4. For each **shoulder face**, the tool is brought to the new diameter at the shoulder Z-position and then fed radially (X-direction) — this is a **facing-type cut on the shoulder**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Step shafts are foundational components in **gearboxes, couplings, pulley mounts, and motor rotors**. CNC step turning replaces multiple tool setups in a conventional lathe, drastically reducing cycle time and improving concentricity (each step shares the same axis of rotation).

### 2.3 Taper Turning — Theoretical Foundation

A **taper** is a conical surface whose diameter changes linearly with length. The geometric descriptors are:

- **Taper (T):** $T = \dfrac{D - d}{L}$ (mm/mm, dimensionless), where $D$ = large diameter, $d$ = small diameter, $L$ = length of taper.
- **Taper ratio:** $T = 1 : \dfrac{L}{D-d}$
- **Included angle (α):** $\tan\left(\dfrac{\alpha}{2}\right) = \dfrac{(D - d)/2}{L} = \dfrac{T}{2}$

#### Programming Method 1 — Direct Linear Interpolation (G01)

Compute the **end coordinates** of the taper cut:
- Start: $(X_s, Z_s)$ — current tool position (e.g., at the face of the larger diameter)
- End: $X_e = d$ (small diameter), $Z_e = -(L_{taper} + Z_s)$

The single block `G01 X[end_X] Z[end_Z] F[feed]` produces a straight-line tool path along the taper.

#### Programming Method 2 — Taper Turning with Multiple Passes

For deep tapers, multiple passes are required. Each pass uses a different start Z (offset radially) to progressively remove material:
- **Pass k:** $X_{s,k} = D - 2k\Delta r$, $Z_{s,k} = \Delta z \cdot k$ (where $\Delta r$ and $\Delta z$ maintain the same slope $T$)

### 2.4 Thread Cutting — Theoretical Foundation

**Thread cutting on a CNC lathe** uses a single-point threading tool with a profile matching the thread form (e.g., 60° V-profile for metric ISO threads). The tool must be **synchronized with the spindle** — the tool moves one **pitch (P)** in Z for every spindle revolution.

#### Critical Parameters for Metric Thread (ISO)

For a metric thread **M (nominal) × P (pitch)**:

- **Thread depth (single-side radial infeed):** $h_1 = 0.6134 \times P$
- **Total radial material to remove:** $d_{rem} = D_{blank} - d_{minor} = 2h_1 = 1.2268 \times P$
  - For example, **M20 × 1.5**: $h_1 = 0.92$ mm; total radial removal = 1.84 mm.
- **Minor diameter (tap drill size for internal):** $D_{minor} \approx D_{nominal} - 1.0825 P$
- **Spindle speed constraint:** For thread cutting, the **feedrate = pitch = P (mm/rev)** is fixed by the pitch — do **NOT** use G99 feed override; thread-cutting blocks typically use G97 to lock RPM and may disable feed-rate override.

#### Multi-Pass Strategy (G92 — Threading Cycle)

Threads are cut in **multiple spring passes** with progressively decreasing X (radial depth) to manage:
- **Cutting force** reduction (smaller chip area per pass)
- **Heat dissipation** between passes
- **Tool deflection** (less likely on shallow cuts)

A typical **equal-depth pass schedule** for M20 × 1.5 (total radial 1.84 mm) in 8 passes:
- Pass 1: X = 19.00 (depth 0.50 mm)
- Pass 2: X = 18.50 (depth 0.50 mm)
- Pass 3: X = 18.10 (depth 0.40 mm)
- Pass 4: X = 17.80 (depth 0.30 mm)
- Pass 5: X = 17.55 (depth 0.25 mm)
- Pass 6: X = 17.35 (depth 0.20 mm)
- Pass 7: X = 17.20 (depth 0.15 mm)
- Pass 8: X = 17.10 (depth 0.10 mm) — spring pass (no further infeed, just repeat)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why spring passes?** The last 2–3 passes are "spring" or "clean-up" passes where X is **not reduced** — only the tool touches the existing thread profile to **improve surface finish and dimensional accuracy** without taking a real chip. This is the difference between a thread that *fits* and one that *jams*.

### 2.5 Ball Turning — Theoretical Foundation

**Ball turning** produces a **spherical (ball-shaped) profile** at the end or middle of a workpiece using **G02** (CW) or **G03** (CCW) **circular interpolation** with an **R (radius)** or **I, K (centre vector)** address.

For an **end ball** (hemisphere at the right end, away from chuck):
- Diameter at the ball base: $D_{ball} = 2R$ (where R = ball radius)
- The tool starts at $(X_{ball-start}, Z_{start})$ and ends at $(X_{ball-start}, Z_{start} - 2R)$ — **same X, Z differs by 2R** (one diameter axial extent for a full ball).

For a **middle ball** (e.g., a ball bearing journal):
- The tool uses **G02 or G03** to arc from one side of the ball to the other.
- **Critical:** The start and end X coordinates must match (since both points lie on the same ball diameter), but their Z coordinates differ by 2R.

> [!NOTE]
> **Geometry Recap for a Ball on a Shaft:**
> If the ball has radius R, then it spans axially over a length of 2R and radially over a diameter of 2R. The centre of the ball circle is offset from both endpoints by R in both X (radius) and Z (half-length).

### 2.6 Cup Turning — Theoretical Foundation

**Cup turning** produces a **concave / hollow cup-shaped cavity** in the face or external surface of a workpiece, or a **cylindrical cup** when turning the OD.

- For an **external cup (recessed outer surface):** use **G02 (CW)** arc to plunge from the larger diameter to a smaller diameter and back, forming a U-shaped cross-section.
- For an **internal cup (hollow bore with curved bottom):** use a boring bar and **G02/G03** to machine the curved internal bottom of a cup.

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Lathe Operations)

| Operation | Key Formula | Variable Definitions | Units |
|-----------|-------------|----------------------|-------|
| **Spindle RPM from cutting speed** | $N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi \cdot D}$ | $V_c$ = cutting speed, $D$ = workpiece diameter | $N$ = rpm, $V_c$ = m/min, $D$ = mm |
| **Material removal rate (turning)** | $MRR = V_c \cdot f \cdot a_p$ | $f$ = feed/rev, $a_p$ = depth of cut | mm³/min |
| **Taper (per mm of length)** | $T = \dfrac{D - d}{L}$ | $D, d$ = large/small Ø, $L$ = length | mm/mm |
| **Taper included angle** | $\tan\left(\dfrac{\alpha}{2}\right) = \dfrac{D - d}{2L}$ | $\alpha$ = full included angle | degrees |
| **Taper end X-coord (start)** | $X_{e,start} = D - 2\Delta r$ | $\Delta r$ = radial infeed/pass | mm |
| **Thread depth (V-thread metric)** | $h_1 = 0.6134 \cdot P$ | $P$ = pitch | mm |
| **Thread total radial stock** | $\Delta_{total} = 1.2268 \cdot P$ | $P$ = pitch | mm |
| **Minor Ø (external thread)** | $d_{minor} = D_{nom} - 1.0825 P$ | — | mm |
| **Pitch = feed in threading** | $F = P$ | $P$ = pitch | mm/rev |
| **Ball Z-extent** | $L_{ball} = 2R$ | $R$ = ball radius | mm |
| **Cup radius (G02/G03 R-addr)** | $R_{cup} = \dfrac{D_{outer} - D_{inner}}{2}$ | $D_{outer}$, $D_{inner}$ = cup rim/base Ø | mm |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility of these formulas:**
> - The **$N$ formula** is used every time a KTU lab exam asks you to specify the S-address: e.g., "for mild steel $V_c = 120$ m/min on Ø30 mm blank, $N \approx 1273$ rpm."
> - The **thread formulas** appear in nearly every CNC turning exam — knowing the 0.6134 coefficient (it comes from $H/\sqrt{3}$ for a 60° thread where $H = 0.866 P$) and the spring-pass logic is the difference between a full-mark and half-mark answer.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations, Sample Programs & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Sample Part Program 1 — PLAIN TURNING

**Workpiece:** Ø40 × 50 mm mild steel blank, reduce to Ø30 over a length of 35 mm.
**Tool:** CNMG 120408 (roughing).
**Cutting parameters:** $V_c = 150$ m/min → $N = \dfrac{1000 \times 150}{\pi \times 40} = 1193$ rpm (use 1200), $f = 0.2$ mm/rev.

```
O0001;                          (PROGRAM NUMBER)
N10 G21 G99 G97 G90;            (SAFETY BLOCK: metric, feed/rev, RPM mode, abs)
N20 M08;                        (COOLANT ON)
N30 M03 S1200;                  (SPINDLE CW AT 1200 RPM)
N40 G00 X42.0 Z2.0;             (RAPID TO START: 2 mm above face, 2 mm radial clearance)
N50 G00 X38.0 Z0.5;             (APPROACH ENGAGEMENT: at start of cut, 1 mm before face)
N60 G01 Z-35.0 F0.2;            (PLAIN TURNING: feed to Z=-35 at 0.2 mm/rev)
N70 G00 X42.0;                  (RADIAL RETRACT after cut completes)
N80 G00 Z2.0;                   (RETURN TO START Z)
N85 G00 X36.0 Z0.5;             (SECOND PASS: start at X=36, 1 mm from face)
N90 G01 Z-35.0 F0.2;            (SECOND TURNING PASS)
N95 G00 X42.0;
N100 G00 Z2.0;
N110 G00 X34.0 Z0.5;            (THIRD PASS: X=34)
N120 G01 Z-35.0 F0.2;
N130 G00 X42.0;
N140 G00 Z2.0;
N150 G00 X31.0 Z0.5;            (FOURTH PASS: X=31, 1 mm above final)
N160 G01 Z-35.0 F0.2;           (FINISH PASS LEAVES 0.5 mm FOR FINISHING)
N170 G00 X42.0;
N180 G00 Z2.0;
N190 G00 X30.0 Z0.5;            (FINISH PASS: at final Ø30)
N200 G01 Z-35.0 F0.1;           (FINISHING AT LOWER FEED 0.1 mm/rev FOR SURFACE FINISH)
N210 G00 X42.0;
N220 G00 Z2.0;
N230 M09;                       (COOLANT OFF)
N240 M05;                       (SPINDLE STOP)
N250 M30;                       (PROGRAM END)
%
```

#### Derivation of Spindle Speed

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \frac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi \cdot D} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1000 \times 150 \text{ m/min}}{\pi \times 40 \text{ mm}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{150{,}000}{125.66} \text{ rpm} \\[6pt]
&\approx 1193.7 \text{ rpm} \quad \text{(rounded to 1200 rpm)}
\end{aligned}
$$

The **conversion logic** here: $V_c$ is in m/min, $D$ in mm; we multiply $V_c$ by 1000 to convert to mm/min, then divide by the circumference $\pi D$ to get revolutions per minute.

---

### 3.2 Sample Part Program 2 — STEP TURNING

**Workpiece:** Ø50 × 80 mm blank, produce steps: Ø45 (length 30), Ø35 (length 25), Ø25 (length 15).
**Total axial layout:** 30 + 25 + 15 = 70 mm (with 10 mm remaining in chuck).

```
O0002;
N10 G21 G99 G97 G90;
N20 M08;
N30 M03 S1000;                 (RPM for Ø50 blank at Vc=157 m/min)
N40 G00 X52.0 Z2.0;            (SAFE START)
N50 G00 X50.0 Z0;              (TOUCH FACE)
N60 G01 X-1.0 F0.15;           (FACE OFF — tool goes past centre to clean face)
N70 G00 X52.0 Z2.0;            (RETRACT)

N80 G00 X47.0 Z0.5;            (STEP 1 APPROACH)
N90 G01 Z-30.0 F0.2;           (TURN Ø45 FOR LENGTH 30)
N100 G00 X52.0;
N110 G00 Z2.0;

N120 G00 X37.0 Z-30.0;         (STEP 2 APPROACH: at shoulder of Ø45 step)
N130 G01 X35.0 F0.15;          (RADIAL INFED: form shoulder face to Ø35)
N140 G01 Z-55.0 F0.2;          (TURN Ø35 FOR LENGTH 25: from Z=-30 to Z=-55)
N150 G00 X52.0;
N160 G00 Z2.0;

N170 G00 X27.0 Z-55.0;         (STEP 3 APPROACH: at shoulder of Ø35 step)
N180 G01 X25.0 F0.15;          (RADIAL INFED: form shoulder face to Ø25)
N190 G01 Z-70.0 F0.2;          (TURN Ø25 FOR LENGTH 15: from Z=-55 to Z=-70)
N200 G00 X52.0;
N210 G00 Z2.0;

N220 M09;
N230 M05;
N240 M30;
%
```

---

### 3.3 Sample Part Program 3 — TAPER TURNING

**Workpiece:** Ø40 × 50 mm blank, produce a taper from Ø40 (at face) to Ø25 (at Z = −30).
**Taper:** $T = \dfrac{40 - 25}{30} = 0.5$ mm/mm (a steep, half-millis taper).

#### Geometric Analysis

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \frac{D - d}{L} = \frac{40 - 25}{30} = 0.5 \\[6pt]
\tan\left(\frac{\alpha}{2}\right) &= \frac{T}{2} = 0.25 \\[6pt]
\frac{\alpha}{2} &= \arctan(0.25) = 14.04^\circ \\[6pt]
\alpha &= 28.07^\circ \quad \text{(full included angle)}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Program (G01 Direct Method with Multi-Pass)

For 5 passes, radial reduction of 1.5 mm per pass, axial offset of $\Delta z = 1.5 / 0.5 = 3.0$ mm per pass (to maintain slope).

```
O0003;
N10 G21 G99 G97 G90;
N20 M08;
N30 M03 S1200;
N40 G00 X42.0 Z2.0;             (SAFE START)

N50 G00 X40.0 Z0;               (APPROACH FACE)
N60 G01 X38.5 Z-3.0 F0.2;       (PASS 1: D=38.5 at Z=0 → d=38.5 at Z=-3, slope=0.5)
N70 G00 X42.0;                  (RADIAL RETRACT)
N80 G00 Z2.0;

N90 G00 X37.0 Z0;               (PASS 2: D=37 at Z=0)
N100 G01 X35.5 Z-3.0 F0.2;
N110 G00 X42.0;
N120 G00 Z2.0;

N130 G00 X35.5 Z0;              (PASS 3: D=35.5 at Z=0)
N140 G01 X34.0 Z-3.0 F0.2;
N150 G00 X42.0;
N160 G00 Z2.0;

N170 G00 X34.0 Z0;              (PASS 4: D=34 at Z=0)
N180 G01 X32.5 Z-3.0 F0.2;
N190 G00 X42.0;
N200 G00 Z2.0;

N210 G00 X32.5 Z0;              (PASS 5: D=32.5 at Z=0)
N220 G01 X31.0 Z-3.0 F0.2;
N230 G00 X42.0;
N240 G00 Z2.0;

N250 G00 X31.0 Z0;              (FINISH PASS 1: D=31 at Z=0)
N260 G01 X27.5 Z-7.0 F0.15;     (FINISH: D=40 at Z=0 → d=25 at Z=-30 - extended to remove rest)
N270 G01 X25.0 Z-30.0 F0.1;     (FINISHING: full taper in one go at low feed)
N280 G00 X42.0;
N290 G00 Z2.0;

N300 M09;
N310 M05;
N320 M30;
%
```

#### Analytical Matrix — Pass Schedule (5 roughing + 1 finish)

| Pass # | Start X (Ø) | End X (Ø) | Start Z | End Z | Feed (mm/rev) | Notes |
|--------|-------------|-----------|---------|-------|----------------|-------|
| 1 | 38.5 | 38.5 | 0 | −3.0 | 0.20 | Roughing |
| 2 | 37.0 | 35.5 | 0 | −3.0 | 0.20 | Roughing |
| 3 | 35.5 | 34.0 | 0 | −3.0 | 0.20 | Roughing |
| 4 | 34.0 | 32.5 | 0 | −3.0 | 0.20 | Roughing |
| 5 | 32.5 | 31.0 | 0 | −3.0 | 0.20 | Roughing |
| F | 31.0 | 25.0 | 0 | −30.0 | 0.10 | Full finish |

---

### 3.4 Sample Part Program 4 — THREAD CUTTING (M20 × 1.5)

**Workpiece:** Ø20 (nominal) mild steel, pre-turned to Ø20 (no allowance for thread since the thread OD equals nominal for external threads, but blank is pre-turned to ~Ø19.9 for clearance). 
- Pitch $P = 1.5$ mm
- Thread depth $h_1 = 0.6134 \times 1.5 = 0.92$ mm
- Total radial stock: $1.84$ mm
- Minor Ø: $20 - 1.0825 \times 1.5 = 18.376$ mm
- Spindle RPM: 600 (kept low for thread cutting; tool must have time to engage and disengage cleanly)

```
O0004;
N10 G21 G99 G97 G90;            (SAFETY: metric, feed/rev, RPM, abs)
N20 M08;
N30 M03 S600;                   (SPINDLE CW AT 600 RPM)
N40 G00 X22.0 Z5.0;             (RAPID TO START, 5 mm CLEAR OF FACE)

N50 G00 X19.00 Z2.0;            (PASS 1: X=19.00, depth 0.50 mm)
N60 G92 X19.00 Z-22.0 F1.5;     (G92 THREAD CYCLE: PITCH 1.5)
N70 X18.50;                     (PASS 2: X=18.50, depth 0.50 mm)
N80 X18.10;                     (PASS 3: X=18.10, depth 0.40 mm)
N90 X17.80;                     (PASS 4: X=17.80, depth 0.30 mm)
N100 X17.55;                    (PASS 5: X=17.55, depth 0.25 mm)
N110 X17.35;                    (PASS 6: X=17.35, depth 0.20 mm)
N120 X17.20;                    (PASS 7: X=17.20, depth 0.15 mm)
N130 X17.10;                    (PASS 8: X=17.10, depth 0.10 mm — clean-up)
N140 X17.10;                    (PASS 9: SPRING PASS — same X, just smooth)
N150 X17.10;                    (PASS 10: SPRING PASS)
N160 G00 X22.0;                 (RADIAL RETRACT)
N170 G00 Z5.0;                  (RETURN TO START Z)
N180 M09;
N190 M05;
N200 M30;
%
```

#### Derivation of Threading Parameters

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_1 &= 0.6134 \cdot P = 0.6134 \times 1.5 = 0.9201 \text{ mm} \\[4pt]
\Delta_{total} &= 1.2268 \cdot P = 1.2268 \times 1.5 = 1.8402 \text{ mm} \\[4pt]
d_{minor} &= D_{nom} - 1.0825 \cdot P = 20 - 1.0825 \times 1.5 = 20 - 1.62375 = 18.376 \text{ mm} \\[4pt]
\text{Pitch feed } F &= P = 1.5 \text{ mm/rev (locked, no override)}
\end{aligned}
$$

The **0.6134 coefficient** is derived as follows: for a 60° V-thread, the thread height $H = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} P = 0.866 P$. The actual thread depth is $h_1 = \frac{5}{8} H = \frac{5}{8} \times 0.866 P = 0.5413 P$ for ISO metric threads. However, on a CNC lathe, the conventional *radial infeed per side* value used for depth-of-cut scheduling is $0.6134 P$ (this accounts for tool nose geometry and the **single-point threading tool** where the depth must be measured radially, not normal to the thread flank). The conversion logic: $0.6134 = \frac{0.5413}{\cos(30°)}$ adjusting for the radial projection of the thread depth.

---

### 3.5 Sample Part Program 5 — BALL TURNING (External Ball at End)

**Workpiece:** Ø30 × 60 mm blank. Machine a Ø30 cylinder of length 20 mm, then a **ball of radius 10 mm** at the end (Z from −20 to −40, full ball).

```
O0005;
N10 G21 G99 G97 G90;
N20 M08;
N30 M03 S1500;
N40 G00 X32.0 Z2.0;

N50 G00 X30.0 Z0;               (APPROACH FACE)
N60 G01 Z-20.0 F0.2;            (TURN CYLINDRICAL SHAFT Ø30, LENGTH 20)
N70 G00 X32.0;
N80 G00 Z2.0;

N90 G00 X30.0 Z-20.0;           (APPROACH BALL START — at end of shaft)
N100 G02 X30.0 Z-40.0 R10.0 F0.1;  (G02 CW ARC: ball of R=10 from Z=-20 to Z=-40)
N110 G00 X32.0;
N120 G00 Z2.0;

N130 M09;
N140 M05;
N150 M30;
%
```

#### Geometric Verification

The G02 arc: starting at (X=30, Z=−20), ending at (X=30, Z=−40), with arc radius R=10.
- The two endpoints are **diametrically opposite points on a circle of diameter 20** (radius 10). 
- The centre of the arc is at (X=20, Z=−30) — the centre of the ball.
- The arc traces a **semicircle** from the top of the ball at (X=30, Z=−20) over to the bottom of the ball at (X=30, Z=−40).
- Wait — both endpoints have X=30 (diameter), meaning the ball has its **maximum diameter at both endpoints** and a minimum at the centre. The ball is therefore a **sphere of diameter 20 mm** that bulges outward... actually no: with both endpoints at X=30 and the centre at radius 10 from them, the ball is a **semicircular arc in the X-Z plane** which, when revolved about Z, gives a **sphere (ball) of Ø20** embedded with its centre at Z=−30.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Pitfall Alert:** If the student writes `G03` instead of `G02` for an external ball at the right end of the workpiece, the tool will arc in the **wrong direction** — producing a "valley" instead of a "hill". Use this mental rule: **External ball at the end (away from chuck) → G02**. Internal ball (concave) → G03. Viewed from the operator's standard viewpoint looking down the spindle axis toward the chuck.

---

### 3.6 Sample Part Program 6 — CUP TURNING (External Recess / Cup)

**Workpiece:** Ø50 × 60 mm blank. Machine an **external cup (recessed profile)** with cup Ø40, cup depth 5 mm, cup radius R=3.

```
O0006;
N10 G21 G99 G97 G90;
N20 M08;
N30 M03 S1200;
N40 G00 X52.0 Z2.0;

N50 G00 X50.0 Z-15.0;           (APPROACH CUP LOCATION AT Z=-15)
N60 G01 X40.0 F0.15;            (RADIAL INFED: form shoulder from Ø50 to Ø40)
N70 G01 X40.0 Z-20.0 F0.2;      (STRAIGHT CYLINDRICAL SECTION OF CUP WALL, LENGTH 5)
N80 G02 X46.0 Z-23.0 R3.0 F0.1; (G02 ARC: cup bottom curving from Ø40 to Ø46, R=3)
N90 G01 Z-30.0 F0.2;            (RETURN TO OUTER CYLINDER Ø46)
N100 G00 X52.0;
N110 G00 Z2.0;

N120 M09;
N130 M05;
N140 M30;
%
```

#### Geometric Verification

- The cup wall at Ø40 spans Z = −15 to Z = −20.
- The cup bottom curves from (X=40, Z=−20) to (X=46, Z=−23) using a CW arc of R=3 — this forms the **concave inner bottom** of the cup.
- The wall then returns to Ø46, completing the cup-like profile.

---

### 3.7 Summary Matrix — G-Code Usage by Operation

| Operation | Primary G-Codes | Tool Type | Typical Feed | Typical Vc (mild steel) |
|-----------|----------------|-----------|--------------|-------------------------|
| Plain turning | G01, G00, G90 | CNMG roughing/finishing | 0.15–0.30 mm/rev | 120–180 m/min |
| Step turning | G01, G00 | CNMG | 0.15–0.25 mm/rev | 120–150 m/min |
| Taper turning | G01 (multi-pass) | CNMG | 0.10–0.20 mm/rev | 120–150 m/min |
| Thread cutting | G92, G32, G76 | Threading insert (60° V) | = Pitch (locked) | 30–80 m/min |
| Ball turning | G02 / G03 (R-arc) | CNMG with nose radius compensation | 0.05–0.15 mm/rev | 100–150 m/min |
| Cup turning | G01, G02, G03 | CNMG (or boring bar for internal) | 0.10–0.20 mm/rev | 100–150 m/min |

## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 CNC Lathe Functional Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Part Drawing and Process Plan] --> B[Manual Part Program G code and M code]
    B --> C[Verification on CNC Simulator]
    C --> D{Program Verified}
    D -- No --> E[Edit Program Debug]
    E --> C
    D -- Yes --> F[Transfer Program via DNC or USB]
    F --> G[CNC Controller Fanuc or Siemens]
    G --> H[Spindle Drive M03 S command]
    G --> I[X Axis Servo Drive]
    G --> J[Z Axis Servo Drive]
    H --> K[Workpiece Rotation in Chuck]
    I --> L[Cross Slide Tool X motion]
    J --> M[Carriage Tool Z motion]
    K --> N[Material Removal by SPCT]
    L --> N
    M --> N
    N --> O[Finished Component]
    G --> P[Coolant Valve M08 M09]
    P --> N
```

### 4.2 Part Program Execution Flow (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Program Start O NNNN] --> S2[Safety Block G21 G99 G97 G90]
    S2 --> S3[Coolant ON M08]
    S3 --> S4[Spindle CW M03 S rpm]
    S4 --> S5[Rapid to Start G00 X Z]
    S5 --> S6[Cutting Block G01 G02 G03]
    S6 --> S7{More Passes}
    S7 -- Yes --> S6
    S7 -- No --> S8[Rapid Retract G00 Xsafe Zsafe]
    S8 --> S9[Coolant OFF M09]
    S9 --> S10[Spindle Stop M05]
    S10 --> S11[Program End M30]
```

### 4.3 Taper Turning — Multiple-Pass Strategy (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Compute Taper T equals D minus d over L] --> P1[Choose Number of Passes N]
    P1 --> P2[Compute Radial Infeed Delta r per Pass]
    P2 --> P3[Compute Axial Offset Delta z equals Delta r over T]
    P3 --> P4[For Each Pass k from 1 to N]
    P4 --> P5[Compute Start X equals D minus 2 k Delta r]
    P4 --> P6[Compute End X equals D minus 2 k plus 1 Delta r]
    P4 --> P7[Compute Start Z equals k times Delta z minus 1]
    P4 --> P8[Compute End Z equals k times Delta z]
    P5 --> P9[G00 to Start X Start Z]
    P6 --> P10
    P7 --> P9
    P8 --> P10
    P9 --> P10[G01 to End X End Z at Feed]
    P10 --> P11{Last Pass}
    P11 -- No --> P4
    P11 -- Yes --> P12[Finish Pass at Low Feed]
    P12 --> P13[Retract G00 to Safe]
```

### 4.4 Thread Cutting — Multi-Pass Spring-Pass Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T0[Set Pitch P and Nominal D] --> T1[Compute h1 equals 0.6134 times P]
    T1 --> T2[Compute Total Radial Stock equals 1.2268 times P]
    T2 --> T3[Compute Minor d equals D minus 1.0825 P]
    T3 --> T4[Plan N Roughing Passes Decreasing Depth]
    T4 --> T5[Pass 1 Largest Depth]
    T5 --> T6[Pass 2 Smaller Depth]
    T6 --> T7[Pass N Smallest Roughing Depth]
    T7 --> T8[Spring Pass 1 No Radial Infeed]
    T8 --> T9[Spring Pass 2 No Radial Infeed]
    T9 --> T10[Final Thread Profile Achieved]
```

### 4.5 CNC Lathe Axis Layout (Top-View Schematic via Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CHK[3 Jaw Chuck Left Side] -- Workpiece rotation --> WP[Cylindrical Workpiece]
    WP --> TS[Tool Post Right Side]
    TS --> XAX[X Axis Cross Slide Radial]
    TS --> ZAX[Z Axis Carriage Axial]
    XAX --> MTRX[X Axis Servo Motor]
    ZAX --> MTRZ[Z Axis Servo Motor]
    MTRX --> CTRL[CNC Controller]
    MTRZ --> CTRL
    CHK --> SPINDLE[Spindle Motor]
    SPINDLE --> CTRL
```

### 4.6 Ball Turning — Geometric Construction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B0[Ball Radius R Given] --> B1[Start Point X equals 2R Z equals Zs]
    B1 --> B2[End Point X equals 2R Z equals Zs minus 2R]
    B2 --> B3[Arc Centre at X equals 0 Z equals Zs minus R]
    B3 --> B4[Select G02 for External Bulge]
    B3 --> B5[Select G03 for Internal Cavity]
    B4 --> B6[Single Block G02 or G03 X end Z end R R]
    B5 --> B6
    B6 --> B7[Verify by Simulation that Arc Centre is Correct]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Block Diagrams Instead of 2D Physical Drawings?**
> Mermaid does not support CAD-style geometric drawings (circles, tapers, threads). The block diagrams above are functionally equivalent for understanding the *control flow* of the part program, which is the cognitive skill KTU tests. For a 2D sketch of the resulting geometry, students are expected to draw by hand in the exam.
