## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*List any six G-codes used in CNC turning and state their functions.*

**Model Answer:**

| G-Code | Function |
|--------|----------|
| G00 | Rapid positioning (non-cutting motion) |
| G01 | Linear interpolation — straight cut at feed F |
| G02 | Circular interpolation — Clockwise (CW) |
| G03 | Circular interpolation — Counter-Clockwise (CCW) |
| G21 | Metric input (mm) |
| G92 | Thread cutting cycle (single-block, modal) |

(Any six correctly paired code–function → 3 marks: ½ mark per correct pair.)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between G90 and G92 in a CNC lathe program. When is each preferred?*

**Model Answer:**

- **G90 — Outer Diameter / Longitudinal Turning Cycle.** Performs roughing of a straight cylindrical or tapered section in **multiple passes automatically** with a single block. Preferred for **plain/step turning** where multiple roughing passes are needed.
- **G92 — Threading Cycle.** Performs a **single-point threading pass** with a commanded pitch F. The tool retracts at the end of the thread back to the start point. Used **only for thread cutting** (G92 in milling means absolute coordinate, but in **lathe** G92 = threading cycle).

---

**Q3. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
*For an M16 × 2.0 metric thread, calculate the thread depth, the minor diameter, and the total radial stock to be removed. Use the standard ISO 60° V-thread formulas.*

**Model Answer:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_1 &= 0.6134 \times P = 0.6134 \times 2.0 = 1.227 \text{ mm} \\[4pt]
d_{minor} &= D - 1.0825 P = 16 - 1.0825 \times 2.0 = 16 - 2.165 = 13.835 \text{ mm} \\[4pt]
\Delta_{total} &= 1.2268 \times P = 1.2268 \times 2.0 = 2.454 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

(1 mark per correct value with formula.)

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a)** *For a CNC lathe turning operation on a Ø50 mm mild steel blank, calculate the spindle RPM for a cutting speed of 150 m/min. If the feed is 0.2 mm/rev and depth of cut is 2 mm, compute the material removal rate. State the program blocks needed to start the spindle and establish cutting.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Write a complete manual part program in FANUC G-code to turn a step shaft with the following geometry on a Ø60 × 100 mm mild steel blank:*
- *Step 1: Ø52 mm, length 30 mm (from face)*
- *Step 2: Ø44 mm, length 25 mm*
- *Step 3: Ø36 mm, length 20 mm*
- *Use suitable cutting parameters and a CNMG tool. Assume the chuck is on the left.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

Spindle speed:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \frac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi \cdot D} \\[4pt]
  &= \frac{1000 \times 150}{\pi \times 50} \\[4pt]
  &= \frac{150{,}000}{157.08} \approx 955 \text{ rpm} \quad \text{(round to 1000 rpm)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Material removal rate:

$$
\begin{aligned}
MRR &= V_c \cdot f \cdot a_p \quad \text{(where } V_c \text{ in mm/min, } f \text{ in mm/rev, } a_p \text{ in mm)} \\
    &= 150 \times 1000 \times 0.2 \times 2 = 60{,}000 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min} = 60 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min}
\end{aligned}
$$

Program blocks (startup):

```
N10 G21 G99 G97 G90;        (Safety block: mm, feed/rev, RPM, absolute)
N20 M08;                    (Coolant on)
N30 M03 S1000;              (Spindle CW at 1000 rpm)
N40 G00 X54.0 Z2.0;         (Rapid to 2 mm above face, 2 mm radial clearance)
N50 G01 X48.0 F0.2;         (Radial infeed 2 mm at 0.2 mm/rev)
N60 G01 Z-30.0 F0.2;        (Longitudinal turning cut)
```

(Marks: 2 marks for RPM calc, 2 marks for MRR, 3 marks for program logic and safety block.)

**Model Solution for (b):**

Program structure (abbreviated — full program with multiple passes per step):

```
O0100;
N10 G21 G99 G97 G90;        (Safety)
N20 M08;
N30 M03 S1000;              (Spindle for Ø60 at Vc≈188 m/min)
N40 G00 X62.0 Z2.0;         (Safe start)

(--- STEP 1: Ø52 over 30 mm ---)
N50 G00 X54.0 Z0;           (Approach)
N60 G01 X50.0 F0.2;         (Radial infeed to Ø50 stock-removal pass 1)
N70 G01 Z-30.0 F0.2;        (Longitudinal cut to Z=-30)
N80 G00 X62.0;
N85 G00 Z2.0;
(Final pass Ø52:)
N90 G00 X54.0 Z0;
N100 G01 X52.0 F0.1;        (Finish radial)
N110 G01 Z-30.0 F0.1;       (Finish longitudinal)

(--- STEP 2: Ø44 over next 25 mm (Z = -30 to -55) ---)
N120 G00 X46.0 Z-30.0;
N130 G01 X42.0 F0.2;        (Shoulder face rough)
N140 G01 Z-55.0 F0.2;       (Turn Ø42 for 25 mm)
N150 G00 X62.0;
N155 G00 Z2.0;
(Finish:)
N160 G00 X46.0 Z-30.0;
N170 G01 X44.0 F0.1;
N180 G01 Z-55.0 F0.1;

(--- STEP 3: Ø36 over next 20 mm (Z = -55 to -75) ---)
N190 G00 X38.0 Z-55.0;
N200 G01 X34.0 F0.2;
N210 G01 Z-75.0 F0.2;
N220 G00 X62.0;
N225 G00 Z2.0;
(Finish:)
N230 G00 X38.0 Z-55.0;
N240 G01 X36.0 F0.1;
N250 G01 Z-75.0 F0.1;

N260 G00 X62.0 Z2.0;
N270 M09;
N280 M05;
N290 M30;
%
```

(Marks: 2 marks for safety block, 2 marks for step-1 logic, 2 marks for step-2/3 logic, 1 mark for end sequence.)

---

#### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**(a)** *Explain the difference between plain turning, taper turning, and step turning. For a Ø40 to Ø25 taper over a length of 50 mm, compute the taper ratio, the included angle, and write a single-finish G01 block to produce the taper (assume face is Z=0, tool starts at the face of Ø40, and the finish pass begins at the face).* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *A ball of radius 8 mm is to be machined at the end of a Ø30 mm shaft, after a 25 mm cylindrical section. Write the G-code program blocks (in FANUC format) to produce the cylindrical section and the ball. State any assumptions.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Conceptual differences:**

- **Plain turning:** Cylindrical reduction of constant diameter.
- **Step turning:** Multiple diameters separated by radial shoulders on the same workpiece.
- **Taper turning:** Diameter changes linearly along the length, producing a conical surface.

**Taper calculations:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \frac{D - d}{L} = \frac{40 - 25}{50} = 0.3 \text{ mm/mm} \\[4pt]
\tan\left(\frac{\alpha}{2}\right) &= \frac{T}{2} = 0.15 \\[4pt]
\frac{\alpha}{2} &= \arctan(0.15) = 8.531^\circ \\[4pt]
\alpha &= 17.06^\circ \quad \text{(full included angle)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**G01 block for finish pass** (from face at Z=0 with tool at Ø40, going to Ø25 at Z=−50):
Assuming a clearance approach of 1 mm radial, the finish block can be written as:
```
G01 X25.0 Z-50.0 F0.1;
```
(where the tool starts at the face at Ø40 — i.e., X40, Z0 — and feeds in a single straight line to X=25, Z=−50).

(2 marks for each concept distinction, 3 marks for taper calc, 2 marks for G01 block.)

**Model Solution for (b):**

Assumptions: tool at start of cylinder is at (X=30, Z=0); cylindrical section ends at Z=−25; ball of R=8 spans Z=−25 to Z=−41 (length 2R = 16).

```
O0200;
N10 G21 G99 G97 G90;
N20 M08;
N30 M03 S1500;               (RPM for Ø30, Vc≈141 m/min)
N40 G00 X32.0 Z2.0;          (Safe start)

N50 G00 X30.0 Z0;            (Approach face)
N60 G01 Z-25.0 F0.15;        (Turn cylinder Ø30, length 25)
N70 G00 X32.0;
N80 G00 Z2.0;
N85 G00 X30.0 Z-25.0;        (Approach ball start)

N90 G02 X30.0 Z-41.0 R8.0 F0.1;  (G02 CW arc: ball of R=8 from Z=-25 to Z=-41)
N100 G00 X32.0;
N110 G00 Z2.0;
N120 M09;
N130 M05;
N140 M30;
%
```

(Marks: 2 for cylinder block, 2 for ball G02 geometry, 1 for explanation of why G02 is used, 2 for full program frame.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark-Deduction Triggers:**
> 1. **X-axis as diameter forgotten:** Writing `X=20` thinking it is the radius — this produces Ø40, not Ø20. **−2 marks** in any taper/thread/ball question.
> 2. **Missing safety block at the start of the program:** G21 G99 G97 G90 (or G98) is required. **−1 mark**.
> 3. **Pitch written as feed in G92:** Pitch `F` in threading must equal thread pitch. Writing `F0.1` for a 1.5 mm pitch thread is a **fatal logical error (−3 marks)**.
> 4. **Wrong G02/G03 selection for ball:** External ball on the right end uses G02. If the student writes G03, the ball arcs *inward*, producing a hollow. **−2 marks**.
> 5. **No spring / finish passes in threading:** Examiner expects at least 1 spring pass for full marks. **−1 mark** if only roughing passes are listed.
> 6. **Forgetting to switch from M03 (threading on left side) consideration:** Right-hand threads use M03 CW spindle; left-hand threads use M04 CCW. For standard right-hand metric, M03 is correct, but stating this in a long answer shows conceptual clarity. **Bonus +1 mark** in some KTU papers.
> 7. **Omitting coolant (M08) and end block (M30 %):** **−1 mark total**.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- **X = Diameter** (not radius) — most common error on CNC lathe exams.
- **Safety block** `G21 G99 G97 G90` (metric, feed/rev, constant RPM, absolute) is **mandatory** at the start of every program.
- **M03 S** starts the spindle CW; **M05** stops it; **M30** ends the program; **M08/M09** controls coolant.
- **Plain turning:** `G01 X[d] Z[-L] F[f]` — straightforward longitudinal cut.
- **Step turning:** Multiple `G01` blocks, one per step; each step has a radial infeed (`X`) followed by a longitudinal feed (`Z`).
- **Taper turning:** Compute $T = (D-d)/L$; the finish G01 block goes from face (X=D, Z=0) to end of taper (X=d, Z=−L).
- **Thread cutting (G92):** Use the formula $h_1 = 0.6134 P$, $d_{minor} = D - 1.0825 P$, total radial = $1.2268 P$; multiple passes with decreasing depth; final 2–3 passes are **spring passes** (same X); feed `F` = pitch `P`.
- **Ball turning:** Use G02 for external bulge, G03 for internal cavity; the arc spans axially over **2R** (one diameter of length); the start and end X must be the same (= ball's maximum diameter).
- **Cup turning:** Combines G01 (straight walls) with G02/G03 (curved bottom); for an external cup, plunge to inner Ø, machine straight wall, arc outward to the rim using a CW arc.
- **Spindle speed** from cutting speed: $N = \dfrac{1000 V_c}{\pi D}$.
- **Always end** with `M09 M05 M30 %` — coolant off, spindle stop, program end.
- **Verification is part of the lab process:** KTU expects you to **simulate** the program (in SinuTrain, CNCSimulator, or FANUC ladder) before touching the real machine — mention this in viva for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Study and Preparation of Program, Simulation and Exercise on CNC Milling Machine: Surface Milling, Pocket Milling, Contour Milling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMEL408 - MANUFACTURING TECHNOLOGY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Study and Preparation of Program, Simulation and Exercise on CNC Milling Machine: Surface Milling, Pocket Milling, Contour Milling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 CNC Milling Machine — Formal Definition

A **CNC (Computer Numerical Control) Milling Machine** is a multi-axis, multi-point (multi-tooth) subtractive manufacturing system in which a rotating multi-tooth cutting tool removes material from a stationary workpiece under the automatic, coordinated control of a computer executing a stored G-code (NC) part program. The machine is classified under **VMC (Vertical Machining Centre)** when the spindle axis is vertical (Z-axis), and the table traverses in X and Y.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Tone):**
> *CNC Milling is a discrete-part manufacturing process in which a rotating cylindrical or end-milling cutter, mounted in a numerically controlled spindle, executes linear (G00, G01) and circular (G02, G03) interpolations in 2.5D/3D space to generate flat surfaces, pockets, slots, and contoured profiles on a clamped workpiece.*

---

## 1.2 The Three Canonical Milling Operations

| Operation | What is Produced | Real-World Analogy |
|---|---|---|
| **Surface Milling (Facing)** | A flat, planar surface on top of the workpiece | Using a cheese slicer to shave the top off a cheese block until the surface is mirror-flat |
| **Pocket Milling** | A closed, recessed cavity of defined depth inside a workpiece profile | Scooping out the soft centre of a bread loaf with a spoon, leaving a defined border wall |
| **Contour Milling** | A 2D external or internal profile that follows a closed geometric path | Tracing a stencil outline with a scalpel on a sheet of metal, cutting exactly along the drawn line |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why KTU Tests All Three:**
> These three operations cover **~80%** of all practical CNC milling exercises in the KTU lab syllabus. Each operation demands a *different cutter path strategy* and a *different set of G-code preparatory (G) and miscellaneous (M) functions*. Mastering all three is essential for the end-semester practical examination and viva.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Invisible Sculptor"

Imagine you are a blind sculptor asked to carve a defined shape from a solid block of marble, but you are given a precise written instruction sheet. You cannot see the marble, only the coordinates of every chisel strike. The CNC milling machine is your chisel-wielding robot, and the **G-code program is your instruction sheet**. Three types of instructions exist:

1. **"Shave the top flat from X10 to X90 at depth Z0"** → **Surface Milling**
2. **"Carve a 50×50 mm cavity to depth 5 mm inside the 100×100 block"** → **Pocket Milling**
3. **"Walk along the closed curve forming the letter 'K' to depth 5 mm"** → **Contour Milling**

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Takeaway:** Surface milling is *1D traversal* (one plane), pocket milling is *2D rastering inside a closed boundary*, and contour milling is *2D tracing along a closed boundary*. The complexity, therefore, increases in the order: **Surface → Pocket → Contour**.

---

## 1.4 Key Vocabulary (KTU Examiner-Expected Terms)

- **Workpiece Zero / Work Origin (G54):** The user-defined datum on the workpiece from which all coordinates are measured.
- **Machine Zero:** The permanent, fixed origin of the machine (home position).
- **Rapid Traverse (G00):** Non-cutting fast positioning move at maximum feed.
- **Linear Interpolation (G01):** Cutting move in a straight line at programmed feed.
- **Circular Interpolation (G02/G03):** Cutting move along an arc (CW/CCW).
- **Cutter Diameter Compensation (CDC):** G41 (left)/G42 (right) — offsets the tool path from the programmed path by the cutter radius.
- **Tool Length Offset (G43/H-code):** Compensates for the actual measured length of each tool.
- **Step-Over (ae):** Lateral engagement of the cutter during successive passes.
- **Depth of Cut (ap):** Axial engagement (vertical) per pass.
- **Step-Down:** Incremental Z-depth taken per plunging pass in pocket milling.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of a CNC Vertical Machining Centre (VMC)

The **VMC** is the standard CNC milling platform used in KTU-affiliated labs. Its functional architecture can be decomposed into seven coordinated sub-systems.

| Sub-System | Function | Key Components |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanical Structure** | Provides rigidity and motion axes | Column, bed, saddle, table, spindle head |
| **Axes & Drives** | Convert NC commands to physical motion | Servo motors, ball screws, linear guides (X, Y, Z) |
| **Spindle Assembly** | Holds and rotates the cutting tool | Spindle motor, tool holder (BT-40 / CAT-40), drawbar |
| **Automatic Tool Changer (ATC)** | Swaps tools per program call | Tool magazine (carousel / chain), tool arm |
| **Coolant & Chip Management** | Lubricates and evacuates chips | Flood coolant nozzle, chip conveyor, tank |
| **Controller (MCU)** | Interprets and executes G-code | Fanuc / Siemens / Mitsubishi / Haas controller |
| **Feedback System** | Closes the position loop | Linear scales, rotary encoders, resolvers |

---

## 2.2 Standard Coordinate System — The Right-Hand Rule

The CNC milling coordinate system follows **ISO 841 / EIA RS-267** and is established using the **right-hand rule**:

| Axis | Direction (Standard VMC) | Points Towards |
|---|---|---|
| **+X** | Longest table travel | Operator's right when facing the machine |
| **+Y** | Cross travel | Away from the operator (towards the column) |
| **+Z** | Vertical | Up, away from the table (away from workpiece) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Programming Convention in KTU Labs:** All coordinates in the part program are written with respect to the **workpiece zero** (G54), NOT the machine zero. The controller automatically offsets the program coordinates to machine coordinates internally.

---

## 2.3 Essential G-Codes and M-Codes (Lab Vital Five)

| Code | Category | Function | Typical Use in This Lab |
|---|---|---|---|
| **G00** | Motion | Rapid traverse (non-cutting) | Tool positioning above workpiece |
| **G01** | Motion | Linear interpolation at feed | All cutting straight-line moves |
| **G02 / G03** | Motion | Circular interpolation CW / CCW | Circular pockets, arcs, fillets |
| **G17 / G18 / G19** | Plane selection | XY / XZ / YZ plane | G17 (XY) is the default milling plane |
| **G20 / G21** | Units | Inches / Millimetres | **G21** mandatory in KTU labs |
| **G28** | Reference | Return to machine home | Safe end-of-program return |
| **G40** | Compensation | Cancel cutter diameter comp. | Default state |
| **G41 / G42** | Compensation | Cutter comp. left / right | Contour milling offsetting |
| **G43** | Compensation | Tool length compensation + | Every tool change after T call |
| **G54–G59** | Work offset | Select workpiece datum | **G54** is the standard part zero |
| **G80** | Modal cancel | Cancel fixed cycles | Safety reset |
| **G81 / G82 / G83** | Canned cycle | Drilling / counterbore / peck | Hole-making in pockets |
| **G90 / G91** | Distance mode | Absolute / Incremental | G90 is the KTU lab default |
| **G94 / G95** | Feed mode | Feed per minute / per rev | **G94 mm/min** in milling |
| **M03 / M04** | Spindle | CW / CCW rotation | **M03** for conventional milling |
| **M05** | Spindle | Stop | Safe tool change / end of op |
| **M06** | Tool change | Automatic tool change | T01 M06 syntax |
| **M08 / M09** | Coolant | ON / OFF | M08 during cut, M09 before M05 |
| **M30** | Program end | Rewind & reset | Terminal line of every program |

---

## 2.4 Cutting Parameter Theory — The Machining Triangle

The **Machining Triangle** is the foundation of every CNC milling parameter calculation. The three vertices are interconnected by four governing equations.

### Equation 1: Cutting Speed ($V_c$)

$$V_c = \pi \cdot D \cdot N$$

Where:
- $V_c$ = cutting speed, in **m/min**
- $D$ = nominal cutter diameter, in **mm**
- $N$ = spindle rotational speed, in **rpm**

### Equation 2: Spindle Speed ($N$)

$$N = \frac{V_c \times 1000}{\pi \cdot D}$$

### Equation 3: Feed Rate ($v_f$)

$$v_f = f_z \cdot z \cdot N$$

Where:
- $v_f$ = table feed rate, in **mm/min**
- $f_z$ = feed per tooth, in **mm/tooth**
- $z$ = number of effective cutting teeth (flutes)

### Equation 4: Material Removal Rate ($MRR$)

$$MRR = a_e \cdot a_p \cdot v_f$$

Where:
- $a_e$ = radial depth of cut (step-over), in **mm**
- $a_p$ = axial depth of cut, in **mm**

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Standard Unit | KTU Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cutting speed | $V_c$ | $\pi \cdot D \cdot N$ | **m/min** | 80 – 200 (mild steel) |
| 2 | Spindle speed | $N$ | $\dfrac{V_c \times 1000}{\pi \cdot D}$ | **rpm** | 800 – 3000 |
| 3 | Feed rate | $v_f$ | $f_z \cdot z \cdot N$ | **mm/min** | 100 – 800 |
| 4 | Feed per tooth | $f_z$ | $\dfrac{v_f}{z \cdot N}$ | **mm/tooth** | 0.05 – 0.20 |
| 5 | Feed per revolution | $f_n$ | $f_z \cdot z$ | **mm/rev** | 0.10 – 0.50 |
| 6 | Material removal rate | $MRR$ | $a_e \cdot a_p \cdot v_f$ | **mm³/min** | 1000 – 50000 |
| 7 | Power required | $P_c$ | $\dfrac{MRR \cdot k_c}{60 \times 10^6}$ | **kW** | 0.5 – 5.0 |
| 8 | Specific cutting force | $k_c$ | Material table value | **N/mm²** | 1500 – 2500 (steel) |
| 9 | Number of passes (facing) | $n_p$ | $\dfrac{W}{a_e}$ | dimensionless | integer $\geq 1$ |
| 10 | Pocket step-down | $a_{p,\text{pock}}$ | $\dfrac{D}{2}$ rule | **mm** | 0.5D to 1.0D |
| 11 | Cutter comp. lead-in length | $L_{\text{lead}}$ | $\geq 1.5 \times R_c$ | **mm** | 7.5 (for $\phi 10$) |
| 12 | Machining time | $T_m$ | $\dfrac{L_{\text{path}}}{v_f}$ | **min** | computed at end |
| 13 | Arc end-point check | $R$ | $\sqrt{X^2 + Y^2}$ | **mm** | verify $R = r$ |
| 14 | Tool deflection rule | $\delta$ | $\dfrac{F \cdot L^3}{3 \cdot E \cdot I}$ | **mm** | $\delta \leq 0.05$ mm |

> [!NOTE]
> **Substitution Rule for Tables:** When writing $f_z \cdot z \cdot N$ inside any markdown table, ALWAYS wrap with LaTeX and use `\cdot` for the multiplication dot. Never use `|f_z|` to denote absolute feed — use `\vert f_z \vert` instead. The vertical pipe `|` inside table cells **breaks** the KTU-pipeline markdown parser.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Utility of the System

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **Aerospace** | Pocket milling of rib pockets in wing spars; contour milling of aerodynamic leading edges |
| **Automotive** | Surface milling of engine block decks; pocket milling of transmission valve bodies |
| **Die & Mould** | Contour milling of forging dies, EDM electrodes, injection mould cavities |
| **Medical Devices** | Contour milling of titanium orthopaedic implants with $\pm 0.01$ mm tolerance |
| **General Tool Room** | Slot cutting, keyway milling, drilling patterns in fixtures and jigs |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **G-code programming discipline** you master in this lab directly translates to **5-axis simultaneous milling, Swiss turning, and wire-EDM** programming at the industrial level. The logic is identical; only the kinematic chain differs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Program Development & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Surface Milling (Facing)

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement

> Machine a **100 mm × 80 mm × 15 mm** mild steel workpiece. Perform a **facing operation** to clean the top surface, removing **0.5 mm** stock. The cutter is a **$\phi 50$ mm HSS face mill with 8 inserts** ($z = 8$). Use $V_c = 60$ m/min, $f_z = 0.10$ mm/tooth. Stock allowance for facing: **0.5 mm**.

### 3.1.2 Parameter Calculation — Step by Step

**Step 1 — Compute the spindle speed $N$:**

$$
N = \frac{V_c \times 1000}{\pi \cdot D} = \frac{60 \times 1000}{\pi \times 50}
$$

$$
N = \frac{60000}{157.0796} = 381.97 \text{ rpm}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply KTU rounding rule (round to nearest 50 rpm):**

$$N_{\text{programmed}} = 400 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the feed rate $v_f$:**

$$
v_f = f_z \cdot z \cdot N = 0.10 \times 8 \times 400 = 320 \text{ mm/min}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the number of facing passes $n_p$ (with 60% cutter engagement strategy):**

$$
a_e = 0.6 \times D = 0.6 \times 50 = 30 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
n_p = \frac{W}{a_e} = \frac{80}{30} = 2.67 \rightarrow 3 \text{ passes}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute MRR for verification:**

$$
MRR = a_e \cdot a_p \cdot v_f = 30 \times 0.5 \times 320 = 4800 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}
$$

### 3.1.3 G-Code Program — Surface Milling

```gcode
O0001 (FACE MILLING - 100x80x15 MS BLOCK)
G21 G90 G94 G17 G54        (MM, ABS, FEED/MIN, XY PLANE, G54 ZERO)
G00 Z25.0                   (SAFE Z ABOVE STOCK)
T01 M06                     (TOOL 1 = FACE MILL D50)
M03 S400                    (SPINDLE CW @ 400 RPM)
G00 X-30.0 Y-15.0           (POSITION OUTSIDE WORKPIECE CORNER)
M08                         (COOLANT ON)
G00 Z2.0                    (RAPID TO 2MM ABOVE FACE)
G01 Z-0.5 F50               (PLUNGE TO DEPTH AT 50MM/MIN)

(--- PASS 1 ---)
G01 X-30.0 Y35.0 F320       (LEAD IN)
G01 X130.0                  (CUT ACROSS TO FAR EDGE)
G00 Y5.0                    (STEP OVER 20MM)
G01 X-30.0                  (RETURN CUT)
G00 Y25.0                   (STEP OVER 20MM)
G01 X130.0                  (FINAL CUT)

(--- PASS 2,3 - REPEAT WITH Y OFFSET 5, 25 ---)

G00 Z25.0                   (RETRACT)
M09                         (COOLANT OFF)
M05                         (SPINDLE STOP)
G28 X0 Y0 Z0                (RETURN TO HOME)
M30                         (PROGRAM END)
```

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Pocket Milling (Rectangular)

### 3.2.1 Problem Statement

> Mill a **60 mm × 40 mm rectangular pocket** to a depth of **4 mm** in the centre of a 100 × 80 × 15 mm mild steel plate. Use a **$\phi 10$ mm 4-flute carbide end mill**. Cutter engagement strategy: **50% step-over, 1.0D step-down** (peck). $V_c = 80$ m/min, $f_z = 0.05$ mm/tooth.

### 3.2.2 Parameter Calculation

**Step 1 — Spindle speed:**

$$
N = \frac{80 \times 1000}{\pi \times 10} = 2546.48 \text{ rpm}
$$

**Step 2 — Round to nearest 50 rpm:**

$$N_{\text{programmed}} = 2550 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 3 — Feed rate:**

$$
v_f = 0.05 \times 4 \times 2550 = 510 \text{ mm/min}
$$

**Step 4 — Step-over for pocket milling (50% engagement rule):**

$$
a_e = 0.5 \times D = 0.5 \times 10 = 5 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 5 — Step-down (1.0D rule):**

$$
a_{p,\text{peck}} = 1.0 \times D = 1.0 \times 10 = 10 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 6 — Number of Z passes (depth of 4 mm):**

$$
n_{Z} = \frac{4}{10} = 0.4 \rightarrow 1 \text{ peck pass (4 mm ≤ 10 mm)}
$$

**Step 7 — Number of XY passes for a 60 mm length:**

$$
n_{XY} = \frac{60}{5} = 12 \text{ raster lines}
$$

**Step 8 — Machining time estimation:**

$$
L_{\text{path}} \approx 60 \times 12 = 720 \text{ mm (roughing)}
$$

$$
T_m = \frac{L_{\text{path}}}{v_f} = \frac{720}{510} \approx 1.41 \text{ min}
$$

### 3.2.3 G-Code Program — Pocket Milling (Zig-Zag Raster)

```gcode
O0002 (RECTANGULAR POCKET 60x40x4 MS)
G21 G90 G94 G17 G54
G00 Z25.0
T02 M06                     (TOOL 2 = D10 END MILL)
M03 S2550
G00 X-35.0 Y-25.0           (START ABOVE BOTTOM-LEFT CORNER)
M08
G00 Z2.0
G01 Z-4.0 F60               (PLUNGE TO FULL DEPTH AT 60MM/MIN)

(--- ZIG-ZAG RASTER INSIDE POCKET, STEP-OVER = 5MM ---)
G01 Y20.0 F510              (MOVE TO FAR Y)
G01 X30.0                   (CUT TO X30)
G01 Y15.0                   (STEP OVER -5)
G01 X-30.0                  (RETURN)
G01 Y10.0
G01 X30.0
G01 Y5.0
G01 X-30.0
G01 Y0.0
G01 X30.0
G01 Y-5.0
G01 X-30.0
G01 Y-10.0
G01 X30.0
G01 Y-15.0
G01 X-30.0
G01 Y-20.0
G01 X30.0

(--- FINISHING PASS WITH CUTTER COMPENSATION ---)
G01 Y-25.0                  (RETURN TO START CORNER)
G00 Z25.0
M09
M05
G28 X0 Y0 Z0
M30
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Lab Shortcut:** For a clean pocket wall, the student is expected to write a **separate finishing block** using `G41 D02` (left comp, offset register 2 storing the $\phi 10$ cutter radius = 5.0 mm), with a lead-in arc `G02/G03` of radius $\geq 1.5 \times R_c$.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Contour Milling (Closed Profile)

### 3.3.1 Problem Statement

> Profile-mill the **external contour of an irregular "L-shaped" pocket** in 100 × 80 × 15 mm aluminium block. Pocket outline defined by 6 vertices. Use a **$\phi 8$ mm 2-flute HSS end mill**. Pocket depth = **3 mm**, finishing pass required. $V_c = 150$ m/min, $f_z = 0.04$ mm/tooth.

### 3.3.2 Pocket Vertex Table

| Vertex | X (mm) | Y (mm) | Segment Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| **P1** | 10 | 10 | Start |
| **P2** | 80 | 10 | G01 Linear |
| **P3** | 80 | 40 | G01 Linear |
| **P4** | 50 | 40 | G01 Linear |
| **P5** | 50 | 70 | G01 Linear |
| **P6** | 10 | 70 | G01 Linear → back to P1 |

### 3.3.3 Parameter Calculation

**Step 1 — Spindle speed:**

$$
N = \frac{150 \times 1000}{\pi \times 8} = 5968.31 \text{ rpm}
$$

**Step 2 — Round to nearest 100 rpm (high-speed aluminium):**

$$N_{\text{programmed}} = 6000 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 3 — Feed rate:**

$$
v_f = 0.04 \times 2 \times 6000 = 480 \text{ mm/min}
$$

**Step 4 — Cutter radius for compensation register D03:**

$$
R_c = \frac{D}{2} = \frac{8}{2} = 4.0 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 5 — Total path length (rough):**

$$
L = \vert P1P2 \vert + \vert P2P3 \vert + \vert P3P4 \vert + \vert P4P5 \vert + \vert P5P6 \vert + \vert P6P1 \vert
$$

$$
L = 70 + 30 + 30 + 30 + 40 + 60 = 260 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 6 — Machining time:**

$$
T_m = \frac{260}{480} \approx 0.54 \text{ min}
$$

### 3.3.4 G-Code Program — Contour Milling with CDC

```gcode
O0003 (L-SHAPED POCKET CONTOUR MILLING)
G21 G90 G94 G17 G54
G00 Z25.0
T03 M06                     (TOOL 3 = D8 END MILL)
M03 S6000
G00 X-10.0 Y-10.0           (LEAD-IN POINT, OUTSIDE POCKET)
M08
G00 Z2.0
G01 Z-3.0 F80               (PLUNGE TO DEPTH)

(--- LEAD-IN ARC WITH CUTTER COMPENSATION ---)
G41 D03 X0 Y0 F200          (ACTIVATE LEFT CDC, LEAD TO P1)
G01 X10.0 Y10.0 F480        (POSITION AT P1)
G01 X80.0 Y10.0             (P1 -> P2)
G01 X80.0 Y40.0             (P2 -> P3)
G01 X50.0 Y40.0             (P3 -> P4)
G01 X50.0 Y70.0             (P4 -> P5)
G01 X10.0 Y70.0             (P5 -> P6)
G01 X10.0 Y10.0             (P6 -> P1 CLOSE)

(--- LEAD-OUT ARC AND CANCEL CDC ---)
G02 X0 Y0 I-10.0 J0 F300    (LEAD-OUT ARC)
G40 G00 X-10.0 Y-10.0       (CANCEL CDC)
G00 Z25.0
M09
M05
G28 X0 Y0 Z0
M30
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why G41 (Left) and not G42?** Looking **down the +Z axis** at the pocket, the cutter is on the **left** side of the programmed path as you traverse P1→P2→P3→… in the **CCW** direction. If you reverse the direction to CW, you must use **G42**. KTU examiners deduct 2 marks for swapping G41/G42 incorrectly.

---

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Three Operations

| Parameter | Surface Milling | Pocket Milling | Contour Milling |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary motion** | Tool rotates; table traverses in Y | Tool rotates; tool path rasters in XY | Tool rotates; tool path traces a closed 2D profile |
| **Tool used** | Face mill (multi-insert) | End mill (bull-nose for finish) | End mill (flat or ball-nose) |
| **Depth of cut strategy** | Single pass (thin facing) | Step-down pecking (multi-Z) | Full depth plunge or ramped |
| **Cutter comp. needed?** | No (open pass) | No (interior raster) | **Yes (G41/G42 mandatory)** |
| **Lead-in / lead-out** | None (entry from outside) | None (ramp preferred) | **Mandatory** (arc/linear lead) |
| **Step-over logic** | $a_e = 0.6D$ to $0.8D$ | $a_e = 0.4D$ to $0.5D$ | 0 (single pass along path) |
| **Programming difficulty** | Easy (1D) | Medium (2D area fill) | Hard (CDC + arc logic) |
| **Typical KTU mark weight** | 3–5 marks | 5–7 marks | 7–10 marks |

---

## 3.5 Common Parameter Tables (Forged for KTU Viva)

| Workpiece Material | $V_c$ (m/min) HSS | $V_c$ (m/min) Carbide | $f_z$ (mm/tooth) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mild Steel (MS) | 30 – 50 | 80 – 120 | 0.05 – 0.10 |
| Aluminium 6061 | 100 – 150 | 200 – 400 | 0.04 – 0.10 |
| Cast Iron | 20 – 35 | 60 – 100 | 0.05 – 0.08 |
| Brass | 70 – 100 | 150 – 250 | 0.05 – 0.10 |
| Stainless Steel (304) | 15 – 25 | 50 – 80 | 0.03 – 0.06 |

| Cutter Type | Typical $D$ (mm) | Typical $z$ (flutes) | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Face mill | 50 – 200 | 4 – 12 (inserts) | Surface milling |
| End mill (flat) | 3 – 25 | 2 – 4 | Pocket, contour |
| Ball-nose end mill | 3 – 20 | 2 – 4 | 3D contour, moulds |
| Bull-nose end mill | 6 – 25 | 4 – 6 | Pocket finishing |
| Slot mill | 2 – 12 | 2 – 4 | Deep slotting |

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 CNC VMC Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid

    PG[Part Program G-Code] --> CN[Controller MCU]
    CN --> DSP[Spindle Drive]
    CN --> DX[X-Axis Servo]
    CN --> DY[Y-Axis Servo]

    DZ --> BSZ[Ball Screw Z]

    TH --> CUT[Cutting Tool]

    BSY --> TB
    BSZ --> SP
    TB --> WP[Workpiece]
    CUT --> WP
    SP --> ENC[Spindle Encoder]
    BSX --> SX[Linear Scale X]
    BSY --> SY[Linear Scale Y]
    BSZ --> SZ[Linear Scale Z]
    ENC --> CN
    SX --> CN
    SY --> CN
    SZ --> CN

    NK --> WP
    ATC --> MG[Tool Magazine]

```

## 4.2 CNC Programming Workflow — Part-to-Cut

```mermaid

    A[Engineering Drawing] --> B[Process Planning]
    B --> C[Select Workpiece Zero G54]
    C --> D[Select Tools and Holders]
    D --> E[Compute Cutting Parameters]
    E --> F[Write G-Code Program]
    F --> G[Backplot or Simulation]
    G --> H{Simulation OK}
    H -->|No| F
    H -->|Yes| I[Transfer to Machine]
    I --> J[Setup Workpiece and Tools]
    J --> K[Set Work Offsets G54]
    K --> L[Run Dry Run Z+5mm]
    L --> M{Dry Run OK}
    M -->|No| F
    M -->|Yes| N[Run Full Program]
    N --> O[Inspect Dimensions]
    O --> P{Within Tolerance}
    P -->|No| F
    P -->|Yes| Q[Production Complete]
```

---

## 4.3 Tool-Path Strategy Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A[Surface Milling]
        A1[Pass 1: Outside to Inside Y] --> A2[Pass 2: Step Over 0.6D] --> A3[Pass 3: Final Pass]
    end
    subgraph B[Pocket Milling]
        B1[Ramp In or Plunge] --> B2[Raster Zig-Zag XY] --> B3[Peripheral Finish G41]
    end
    subgraph C[Contour Milling]
        C1[Lead In Arc] --> C2[Profile Trace Closed] --> C3[Lead Out Arc]
    end
```

---

## 4.4 Cutter Compensation Geometry (Contour)

```mermaid
    subgraph INNER[Programmed Path Centerline]
        P1[Vertex P1] --> P2[Vertex P2] --> P3[Vertex P3] --> P4[Vertex P4]
    end
    subgraph OUTER[Offset Path with G41]
        Q1[Offset P1 by Rc] --> Q2[Offset P2 by Rc] --> Q3[Offset P3 by Rc] --> Q4[Offset P4 by Rc]
    end
    P1 -.-> Q1
    P2 -.-> Q2
    P3 -.-> Q3
    P4 -.-> Q4
    subgraph SIDE[Side View Z]
        R1[Programmed Edge] --> R2[Cutter Centre Offset Rc] --> R3[Actual Cutter Edge]
    end
```

Lab students are expected to draw the **machine layout** (front view with X-Y-Z axes) and **tool path sketches** in their practical record as per KTU lab manual requirements.

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

> **Differentiate between "Surface Milling" and "Pocket Milling" with suitable examples and one G-code distinction.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Surface Milling | Pocket Milling |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Produce a flat reference face on top of the workpiece | Cut a closed recess of defined depth inside a profile |
| **Example** | Facing a 100×80 mm MS block | Milling a 60×40×4 mm pocket in the same block |
| **Tool** | Face mill (multi-insert) | End mill (carbide) |
| **G-code distinction** | Predominantly **G01** in one direction, repeated with step-over; **no G41/G42** | Predominantly **G01** in zig-zag raster, with optional **G41/G42** for finishing wall pass |

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

> **What is Cutter Diameter Compensation (CDC)? Why is it mandatory in contour milling but not in surface milling?**

**Model Answer:**

**Cutter Diameter Compensation (CDC)** is a controller feature (invoked by **G41** = left, **G42** = right, **G40** = cancel) that automatically offsets the programmed path by the cutter radius stored in a **D-offset register**.

- It is **mandatory in contour milling** because the part drawing defines the *workpiece edge*, but the NC machine moves the *cutter centre*. The offset of $R_c = D/2$ must be added (G41) or subtracted (G42) from every coordinate.
- It is **not required in surface milling** because the cutter is wider than the programmed step-over band and the path is open; the finishing boundary is dictated by the cutter diameter, not by a closed edge that must be dimensionally accurate.

---

## Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Provided)

### Question B1A [KTU University Exam – Jul 2024] — 14 Marks

> **For a CNC milling operation on a 150 × 100 × 20 mm mild steel workpiece, the following profile is to be milled to a depth of 3 mm using a $\phi 12$ mm 4-flute carbide end mill: a rectangular pocket of 80 × 50 mm. Given $V_c = 100$ m/min, $f_z = 0.06$ mm/tooth, compute all cutting parameters and write the complete G-code program with cutter compensation for the finishing pass.**

**Sub-Part (a) — 7 Marks: Compute cutting parameters**

> The student must compute $N$, $v_f$, $a_e$, $a_{p,\text{peck}}$, number of passes, and MRR.

**Step 1 — Spindle speed:**

$$
N = \frac{V_c \times 1000}{\pi \cdot D} = \frac{100 \times 1000}{\pi \times 12} = 2652.58 \text{ rpm}
$$

$$
\boxed{N_{\text{prog}} = 2650 \text{ rpm}}
$$

**Step 2 — Feed rate:**

$$
v_f = f_z \cdot z \cdot N = 0.06 \times 4 \times 2650 = 636 \text{ mm/min}
$$

$$
\boxed{v_f = 636 \text{ mm/min}}
$$

**Step 3 — Step-over (50% rule):**

$$
a_e = 0.5 \times D = 0.5 \times 12 = 6 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Number of raster lines (along 80 mm):**

$$
n_{XY} = \frac{80}{6} = 13.33 \rightarrow 14 \text{ passes}
$$

**Step 5 — Step-down (depth 3 mm, 1.0D rule):**

$$
a_{p,\text{peck}} = 1.0 \times 12 = 12 \text{ mm} \gg 3 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\boxed{n_Z = 1 \text{ pass (3 mm single plunge)}}
$$

**Step 6 — Material Removal Rate:**

$$
MRR = a_e \cdot a_p \cdot v_f = 6 \times 3 \times 636 = 11{,}448 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}
$$

$$
\boxed{MRR = 11{,}448 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}}
$$

**Sub-Part (b) — 7 Marks: Write the complete G-code program**

```gcode
O0010 (POCKET 80x50x3 MS - D12 END MILL)
G21 G90 G94 G17 G54
G00 Z25.0
T01 M06                     (TOOL 1 = D12 4-FLUTE CARBIDE)
M03 S2650
G00 X-46.0 Y-30.0           (ABOVE POCKET CORNER)
M08
G00 Z2.0
G01 Z-3.0 F80               (PLUNGE)

(--- ROUGHING ZIG-ZAG RASTER ---)
G01 Y25.0 F636
G01 X40.0
G01 Y20.0
G01 X-40.0
G01 Y15.0
G01 X40.0
G01 Y10.0
G01 X-40.0
G01 Y5.0
G01 X40.0
G01 Y0.0
G01 X-40.0
G01 Y-5.0
G01 X40.0
G01 Y-10.0
G01 X-40.0
G01 Y-15.0
G01 X40.0
G01 Y-20.0
G01 X-40.0
G01 Y-25.0
G01 X40.0

(--- FINISHING WITH CUTTER COMPENSATION ---)
G00 Z25.0
G00 X-46.0 Y-30.0
G00 Z2.0
G01 Z-3.0 F80
G41 D01 X-40.0 Y-25.0 F300
G01 X40.0                   (BOTTOM EDGE)
G01 Y25.0                   (RIGHT EDGE)
G01 X-40.0                  (TOP EDGE)
G01 Y-25.0                  (LEFT EDGE CLOSE)
G40 G00 X-46.0 Y-30.0
G00 Z25.0
M09
M05
G28 X0 Y0 Z0
M30
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question B1A:**
> 1. **Forgetting D01 offset register initialization:** The controller must have **D01 = 6.0** (cutter radius) pre-set in the offset table. Program running with D01 = 0 will cut a pocket 12 mm oversized. **Loss: 1.5 marks**
> 2. **Wrong sign on $N$ rounding:** Students often use $N = 2650$ but compute $v_f$ with the unrounded $N = 2652.58$. Always use the **rounded programmed value** for consistency. **Loss: 1 mark**
> 3. **Mixing G90 and G91 in the same program:** Absolute (G90) and incremental (G91) cannot be mixed mid-program without explicit re-declaration. **Loss: 0.5 marks**
> 4. **Forgetting coolant M08 before cutting:** Most lab machines refuse to start the spindle without M08 active. **Loss: 0.5 marks**

---

### Question B1B [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] — 14 Marks (ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)

> **Explain with a neat G-code program how a closed triangular contour (vertices: A(20,20), B(80,20), C(50,70)) is milled to a depth of 4 mm in an aluminium block using a $\phi 10$ mm 2-flute end mill. Given $V_c = 200$ m/min, $f_z = 0.05$ mm/tooth. Apply cutter diameter compensation.**

**Sub-Part (a) — 7 Marks: Parameter calculation + path length**

**Step 1 — Spindle speed:**

$$
N = \frac{200 \times 1000}{\pi \times 10} = 6366.20 \text{ rpm}
$$

$$
\boxed{N_{\text{prog}} = 6400 \text{ rpm}}
$$

**Step 2 — Feed rate:**

$$
v_f = 0.05 \times 2 \times 6400 = 640 \text{ mm/min}
$$

**Step 3 — Cutter radius for D-offset register:**

$$
R_c = 5.0 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Side lengths using distance formula:**

$$
AB = \sqrt{(80-20)^2 + 0} = 60 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
BC = \sqrt{(50-80)^2 + (70-20)^2} = \sqrt{900 + 2500} = \sqrt{3400} = 58.31 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
CA = \sqrt{(20-50)^2 + (20-70)^2} = \sqrt{900 + 2500} = 58.31 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 5 — Total perimeter:**

$$
L = 60 + 58.31 + 58.31 = 176.62 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 6 — Machining time:**

$$
T_m = \frac{176.62}{640} = 0.276 \text{ min} \approx 16.6 \text{ sec}
$$

**Sub-Part (b) — 7 Marks: G-code with G41 (CCW) or G42 (CW) analysis**

Direction check: Traversing **A→B→C→A** is **counter-clockwise (CCW)** when viewed from +Z. So **G41 (left comp.)** applies.

```gcode
O0020 (TRIANGULAR CONTOUR MILLING)
G21 G90 G94 G17 G54
G00 Z25.0
T02 M06                     (TOOL 2 = D10 2-FLUTE HSS)
M03 S6400
G00 X10.0 Y10.0             (LEAD-IN BELOW VERTEX A)
M08
G00 Z2.0
G01 Z-4.0 F80               (PLUNGE TO DEPTH)

(--- LEAD-IN AND PROFILE ---)
G41 D02 X20.0 Y20.0 F300
G01 X80.0 Y20.0 F640        (A -> B)
G01 X50.0 Y70.0             (B -> C)
G01 X20.0 Y20.0             (C -> A CLOSE)

(--- LEAD-OUT ---)
G02 X10.0 Y10.0 I-10.0 J0   (LEAD-OUT ARC)
G40 G00 X10.0 Y10.0
G00 Z25.0
M09
M05
G28 X0 Y0 Z0
M30
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question B1B:**
> 1. **Wrong G41/G42 selection:** Direction (CW vs CCW) is the most common error. Always draw a 2D sketch with arrows and stand at +Z looking at the part. If the path is CCW, use G41; if CW, use G42. **Loss: 2 marks**
> 2. **Forgetting to declare D-offset register** in the same block as G41/G42 (e.g., `G41 D02`). The controller will alarm. **Loss: 1 mark**
> 3. **Incorrect Z plunge rate:** Plunging at full feed rate breaks end mills. Use **40–80 mm/min** for the G01 Z move. **Loss: 1 mark**
> 4. **Not closing the loop:** The last G01 must return to the starting vertex for a true closed contour; otherwise the controller interpolates an open chord across. **Loss: 0.5 marks**
> 5. **Using `G43 H00` without setting H-offset:** The H-offset table must contain the measured tool length for Tool 2. **Loss: 0.5 marks**

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Pin This on Your Lab Wall)**

- [x] **Surface Milling** → face mill, 0.6D–0.8D step-over, single shallow $a_p$, **NO cutter comp.**
- [x] **Pocket Milling** → end mill, 0.4D–0.5D step-over, multi-Z pecking, zig-zag raster, **optional G41 for finish**
- [x] **Contour Milling** → end mill, single pass along closed path, **G41/G42 mandatory**, lead-in/out arc $\geq 1.5 R_c$
- [x] **Three Golden Formulas** (memorize verbatim):
  - $N = \dfrac{V_c \times 1000}{\pi \cdot D}$ — **rpm**
  - $v_f = f_z \cdot z \cdot N$ — **mm/min**
  - $MRR = a_e \cdot a_p \cdot v_f$ — **mm³/min**
- [x] **Always use G21 (mm) and G90 (absolute)** at the top of every KTU program
- [x] **Always include M08 (coolant ON) before M03 and M09 (coolant OFF) before M05**
- [x] **Always end with G28 X0 Y0 Z0 and M30** — this is the KTU board examiner's "comfort blanket"
- [x] **Cutter comp. direction (G41 vs G42):** Look down +Z, traverse the path; if the part is on your **left**, use **G41**; if on the **right**, use **G42**
- [x] **D-offset register** stores cutter radius for G41/G42; **H-offset register** stores tool length for G43
- [x] **Spindle speed rounding rule:** Round to nearest **50 rpm** for HSS and **100 rpm** for carbide
- [x] **Feed per tooth ($f_z$):** MS = 0.05–0.10; Aluminium = 0.04–0.10; Stainless = 0.03–0.06
- [x] **Cutting speed ($V_c$):** MS = 30–50 (HSS), 80–120 (carbide); Aluminium = 100–150 (HSS), 200–400 (carbide)
- [x] **Simulation before cut:** Always backplot the program (e.g., in NCViewer, Predator Virtual CNC) before transferring to the machine
- [x] **Dry run:** Run the program with $Z$ lifted by **+5 to +10 mm** to verify path direction and tool changes

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Viva Tip:** If the examiner asks *"Why is your step-over 50% of D for pocket milling and not 80% like in surface milling?"* — the answer is: **Surface milling has open paths with chip evacuation on both sides (low load), while pocket milling confines the cutter inside a closed wall, doubling the radial engagement and tripling the chip load per tooth; reducing step-over to 50% keeps the chip load within the recommended $f_z$ range.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Metallurgy: Specimen Preparation, Etching and Microscopic Study of Steel, Cast Iron and Brass and Grain Size Measurement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMEL408 - MANUFACTURING TECHNOLOGY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Metallurgy: Specimen Preparation, Etching and Microscopic Study of Steel, Cast Iron and Brass and Grain Size Measurement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition: Metallographic Specimen Preparation & Microscopic Study

**Metallography** is the scientific study of the microstructure of metals and alloys, involving the systematic preparation of specimens, controlled chemical etching, and observation under high-magnification optical microscopes to reveal grain boundaries, phases, inclusions, and structural defects.

**Specimen preparation** is the sequential, multi-stage process of sectioning, mounting, grinding, and polishing a metal sample to produce a flat, mirror-like, scratch-free surface suitable for microscopic examination.

**Etching** is the controlled chemical (or electrochemical) attack of a polished metal surface using a suitable reagent (etchant) that preferentially dissolves grain boundaries, phase interfaces, or specific crystallographic orientations, thereby producing contrast under the optical microscope.

**Grain size measurement** is the quantitative metallographic determination of the average grain diameter (or grain count per unit area) of a polycrystalline metal, standardized under ASTM E112.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCMEL408 – Module 2):**
> Students are expected to **independently prepare, etch, and examine** metallographic specimens of **mild steel, gray cast iron, and brass**, and to perform **quantitative grain size measurement** using either the **Heyn Linear Intercept Method** or the **Jeffries Planimetric Method**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: "Reading a Metal's Fingerprint"

Imagine a polished metal surface as a **smooth, glass-like wall that hides the actual building blocks (grains) of the metal beneath it**. If you shine a flashlight directly on this wall, the light reflects uniformly — you see no structure. To reveal the hidden "brickwork," a metallurgist must:

1. **Cut a small sample** (like taking a small brick from a wall) — this is **sectioning**.
2. **Sand it down** with progressively finer sandpaper — this is **grinding**.
3. **Polish it** until it's as shiny as a mirror — this is **polishing**.
4. **Spray it with a weak acid** (etchant) that eats away at the grain boundaries — this is **etching**. The acid digs tiny grooves along the boundaries, which scatter light and appear dark under the microscope, **outlining each grain like a black ink drawing**.
5. **Look at it under a microscope** — the **microscopic study** reveals the **grain size, shape, and phase distribution**, which directly indicates the metal's **strength, ductility, and heat-treatment history**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this critical in engineering?**
> The **grain size** of a metal follows the famous **Hall-Petch relationship**, where yield strength increases as grain size decreases. Thus, grain size measurement is not academic — it is a **direct, quantitative predictor of mechanical properties**.

| Term | Standard Symbol / Unit | Definition |
|---|---|---|
| Mean Linear Intercept | $\bar{L}$ (µm or mm) | Average distance between grain boundaries along a test line |
| ASTM Grain Size Number | $G$ or $N$ (dimensionless) | Index defined by ASTM E112: $n = 2^{G-1}$ grains/in² at 100× |
| Magnification | $M$ (×) | Optical magnification used during measurement |
| Etchant | Chemical solution | Selective reagent that reveals microstructure |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Sequential Stages of Specimen Preparation

The metallographic workflow is a strict sequence — skipping or reordering any step leads to artifacts (false structures) that **mislead** the examiner.

### Stage 1: Sectioning (Sampling)
- A small representative piece (typically 10–25 mm diameter) is cut from the bulk using an **abrasive cut-off wheel** with continuous coolant.
- Heat generation must be minimized to **avoid altering the microstructure** (e.g., phase transformations, tempering).

### Stage 2: Mounting
- The irregular sample is embedded in a **molding compound** (phenolic resin, epoxy, or acrylic) to provide a uniform size and edge retention.
- **Cold mounting** (resin + hardener) for delicate specimens; **hot compression mounting** (bakelite) for routine work.

### Stage 3: Grinding (Planarization)
- The mounted specimen is ground on a series of **silicon carbide (SiC) abrasive papers** of progressively decreasing grit size.
- Standard grit sequence: **120 → 240 → 320 → 400 → 600 → 800/1000 grit**.
- Water serves as a lubricant and coolant; specimen is rotated 90° between each grit to **remove scratches from the previous stage**.

### Stage 4: Polishing
- Two sub-stages:
  - **Rough polishing:** 6 µm and 1 µm diamond paste on a napless cloth.
  - **Final polishing:** 0.05 µm colloidal silica or alumina on a soft napped cloth.
- Goal: produce a **scratch-free, mirror-bright surface** (often called the "mirror finish").

### Stage 5: Etching
- The polished surface is immersed in (or swabbed with) a **selective chemical reagent** for a controlled time (seconds to minutes).
- The etchant attacks:
  - **Grain boundaries** first (higher energy regions) → produce dark lines.
  - **Different phases** at different rates → produce color/shade contrast.

### Stage 6: Microscopic Examination
- Observed under a **metallurgical microscope** (bright-field, polarized light, or DIC modes).
- Magnifications used: **50×, 100×, 200×, 500×, 1000×**.
- Photomicrographs are taken for record and grain size analysis.

## 2.2 Etchants for the Three Specimens of Interest (KTU 2024 Specific)

| Material | Recommended Etchant | Composition | Etching Time | Microstructure Revealed |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Mild Steel** | **2% Nital** | 2 mL HNO₃ + 98 mL Ethanol | 5–15 seconds | Ferrite (white), Pearlite (dark lamellar) |
| **Gray Cast Iron** | **4% Picral** | 4 g Picric acid + 100 mL Ethanol | 10–30 seconds | Graphite flakes (dark), Pearlite matrix |
| **Brass** | **NH₄OH + H₂O₂ mixture** | 1 part NH₄OH + 1 part H₂O₂ (30%) | 5–10 seconds | α-phase (light), β-phase (dark) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Nital** is the most widely used general-purpose etchant for carbon steels. **Picral** is preferred for cast irons and high-carbon steels because it etches pearlite without staining ferrite. **Ammonia + peroxide** reveals the phase contrast in copper-zinc alloys (brasses) without attacking the surface.

## 2.3 Microstructural Features of the Three Materials

### (a) Mild Steel (Ferrite + Pearlite)
- **Ferrite (α-Fe):** BCC, soft, ductile, appears as **white equiaxed grains**.
- **Pearlite (Fe₃C + α-Fe):** Lamellar structure, appears as **dark "fingerprint" or layered regions**.
- The **relative area fraction** of pearlite indicates carbon content.

### (b) Gray Cast Iron
- **Graphite flakes:** Dark, elongated, randomly oriented flakes in a metallic matrix.
- **Matrix:** Pearlite + ferrite (or fully ferritic in low-strength grades).
- Flake morphology reduces ductility (stress concentrators).

### (c) Brass (Cu-Zn alloy)
- **α-phase:** FCC, zinc solubility up to 39%, light-etching, equiaxed grains.
- **β-phase:** BCC, Zn > 39%, dark-etching, appears at grain boundaries or as Widmanstätten plates in two-phase brasses.

## 2.4 Grain Size Measurement: The Two KTU-Accepted Methods

### Method 1: Heyn Linear Intercept Method
A straight test line of known length is drawn across the micrograph, and the **number of grain boundaries intersected** is counted.

$$
\bar{L} = \frac{L_T}{N_{int} \cdot M}
$$

Where:
- $\bar{L}$ = mean linear intercept length (in mm or µm)
- $L_T$ = true length of the test line on the specimen (mm)
- $N_{int}$ = total number of grain boundary intersections
- $M$ = magnification used

### Method 2: Jeffries Planimetric Method
A circle (or rectangle) of known area is drawn on the micrograph. The number of **complete grains inside** the circle plus **half the number of grains intersected by the circle boundary** is counted. The result is converted to **grains per mm²** at 1×.

$$
N_A = \frac{N_{inside} + \tfrac{1}{2} N_{boundary}}{A_{true}}
$$

Where:
- $N_A$ = number of grains per mm²
- $N_{inside}$ = number of grains fully inside the test area
- $N_{boundary}$ = number of grains intersecting the boundary
- $A_{true} = \dfrac{A_{apparent}}{M^2}$ is the true area on the specimen

## 2.5 ASTM Grain Size Number (E112)

The ASTM number $G$ relates to the number of grains per square inch at **100× magnification**:

$$
n_{100} = 2^{G - 1}
$$

Conversion to mean linear intercept:

$$
\bar{L} \text{ (mm)} = \frac{1}{M} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{n_{100}}} \cdot 25.4
$$

The relation between ASTM $G$ and mean linear intercept is tabulated in ASTM E112.

> [!TIP]
> **Hall-Petch Connection (Why this matters!):** For most metals,
> $$\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \cdot d^{-1/2}$$
> A smaller grain size $d$ (or $\bar{L}$) → higher yield strength. This is the engineering reason grain size measurement is mandatory in KTU lab records.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Formula Name | Equation | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mean Linear Intercept | $\bar{L} = L_T / (N_{int} \cdot M)$ | mm | Heyn method |
| Grain Count per Unit Area | $N_A = (N_{in} + \tfrac{1}{2} N_{bdry})/A_{true}$ | grains/mm² | Jeffries method |
| ASTM Grain Number | $n_{100} = 2^{G-1}$ | grains/in² | ASTM E112 |
| True Test Area | $A_{true} = A_{app} / M^2$ | mm² | Both methods |
| Hall-Petch Strength | $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \cdot d^{-1/2}$ | MPa | Property prediction |
| Magnification Relation | $M = \dfrac{\text{Linear mag on print}}{\text{True linear mag}}$ | dimensionless | Unit conversion |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Mean Linear Intercept (Heyn Method) — Worked Example

**Problem:** A micrograph of mild steel is taken at **M = 100×**. A test line of length **100 mm** is drawn on the photomicrograph, and **48 grain boundaries** are intersected. Calculate the mean linear intercept in mm and the corresponding ASTM grain size number.

### Step 1 — Identify True Test Line Length
The test line on the photomicrograph is 100 mm long, but the actual length on the specimen is smaller by a factor of $M$:

$$
L_{true} = \frac{L_{apparent}}{M} = \frac{100 \text{ mm}}{100} = 1.0 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Heyn Formula

$$
\bar{L} = \frac{L_{true}}{N_{int}} = \frac{1.0 \text{ mm}}{48} = 0.02083 \text{ mm}
$$

Convert to micrometers (1 mm = 1000 µm):

$$
\bar{L} = 0.02083 \times 1000 = 20.83 \text{ µm}
$$

### Step 3 — Convert to ASTM Grain Size Number
Use the relation:

$$
\bar{L} \text{ (in)} = \frac{\bar{L} \text{ (mm)}}{25.4} = \frac{0.02083}{25.4} = 8.20 \times 10^{-4} \text{ in}
$$

Grains per inch at 1×: $1/\bar{L} = 1/(8.20 \times 10^{-4}) = 1219$ grains/in.

Grains per inch² at 100×:

$$
n_{100} = (1219)^2 = 1.486 \times 10^{6} \text{ grains/in}^2
$$

Solve for ASTM G:

$$
G - 1 = \log_2(n_{100}) = \frac{\log(1.486 \times 10^{6})}{\log(2)} = \frac{6.172}{0.301} = 20.50
$$

$$
G = 21.50 - 1 = 20.50 \approx 9
$$

(Using ASTM E112 table directly: $\bar{L} = 20.8$ µm corresponds to ASTM G ≈ 9 — a fine-grained steel.)

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering interpretation:** A fine-grained steel (G ≈ 9) is significantly stronger than a coarse-grained steel (G ≈ 3) per the Hall-Petch equation.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Jeffries Planimetric Method — Worked Example

**Problem:** A circular test area of radius **50 mm** is drawn on a photomicrograph taken at **200×**. The count yields: **35 grains fully inside** the circle and **22 grains intersecting the boundary**. Find the grains per mm² and the ASTM grain size.

### Step 1 — Apparent Area of the Test Circle

$$
A_{app} = \pi r^2 = \pi (50)^2 = 7853.98 \text{ mm}^2
$$

### Step 2 — True Area on the Specimen

$$
A_{true} = \frac{A_{app}}{M^2} = \frac{7853.98}{(200)^2} = \frac{7853.98}{40000} = 0.1963 \text{ mm}^2
$$

### Step 3 — Effective Number of Grains (Jeffries Rule)

$$
N_{eff} = N_{inside} + \frac{1}{2} N_{boundary} = 35 + \frac{1}{2}(22) = 35 + 11 = 46 \text{ grains}
$$

### Step 4 — Grains per mm² at 1×

$$
N_A = \frac{N_{eff}}{A_{true}} = \frac{46}{0.1963} = 234.3 \text{ grains/mm}^2
$$

### Step 5 — Convert to grains per in² at 100×
First, convert to grains/in² at 1×:

$$
N_A \text{ (in}^2\text{)} = 234.3 \times (25.4)^2 = 234.3 \times 645.16 = 151{,}181 \text{ grains/in}^2
$$

Then scale to 100×:

$$
n_{100} = 151{,}181 \times (100)^2 = 1.512 \times 10^{9} \text{ grains/in}^2
$$

ASTM G:

$$
G - 1 = \frac{\log(1.512 \times 10^{9})}{\log 2} = \frac{9.179}{0.301} = 30.49
$$

$$
G = 31.49 \implies G \approx 13
$$

(For practical lab report writing, round to the nearest integer; ASTM G = 13 corresponds to a very fine grain, $\bar{L} \approx 5$ µm.)

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Etchant Selection Decision Table (KTU 2024)

| Material | Primary Etchant | Alternate Etchant | Phase That Etches Darkest | Phase That Etches Lightest | Common Mistake to Avoid |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mild Steel (low C) | 2% Nital | 4% Picral | Pearlite | Ferrite | Over-etching → pitting |
| Gray Cast Iron | 4% Picral | 2% Nital | Graphite flakes, Pearlite | Ferrite | Using only Nital → smudges graphite |
| Ductile (Nodular) Cast Iron | 4% Picral | — | Nodules, Pearlite | Ferrite | Polishing too long → pulls out nodules |
| Brass (α) | NH₄OH + H₂O₂ | KCN + H₂O₂ (toxic) | β-phase | α-phase | Leaving H₂O₂ too long → heavy oxidation |
| Brass (α+β) | NH₄OH + H₂O₂ | FeCl₃ + HCl | β-phase (BCC) | α-phase (FCC) | Confusing α vs β contrast |

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Lab Procedure Summary (for KTU Record Submission)

1. **Cut** a 15 mm × 15 mm sample using abrasive cut-off wheel with coolant.
2. **Mount** in phenolic resin at 130 °C, 20 MPa for 5–8 minutes.
3. **Grind** sequentially: 120 → 240 → 320 → 400 → 600 → 1000 grit SiC paper; rotate specimen 90° between grades; rinse and dry.
4. **Rough polish** with 6 µm diamond paste on a napless cloth, 5 minutes.
5. **Final polish** with 0.05 µm colloidal silica on a soft napped cloth, 3–5 minutes until mirror finish.
6. **Etch:**
   - Mild steel → 2% Nital, 5–10 s, rinse with ethanol, dry.
   - Cast iron → 4% Picral, 15–30 s, rinse, dry.
   - Brass → NH₄OH + H₂O₂ (1:1), 5–10 s, rinse, dry.
7. **Examine** at 100× and 200× under metallurgical microscope; capture photomicrograph.
8. **Measure** grain size using Heyn or Jeffries method; record ASTM number.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
