# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Q: Define etching. Why is 2% Nital used as an etchant for mild steel?**

**Model Answer:**
Etching is the controlled chemical attack of a polished metal surface using a selective reagent that preferentially dissolves grain boundaries and phase interfaces, producing optical contrast under a metallurgical microscope.

2% Nital (2 mL HNO₃ + 98 mL ethanol) is used for mild steel because:
1. It preferentially attacks **grain boundaries** and the **pearlite phase**, leaving ferrite relatively unattacked.
2. It produces **clear, sharp contrast** between ferrite (white) and pearlite (dark lamellar).
3. It is **fast-acting and reproducible**, ideal for routine lab examination within 5–15 seconds of immersion.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often answer "Nital is used to corrode the metal" — this is too vague. Use the precise metallurgical phrase: *"preferential dissolution of grain boundaries to produce contrast."*

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**Q: List the sequential steps involved in the preparation of a metallographic specimen.**

**Model Answer:**
The sequential steps are:
1. **Sectioning** – cutting a representative sample using an abrasive cut-off wheel with coolant.
2. **Mounting** – embedding the sample in phenolic/epoxy resin for easy handling.
3. **Grinding** – sequential abrasion on SiC papers of 120, 240, 320, 400, 600, 1000 grit.
4. **Polishing** – rough polishing with 6 µm diamond paste, then final polishing with 0.05 µm colloidal silica to a mirror finish.
5. **Etching** – chemical attack using a suitable reagent to reveal microstructure.
6. **Microscopic examination** – observation at 50×–1000× under a metallurgical microscope.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **Heyn Linear Intercept Method** for grain size measurement. Derive the formula and state its significance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A micrograph of brass is taken at **M = 200×**. A test line of **80 mm** drawn on the photograph intersects **36 grain boundaries**. Calculate the mean linear intercept and the ASTM grain size number. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):
- Heyn's method uses a straight test line of known length $L_T$ drawn randomly across the micrograph.
- The number of grain boundaries intersected, $N_{int}$, is counted.
- The mean linear intercept is given by:

$$
\bar{L} = \frac{L_{true}}{N_{int}} = \frac{L_{apparent}}{M \cdot N_{int}}
$$

- **Significance:**
  1. It is the **simplest and most direct** grain size measurement technique.
  2. It directly yields a **mean linear dimension** that can be plugged into the **Hall-Petch equation** for strength estimation.
  3. ASTM E112 standardized tabulated values allow direct conversion of $\bar{L}$ to ASTM grain size number $G$.

#### Model Solution for (b):
**Step 1:** True length of test line:

$$
L_{true} = \frac{L_{apparent}}{M} = \frac{80 \text{ mm}}{200} = 0.40 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2:** Mean linear intercept:

$$
\bar{L} = \frac{0.40}{36} = 0.01111 \text{ mm} = 11.11 \text{ µm}
$$

**Step 3:** Convert to ASTM grain size.
$\bar{L} = 11.11$ µm. From ASTM E112 Table 4, this corresponds approximately to **ASTM G ≈ 10** (fine grain).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students forget to convert the test line length from "apparent" (on the photograph) to "true" (on the specimen). Always divide apparent length by magnification $M$. Forgetting this step yields an answer that is **200× too large** — guaranteed loss of 2 marks.

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Compare the microstructures of **mild steel, gray cast iron, and brass** with suitable diagrams. List the etchants used for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the **Jeffries Planimetric Method**, a circular test area of **40 mm radius** on a micrograph taken at **M = 100×** contains **40 full grains** and **18 boundary-intersecting grains**. Compute the ASTM grain size number. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

| Material | Microstructure | Etchant | Phases Visible |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mild Steel** | Equiaxed ferrite grains (white) with interspersed pearlite colonies (dark, lamellar) | 2% Nital | α-Fe (light) + Pearlite (dark) |
| **Gray Cast Iron** | Graphite flakes (dark, needle-like) in a pearlitic/ferritic matrix | 4% Picral | Graphite (dark) + Matrix (mixed) |
| **Brass** | Equiaxed α-grains (light); β-phase (dark) at boundaries or as plates | NH₄OH + H₂O₂ | α-phase (light) + β-phase (dark) |

- Mild steel derives strength from **fine ferrite + fine pearlite** distribution.
- Gray cast iron has **low ductility** because graphite flakes act as stress concentrators.
- Brass with higher Zn content shows **two-phase (α+β)** structure with increasing hardness.

#### Model Solution for (b):
**Step 1:** Apparent area of the test circle:

$$
A_{app} = \pi r^2 = \pi (40)^2 = 5026.55 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 2:** True area on specimen:

$$
A_{true} = \frac{A_{app}}{M^2} = \frac{5026.55}{100^2} = 0.5027 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 3:** Effective grain count:

$$
N_{eff} = 40 + \frac{1}{2}(18) = 40 + 9 = 49 \text{ grains}
$$

**Step 4:** Grains per mm²:

$$
N_A = \frac{49}{0.5027} = 97.47 \text{ grains/mm}^2
$$

**Step 5:** Convert to grains per in² at 1×:

$$
N_A (\text{in}^2) = 97.47 \times (25.4)^2 = 97.47 \times 645.16 = 62{,}886 \text{ grains/in}^2
$$

**Step 6:** At 100×:

$$
n_{100} = 62{,}886 \times 100^2 = 6.29 \times 10^8 \text{ grains/in}^2
$$

**Step 7:** ASTM G:

$$
G - 1 = \frac{\log(6.29 \times 10^8)}{\log 2} = \frac{8.799}{0.301} = 29.23
$$

$$
G = 30.23 \implies G \approx 7
$$

**Result:** ASTM grain size number **G ≈ 7**, indicating a **medium-to-coarse grain** structure.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall – Common Mark Loss Points:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $M^2$ factor** when converting apparent area to true area (loses 2 marks).
> 2. **Incorrect Jeffries rule:** students sometimes use $N_{inside} + N_{boundary}$ instead of $N_{inside} + \tfrac{1}{2} N_{boundary}$ (loses 1 mark).
> 3. **Mixing up units:** grains/mm² must be converted to grains/in² before applying ASTM relation $n_{100} = 2^{G-1}$ (loses 1 mark).
> 4. **No unit conversion of test circle area:** if radius is in mm, the apparent area is already in mm²; do not convert to m² prematurely.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (High-Density Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Rapid Revision Checklist – Module 2 (Metallurgy Lab)**

**Specimen Preparation:**
- Six stages: **Sectioning → Mounting → Grinding → Polishing → Etching → Microscopy**.
- SiC paper sequence: **120 → 240 → 320 → 400 → 600 → 1000 grit**.
- Rotate specimen **90°** between successive grit sizes.
- Use **diamond paste (6 µm)** for rough polish; **colloidal silica (0.05 µm)** for final polish.
- Final surface must be a **mirror finish** — scratch-free.

**Etchants:**
- **Mild steel → 2% Nital** (HNO₃ + ethanol); 5–15 s.
- **Gray cast iron → 4% Picral** (picric acid + ethanol); 15–30 s.
- **Brass → NH₄OH + H₂O₂ (1:1)**; 5–10 s.
- **Never** use Nital on stainless steel; it stains the surface.
- **Always** rinse with ethanol and dry with hot air after etching.

**Microstructures (KTU 2024 Specific):**
- **Mild steel:** white ferrite + dark pearlite.
- **Gray cast iron:** dark graphite flakes + pearlitic matrix.
- **Brass:** light α-phase + (optional) dark β-phase.

**Grain Size Formulas (MUST MEMORIZE):**
- **Heyn:** $\bar{L} = L_{true} / (M \cdot N_{int})$.
- **Jeffries:** $N_A = (N_{in} + \tfrac{1}{2} N_{bdry}) / A_{true}$.
- **ASTM:** $n_{100} = 2^{G-1}$.
- **Hall-Petch:** $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y d^{-1/2}$ (smaller grain → higher strength).

**Common Exam Traps:**
- Forgetting to convert **apparent** dimensions to **true** dimensions.
- Confusing **mm²** with **in²** in ASTM conversions.
- Reporting grain size without units of $\bar{L}$ (µm or mm).
- Etching too long → **pitting** (false structure, zero marks for micrograph).

**Examiner's Golden Rule:**
A well-drawn, labeled micrograph in your record carries **2 bonus marks** in the lab exam. Always label the **etchant, magnification, and phases** directly on the photomicrograph.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Exercises on Part Quality Inspection Using Machine Vision Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMEL408 - MANUFACTURING TECHNOLOGY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Exercises on Part Quality Inspection Using Machine Vision Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Machine Vision System (MVS)** is an integrated opto-electromechanical arrangement of image-capturing hardware (lenses, illumination, image sensors), image-processing electronics, and computational algorithms that automatically extracts, analyses, and interprets information from two-dimensional (2D) or three-dimensional (3D) images of physical objects for the purpose of non-contact quality assessment, dimensional gauging, surface-finish verification, and defect detection in a manufacturing environment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot:** Machine vision is officially defined as the use of computers and associated hardware (cameras, frame grabbers, lighting) to perform inspections that rely on the visual properties of manufactured components — replacing or augmenting the human eye in repetitive, high-speed, hazardous, or sub-micron-tolerance tasks.

**Part Quality Inspection** in the context of this experiment refers to the systematic verification of manufactured components against pre-defined geometric, dimensional, surface, and structural acceptance criteria using automated visual sensing, as opposed to traditional contact-based metrology (CMM, Vernier, micrometer).

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Robotic Eyeball Inspector":**
> Imagine a quality inspector standing at the end of a conveyor belt, holding a magnifying glass, a ruler, and a checklist. The human eye scans every component, brain judges if it matches the "golden sample" stored in memory, and the hand stamps "PASS" or "REJECT." A machine vision system is a silicon replica of this exact workflow: the **camera** is the eyeball, the **illumination** is the uniform shop-floor lighting, the **lens** is the magnifying glass, the **frame grabber + processor** is the brain, and the **actuator / sorting mechanism** is the stamping hand. The crucial upgrade is **consistency**: the silicon eyeball never tires, never blinks, and never has a bad Monday.

## 1.3 Classification of Inspection Tasks in Lab Context

| Inspection Category | What is Checked? | Typical Industrial Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Dimensional Gauging** | Length, diameter, hole position | Bearing inner race diameter |
| **Surface Defect Detection** | Scratches, cracks, pits | Sheet metal paint inspection |
| **Assembly Verification** | Presence/absence of components | PCB component placement check |
| **Surface Finish / Texture** | Roughness, gloss | Polished gear tooth surface |
| **Geometric / Form Errors** | Squareness, roundness, concentricity | Shaft runout measurement |
| **Character / Code Reading** | OCR, barcodes, datamatrix | Traceability marking on parts |

## 1.4 Standard Industrial Metrics Highlighted in the KTU Syllabus

- **Resolution** — expressed in **pixels (px)**, **line pairs per millimetre (lp/mm)**, or **micrometres (µm)**.
- **Field of View (FOV)** — physical area imaged, in **mm** or **m**.
- **Repeatability** — typically **±0.01 mm to ±0.05 mm** in industrial vision systems.
- **Throughput** — parts inspected per minute (**ppm**), commonly 60–6000 ppm.
- **Working Distance (WD)** — lens-to-object gap, in **mm**.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Eye-Catcher:** Always state the units explicitly when quoting any vision metric. A 5 MP camera is meaningless without the corresponding FOV and lens magnification that converts pixels into millimetres.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Architecture of a Machine Vision Station

A typical vision-based inspection cell comprises the following functional blocks arranged in series:

1. **Part Presentation Stage** — conveyor, vibratory bowl feeder, rotary indexing table, or robot-held fixture delivering the component to the **inspection zone** (often a fixed, repeatable coordinate frame).
2. **Illumination Subsystem** — the *single most important variable* in vision. Common geometries include:
   - **Diffuse dome / cloud light** (eliminates specular hot-spots on shiny surfaces)
   - **Ring light** (uniform frontal illumination, generic inspection)
   - **Backlight** (creates high-contrast silhouette — ideal for dimensional gauging)
   - **Coaxial light** (light travels along the optical axis — for flat reflective parts)
   - **Structured / line laser light** (for 3D profile extraction)
3. **Optics (Lens Assembly)** — determines magnification, FOV, and depth of field. Fixed focal length, varifocal, or telecentric (zero-magnification-error lens used for precise metrology).
4. **Image Sensor (Camera)** — **CCD** (Charge-Coupled Device) for low noise / high uniformity, or **CMOS** (Complementary Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor) for high speed and on-chip processing. Sensor format commonly **1/3″, 1/2″, 2/3″, 1″**.
5. **Frame Grabber / Interface** — converts analogue camera signal to digital or directly streams digital data via **GigE Vision, USB3 Vision, Camera Link, or CoaXPress**.
6. **Processing Unit** — industrial PC, embedded smart camera, or FPGA-based processor.
7. **Software Stack** — image acquisition, pre-processing, segmentation, feature extraction, classification, decision logic, and **I/O** (pass/fail signal to PLC, conveyor diverter, rejection pusher).
8. **Communication / Reject Mechanism** — pneumatic pusher, robotic pick-and-place, or label printer.

> [!NOTE]
> **The 80/20 Rule of Machine Vision:** Approximately 80% of failed vision installations are attributable to poor or inconsistent lighting — *not* the camera, software, or algorithm. Selecting the correct illumination geometry is therefore the most cost-effective engineering decision in any inspection cell.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following equations are the *high-frequency* items examiners set in calculation problems.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Variable Definitions | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Magnification (M)** | $M = \dfrac{h_{i}}{h_{o}} = \dfrac{\text{Sensor Size}}{\text{Field of View}}$ | $h_{i}$ = image height on sensor, $h_{o}$ = object height | unitless |
| 2 | **Field of View (FOV)** | $\text{FOV} = \dfrac{\text{Sensor Size}}{M}$ | Sensor active dimension | mm |
| 3 | **Object Resolution (R)** | $R = \dfrac{\text{FOV}}{N_{p}}$ | $N_{p}$ = number of pixels along that axis | mm/px |
| 4 | **Pixel Pitch (p)** | $p = \dfrac{\text{Sensor Size}}{N_{p}}$ | Sensor size along one axis | µm/px |
| 5 | **Lens Focal Length (f)** | $\dfrac{1}{f} = \dfrac{1}{u} + \dfrac{1}{v}$ | $u$ = object distance, $v$ = image distance | mm |
| 6 | **Working Distance (WD)** | $\text{WD} = \dfrac{f \times (1 + M)}{M}$ | $f$ = focal length, $M$ = magnification | mm |
| 7 | **Depth of Field (DOF)** | $\text{DOF} \approx \dfrac{2 \times N \times c \times (1 + M)}{M^{2}}$ | $N$ = f-number, $c$ = circle of confusion | mm |
| 8 | **Number of Pixels Needed** | $N_{p,\min} = \dfrac{\text{Tolerance}}{R_{\text{desired}}}$ | Tolerance = smallest feature to be resolved | px |
| 9 | **Required Sensor Resolution** | $\text{Res}_{\text{req}} = \dfrac{\text{FOV}}{\text{Tolerance}} \times 2$ | Factor of 2 (Nyquist criterion: ≥ 2 px per feature) | px |
| 10 | **Image Scale (mm/pixel)** | $S = \dfrac{\text{FOV}}{N_{p}}$ | Inverse of resolution | mm/px |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Nyquist Sampling Theorem in Vision:** A feature (edge, line, defect) must be covered by **at least 2 pixels** to be detectable. The strict KTU bench-mark is **≥ 4 pixels per smallest resolvable feature** to ensure sub-pixel accuracy. Always apply this factor in tolerance-versus-pixel calculations.

## 2.3 Image Processing Pipeline (Step Logic)

A canonical vision algorithm executes the following sequential stages. Each step has a specific *Why* and *How*:

- **Step 1 — Image Acquisition:** Capture a single frame or video stream from the sensor.
  *Why?* A clean, repeatable, well-lit image is the foundation of every downstream operation.
  *How?* Synchronise capture with an external trigger (encoder, photo-eye) so the part is always imaged at the same world-coordinate location.
- **Step 2 — Pre-processing:** Noise reduction (Gaussian, median filtering), histogram equalisation, shading correction.
  *Why?* Sensor noise, dust, lens vignetting, and ambient light variation introduce artefacts that destroy measurement accuracy.
- **Step 3 — Segmentation:** Isolate the region of interest (ROI) from the background.
  *Why?* Reduces the data to be processed, eliminates false features.
  *How?* Thresholding (global, adaptive Otsu), colour-space conversion, edge detection (Sobel, Canny, Laplacian of Gaussian).
- **Step 4 — Feature Extraction:** Quantify geometric or statistical properties of the ROI.
  *Why?* Converts pixel data into engineering units.
  *How?* Blob analysis (area, centroid, bounding box), contour extraction, moment invariants, Hough transforms (lines, circles).
- **Step 5 — Measurement / Classification:** Compare extracted features to reference values.
  *Why?* Yields a yes/no decision or a graded output.
  *How?* Geometric dimensioning (distance, angle, diameter), template matching, statistical pattern recognition, deep-learning CNN classification.
- **Step 6 — Decision & Output:** Generate pass/fail, log results, drive actuators, communicate with PLC via **OPC UA, Profinet, or digital I/O**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry:** 100% inline inspection of engine blocks, pistons, gears — replacing hand-gauge cells.
- **Pharmaceutical:** Vial fill-level, label orientation, blister-pack integrity, character verification.
- **Electronics:** Pick-and-place verification, solder-paste inspection (SPI), AOI of PCBs.
- **Food & Beverage:** Foreign object detection, cap seal integrity, fill volume.
- **Additive Manufacturing / 3D Printing:** Layer-wise anomaly detection, geometric tolerance verification of finished prints.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Field of View from Sensor Format and Working Distance

A **1/3″ CMOS sensor** has an active area of **4.8 mm × 3.6 mm** (diagonal ≈ **6 mm**). A lens of focal length **f = 8 mm** is fitted. The part must be inspected at a working distance of **WD = 200 mm**. Find the on-axis FOV.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Compute image distance (v) from the thin-lens equation.**

The thin-lens conjugate relation is:

$$
\dfrac{1}{f} = \dfrac{1}{u} + \dfrac{1}{v}
$$

For object distance $u = \text{WD} = 200$ mm and $f = 8$ mm:

$$
\dfrac{1}{v} = \dfrac{1}{f} - \dfrac{1}{u} = \dfrac{1}{8} - \dfrac{1}{200}
$$

Finding a common denominator:

$$
\dfrac{1}{v} = \dfrac{25}{200} - \dfrac{1}{200} = \dfrac{24}{200}
$$

Therefore:

$$
v = \dfrac{200}{24} = 8.333 \text{ mm}
$$

> The image forms only slightly beyond the back focal point because the object is far relative to the focal length.

**Step 2 — Compute lateral magnification (M).**

$$
M = \dfrac{v}{u} = \dfrac{8.333}{200} = 0.04167
$$

This corresponds to a *demagnification* — the object's image is smaller than the object itself, which is the standard case in machine vision.

**Step 3 — Compute Field of View.**

$$
\text{FOV}_{\text{horizontal}} = \dfrac{\text{Sensor Width}}{M} = \dfrac{4.8}{0.04167}
$$

$$
\text{FOV}_{\text{horizontal}} = 115.2 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\text{FOV}_{\text{vertical}} = \dfrac{\text{Sensor Height}}{M} = \dfrac{3.6}{0.04167} = 86.4 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Summary of results.**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Image distance $v$ | **8.333 mm** |
| Magnification $M$ | **0.04167 (×)** |
| Horizontal FOV | **115.2 mm** |
| Vertical FOV | **86.4 mm** |

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Number of Pixels Required for a Given Tolerance

A machined washer has an outer diameter tolerance of **±0.05 mm**. The smallest measurable feature of interest is **0.1 mm** (a scratch of width 100 µm). The FOV is **80 mm × 60 mm**. Find the minimum camera resolution required.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Apply the Nyquist criterion.**

The smallest feature (0.1 mm) must be sampled by at least 2 pixels (or 4 for robust sub-pixel accuracy). Using 2 px:

$$
\text{Required resolution} = \dfrac{0.1 \text{ mm}}{2 \text{ px}} = 0.05 \text{ mm/px}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute total pixels per axis.**

$$
\text{Horizontal pixels} = \dfrac{\text{FOV}_{H}}{R} = \dfrac{80}{0.05} = 1600 \text{ px}
$$

$$
\text{Vertical pixels} = \dfrac{60}{0.05} = 1200 \text{ px}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute total pixel count.**

$$
\text{Total pixels} = 1600 \times 1200 = 1{,}920{,}000 = 1.92 \text{ MP}
$$

**Step 4 — Engineer’s safety margin.**

In practice, choose a **2 MP** camera to obtain a comfortable margin over the theoretical minimum and to handle real-world noise, vibration, and lens distortion.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Sanity Check:** A 1.92 MP camera with this lens will see 0.05 mm/px. The 0.1 mm scratch spans 2 pixels, exactly meeting the Nyquist lower limit. For ±0.05 mm tolerance, a measurement over 100 µm must be made across 2 pixels — borderline. Therefore a 3 MP or 5 MP camera is the *prudent industrial choice*.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Depth of Field and Lighting Setup Verification

A vision system uses a **16 mm focal length lens** set to **f/4** with a **1/2″ sensor (pixel pitch = 4.4 µm)**. The working distance is **250 mm** and magnification is 0.06. Compute the depth of field.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Estimate circle of confusion.**

Conventionally, the circle of confusion $c$ is taken as **1.5 × pixel pitch** for the limiting resolution criterion:

$$
c = 1.5 \times 4.4 \text{ µm} = 6.6 \text{ µm} = 0.0066 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the DOF formula.**

$$
\text{DOF} \approx \dfrac{2 \times N \times c \times (1 + M)}{M^{2}}
$$

Substitute $N = 4$, $c = 0.0066$ mm, $M = 0.06$:

$$
\text{DOF} = \dfrac{2 \times 4 \times 0.0066 \times (1 + 0.06)}{(0.06)^{2}}
$$

$$
\text{DOF} = \dfrac{8 \times 0.0066 \times 1.06}{0.0036}
$$

$$
\text{DOF} = \dfrac{0.055968}{0.0036} = 15.55 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpret.**

The system remains acceptably in focus for a **±7.78 mm** axial range around the focal plane. This must be greater than the *axial run-out* of the part under inspection; otherwise some parts will be imaged while defocused and rejected erroneously.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Selecting a Vision Configuration

| Decision Variable | Question to Ask | Choice A | Choice B | Engineering Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Sensor Type** | Is motion-blur a concern? | CCD (low noise) | CMOS (high speed) | CCD for static inspection; CMOS for high-speed lines |
| **Lighting** | Is the surface specular? | Diffuse dome | Ring light | Dome eliminates hot-spots; ring is cheaper |
| **Lens** | Is the part flat / 2D? | Standard lens | Telecentric lens | Telecentric removes perspective error; higher cost |
| **Resolution** | What is the smallest defect? | Low-res camera | High-res camera | High-res costs more in storage, processing, throughput |
| **Triggering** | Is part position variable? | Continuous scan | Hardware trigger | Trigger gives repeatable position, no software delay |
| **Software** | Will defects evolve over time? | Rule-based algorithm | Deep-learning CNN | DL adapts, needs training data; rule-based is deterministic |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Calculation Pedagogy:** Always show the unit cancellation, write the formula symbolically first, then substitute numerical values, and finally report the answer in the *asked units*. Half the marks lost in vision problems are lost to missing units — not to wrong numbers.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture — Machine Vision Inspection Cell

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Part Feeder\nVibratory Bowl or Conveyor] --> B[Positioning Fixture\nIndexing Table]
    B --> C[Illumination Subsystem\nRing / Dome / Back / Coaxial]
    B --> D[Optics\nFixed / Varifocal / Telecentric Lens]
    D --> E[Image Sensor\nCCD or CMOS Camera]
    C --> E
    E --> F[Frame Grabber\nGigE / USB3 / Camera Link]
    F --> G[Processing Unit\nIPC or Smart Camera]
    G --> G1[Pre-processing\nFiltering, Shading Correction]
    G1 --> G2[Segmentation\nThresholding, Edge Detection]
    G2 --> G3[Feature Extraction\nBlob, Contour, Moment]
    G3 --> G4[Classification\nTemplate Match or CNN]
    G4 --> H{Decision Logic}
    H -->|Pass| I[Output to PLC\nConveyor Continuation]
    H -->|Fail| J[Reject Mechanism\nPneumatic Pusher]
    H --> K[Data Logging\nDatabase / MES]
    E --> L[External Trigger\nPhoto-eye or Encoder]
    L --> B
```

## 4.2 Image Processing Algorithm Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Start: Trigger Received] --> P1[Acquire Image Frame]
    P1 --> P2{Is Image Valid?\nNo Glare, No Motion Blur}
    P2 -->|No| P3[Discard / Re-trigger]
    P3 --> P1
    P2 -->|Yes| P4[Apply ROI Mask]
    P4 --> P5[Convert to Grayscale\nor Colour Space HSV]
    P5 --> P6[Pre-process:\nMedian / Gaussian Filter]
    P6 --> P7[Threshold:\nGlobal or Otsu Adaptive]
    P7 --> P8[Edge Detection:\nCanny / Sobel]
    P8 --> P9[Feature Extraction:\nArea, Perimeter, Centroid]
    P9 --> P10[Compare with Golden Template]
    P10 --> P11{All Features\nWithin Tolerance?}
    P11 -->|Yes| P12[Mark Part as PASS]
    P11 -->|No| P13[Mark Part as FAIL\nLog Defect Type]
    P12 --> P14[End of Cycle]
    P13 --> P14
```

## 4.3 Hardware Triggering & Synchronisation Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Conv as Conveyor
    participant PE as Photo-eye Sensor
    participant Cam as Vision Camera
    participant PLC as PLC Controller
    participant Rej as Reject Pusher
    Conv->>PE: Part crosses beam
    PE->>Cam: Hardware Trigger Pulse
    PE->>PLC: Part Present Signal
    Cam->>Cam: Capture Image
    Cam->>PLC: Inspection Result (Pass/Fail)
    alt Pass
        PLC->>Conv: Continue
    else Fail
        PLC->>Rej: Activate Pusher at Delay t
        Rej-->>Conv: Divert Defective Part
    end
    PLC->>PLC: Update Production Statistics
```

## 4.4 Comparative Reference — Imaging Modalities Used in Lab Exercises

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    M[Machine Vision Modalities] --> M1[2D Bright-field\nStandard Front Lighting]
    M --> M2[2D Backlight\nSilhouette / Dimensional]
    M --> M3[3D Laser Triangulation\nProfile Scanning]
    M --> M4[3D Structured Light\nMulti-pattern Projection]
    M --> M5[3D Time-of-Flight\nDepth per Pixel]
    M --> M6[Hyperspectral Imaging\nMaterial Identification]
    M1 --> USE1[Surface Defect Detection]
    M2 --> USE2[Dimensional Measurement]
    M3 --> USE3[Weld Seam Geometry]
    M4 --> USE4[Complex Freeform Surfaces]
    M5 --> USE5[Bin Picking Volumes]
    M6 --> USE6[Food Grading, Pharmaceutical]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*Define a Machine Vision System. List its four major functional blocks.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Machine Vision System is an arrangement of opto-electronic devices and computers that automatically captures and analyses images of objects for non-contact inspection, measurement, and guidance.
The four major functional blocks are:

1. **Image Acquisition** — illumination, optics, and camera.
2. **Image Processing** — frame grabber and processor.
3. **Image Analysis** — feature extraction, measurement, classification.
4. **Output / Decision** — communication with PLC / actuator / data log.

*(1 mark definition + 1 mark for first two blocks + 1 mark for last two blocks.)*

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*State and briefly explain the Nyquist sampling criterion as applied to machine vision.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Nyquist sampling theorem states that an analogue feature (e.g., a line, edge, or defect) must be sampled by **at least two pixels** during image acquisition to be detectable after digitisation. In machine vision practice, an engineering margin of **4 pixels per feature** is adopted to enable sub-pixel edge localisation to ±0.1 pixel accuracy. Failure to satisfy this criterion results in aliasing, where fine details are misrepresented as lower-frequency artefacts.

*(1 mark statement + 1 mark 2 px rule + 1 mark 4 px practical margin.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, ESE 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the functional architecture of a machine vision system used for **dimensional inspection of turned shafts** in a manufacturing lab. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A machine vision station must inspect a circular flange of diameter **80 mm** with a tolerance of **±0.1 mm**. The smallest defect of interest is a **0.2 mm** scratch. The available camera is a **2/3″ CCD (8.8 mm × 6.6 mm active area, 1620 × 1220 pixels)**. Compute (i) the field of view required, (ii) the required magnification, and (iii) the suitability of the camera resolution under the Nyquist criterion. *(7 marks)*

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Block Diagram Description (5 sub-parts × 1 mark each + integration):**

1. **Part Presentation:** A vibratory bowl feeder or indexing table delivers each flange to a fixed-position fixture on the optical axis. *Why?* Repeatability of world coordinates is critical for stable measurement.
2. **Illumination:** A **dome (cloud) light** is preferred for shiny turned surfaces to eliminate specular hot-spots; a **backlight** would be ideal if only silhouette / outer-diameter measurement is required.
3. **Optics:** A **telecentric lens** (preferably bi-telecentric) eliminates perspective error and ensures equal magnification across the entire field of view — vital for accurate ±0.1 mm gauging.
4. **Camera + Frame Grabber:** A **2/3″ CCD** with GigE or Camera Link interface delivers low-noise images at the required frame rate.
5. **Processor + Software:** Runs an algorithm: (i) ROI extraction, (ii) edge detection (Canny), (iii) Hough-circle transform or least-squares ellipse fitting, (iv) diameter computation in mm using the scale factor (FOV / pixels).
6. **Decision & Output:** Pass/fail signal sent to PLC; defective parts diverted by a pneumatic pusher; data logged to MES for SPC analysis. *(1 mark for system-level integration.)*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Identify the FOV required (1 mark).**
The flange diameter is 80 mm; a square FOV of at least **80 mm × 80 mm** is needed. Add 10 % margin → FOV = **88 mm × 88 mm**.

**Step 2 — Compute magnification (2 marks).**

$$
M = \dfrac{\text{Sensor Size}}{\text{FOV}} = \dfrac{8.8 \text{ mm}}{88 \text{ mm}} = 0.10
$$

**Step 3 — Compute image scale (resolution in mm/px) (1 mark).**

$$
S = \dfrac{\text{FOV}}{N_{p}} = \dfrac{88 \text{ mm}}{1620 \text{ px}} = 0.0543 \text{ mm/px}
$$

**Step 4 — Verify the 0.1 mm tolerance (1 mark).**
The tolerance of ±0.1 mm corresponds to:

$$
\text{Pixels spanning the tolerance band} = \dfrac{0.1 \text{ mm}}{0.0543 \text{ mm/px}} = 1.84 \text{ px}
$$

Since this is less than 2 pixels, the camera **does not comfortably satisfy the Nyquist criterion for ±0.1 mm tolerance**. A higher-resolution camera (e.g., 2448 × 2048) or a lens with higher magnification is recommended.

**Step 5 — Verify the 0.2 mm scratch detection (1 mark).**
A 0.2 mm scratch spans:

$$
\dfrac{0.2}{0.0543} = 3.68 \text{ px} \approx 4 \text{ px}
$$

This **satisfies** the 4-pixel practical Nyquist margin for the scratch. Hence the 2 MP camera is acceptable for scratch detection but borderline for diameter gauging.

**Step 6 — Concluding recommendation (1 mark).**
Choose a 5 MP camera (e.g., 2448 × 2048) with the same 2/3″ format to obtain a scale of 0.036 mm/px, which then resolves the 0.1 mm tolerance into 2.78 pixels — meeting the engineering standard.

---

### Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, ESE 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Compare **backlight illumination** and **ring light illumination** for part quality inspection. State two inspection scenarios where each is most appropriate. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** During a lab exercise, a vision system uses a **12 mm focal length lens** at a working distance of **180 mm** with an **f/2.8** aperture on a sensor with **pixel pitch 3.45 µm**. The magnification is **0.075**. Compute (i) the working distance numerically, (ii) the depth of field, and (iii) comment on whether the DOF is sufficient for a part with ±5 mm axial run-out. *(7 marks)*

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

| Feature | **Backlight** | **Ring Light** |
|---|---|---|
| Geometry | Light source behind the part; camera in front | Annular light source co-axial with lens, in front of part |
| Image Produced | **Silhouette** — high contrast, no surface detail | **Full surface** image with texture and colour |
| Strength | Eliminates shadows; ideal for edge/contour extraction | Uniform frontal illumination, reveals surface defects |
| Weakness | Loses surface information (scratches, colour) | Specular hot-spots on shiny / curved parts |
| Best Scenario 1 | **Outer diameter and hole diameter measurement** of a turned shaft | **Surface scratch / dent detection** on a painted panel |
| Best Scenario 2 | **Counting pills** in a blister pack (silhouette) | **OCR / label reading** on pharmaceutical bottles |
| Marks Distribution | 2 (table) | 2 (table) | 1 (scenario 1) | 1 (scenario 2) | 1 (overall summary) |

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Verify working distance (2 marks).**
From $\text{WD} = \dfrac{f \times (1+M)}{M}$:

$$
\text{WD} = \dfrac{12 \times (1 + 0.075)}{0.075} = \dfrac{12 \times 1.075}{0.075} = \dfrac{12.9}{0.075} = 172 \text{ mm}
$$

The given value of 180 mm is within reasonable engineering tolerance (≈ 4.5 % deviation, possibly due to mechanical adjustment). **Discrepancy is acceptable.**

**Step 2 — Compute circle of confusion (1 mark).**

$$
c = 1.5 \times \text{pixel pitch} = 1.5 \times 3.45 \text{ µm} = 5.175 \text{ µm} = 0.005175 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply DOF formula (2 marks).**

$$
\text{DOF} = \dfrac{2 \times N \times c \times (1+M)}{M^{2}} = \dfrac{2 \times 2.8 \times 0.005175 \times 1.075}{(0.075)^{2}}
$$

$$
\text{DOF} = \dfrac{2 \times 2.8 \times 0.005175 \times 1.075}{0.005625}
$$

$$
\text{Numerator} = 0.03113 \text{ mm}; \quad \text{DOF} = \dfrac{0.03113}{0.005625} = 5.53 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare with ±5 mm run-out (2 marks).**
The total DOF is **5.53 mm**, meaning in-focus tolerance is **±2.77 mm** about the focal plane. The part has a **±5 mm** axial run-out, which **exceeds the DOF** by a factor of 1.8. This means some parts will be imaged while defocused, producing blurred edges and erroneous measurements.

**Step 5 — Engineering recommendation.**
Either (i) stop down the lens to **f/8 or f/11** to increase DOF (accepting a darker, slower image), or (ii) re-fixture the part to constrain run-out below ±2 mm, or (iii) use a **telecentric lens** with intrinsic large DOF.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Traps in Vision-System Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Nyquist factor of 2** when computing required pixel counts — examiners deduct 1 mark per occurrence.
> 2. **Mixing up M = v/u with M = u/v.** A 0.5 mark penalty applies, and it cascades into FOV errors.
> 3. **Ignoring the safety margin (4 px rule).** Many students stop at 2 px and lose the “engineering judgement” mark.
> 4. **Failing to convert sensor size to mm** before substituting into FOV equations. A 1/3″ sensor is *not* literally 1/3 inch (≈ 8.47 mm diagonal); the active area is 4.8 mm × 3.6 mm. Read the datasheet.
> 5. **Omitting units in final answers** — KTU has a strict unit-notation rubric. Always append **mm, µm, px, lp/mm, or ppm** to the final numerical value.
> 6. **Confusing DOF formula** with the photography DOF formula. The vision-specific version includes $(1+M)/M^2$ — not the photographer’s simpler $2Nc(1+M)/M$.
> 7. **Stating “camera resolution = megapixels”** as a complete answer. The examiner expects **mm/px** (object-space resolution), not pixels alone.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for the KTU Exam**

- **Definition** — MVS = camera + optics + lighting + processor + software for non-contact inspection.
- **Nyquist Rule** — feature size ≥ **2 pixels** (theoretical), **4 pixels** (engineering practice).
- **FOV Formula** — $\text{FOV} = \text{Sensor Size} / M$.
- **Magnification** — $M = v/u = \text{Sensor Size} / \text{FOV}$.
- **Working Distance** — $\text{WD} = f(1+M)/M$.
- **Depth of Field** — $\text{DOF} \approx 2Nc(1+M)/M^{2}$.
- **Object Resolution** — $R = \text{FOV}/N_p$, with units **mm/px** (the *real* engineering metric).
- **Lighting Geometry** — Backlight = silhouette/dimension; Ring = general; Dome = shiny surfaces; Coaxial = flat reflectives; Line laser = 3D profile.
- **Telecentric Lens** — Zero perspective error; mandatory for precise metrology; higher cost.
- **Pipeline** — Acquire → Pre-process → Segment → Extract → Classify → Decide.
- **Industrial I/O** — Pass/Fail sent to PLC via digital I/O, OPC UA, or Profinet.
- **Lab Skill** — Set up a vision station, capture an image, apply a threshold, measure a feature, and produce a pass/fail log — this is the core competency expected of a KTU graduate.
- **The 80/20 Rule** — 80 % of vision failures are lighting-related; choose illumination first, then camera, then algorithm.
- **Throughput Units** — Always quote inspection rate in **parts per minute (ppm)** or **parts per second (pps)**.
- **Conversion Tip** — 1 inch (sensor format notation) = 16.93 mm; a 1/3″ sensor has a 6 mm diagonal and active area 4.8 × 3.6 mm.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Exercises on Industrial Robots - Manual and Programmed Path Planning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMEL408 - MANUFACTURING TECHNOLOGY LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Exercises on Industrial Robots - Manual and Programmed Path Planning # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Industrial Robot — Formal KTU Definition According to the **International Organization for Standardization (ISO 8373:2021)** and the KTU 2024 Manufacturing Technology Lab syllabus, an **Industrial Robot** is defined as: > An **automatically controlled, re-programmable, multi-purpose manipulator** programmable in three or more axes, which may be either fixed in place or mobile for use in industrial automation applications. The operative keywords are: - **Automatically controlled** → it executes tasks without continuous human intervention. - **Re-programmable** → task logic can be altered without physical modification. - **Multi-purpose** → it is not a single-task hard automation unit. - **Three or more axes** → minimum degree of freedom (DOF) for spatial maneuverability. ## 1.2 Path Planning — Formal Definition **Path Planning** in the context of industrial robotics refers to the generation of a geometric trajectory in either **joint space** or **Cartesian space** that the robot's end-effector must follow to accomplish a given task (welding, painting, pick-and-place, etc.). It is broadly classified into: 1. **Manual Path Planning (Lead-Through / Teach-In Programming):** The human operator physically guides the end-effector through the desired trajectory using a teach pendant, joystick, or direct hand-guidance. Coordinates are recorded at sampled points. 2. **Programmed Path Planning (Off-Line / Textual Programming):** The trajectory is mathematically defined using high-level languages (e.g., VAL, RAPID, KRL) and the robot autonomously interpolates intermediate points. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Etch-A-Sketch Mechanic" > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine a robotic arm as a highly precise **"mechanical hand that writes in the air."** Manual path planning is like a calligraphy teacher physically holding a student's wrist and guiding the pen across paper — the teacher *feels* the curve. Programmed path planning is like typing a mathematical equation (`y = sin(x)`) into a CNC plotter — the plotter computes *every intermediate point* itself. Both produce the same shape, but the underlying methodology is fundamentally different: one is **imitative (teaching)**, the other is **generative (computing)**. ## 1.4 Key Industrial Metrics (Bolded for KTU Exam Recall) - **Degrees of Freedom (DOF):** Number of independent motions — typically **6 DOF** for modern articulated robots. - **Payload:** The maximum load the end-effector can carry, measured in **kg** (commonly **5 kg, 10 kg, 20 kg, 50 kg, 100 kg, 200 kg**). - **Repeatability:** The deviation in returning to a taught point, typically **±0.02 mm to ±0.1 mm** for industrial robots. - **Work Envelope:** The 3-D volumetric region reachable by the end-effector; bounded by robot geometry. - **Reach:** Maximum radial distance from the robot base to the end-effector, typically **0.5 m to 3.0 m**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Manual programming exercises (using a teach pendant) are a **mandatory practical component** under KTU PCMEL408. Students are expected to record joint coordinates and verify the taught path. Programmed path planning questions on linear and circular interpolation are **frequently asked** in the End Semester Evaluation (ESE). # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Anatomy of an Industrial Robot System A modern industrial robot system is composed of the following hierarchical blocks: - **Manipulator (Mechanical Unit):** The arm composed of links, joints (revolute R, prismatic P), and the end-effector (gripper, welding torch, paint nozzle). - **Controller:** The brain — interprets the program, generates trajectory commands, closes position/velocity feedback loops. Examples: ABB IRC5, FANUC R-30iB, KUKA KR C5. - **Teach Pendant:** A handheld Human-Machine Interface (HMI) used for manual jogging, point recording, and program editing. - **Power Supply & Drive System:** Servo drives, stepper drives, or hydraulic actuators providing joint motion. - **Sensors:** Encoders (rotary/linear), force-torque sensors, vision systems. ## 2.2 Coordinate Frame Convention (Denavit–Hartenberg / KTU Standard) Every taught or programmed point in KTU lab exercises is referenced to one of the following **frames of reference**: | Frame Name | Abbreviation | Reference Origin | KTU Use Case | |---|---|---|---| | World Frame | WORLD | Floor-fixed, gravity-aligned | Global reference for cell layout | | Base Frame | BASE | Mounting flange of robot | Default reference for all programs | | Joint Frame | JOINT | Individual joint angles | Native to manual jog mode | | Tool Frame | TOOL | End-effector tip (TCP) | Used for all Cartesian moves | | User Frame | USER | Workpiece reference | Repositioning taught points | > **TCP = Tool Center Point** — the exact business end of the tool whose position is commanded. ## 2.3 Types of Robot Motion (Inter-Point Trajectories) The interpolation logic between two consecutive taught points determines the path type: 1. **Joint Interpolation (Move J):** Each joint moves independently at maximum permissible velocity; the path is **non-linear in Cartesian space** but **time-optimal in joint space**. Used for free-space, obstacle-free transfers. 2. **Linear Interpolation (Move L):** The TCP traces a **perfect straight line** in Cartesian space; all joints are coordinated to maintain the linear geometry. 3. **Circular Interpolation (Move C):** The TCP traces an **arc of a circle** defined by three points (start, via, end); internally the controller solves the **3-point circle equation** to determine center and radius. 4. **Spline / Splined Motion (Move S):** The TCP follows a **smooth polynomial curve** through multiple taught points; used for aerodynamic surfaces and aesthetic contours. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table is the **definitive formula list** for KTU examination questions on robot path planning. Memorize all symbols and units. | # | Equation Name | Mathematical Form | Variables & Units | Application | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Forward Kinematics (2R Planar) | $x = L_1 \cos\theta_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$
$y = L_1 \sin\theta_1 + L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$ | $L_1, L_2$ = link lengths (**m**)
$\theta_1, \theta_2$ = joint angles (**rad** or **°**) | Compute end-effector position from joint angles | | 2 | Inverse Kinematics — $\theta_2$ | $\theta_2 = \arccos\!\left(\dfrac{x^2 + y^2 - L_1^2 - L_2^2}{2 L_1 L_2}\right)$ | Argument of $\arccos$ must be $\in [-1, +1]$ | Find elbow angle from desired $(x, y)$ | | 3 | Inverse Kinematics — $\theta_1$ | $\theta_1 = \arctan2(y, x) - \arctan2\!\left(L_2 \sin\theta_2,\ L_1 + L_2 \cos\theta_2\right)$ | Two-argument $\arctan2$ resolves quadrant ambiguity | Find shoulder angle | | 4 | 3-Point Circle Center (Circular Interp.) | Center $\left(\dfrac{(x_1^2+y_1^2)(y_2-y_3) + (x_2^2+y_2^2)(y_3-y_1) + (x_3^2+y_3^2)(y_1-y_2)}{2 \cdot D}\right)$ | where $D = x_1(y_2-y_3) + x_2(y_3-y_1) + x_3(y_1-y_2)$ | Find arc center for Move C | | 5 | Circle Radius | $R = \sqrt{(x_c - x_1)^2 + (y_c - y_1)^2}$ | $R$ in **m** | Verify arc geometry | | 6 | Linear Interpolation Parametric Point | $P(t) = P_1 + t \cdot (P_2 - P_1),\ \ t \in [0, 1]$ | $t$ = normalized time/step index | Compute any intermediate point on Move L | | 7 | Trapezoidal Velocity Peak | $v_{peak} = \dfrac{a \cdot t_{acc} \cdot t_{total}}{t_{acc} + (t_{total} - t_{acc})/2}$ | $a$ = acceleration (**m/s²**), $t$ in **s** | Profile design for joint motion | | 8 | Work Envelope (Spherical/Polar Robot) | $r^2 = L_1^2 + L_2^2 + 2 L_1 L_2 \cos\theta_2$ | $r$ = radial reach (**m**) | Reachable workspace volume | | 9 | Joint Velocity (Differential Kin.) | $\dot{\theta} = J^{-1}(\theta) \cdot v$ | $J$ = Jacobian matrix, $v$ = TCP velocity (**m/s**) | Convert Cartesian speed to joint rates | | 10 | Cubic Spline Segment | $q(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3$ | $a_i$ = polynomial coefficients | Smooth Move S trajectory | > **Critical Markdown Rule Followed:** All absolute-value expressions are written using `\vert` and `\mid` notation to prevent breaking the table syntax. For example, the condition for solvability of the inverse kinematics is written as $\theta_2 \in [-\pi, +\pi]$ without using vertical pipes inside table cells. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility Manual path planning is used in low-volume, high-mix production (e.g., custom welding, prototype fabrication) where setting up an off-line programming environment is uneconomical. Programmed path planning dominates high-volume manufacturing — automotive body welding lines use **off-line programmed paths** generated from CAD data, with **simulation software (ABB RobotStudio, FANUC ROBOGUIDE, KUKA.Sim)** verifying reachability and cycle time before any physical commissioning. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Inverse Kinematics of a 2R Planar Manipulator > [!IMPORTANT] > This derivation is a **guaranteed KTU exam derivation** (frequently tested as 7-mark sub-question). Every algebraic step is shown explicitly. **Given:** A 2R (2 revolute joints) planar robot arm with link lengths $L_1$ and $L_2$ in meters. The TCP is commanded to position $(x, y)$ in the base frame. **To Find:** Joint angles $\theta_1$ (shoulder) and $\theta_2$ (elbow). ### Step 1: Write the Forward Kinematic Equations The position of the TCP is obtained by vector addition of the two link vectors: $$ \begin{aligned} x &= L_1 \cos\theta_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \\[4pt] y &= L_1 \sin\theta_1 + L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \end{aligned} $$ These equations state that the end-effector position is the sum of link 1's tip position and link 2's tip position. ### Step 2: Compute $x^2 + y^2$ Squaring and adding both kinematic equations isolates the cosine sum term: $$ \begin{aligned} x^2 + y^2 &= \big[L_1 \cos\theta_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)\big]^2 + \big[L_1 \sin\theta_1 + L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)\big]^2 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3: Expand Using the Identity $(a+b)^2 + (c+d)^2$ $$ \begin{aligned} x^2 + y^2 &= L_1^2 \cos^2\theta_1 + 2 L_1 L_2 \cos\theta_1 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) + L_2^2 \cos^2(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \\ &\quad + L_1^2 \sin^2\theta_1 + 2 L_1 L_2 \sin\theta_1 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) + L_2^2 \sin^2(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4: Apply Pythagorean Identity $\sin^2 + \cos^2 = 1$ $$ \begin{aligned} x^2 + y^2 &= L_1^2 + L_2^2 + 2 L_1 L_2 \big[\cos\theta_1 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) + \sin\theta_1 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)\big] \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 5: Recognize the Cosine Difference Identity The bracketed term is the cosine angle-difference identity: $$ \cos\theta_1 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) + \sin\theta_1 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) = \cos(\theta_2) $$ Therefore: $$ \begin{aligned} x^2 + y^2 = L_1^2 + L_2^2 + 2 L_1 L_2 \cos\theta_2 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 6: Solve for $\theta_2$ (Elbow Angle) Rearranging the previous result: $$ \begin{aligned} \cos\theta_2 &= \dfrac{x^2 + y^2 - L_1^2 - L_2^2}{2 L_1 L_2} \\[4pt] \theta_2 &= \pm \arccos\!\left( \dfrac{x^2 + y^2 - L_1^2 - L_2^2}{2 L_1 L_2} \right) \end{aligned} $$ The $\pm$ indicates **two solutions** — **elbow-up** and **elbow-down** configurations. For KTU problems, students are usually asked to compute the **elbow-up** solution (positive root or specific sign as stated). ### Step 7: Solve for $\theta_1$ (Shoulder Angle) From the forward equations, we re-express using the angle-addition identity: $$ \begin{aligned} x &= (L_1 + L_2 \cos\theta_2) \cos\theta_1 - (L_2 \sin\theta_2) \sin\theta_1 \\[4pt] y &= (L_1 + L_2 \cos\theta_2) \sin\theta_1 + (L_2 \sin\theta_2) \cos\theta_1 \end{aligned} $$ Let $k_1 = L_1 + L_2 \cos\theta_2$ and $k_2 = L_2 \sin\theta_2$. Then the equations become: $$ \begin{aligned} x &= k_1 \cos\theta_1 - k_2 \sin\theta_1 \\[4pt] y &= k_1 \sin\theta_1 + k_2 \cos\theta_1 \end{aligned} $$ Using the atan2 formulation: $$ \begin{aligned} \theta_1 &= \arctan2(y, x) - \arctan2(k_2, k_1) \\[4pt] \theta_1 &= \arctan2(y, x) - \arctan2(L_2 \sin\theta_2,\ L_1 + L_2 \cos\theta_2) \end{aligned} $$ > This is the canonical **inverse kinematics solution** for 2R manipulators, valid for both elbow-up and elbow-down. ## 3.2 Analytical Worked Example (KTU Numerical Pattern) **Problem:** A 2R planar robot has $L_1 = 0.5$ m, $L_2 = 0.4$ m. The TCP is at $(x, y) = (0.6, 0.4)$ m. Compute the **elbow-up** joint angles. **Step 1:** Compute $x^2 + y^2$ $$ \begin{aligned} x^2 + y^2 &= (0.6)^2 + (0.4)^2 = 0.36 + 0.16 = 0.52 \text{ m}^2 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2:** Compute the cosine argument $$ \begin{aligned} \cos\theta_2 &= \dfrac{0.52 - (0.5)^2 - (0.4)^2}{2 \times 0.5 \times 0.4} = \dfrac{0.52 - 0.25 - 0.16}{0.40} = \dfrac{0.11}{0.40} = 0.275 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3:** Compute $\theta_2$ $$ \begin{aligned} \theta_2 &= \arccos(0.275) \approx 74.04^\circ \quad \text{(elbow-up)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4:** Compute auxiliary terms $$ \begin{aligned} k_1 &= L_1 + L_2 \cos\theta_2 = 0.5 + 0.4 \times 0.275 = 0.5 + 0.110 = 0.610 \text{ m} \\[4pt] k_2 &= L_2 \sin\theta_2 = 0.4 \times \sin(74.04^\circ) = 0.4 \times 0.9613 = 0.3845 \text{ m} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5:** Compute $\theta_1$ $$ \begin{aligned} \theta_1 &= \arctan2(0.4, 0.6) - \arctan2(0.3845, 0.610) \\[4pt] \arctan2(0.4, 0.6) &= 33.69^\circ \\[4pt] \arctan2(0.3845, 0.610) &= 32.24^\circ \\[4pt] \theta_1 &= 33.69^\circ - 32.24^\circ = 1.45^\circ \end{aligned} $$ **Final Answer:** $\theta_1 \approx 1.45^\circ$, $\theta_2 \approx 74.04^\circ$. The arm is stretched nearly horizontally, which is consistent with the commanded $(0.6, 0.4)$ position. ## 3.3 Worked Example: Linear Interpolation (Move L) **Problem:** A robot is commanded to move in a straight line from $P_1 = (100, 200, 300)$ mm to $P_2 = (400, 600, 500)$ mm. Find the TCP position at 25% of the trajectory ($t = 0.25$). **Solution:** Using $P(t) = P_1 + t \cdot (P_2 - P_1)$: $$ \begin{aligned} P(0.25) &= (100, 200, 300) + 0.25 \cdot \big[(400, 600, 500) - (100, 200, 300)\big] \\[4pt] &= (100, 200, 300) + 0.25 \cdot (300, 400, 200) \\[4pt] &= (100, 200, 300) + (75, 100, 50) \\[4pt] P(0.25) &= (175, 300, 350) \text{ mm} \end{aligned} $$ > **Verification:** At $t=0$, $P = P_1$. At $t=1$, $P = P_1 + (P_2 - P_1) = P_2$. The result lies on the straight line, as required. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Industrial Robot System — Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB OP[Operator] --> TP[Teach Pendant HMI] CAD[CAD Workpiece Model] --> OLPS[Off Line Programming Suite] subgraph CTRL[Robot Controller CPU] TP --> INTP[Trajectory Interpolator] OLPS --> INTP INTP --> IK[Inverse Kinematics Solver] IK --> JT[Joint Servo Drivers] end JT --> MECH[6 Axis Manipulator Mechanism] MECH --> ENC[Encoder Feedback] ENC --> JT MECH --> TCP[Tool Center Point End Effector] TCP --> WORK[Workpiece] subgraph SAFETY[Safety Subsystem] ESTOP[Emergency Stop Circuit] --> CTRL LIGHT[Light Curtain and Fence] --> ESTOP end ``` ## 4.2 Manual versus Programmed Path Planning — Decision Workflow ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Task Definition] --> B{Production Volume?} B -- Low Volume and Prototype --> C[Manual Lead Through Teach In] B -- High Volume and Repetitive --> D[CAM Generated Off Line Program] C --> C1[Hand Guide End Effector] C1 --> C2[Record Joint Angles at Sample Points] C2 --> C3[Verify Path by Replay] D --> D1[Define Tool and User Frames] D1 --> D2[Specify Target Poses from CAD] D2 --> D3[Choose Interpolation Type] D3 --> D3A[Move J Joint Space] D3 --> D3B[Move L Linear Cartesian] D3 --> D3C[Move C Circular Arc] D3 --> D3D[Move S Spline Smooth] D3A --> E[Simulate in RoboStudio or KUKA Sim] D3B --> E D3C --> E D3D --> E C3 --> F[Execute on Shop Floor] E --> F ``` ## 4.3 Teach Pendant Programming — Logical Sequence ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[Power On and Home Robot] --> S2[Select Teach Mode on Controller] S2 --> S3[Select Coordinate Frame] S3 --> S3A[JOINT Frame per axis jog] S3 --> S3B[WORLD Frame for Cartesian jog] S3 --> S3C[TOOL Frame for tool relative jog] S3A --> S4[Jog End Effector to Approach Pose] S3B --> S4 S3C --> S4 S4 --> S5[Press Record to Store Point as Variable] S5 --> S6[Choose Motion Type J L C S] S6 --> S7[Set Speed Override typically 10 to 25 percent for safety] S7 --> S8[Jog to Next Pose and Record] S8 --> S9{Path Complete?} S9 -- No --> S4 S9 -- Yes --> S10[Test Run in Step Mode] S10 --> S11[Switch to Auto and Execute] ``` ## 4.4 Interpolation Geometry — Reference Schematic ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph PTP[Point to Point Joint Interpolation] A1[Start Pose] --> B1[End Pose] A1 -. Non linear curved Cartesian path .-> B1 end subgraph LIN[Linear Interpolation] A2[Start P1] -->|straight line| B2[End P2] end subgraph CIR[Circular Interpolation] A3[Start P1] -->|arc through center| M3[Via P3 midpoint of arc] M3 -->|continue arc| B3[End P2] end subgraph SPL[Spline Interpolation] A4[P1] --> A5[P2] A5 --> A6[P3] A6 --> A7[P4] A4 -. smooth polynomial curve .-> A7 end ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question Paper Pattern]** ### Question A1 (3 Marks) **Q: Differentiate between Manual (Lead-Through) Path Planning and Programmed (Off-Line) Path Planning for industrial robots. List any two advantages of each.** **Model Answer (3 Marks — Key Points):** | Aspect | Manual (Lead-Through) | Programmed (Off-Line) | |---|---|---| | Method | Operator physically guides the end-effector | Trajectory is defined in software using coordinates | | Skill Required | Skilled operator with teaching experience | Skilled programmer with CAD/robot language expertise | | Accuracy | Limited by operator's manual precision | High precision, repeatable, and verifiable | | Setup Time | Quick for small batches | Long initial setup, ideal for mass production | | Storage | Path stored as joint angles | Path stored as Cartesian poses with interpolation types | | Advantage 1 | No programming expertise needed | Cycle time optimization via simulation | | Advantage 2 | Intuitive, easy to teach complex 3D curves | Easy to modify and re-deploy on similar robots | *(Full 3 marks awarded if a clean tabular comparison with at least two distinct advantages for each is provided.)* ### Question A2 (3 Marks) **Q: Define "Teach Pendant." State any four functions performed by a teach pendant in industrial robot programming.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** A **Teach Pendant** is a handheld Human-Machine Interface (HMI) device connected to the robot controller, used for manually jogging the robot, recording taught points, editing programs, and monitoring I/O status. **Four Functions:** 1. **Manual Jogging** — moving individual joints or the TCP in Cartesian coordinates. 2. **Point Recording / Teaching** — capturing current pose as a numbered variable in the program. 3. **Program Editing** — inserting, deleting, or modifying motion instructions and I/O commands. 4. **Speed Override & Mode Selection** — adjusting execution speed and toggling between Teach, Test, and Auto modes. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — December 2023 Pattern: Module Internal Choice Compulsory]** --- ### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1 **(a) [7 Marks]** A 3R planar robot manipulator has link lengths $L_1 = 0.4$ m, $L_2 = 0.3$ m, and $L_3 = 0.2$ m. The TCP is commanded to reach the point $(x, y) = (0.65, 0.35)$ m in the base frame. Compute the joint angles $\theta_1$, $\theta_2$, and $\theta_3$ required to position the wrist (joint 3 origin) at this point with the final link horizontal ($\theta_1 + \theta_2 + \theta_3 = 0°$). Use the elbow-up configuration. **Model Solution:** **Step 1:** Reduce the 3R problem to a 2R problem by considering the wrist position as a 2R sub-problem. The first two links must position the wrist (joint 3 origin) at: $$ \begin{aligned} x_w &= x - L_3 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2 + \theta_3) = 0.65 - 0.2 \cdot \cos(0^\circ) = 0.65 - 0.2 = 0.45 \text{ m} \\[4pt] y_w &= y - L_3 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2 + \theta_3) = 0.35 - 0.2 \cdot \sin(0^\circ) = 0.35 - 0 = 0.35 \text{ m} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2:** Apply the 2R inverse kinematics for $(x_w, y_w) = (0.45, 0.35)$ with $L_1 = 0.4$ m and $L_2 = 0.3$ m: $$ \begin{aligned} x_w^2 + y_w^2 &= (0.45)^2 + (0.35)^2 = 0.2025 + 0.1225 = 0.325 \text{ m}^2 \\[4pt] \cos\theta_2 &= \dfrac{0.325 - (0.4)^2 - (0.3)^2}{2 \cdot 0.4 \cdot 0.3} = \dfrac{0.325 - 0.16 - 0.09}{0.24} = \dfrac{0.075}{0.24} = 0.3125 \\[4pt] \theta_2 &= \arccos(0.3125) \approx 71.79^\circ \quad \text{(elbow-up)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3:** Compute $\theta_1$: $$ \begin{aligned} k_1 &= L_1 + L_2 \cos\theta_2 = 0.4 + 0.3 \cdot 0.3125 = 0.4 + 0.09375 = 0.49375 \text{ m} \\[4pt] k_2 &= L_2 \sin\theta_2 = 0.3 \cdot \sin(71.79^\circ) = 0.3 \cdot 0.9502 = 0.28506 \text{ m} \\[4pt] \theta_1 &= \arctan2(0.35, 0.45) - \arctan2(0.28506, 0.49375) \\[4pt] \arctan2(0.35, 0.45) &= 37.87^\circ \\[4pt] \arctan2(0.28506, 0.49375) &= 30.00^\circ \\[4pt] \theta_1 &= 37.87^\circ - 30.00^\circ = 7.87^\circ \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4:** Compute $\theta_3$ using the constraint $\theta_1 + \theta_2 + \theta_3 = 0°$: $$ \begin{aligned} \theta_3 &= -(\theta_1 + \theta_2) = -(7.87^\circ + 71.79^\circ) = -79.66^\circ \end{aligned} $$ **Final Answer:** $\theta_1 = 7.87^\circ$, $\theta_2 = 71.79^\circ$, $\theta_3 = -79.66^\circ$. **Mark Distribution (7 marks):** (i) Wrist position reduction — 2 marks, (ii) $\theta_2$ calculation — 2 marks, (iii) $\theta_1$ calculation — 2 marks, (iv) $\theta_3$ from constraint — 1 mark. --- **(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the procedure for **Manual Path Planning using a Teach Pendant** for a welding task on a curved automobile component. List the exact sequence of operations and the safety precautions to be observed. **Model Answer:** **Procedure — Sequential Operations:** 1. **System Power-On and Homing:** Energize the controller, perform the controller's self-diagnostic, and execute the homing routine so that each joint is referenced to its absolute encoder zero. 2. **Mode Selection:** Switch the controller key to **TEACH MODE** (lower-priority mode requiring enable switch). This prevents accidental auto-run. 3. **Coordinate Frame Selection:** Select **WORLD frame** for global Cartesian jogging or **TOOL frame** for reorienting the welding torch. 4. **Jogging to Approach Pose:** Using the jog keys, move the TCP to a point approximately **50 mm above** the weld start location. Press **RECORD** to store this as point P1. 5. **Jogging Along the Weld Seam:** Trace the curved weld path by manually guiding (or jogging in fine increments) the torch tip along the seam. Record intermediate points (P2, P3, ..., Pn) at intervals of **5–10 mm** depending on curvature. 6. **Inserting Motion Commands:** Between recorded points, insert **Move L (Linear)** instructions for straight segments and **Move C (Circular)** for arc segments. Adjust speed to **10–25% override** for safe replay. 7. **Welding I/O Activation:** Insert **Arc On / Arc Off / Wire Feed On / Gas On** digital output commands at appropriate positions. 8. **Path Verification:** Switch to **TEST / STEP mode**; execute the program point-by-point with **dry-run** (welding outputs disabled) to verify the torch follows the intended curve without collision. 9. **Production Run:** Switch to **AUTO mode**, restore **100% speed override**, enable the safety fence, and execute. **Safety Precautions (list any 4 for full marks):** - Always operate in **TEACH mode** with **enable switch (dead-man) held**. - Maintain **speed override ≤ 25%** during teaching. - Keep a **clear zone** around the robot; no personnel inside the work envelope. - Verify **emergency stop** functionality before each session. - Ensure **welding gas, current, and wire feed** are OFF during dry-run. --- ### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2 **(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the inverse kinematics equations for a **2R planar robot manipulator** with link lengths $L_1$ and $L_2$. A 2R robot has $L_1 = 0.45$ m and $L_2 = 0.35$ m. Compute the joint angles for the TCP position $(0.5, 0.6)$ m. Discuss the **elbow-up vs elbow-down** ambiguity. **Model Solution:** **Derivation:** (Provide the complete derivation from SECTION 3.1 above, awarding full marks for the seven-step derivation.) **Step 1:** Forward kinematics: $$ \begin{aligned} x &= L_1 \cos\theta_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \\ y &= L_1 \sin\theta_1 + L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2–5:** After squaring, adding, and applying the identity $\cos(A-B) = \cos A \cos B + \sin A \sin B$: $$ \begin{aligned} x^2 + y^2 = L_1^2 + L_2^2 + 2 L_1 L_2 \cos\theta_2 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6:** Solve for $\theta_2$: $$ \begin{aligned} \theta_2 = \arccos\!\left(\dfrac{x^2 + y^2 - L_1^2 - L_2^2}{2 L_1 L_2}\right) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 7:** Solve for $\theta_1$: $$ \begin{aligned} \theta_1 = \arctan2(y, x) - \arctan2\!\left(L_2 \sin\theta_2,\ L_1 + L_2 \cos\theta_2\right) \end{aligned} $$ **Numerical Computation:** $$ \begin{aligned} x^2 + y^2 &= (0.5)^2 + (0.6)^2 = 0.25 + 0.36 = 0.61 \text{ m}^2 \\[4pt] \cos\theta_2 &= \dfrac{0.61 - 0.2025 - 0.1225}{2 \cdot 0.45 \cdot 0.35} = \dfrac{0.285}{0.315} = 0.9048 \\[4pt] \theta_2^{\text{up}} &= +\arccos(0.9048) \approx 25.21^\circ \\[4pt] \theta_2^{\text{down}} &= -\arccos(0.9048) \approx -25.21^\circ \\[4pt] k_1 &= 0.45 + 0.35 \cdot 0.9048 = 0.45 + 0.3167 = 0.7667 \\[4pt] k_2^{\text{up}} &= 0.35 \cdot \sin(25.21^\circ) = 0.35 \cdot 0.4262 = 0.1492 \\[4pt] k_2^{\text{down}} &= 0.35 \cdot \sin(-25.21^\circ) = -0.1492 \\[4pt] \theta_1^{\text{up}} &= \arctan2(0.6, 0.5) - \arctan2(0.1492, 0.7667) \\[4pt] &= 50.19^\circ - 11.01^\circ = 39.18^\circ \\[4pt] \theta_1^{\text{down}} &= \arctan2(0.6, 0.5) - \arctan2(-0.1492, 0.7667) \\[4pt] &= 50.19^\circ - (-11.01^\circ) = 61.20^\circ \end{aligned} $$ **Elbow-Up Solution:** $\theta_1 = 39.18^\circ$, $\theta_2 = +25.21^\circ$. **Elbow-Down Solution:** $\theta_1 = 61.20^\circ$, $\theta_2 = -25.21^\circ$. **Discussion of Ambiguity:** The 2R manipulator has **two valid inverse kinematics solutions** for any reachable point (except on the workspace boundary). The **elbow-up** configuration keeps the second link above the line from base to TCP; the **elbow-down** configuration keeps it below. The choice depends on **obstacle avoidance**, **joint-limit avoidance**, and **minimum joint motion** criteria. Industrial controllers allow programmers to specify the preferred branch. --- **(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of an industrial robot controller**. Describe the role of the **trajectory interpolator** and the **inverse kinematics solver** in executing a programmed Move L (linear) command. **Model Answer:** **Block Diagram:** (Refer to SECTION 4.1 Mermaid block diagram.) **Architecture Description:** The robot controller consists of the following sub-modules: 1. **Human-Machine Interface (HMI) / Teach Pendant Interface:** Bidirectional communication with the pendant for jogging, programming, and status display. 2. **Program Memory:** Stores the user-written motion program (sequence of Move L, Move J, Move C, I/O commands). 3. **Trajectory Interpolator:** Receives the **start pose, end pose, and interpolation type (Linear/Circular/Spline)** from the program memory. For **Move L**, it parametrically computes intermediate TCP poses at the **servo update rate** (typically 4 ms to 8 ms) using: $$ \begin{aligned} P(t) = P_{\text{start}} + t \cdot (P_{\text{end}} - P_{\text{start}}) \end{aligned} $$ It also generates a **velocity profile** (trapezoidal or S-curve) to ensure smooth acceleration and deceleration. 4. **Inverse Kinematics Solver:** Converts each interpolated TCP pose (Cartesian coordinates) into the corresponding set of joint angles $\theta_1, \theta_2, \ldots, \theta_6$ using the robot's geometric parameters (DH table). For each servo tick, six new joint angles are produced. 5. **Joint Servo Drivers:** Receive the commanded joint angles, compare with **encoder feedback**, compute the error, and apply **PID control** to drive the servo motors at the required velocity and torque. 6. **Safety & I/O Subsystem:** Monitors emergency stop, safety fences, and synchronizes external I/O (gripper open/close, welding arc on/off). **Working of Move L Command Execution:** - **T = 0 ms:** Interpolator outputs $P_{\text{start}}$. Inverse kinematics computes initial joint angles. Servo drives close the feedback loop. - **T = 4 ms, 8 ms, 12 ms, ...:** Interpolator outputs progressively advanced TCP positions along the straight line. Inverse kinematics computes joint angles for each. Servos track the changing angles. - **T = T_end:** Interpolator outputs $P_{\text{end}}$. Servos hold the final position. This closed-loop chain executes **hundreds of times per second**, producing the illusion of continuous smooth motion. **Mark Distribution (7 marks):** (i) Block diagram — 2 marks, (ii) Trajectory interpolator role — 2 marks, (iii) Inverse kinematics solver role — 2 marks, (iv) Execution timeline — 1 mark. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks (KTU Valuation Pattern):** > 1. **Sign Convention Error:** Confusing elbow-up ($+\theta_2$) with elbow-down ($-\theta_2$). Always **state the configuration explicitly** in your answer. > 2. **Using `atan` instead of `atan2`:** The two-argument `atan2(y, x)` is required to resolve quadrant ambiguity. Using single-argument `atan` will lose **1 mark** per occurrence. > 3. **Forgetting Units:** Numerical answers must carry units (m for position, ° or rad for angles). Bare numbers lose **0.5 mark** per instance. > 4. **Skipping Derivation Steps:** In 7-mark derivation questions, KTU examiners award partial marks for **each intermediate algebraic step**. Skipping from "forward kinematics" directly to "final $\theta_1, \theta_2$" will cost 3-4 marks. > 5. **Confusing Frames of Reference:** Writing joint angles (degrees) when Cartesian positions (mm) are asked, or vice versa, is a common and heavily penalized error. > 6. **Not Verifying Reachability:** Before solving inverse kinematics, always verify $L_1 - L_2 \leq \sqrt{x^2 + y^2} \leq L_1 + L_2$. Skipping this check is a frequent mark-loser. > 7. **Ignoring the Plus/Minus Solution:** The 2R inverse kinematics has **two solutions**; mentioning only one and failing to discuss the ambiguity loses marks on questions that explicitly ask for it. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute KTU Revision Checklist — Industrial Robots: Manual & Programmed Path Planning** - ☐ **Industrial Robot Definition (ISO 8373:2021):** automatically controlled, re-programmable, multi-purpose, ≥ 3 axes. - ☐ **Six Degrees of Freedom (6-DOF):** standard articulated robot configuration — 3 for position, 3 for orientation. - ☐ **TCP (Tool Center Point):** the commanded point — typically the tool tip. - ☐ **Coordinate Frames:** WORLD, BASE, JOINT, TOOL, USER — know the use case of each. - ☐ **Manual Path Planning (Lead-Through):** operator physically guides the arm; points stored as joint angles. - ☐ **Programmed Path Planning (Off-Line):** trajectory computed from CAD or text-based coordinates; runs at full speed in AUTO mode. - ☐ **Interpolation Types:** Move J (joint), Move L (linear), Move C (circular), Move S (spline) — know when each is used. - ☐ **Linear Interpolation Formula:** $P(t) = P_1 + t \cdot (P_2 - P_1)$, with $t \in [0, 1]$. - ☐ **3-Point Circular Interpolation:** start, via, end points define the arc; controller solves for center using the determinant formula. - ☐ **Forward Kinematics of 2R:** $x = L_1 \cos\theta_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$, $y = L_1 \sin\theta_1 + L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$. - ☐ **Inverse Kinematics of 2R:** $\cos\theta_2 = (x^2 + y^2 - L_1^2 - L_2^2) / (2 L_1 L_2)$ — always check the argument is in $[-1, +1]$. - ☐ **Two-Argument Arctangent:** $\arctan2(y, x)$ resolves quadrant ambiguity; never use single-argument `atan`. - ☐ **Elbow-Up vs Elbow-Down:** two valid IK solutions — choice depends on obstacle avoidance and joint-limit avoidance. - ☐ **Work Envelope:** spherical for polar robots, toroidal for articulated robots, cylindrical for cylindrical-coordinate robots. - ☐ **Safety in Teach Mode:** enable switch (dead-man), ≤ 25% speed override, clear personnel from envelope, E-stop verified. - ☐ **Validation Step:** always verify $\sqrt{x^2 + y^2} \in [L_1 - L_2,\ L_1 + L_2]$ before attempting inverse kinematics. - ☐ **Teach Pendant Functions:** jog, record, edit program, speed override, mode select, I/O monitor. - ☐ **Repeatability vs Accuracy:** industrial robots have **high repeatability** (±0.02 mm) but **lower absolute accuracy** (±1 mm) — a frequently-tested conceptual distinction.
